name: insignia_bim_business_glossary
description: Deduplicated business glossary grouped by business-term asset_id with standardized metadata and relationships.

terms.name: (Cash Account) Dollar-based minimum
terms.definition: The minimum balance, in dollar terms, that a Client must maintain in their Cash Account Preferences (i.e. Cash Account Minimum).

terms.name: (Cash Account) Percentage-based minimum
terms.definition: The minimum balance that a Client must maintain in their Cash Account Preferences (i.e. Cash Account Minimum), however expressed as percentage of the Account's total investments.

terms.name: (Cash Account) Top up instructions
terms.definition: See Cash Account Preferences

terms.name: (Employer) Team
terms.definition: Employer Plans are normally assigned to a specific team within Operations to manage their Accounts.

terms.name: (Financial) Advice Fees
terms.definition: Is usually deducted directly from the member's Account and is to pay for personal financial advice that the member has sought from a licensed financial adviser .

terms.name: (Grant of) Probate
terms.definition: A Grant of Probate means a supreme court recognises a Will as legally valid. It enables the Executor to fully represent the estate and distribute assets to the beneficiaries named in the Will.

terms.name: (Lost Member Details) ATO Address Verified
terms.definition:
A member's address can be verified using the ATO Verification portal.
Verification details are captured in this field.

terms.name: (Lost Member Details) Last Contribution Date
terms.definition: The date that member Contributions were last receipted into a Member's  Account

terms.name: (Lost Member Details) Missing Client Information
terms.definition:
If a member is considered a Lost Member, it's usually because they are uncontactable due to the current address details being missing or invalid.
Missing Client information includes the missing/invalid components. Can also include Date of Birth and TFN , which can be used to trace a member via the ATO's website.

terms.name: (Lost Member Details) Returned Mail Details
terms.definition:
Content of returned mail particulars.
Members can be classified as a Lost Member if within the last two years the  member's address cannot be verified as  correct or there is reason to believe the address is incorrect.

terms.name: (Lost) Effective Date
terms.definition:
The date at which an Employer's Lost status/flag was set to Lost.
Usually on a predefined occurrence of receipt of returned mail and/or
a valid Employer's  address has not been obtained.

terms.name: (Lost) Reason
terms.definition:
The reason why an Employer's Lost status was set to Y (Lost) -
Returned mail; or
A valid address has not been obtained

terms.name: (Member AML Details) Risk Rating
terms.definition:
A Risk Rating must be assigned for for each customer by assessing a customer for ML/TF purposes at onboarding and as part of ongoing customer due diligence. 
The assessment must determine if the customer is risk rated as -
i.Unacceptable
ii.High.
iii Medium
iv.Low

terms.name: (Member Family Account) Employer
terms.definition: The Employer  Number/Name linked to the Member/Account Number of the linked Family Account

terms.name: (Member Family Account) Fund
terms.definition: The Member/Account Number of the linked Family Account

terms.name: (Member Family Account)Member
terms.definition: The Fund Number/Name linked to the Member/Account Number of the linked Family Account

terms.name: (Member) Adviser Code
terms.definition: A unique code to identify the Adviser

terms.name: (Member) Date Joined Company
terms.definition:
The date that the member joined the Employer's Business as a member.
Note that this can differ to the Date Joined Fund.

terms.name: (Member) Date Joined Fund
terms.definition: The date that the Employer opened an individual Member Account for the Member in the existing Fund

terms.name: (Member) Date of Birth
terms.definition: The member's birthdate as captured in the registry platform.

terms.name: (Member) Design and Distribution Obligations
terms.definition:
The Design and Distribution Obligations are intended to help consumers obtain appropriate financial products by requiring issuers and distributors to have a customer-centric approach to designing, distributing and reviewing products.
Advisers are required to certify at an account level that the product and other attributes affiliated with an account adheres to their DDO obligations.

terms.name: (Member) Division
terms.definition:
See Division Details.
This is the Divison that the Member's plan belongs to.

terms.name: (Member) Employer Code
terms.definition:
This is the unique identifier for a given Employer.
Also referred to as the member's Employer Number.

terms.name: (Member) Fund
terms.definition: Every individual member superannuation account is a member of a Superannuation Fund, which is responsible for managing the account (via fund appointed fund trustees and service providers).

terms.name: (Member) Gender
terms.definition: Member's sex (Male/Female/Undefined/Other)

terms.name: (Member) Name
terms.definition: The recorded  name of the Member that will be printed/reported on all correspondence

terms.name: (Member) Residual Balances
terms.definition:
Superannuation providers will pay all monies from a member's Account when closure is requested and forward proceeds as per the member's instructions. However, this is not always the case and sometimes there will be money left over in circumstances where the Client has become untraceable or where it has not been possible to return the money to the Client. This is a residual balance.


Residual balances can arise for a number of reasons, including (but not limited to):

  • Cheques that have been sent to the rightful owner but never cashed by them;
  • Members who have not provided instructions on how to deal with the balance;
  • Members who have died where the executors or family are unknown and Clients who have simply disappeared and cannot be traced ;
  • Retention money that has remained unclaimed;
  • Post Account closure credits, such as late incomes received from Investment Funds,
  • historically some platforms/processes permit the Accounts to be reopened, post new credits and then close it again. Although the residual balance is normally a surplus, a deficit may also occur due to cost overruns.

terms.name: (Member) Revenue Paid To
terms.definition:
The adviser that all Fees and Commissions charged to a member's Account are to be directed to.
This can be different to the  Adviser nominated to manage the Account.

terms.name: (Member) TFN
terms.definition:
Member Tax File Number
Each member is required to apply for and provide a unique Tax File Number from the Australian Taxation Office
which is used to identify the member in regulatory reports (or be subject to taxation penalties if not provided).

terms.name: (Number of) Investment Units
terms.definition:
When you deposit money into a managed fund or withdraw money from the fund you are really buying units or redeeming them.
The value of a managed fund's units is determined by the value of the fund's assets divided by the number of issued units.
Investment income can be paid to unit holders on a per unit basis. This will reduce the assets in the managed fund and so reduce the unit price.

terms.name: (Ongoing) Fee Arrangement
terms.definition: An  ongoing  fee  arrangement  (OFA)  is    an  arrangement  where  ongoing  advice services  are  provided  to  a  retail  Client  and  fees  are  paid  by  the  Client  during  a period of more than 12 months after the Client has received personal advice from the adviser.

terms.name: (Periodical Performance) Gross performance
terms.definition: Gross performance means the performance results of a portfolio (or portions of a portfolio) before  the deduction of all fees and expenses that are applicable

terms.name: (Periodical Performance) Net Performance
terms.definition:
Net performance means the performance results of a portfolio (or portions of a portfolio) after the deduction of all fees and expenses including, if applicable -
- Account fees
- advisory fees, 
- fees paid to underlying investment vehicles,
- payments by the investment adviser for which the Client or investor reimburses the investment adviser.

terms.name: (Rollover) Benefit Statement
terms.definition:
Rollover Benefit Statements are prepared when superannuation balances are moved between super funds. it provides a clear audit trail of when and where superannuation balances have been rolled over. 
It is provided by the trustee the sending  super fund to the receiving super provider.

terms.name: Account
terms.definition: An Account can be a Superannuation, Pension, Investment, Self-Managed Super Fund (SMSF), etc.

terms.name: Account Based Pension
terms.definition:
An account-based pension is an income stream purchased with accumulated superannuation savings. It enables the member to choose from a range of investment options for the remaining balance and to select the amount and frequency of income payments, subject to government minimum drawdown requirements. There is no maximum withdrawal limit, and payments continue until the account balance is exhausted.

Regulatory Context:
This term represents an account-based pension within the meaning given in Regulation 1.03 of the Superannuation Industry (Supervision) Regulations 1994 (SIS Regulations).
Reference link: SIS Regulations r.1.03

Key Characteristics:

  • Funded by superannuation savings converted into an income stream.
  • Flexible payment amounts and frequency (subject to minimum drawdown).
  • Investment choice within the pension account.
  • No maximum withdrawal limit.
  • Payments continue until the account balance is depleted.

terms.name: Account Based Pension formula
terms.definition: The amount calculated or requested to fund the pensioner's initial account balance.

terms.name: Account closure: Consolidation of accounts
terms.definition: Represents a member account closed where all benefits in that account have been consolidated with another account of the same member in the same RSE.

terms.name: Account closure: Outward rollover
terms.definition: Account closure: Outward rollover" refers to a situation where: All benefits in a member's superannuation account are transferred (or "rolled over") to another superannuation fund, resulting in the closure of the original account.

terms.full_name: Account closure: Satisfying a condition of release
terms.name: Account closure: Satisfying a condition of release
terms.definition:
An account closure refers to the process of permanently deactivating a financial account, preventing any further transactions. This can occur for various reasons, including customer request, inactivity, regulatory requirements, or financial institution policies. Once closed, the account holder can no longer deposit or withdraw funds, and any remaining balance is typically transferred to another account or refunded.

In banking, financial institutions may close accounts due to fraud concerns, non-compliance, or changes in services. In accounting, account closure can also refer to the process of resetting temporary account balances to zero at the end of a fiscal year.

terms.name: Account closure: Successor fund transfer
terms.definition:
Represents a member account closed where a member’s benefit is transferred without the member’s consent from one RSE to a successor fund within the meaning given in r. 1.03 of the SIS Regulations.

Reference: SIS Regulations, r. 6.29.
Reference link: https://www.legislation.gov.au/F1996B00580/2019-08-10/text

terms.full_name: Account Count, Inforce Count, Contract Count
terms.name: Account Count, Inforce Count, Contract Count
terms.definition:
These terms are often used in business or financial contexts, particularly in industries like insurance or customer service management. Here's a brief explanation:

  • Account Count: This refers to the total number of individual accounts or clients managed by a company. It could represent customer accounts, supplier accounts, or any other type of business relationship.
  • Inforce Count: Commonly used in insurance, this term represents the number of active policies or contracts that are currently in effect and generating premiums
  • Contract Count: This refers to the number of formal agreements or contracts a company has entered into, either with customers, suppliers, or other entities. It encompasses all types of legal arrangements.

terms.name: Account Development
terms.definition: Account development is a strategic process of building and maintaining long-term, profitable relationships with existing key accounts to maximize revenue and customer lifetime value, often involving identifying opportunities for growth, retention, and expansion.

terms.name: Account Keeping Fee
terms.definition: See Administration Fee

terms.name: Account Number
terms.definition: The account number is a unique identifier assigned to a customer's account/s. It is used to track and manage the customer's interactions, transactions, and services associated with their account.

terms.name: Account performance
terms.definition: Account performance provides an indication of the gain or loss of the portfolio over a period. If the portfolio is less than 12 months old it will be calculated from inception.

terms.name: Account performance - Cumulative  performance
terms.definition: Cumulative performance measures an investment's total return over a specific period, accounting for all gains and losses.

terms.name: Account Start Date
terms.definition: I can give you an opinion on this but I think this should really be set by someone else.

terms.name: Accrual Multiple
terms.definition: Multiple(s) used to calculate the accrued benefit of a Defined benefit member.

terms.name: Accrual Rate
terms.definition: The actual rate required for the table, including the basis where per annum (e.g. 15% p.a.)

terms.name: Accrued benefits
terms.definition: In the context of Australian superannuation, accrued benefits refer to the retirement benefits that a member has earned up to a specific point in time, particularly in a defined benefit fund.

terms.name: Accrued Default Amounts
terms.definition: These accounts are a default created account where member did not make a choice - e.g. employer created with default investment/insurance options

terms.name: Accrued Retirement Benefit
terms.definition: The calculation used to determine the Non-insured portion of a Defined Benefit member's Normal Retirement/Death benefit e.g. Withdrawal Benefit or Vested Benefit.

terms.name: Accumulation Fund
terms.definition: In an accumulation fund, the benefit received is based on the contributions made by the member and employer and investment earnings, minus tax and expenses. Accumulation funds are sometimes called defined contribution funds.

terms.name: Accumulation Phase
terms.definition: Period when you are contributing to your account. All contributions made during this phase are locked away (preserved) until your retirement.

terms.name: Achieved
terms.definition: Insignia Financial Goal rating

terms.name: Acting in role
terms.definition: Is current on secondment and temp in role

terms.name: Action
terms.definition:
Actions are tasks that are recorded in a register for assignment, tracking, and reporting purposes.

Protecht.ERM has an Actions register and additional registers can be created for specific purposes, such as audit or quality assurance.

terms.full_name: Action Enquiry Slip - Database system embedded in Lotus Notes.
terms.name: Action Enquiry Slip - Database system embedded in Lotus Notes.
terms.definition: An "action enquiry slip" refers to a form or document used to initiate or record a request for information or clarification regarding a specific action or task, often within a business or organizational context.

terms.name: Action Taken
terms.definition: Acronym used in leaving case notes in IWS on what action has been taken

terms.name: Active member account
terms.definition: Open super fund account that received contributions, rollovers or made benefit payments within the past two years.

terms.name: Active Operations Management
terms.definition:
Active operations management involves overseeing day-to-day business processes to ensure efficiency, effectiveness, and alignment with organizational goals. If you're looking to implement or improve active operations management, here are a few key areas to focus on:

  1. Process Optimization: Streamline workflows to eliminate bottlenecks and improve productivity.
  2. Resource Allocation: Ensure personnel, equipment, and budget are used effectively to meet objectives
  3. Performance Monitoring: Use KPIs (key performance indicators) to track and evaluate progress regularly.
  4. Risk Management: Identify potential challenges and develop proactive strategies to mitigate them.
  5. Technology Integration: Leverage tools like project management software or data analytics platforms to enhance operations.
  6. Continuous Improvement: Foster a culture of innovation and feedback to keep refining processes

terms.name: Activity Fee
terms.definition:
A fee is an activity fee if:
a) the fee relates to costs incurred by the trustee of the superannuation entity that are directly related to an activity of the trustee:

i. that is engaged in at the request, or with the consent, of a member, or
ii. that relates to a member and is required by law, and


b) those costs are not otherwise charged as administration fees and costs, investment fees and costs, transaction costs, a buy-sell spread, a switching fee, an advice fee or an insurance fee.

terms.name: Activity Points
terms.definition: The number of activity points accumulated from completing tasks (in relation to individual advisers or practices)

terms.name: Actual Total Fixed Remuneration
terms.definition: This is the TFR figure which is earned by the employee -  which may be a reduced amount depending on your FTE

terms.name: Actuarial Certificate
terms.definition: Document prepared by an actuary certifying the amount of a superannuation investment earnings come from either the accumulation or retirement phase.

terms.name: Actuarial investigation
terms.definition:
An actuarial investigation is a formal assessment conducted by an actuary to evaluate the financial position of a defined benefit fund or insurance company. It ensures that the fund has sufficient assets to meet its liabilities and comply with regulatory requirements.


In Australia, actuarial investigations are required under superannuation regulations and must be conducted at least every three years for defined benefit funds. These investigations analyze factors such as fund solvency, contribution rates, and future obligations to determine whether corrective actions are needed to maintain financial stability.


Reference: SIS Regulations, r. 9.27, r. 9.29; Prudential Standard SPS 160 Defined Benefit Matters.

Reference link: https://www.legislation.gov.au/F1996B00580/2019-08-10/text

terms.name: Ad-Hoc Addition - MKIS
terms.definition:
Ad-hoc addition refers to a temporary or unplanned inclusion of an entity, often made to address an immediate need. It is not part of a predefined structure or plan and is typically done on the spot to accommodate changes or unforeseen circumstances.

For example in finance, an ad-hoc addition might involve allocating extra funds for an unexpected project.

terms.name: Ad-Hoc Addition Reprint - MKIS
terms.definition: An ad-hoc addition reprint likely refers to a temporary or unscheduled reprint of materials that were not originally planned for reproduction. This might happen due to unexpected demand, corrections, or additional distribution needs.

terms.name: Additional Features - Description
terms.definition: The details of the additional feature for the defined benefits arrangement.

terms.name: Additional Features - Feature
terms.definition: Identifies any additional or unique feature of the defined benefits arrangement that is not captured in the standard fields.

terms.name: Additional Fee Conditions
terms.definition: Details any conditions that apply to the fee charges.

terms.name: Additional Investment Guide
terms.definition: An AIG provides additional information on the relevant product and forms part of the Product Disclosure Statement (PDS)

terms.name: Additional Pension Payment
terms.definition: An additional pension payment is an amount that will be considered as additional income, for example If the member currently receives $10,000 p.a. as their pension payment and they request a one-off pension payment of $5,000. Their new income for the financial year is $10,000 + $5,000 (as this additional payment is considered income will be included in the income test).

terms.name: Address City
terms.definition: Client address city/suburb

terms.name: Address Country
terms.definition: Customer address country

terms.name: Address Country Code
terms.definition: Customer address country code (ISO country code)

terms.name: Address Postcode
terms.definition: Client customer address postcode

terms.name: Address State
terms.definition: Customer address state (eg Victoria, Tasmania)

terms.name: Address Street
terms.definition: Customer address line 1

terms.name: Administration Fee
terms.definition: The administration fee refers to the fees and costs charged for managing and operating the member’s account. It includes a percentage-based component and a fixed dollar component.

terms.name: Administration Fees
terms.definition: This Administration  fee represents the cost to the super fund of looking after the various administrative tasks relating to the  super Account, such as sending out Account statements, providing online services and recording your Account transactions and contributions. If the  fund provides intra-fund advice it also covers this service. Can be fixed fee, percentage of your Account balance, or a mix of the two.

terms.name: Administrative reserve
terms.definition: In a financial or legal context, an Administrative Reserve refers to a cash reserve set aside specifically to cover administrative costs associated with a legal or restructuring process, such as bankruptcy or insolvency proceedings.

terms.name: Administrator
terms.definition: The role of an administrator is responsible for maintenance of financial data, accurate and timely administrative services across various superannuation schemes and products for members and stakeholders.

terms.name: Advice Fee
terms.definition:
Advice fees are amounts paid to a financial adviser for services provided under an advice arrangement.. A fee is an advice fee if:
  • a) the fee relates directly to costs incurred by the trustee of the superannuation entity because of the provision of financial product advice to a member by:
    • i. a trustee of the entity, or
    • ii. another person acting as an employee of, or under an arrangement with, the trustee of the entity, and
  • b) those costs are not otherwise charged as administration fees and costs, investment fees and costs, a switching fee, an activity fee or an insurance fee.

terms.name: Adviser
terms.definition: Also known as a Financial Planners, they work with clients to help manage their finances and make informed decisions on saving and investing money in Superannuation, Retirement, Insurance and Investments.

terms.name: Adviser and Investor Services
terms.definition: Adviser and investor services typically refer to professional guidance and support provided to individuals or organizations in managing their investments. This can include financial planning, investment advice, portfolio management, and other services aimed at helping clients achieve their financial goals. These services are often offered by financial advisers, investment advisers, or firms specializing in wealth management.

terms.name: Adviser Client Experience
terms.definition:
The adviser-client experience refers to the overall quality of interactions and relationship between an adviser (such as a financial planner, consultant, or coach) and their client. It encompasses all touchpoints—from initial contact and onboarding to ongoing communication and service delivery.

A positive adviser-client experience is often characterized by trust, personalized advice, clear communication, empathy, and a proactive approach to meeting client needs. The goal is to ensure that clients feel supported, understood, and satisfied with the adviser's expertise and guidance throughout the entire relationship.

terms.name: Adviser Code
terms.definition: The inique identifier for the adviser

terms.name: Adviser Description
terms.definition: The adviser's name (or business name)

terms.name: Adviser Licensee Management System
terms.definition:
An adviser licensee management system is typically a platform or tool used to oversee and manage the licensing, compliance, and operational requirements of financial advisers or similar professionals. These systems help ensure that advisers meet regulatory standards, maintain up-to-date certifications, and adhere to industry guidelines. They often include features like:

  • License Tracking: Monitoring the status and renewal dates of licenses.
  • Compliance Management: Ensuring adherence to legal and regulatory requirements.
  • Document Management: Storing and organizing important documents like certifications and agreements
  • Reporting and Analytics: Providing insights into adviser performance and compliance metrics.

terms.name: Adviser Payment Services
terms.definition: Adviser Payment Services are an internal team that control processes and payments related to the remuneration of advisers and dealer groups.

terms.name: Adviser Relationship Manager
terms.definition: An adviser relationship manager is a professional who works to build and maintain strong relationships with clients or partners, often in financial services, corporate banking, or other industries. Their role typically involves understanding client needs, providing tailored advice, and ensuring satisfaction to foster long-term partnerships. They may also analyze trends, manage communications, and coordinate with teams to deliver high-quality service.

terms.name: Adviser Service Fee
terms.definition: An Adviser Service Fee is an optional fee, agreed between the Investor and Financial Adviser. They are deducted from the Investment, payable to the Adviser as a fee for the service provided.

terms.name: Adviser Service Information System
terms.definition:
An adviser service information system typically refers to a platform or tool designed to support advisory services. These systems help advisers manage client information, provide recommendations, and streamline decision-making processes. They can be used in various fields, such as financial planning, legal consulting, or healthcare advisory.

For example, such systems might include features like:

  • Client data management: Storing and organizing client profiles and histories
  • Analysis tools: Offering insights based on data to guide recommendations.
  • Communication channels: Facilitating interaction between advisers and clients.

terms.name: Advisor Support Needs
terms.definition:
The Adviser Support Needs (ASN) Indicator is a scoring framework used by the Assurance team to assess the quality of financial advice provided by advisers. It helps determine how frequently an adviser should be reviewed (e.g., monthly, quarterly, or annually) based on the outcomes of assurance reviews.

How is the ASN Score Calculated?
- Each question in the assurance review may be allocated points.
- If an issue is identified, a multiplier is applied based on the root cause of the issue.
- The total score influences the adviser's ASN rating.
Current Assurance Review Questionnaire
The questionnaire includes 15 core questions (plus one additional assessment) across several categories:
Best Interests Obligations
1. Relevant Client Circumstances – Were the client’s personal circumstances properly identified?
2. Scope of Advice – Was the advice scope appropriate?
3. Strategy & Product Investigation – Was a reasonable investigation conducted?
4. Authority/Accreditation – Was advice within the adviser’s authority?
5. Additional Steps – Were other reasonable steps taken?
6. Better Position – Was the client likely left in a better position?
7. Conflict Priority – Were client interests prioritized in case of conflict?
 Non-Approved Product Process
8. APL – Was the approval process followed for non-approved products?
 Advice Documentation
9. Personal Advice in Writing – Was personal advice documented?
10. Correct Document on Time – Were documents provided correctly and on time?
11. Advice Document Contents – Were content requirements met?
Implementation
12. Implementation – Was implementation accurate and timely?
AML/CTF
13. Client Identification & PEP – Was PEP status handled appropriately?
Licensee, Privacy & Record Keeping
14. Compliance – Were licensee, privacy, and record-keeping guidelines met?

 MDA Services
15. MDA Requirements – Were MDA service requirements met?
Additional Assessment
29. Client Detriment Assessment – Is there potential client detriment based on findings?

terms.name: Advisor Transaction Authority
terms.definition: Adviser Transaction Authorities (ATA) allow investors to provide their Adviser with the authority to make certain transactions on their behalf.

terms.name: Age
terms.definition: Defines which age the rate relates to when the rates are separated by age.

terms.name: Age bracket
terms.definition:
An age bracket refers to a specific range of ages with defined upper and lower limits. It is commonly used in demographics, statistics, and marketing to categorize people based on their age group. For example, a survey might classify respondents into age brackets such as 18–24, 25–34, 35–44, and so on.

Age brackets help organizations analyze trends, target specific audiences, and make policy decisions.

terms.name: Age Last Birthday
terms.definition: The age at which the conversion factor is effective.

terms.name: Age Method
terms.definition: Specifies the age method used.

terms.name: Age Next Birthday
terms.definition: Age Next Birthday (ANB) refers to the age of your next birthday and is used mostly for insurance purposes/calculations

terms.name: Age not available
terms.definition: Represents where the date of birth of a member is not known.

terms.name: Agency
terms.definition: An external party that sources candidates for job vacancies on behalf of Insignia Financial.

terms.name: Agent
terms.definition:
Agent (of a client) is a person who is authorised to act for or on behalf of the client in relation to a product or service. An agent authorised to operate a client’s account is termed a signatory. A signatory may be authorised to act under a Power of Attorney.
Agent (of the Insignia Financial DBG) is a person who is authorised to act for or on behalf of the Insignia Financial DBG.

terms.name: Agreement
terms.definition: An agreement that is entered into by both parties to a marriage as to how the benefit will be treated in the event of a divorce.

terms.name: Agreement Type
terms.definition: This can only be internal or external – It relates to whether an adviser is considered an aligned adviser with MLC / Insignia or is considered an IFA (independent Financial Adviser).

terms.name: Alert
terms.definition: A client transaction or activity that has been brought to the attention of the Financial Crime team and requires enhanced client due diligence or further investigation.

terms.name: Alias
terms.definition: Alias or preferred name for person.

terms.name: All Monies
terms.definition:
An indicator showing whether all monies were withdrawn from an  account, by virtue of a withdrawal.
For selected products, a switch of all monies is eligible for withdrawal bonuses (surrender and
reserve bonuses).
Must be true if a maturity or full surrender.
May be true or false if a partial surrender.

terms.name: All Monies Indicator
terms.definition:
An indicator showing whether all monies were withdrawn from an  account, by virtue of a withdrawal.
For selected products, a switch of all monies is eligible for withdrawal bonuses (surrender and
reserve bonuses).
Must be true if a maturity or full surrender.
May be true or false if a partial surrender.

terms.name: Alliance Managers
terms.definition: Employees in the IOOF Advice Division who service the Alliance partners

terms.name: Allocated Pension
terms.definition: A pension/annuity arrangement allows a person to draw regularly from their own account eg.monthly/quarterly, within limits set by legislation.

terms.name: Allocated pension
terms.definition:
An allocated pension, also known as an account-based pension, is a retirement income stream that allows individuals to withdraw regular payments from their superannuation savings once they reach their preservation age.

Key features of an allocated pension include:

  • Flexible withdrawals – You can choose how much and how often to withdraw, subject to minimum annual limits based on age.
  • Tax advantages – Earnings within the pension phase are tax-free, and withdrawals may be tax-free for individuals over 60.
  • Investment control – Funds remain invested, meaning returns depend on market performance

terms.name: AML Third Party Arrangements
terms.definition: A general term encompassing a wide range of commercial agreements by which the Insignia Financial DBG relies on a third party (‘Downstream Arrangement’), or the third party relies on the Insignia Financial DBG (‘Upstream Arrangement’) to deliver or enable the delivery of, a designated service or perform any obligation under applicable AML/CTF legislation.

terms.name: AML/CTF Program
terms.definition: AML/CTF program maintained by the Insignia Financial DBG to identify, mitigate and manage the risk the Insignia Financial DBG may reasonably face from its products and/or services being misused to facilitate ML/TF activities.

terms.name: AML/CTF Rules
terms.definition: Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorism Financing Rules.

terms.name: Annual Leave
terms.definition: Annual leave

terms.name: Annual Performance and Remuneration Review
terms.definition: Annual cycle of assessing employee performance and employee remuneration

terms.name: Annual Performance Review
terms.definition: Annual cycle of assessing employee's performance

terms.name: Annual Remuneration review
terms.definition: Annual cycle of assessing employee remuneration and incentives

terms.name: Annual Renewal Date
terms.definition: The reconciliation date for insurance policies.

terms.name: Annual Report
terms.definition: An annual report describes a company's operations and financial condition to stakeholders and is required by regulators.

terms.name: Annual Review
terms.definition: Annually shortly after 30 June a report will be prepared by MLC Wealth for the Member Office into the impact of backdating for the previous year to 30 June, showing backdating gains and losses, and compensation payments made to each investment option in each NULIS product.

terms.name: Annual Review Fee
terms.definition:
An Annual Review Fee is a charge that businesses, financial institutions, or professional service providers impose for the ongoing evaluation or maintenance of an account, service, or policy. This fee ensures that records remain up to date and that services continue to meet compliance, regulatory, or performance requirements.

The specifics of an annual review fee can vary depending on the industry. For example:

  • Financial services – Banks or investment firms may charge this fee for reviewing portfolios or accounts.
  • Legal or professional services – A company might charge for annual assessments related to contracts or memberships.
  • Insurance – Some insurers apply this fee to maintain or reassess coverage policies.

terms.name: Annuity
terms.definition:
An annuity is a financial product that provides a guaranteed income stream over a fixed period or for life, typically used for retirement planning. It is purchased from a superannuation fund or life insurance company, using either super savings or personal funds.

There are different types of annuities:

  • Fixed-term annuity: Pays income for a set number of years.
  • Lifetime annuity: Provides payments for the rest of your life.
  • Indexed annuity: Adjusts payments based on inflation or a fixed percentage.

Annuities offer predictable income but are generally less flexible than account-based pensions. They can also impact Age Pension eligibility, as they are included in income and asset tests


Reference link: https://www.legislation.gov.au/F1996B00580/2019-08-10/text

terms.name: Anti-Detriment Payment
terms.definition: An additional lump sum amount paid to the eligible dependant of a super fund member who dies (in addition to a lump sum death benefit that is paid to the dependant on the super fund member’s death). The additional lump sum payment is a refund of the 15% contributions tax paid by the super fund member during their life.

terms.name: Anti Money Laundering
terms.definition: Anti-money laundering (AML) refers to the laws, regulations and procedures intended to prevent criminals from disguising illegally obtained funds as legitimate income.

terms.name: ANZ Policy Committee Review reporting
terms.definition:
• The ANZ Policy Committee Review report is generated in the first 2 weeks of each quarter and is designed to enable RMs to have richer conversations with Employers

• Solution currently requires leveraging a non-prod environment, masking of data and monthly extracts from Tech, and takes ~3 days effort

• Data tables are provided to Ive who generate a pdf report for each Employer

• There are comprehensive reviews of the report by stakeholders and data quality validation can be required, which can be time consuming

• Workplace Super would like to roll out this level of reporting to other brands, which would require a more scalable solution to be in place

terms.name: ANZ Portfolio Manager 1
terms.definition: ANZ Portfolio Manager I and II were Master Trusts within TRAD that offered a wide range of investment options and are now fully terminated.

terms.name: ANZ Portfolio Manager 2
terms.definition: ANZ Portfolio Manager I and II were Master Trusts within TRAD that offered a wide range of investment options and are now fully terminated.

terms.name: ANZ Smart Choice Employer
terms.definition: ANZ Smart Choice Employer Super is a superannuation solution offered by ANZ for employers and their employees. It provides an easy and convenient way to manage super obligations, with features like no fees for employers, lifestage investments that adjust automatically based on age, and online tools for managing contributions. Employees benefit from smart investment options, insurance coverage, and the ability to consolidate their super funds.

terms.name: ANZ Smart Choice Pension
terms.definition: The ANZ Smart Choice Pension is a retirement income product designed to provide a regular income stream for retirees. It offers features like investment options tailored to your age or preferences, online management alongside ANZ bank accounts, and insurance coverage for peace of mind.

terms.name: ANZ Smart Choice Super
terms.definition: ANZ Smart Choice Super is a superannuation product offered by ANZ, designed to help individuals save for retirement. It provides features like investment options that adjust based on your age, insurance coverage, and the ability to consolidate multiple super accounts. Members can manage their super through ANZ Internet Banking or the ANZ App.

terms.name: ANZ Super Advantage
terms.definition: ANZ Super Advantage is an employer-sponsored super product that helps you accumulate and grow your super savings in a tax-effective way as you progress through your working life towards retirement.

terms.name: AON
terms.definition: Aon is the name of company who provide remuneration market benchmarking data for a number of different industries across both Australia and worldwide -  they are the 3rd party provider of survey information for FIRG

terms.name: APIR Code
terms.definition: An APIR Code is a unique identifier issued by APIR to participants and products within the Financial Services Industry. Participants have a Participant APIR Code. Products have a Product APIR Code. SPIN level Superannuation products have a SPIN (Superannuation Product Identification Number).

terms.name: Applicable Customer Identification Procedures
terms.definition: Applicable customer identification procedures (ACIP) include: collecting and verifying customer identification information through know your customer procedures. identifying and verifying the beneficial owner(s) of a customer.

terms.name: Applicant
terms.definition: Any Person who applies for a vacancy. It can be internal or External

terms.name: Application
terms.definition:
People
The submitted interest for a vacancy

Risk
An application is a logical grouping of related registers. Applications group related registers under a single menu item.

terms.name: Approved Deposit Fund
terms.definition: An Approved Deposit Fund (ADF) is a type of superannuation fund in Australia. It is designed to hold and manage retirement savings for individuals who have left their employer but have not yet retired or reached the age to access their superannuation benefits. ADFs are regulated under the Superannuation Industry (Supervision) Act 1993 and must comply with specific rules to maintain their approved status

terms.name: Approved Product List
terms.definition: An approved product list typically refers to a curated list of items or materials that meet specific requirements set by an organization, regulatory body, or industry standards. These requirements could include quality, safety, compliance, cost-effectiveness, or compatibility. Organizations use such lists to ensure consistency and reliability in procurement or supply chain processes.

terms.name: APRA Specified role
terms.definition: A role defined by APRA for CPS511 requirements.

terms.name: Aquisition
terms.definition: One company buy most or all of another company's shares, so they will gain control of the target company.

terms.name: Archiving
terms.definition: Storing information that is no longer required for day-to-day business operations or decision-making but that needs to be retained for either regulatory requirements or a justifiable business purpose (see Retention).  Archived information may be stored in a location and format that is less accessible than non-archived information to prioritize preservation, security and cost over timeliness and accessibility.

terms.name: ASF Margin
terms.definition:
ASF (Available Stable Funding) margin refers to the weighted values assigned to various funding sources based on their stability, contractual maturity, and the likelihood of withdrawal by funding providers. It is a key component in calculating the Net Stable Funding Ratio (NSFR), which ensures that financial institutions maintain a stable funding profile over a one-year horizon.

Different types of liabilities and capital receive varying ASF factors, ranging from 100% for highly stable funding sources to 0% for less stable ones. These factors help banks assess their liquidity risk and ensure they have sufficient stable funding to support their operations.

terms.name: Asia Pacific Investment Register
terms.definition: APIR codes are issued to funds and are standard identifiers for products in the financial services industry. An APIR code consists of none alpha/numeric characters; the last two characters are the country code in which the product offer or is legally domiciled.

terms.name: Asset
terms.definition: Anything that can be invested into by an investor - including both equities and managed funds.

terms.name: Asset Allocation
terms.definition: The process where your investments are distributed between or amongst different asset classes such as cash, fixed interest, property and shares. This is also known as your investment mix.

terms.name: Asset Base Commission
terms.definition:
An asset-based commission refers to a fee structure where financial advisors or institutions charge a percentage of the total assets they manage for a client. This is also known as an "asset-based fee." Here's how it typically works:

  1. Percentage-Based Fee: The advisor charges a percentage (e.g., 1%) of the client's assets under management (AUM) annually. For example, if you have $500,000 in assets, a 1% fee would amount to $5,000 per year.
  2. Incentive Alignment: This structure aligns the advisor's incentives with the client's financial growth, as the advisor benefits when the client's portfolio grows
  3. Transparency: Asset-based fees are often considered more transparent than commission-based models, where advisors earn money from selling specific financial products

terms.name: Asset Class
terms.definition: The investment industry refers to groups of similar assets as ‘asset classes’, e.g. shares, bonds, property, infrastructure.

terms.name: Asset Management Incentive Plan
terms.definition: A specialist incentive plan specifically for employees within the Asset Management Division which rewards employees closely aligned to business practice for asset management

terms.name: Asset Value,  Investment Funds
terms.definition:
Asset Value: This refers to the monetary worth of an asset, which could be tangible (like property, machinery, or equipment) or intangible (like intellectual property or patents). Asset value is often determined based on market price, book value, or appraised value, depending on the asset type. It plays a critical role in financial analysis, investment decisions, and balance sheet assessments


Investment Funds: These are collective investment vehicles that pool money from multiple investors to purchase securities, such as stocks, bonds, or other assets. They are managed by professional fund managers and offer investors access to diversified portfolios. Examples include mutual funds, exchange-traded funds (ETFs), and hedge funds. Investment funds aim to achieve specific financial goals, like growth, income, or risk management.

terms.name: Assets Under Management
terms.definition: Much of the wealth management industry charges its clients on a percent of assets under management (AUM) basis.

terms.name: Asset Backed Securities
terms.definition:
Asset-backed securities (ABS) are financial instruments that are backed by a pool of underlying assets, typically ones that generate cash flow, such as loans, leases, or receivables. These securities are created through a process called securitization, where the assets are bundled together and sold to investors as a single financial product.

Key features of ABS include:

  • Income Generation: They provide a steady stream of income to investors, similar to bonds.
  • Diverse Asset Pools: The underlying assets can range from credit card receivables and auto loans to more unique cash flows like movie revenues or royalty payments.
  • Risk Diversification: By pooling assets, the risk is spread across multiple sources, making them attractive to certain investors.

terms.name: Assignment
terms.definition: An assignment is a task or piece of work that is given to someone as part of their responsibilities.

terms.name: Assistant Customer Service Manager
terms.definition: An Assistant Customer Service Manager supports the Customer Service Manager in overseeing customer service operations. Their responsibilities often include supervising staff, ensuring customer satisfaction, handling escalated complaints, and maintaining service standards. They may also assist in training employees, monitoring performance metrics, and implementing strategies to improve service quality.

terms.name: Association of Financial Advisers
terms.definition: A professional body providing financial advisers with lobbying assistance and practice improvement.

terms.name: Attestation
terms.definition: A formal response to a compliance question or a key risk indicator.

terms.name: Attribution Managed Investment Trust
terms.definition:
An Attribution Managed Investment Trust (AMIT) is a type of managed investment trust that has elected to apply the AMIT regime under Australian tax law. The regime provides a framework for attributing taxable income to unit holders on a fair and reasonable basis, rather than relying on present entitlement. Under AMIT rules, investors may be attributed income without receiving a corresponding cash distribution, and cost base adjustments (increase or decrease) are applied to reflect these differences.
Key Characteristics:

Introduced under Tax Laws Amendment (New Tax System for Managed Investment Trusts) Act 2016
Applies to eligible managed investment trusts that elect into the regime
Allows unders and overs adjustments to be carried forward and reconciled in subsequent years
Provides certainty for investors regarding tax treatment of income components

Why is it Critical?

Tax Compliance: Ensures correct attribution of income and cost base adjustments for investors
Investor Reporting: Impacts annual tax statements, including AMIT cost base net increase/decrease disclosures
Data Integrity: Incorrect AMIT calculations can lead to misreporting of taxable income and CGT outcomes
Regulatory Alignment: Required for trusts electing into the AMIT regime under Australian tax law

terms.name: AustChoice Super (previously AustChoice Super Plan)
terms.definition: AustChoice Super is part of IOOF Super, offering various superannuation solutions. It provides flexibility in investment options, insurance coverage, and competitive fees.

terms.name: Australia and New Zealand Bank
terms.definition: ANZ Bank

terms.name: Australian Business Number
terms.definition: An ABN is a unique 11 digit number that identifies your registered business to the government and community.

terms.name: Australian Business Register
terms.definition:
The Australian Business Register (ABR) is a government database that stores details about businesses and organizations registered in Australia. It is primarily used to manage Australian Business Numbers (ABNs), which are unique identifiers for businesses. The ABR provides services such as:

  • ABN Lookup: A free tool to search for publicly available information about businesses using their ABN or name.
  • ABN Registration: Businesses can apply for an ABN through the ABR, which is essential for tax and business operations.
  • Updating ABN Details: Businesses are required to keep their ABN information up-to-date to ensure accurate records.

terms.full_name: Australian Charities and Not Fr Profit Commissions
terms.name: Australian Charities and Not Fr Profit Commissions
terms.definition:
The Australian Charities and Not-for-profits Commission (ACNC) is the national regulator for charities in Australia. Established in 2012, the ACNC's primary goals are to:

  • Maintain and enhance public trust in the not-for-profit sector.
  • Support a robust and independent not-for-profit sector.
  • Reduce unnecessary regulatory obligations for charities.

The ACNC provides resources and guidance to help charities meet their obligations, ensures compliance with Australian laws, and maintains a public register of registered charities. This register allows the public to access information about charities, including their purpose, financial details, and regulatory history.

terms.name: Australian Company Number
terms.definition:
An Australian Company Number (ACN) is a unique nine-digit identifier assigned to every company registered under Australia's Corporations Act 2001. It is issued by the Australian Securities & Investments Commission (ASIC) and must be displayed on all official company documents, including invoices, letterheads, and financial statements.

If a company has an Australian Business Number (ABN), it may use the ABN in place of the ACN on documents, provided the ABN includes the company's ACN.

terms.name: Australian Eligible Rollover Fund
terms.definition: An AERF was a holding account designed to receive the super benefits of lost members and those with low account balances that are no longer receiving contributions. Accounts meeting this criteria are notified to the ATO and then transferred for holding.

terms.name: Australian Executor Trustee Ltd
terms.definition: Australian Executor Trustees (AET) is one of Australia's largest and most experienced non-government providers of professional trustee services. Established in 1880, AET specializes in helping individuals and families manage, protect, and transfer their wealth. Their services include estate planning, trustee services, executor services, philanthropy, and financial management. They also provide tailored solutions for superannuation and Native Title trust management.

terms.full_name: Australian Financial Complaints Authority (Formerly known as Super Complaints Tribunal - SCT)
terms.name: Australian Financial Complaints Authority (Formerly known as Super Complaints Tribunal - SCT)
terms.definition: The Australian Financial Complaints Authority (AFCA) is a free, fair and independent dispute resolution scheme. They consider complaints about financial products and services. AFCA’s service is offered as an alternative to tribunals and courts to resolve complaints consumers and small businesses have with their financial firms. AFCA considers complaints that previously would have been handled by the Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS), the Credit and Investments Ombudsman (CIO) and the Superannuation Complaints Tribunal (SCT).

terms.name: Australian Financial Services License
terms.definition:
Australian Financial Services Licence refers to an Australian Financial Services licence issued by ASIC, which authorises licensees to:
•    provide financial product advice to clients;
•    deal in a financial product;
•    make a market for a financial product;
•    operate a registered scheme;
•    provide a custodial or depository service; and provide traditional trustee company services.

terms.name: Australian Prudential Regulation Authority
terms.definition: A Government agency responsible for prudential regulation of banks, insurance companies, superannuation funds, credit unions, building societies and friendly societies.

terms.name: Australian Registered Body Number
terms.definition:
Some organisations, like associations, can only conduct business in their 'home' state or territory. By becoming a registered Australian body, these organisations can trade throughout all states and territories within Australia.

The following applies to Torrens Title land, Water Access Licences and the General Register of Deeds.

Under the Corporations Act 2001 (Cth) a registrable Australian body, i.e:

  • a body corporate that is not a company
  • certain unincorporated bodies or
  • an association (not incorporated) registered under a State law that is not recognised by other States,

that wishes to:

  • deal with land or a Water Access Licence within New South Wales and/or
  • carry on business outside the State of its origin,

must be registered with the Australian Securities and Investments Commission (ASIC). A 9 digit ARBN is issued to the body.

A dealing or document drawn in the name of a registered Australian body, except for those listed below, must state the ARBN directly after the body's name where it first appears on the dealing or document. The ARBN is not required to be stated again after the first instance. 

Note  Westpac Banking Corporation has corporate status but has retained its original ARBN.

The ARBN does not have to appear in the seal of the registered body.

A foreign company must be registered with ASIC to carry on certain business in Australia and is issued with an ARBN, but is not required to be registered in order to deal with land or a Water Access Licence. For a dealing or document involving a foreign company, the ARBN must be stated or a statutory declaration or letter by an officer of the company is required stating that the company:

  • is a foreign company
  • is not a registered foreign company
  • does not have an ARBN and
  • does not carry on business in Australia.

ARBN exceptions

An ARBN is not required for:

  • Aboriginal Associations and corporations
  • Area Health Services
  • the Association of a Community Title scheme
  • charitable or religious bodies
  • co-operative housing societies
  • incorporated associations, including organisations incorporated and registered pursuant to the Fair Work (Registered Organisations) Act 2009 (Cth)
  • the Owners Corporation of a Strata scheme
  • Reserve Trusts as defined by s92 Crown Lands Act 1989
  • solicitor corporations
  • State owned corporations
  • statutory authorities or
  • statutory health corporations.

A statutory declaration explaining the absence of an ARBN is required in all other cases.

terms.name: Australian Securities and Investment Commission
terms.definition: ASIC is Australia's integrated corporate, markets, financial services and consumer credit regulator.  It is an independent Australian Government body and is set up to administer the Australian Securities and Investments Commission Act 2001 (ASIC Act). The role under the ASIC Act is to maintain, facilitate and improve the performance of the financial system and entities in it and to administer the law effectively and with minimal procedural requirements.

terms.name: Australian Securities Exchange
terms.definition: The Australian Securities Exchange (ASX) is Australia's primary securities exchange. A variety of asset classes and services are available. Amongst others, the exchange offers shares, debt securities, derivatives and commodities.

terms.name: Australian Securitisation Forum
terms.definition: The Australian Securitisation Forum (ASF) is the leading industry body representing participants in the securitisation and covered bond markets in Australia and New Zealand. Established in 1989, its mission is to promote, protect, and strengthen these markets by providing a platform for collaboration, advocating for the industry, and offering education and resources to its members.

terms.name: Australian Standard Transfer Form
terms.definition: A document used to transfer ownership of securities, such as shares, between parties outside of the stock market (non-market transactions). It is commonly used when securities are not held through a broker or the CHESS system (Clearing House Electronic Subregister System). The form requires details about the seller, buyer, and the securities being transferred, along with any necessary certifications or approvals

terms.name: Australian Taxation Office
terms.definition: The Australian Taxation Office (ATO) is the principal revenue collection agency of the Australian Government and its role is to effectively manage and shape the tax and superannuation systems that support and fund services for Australians.

terms.name: Australian Taxation Office Interpretative Decision
terms.definition: An ATO Interpretative Decision (ATO ID) is a summary of a decision made by the Australian Taxation Office (ATO) on an interpretative matter. It provides insight into how the ATO applies a provision of tax law and helps ensure consistency in decision-making. However, ATO IDs are not legally binding rulings and do not constitute formal advice to taxpayers.

terms.full_name: Australian Transaction Reports and Analysis Centre
terms.name: Australian Transaction Reports and Analysis Centre
terms.definition: Australian Transaction Reports and Analysis Centre is the primary AML/CTF Regulator and Financial Intelligence Unit (FIU) in Australia. AUSTRAC’s overview and enforcement powers extend to cover the AML/CTF Act and the AML/CTF Rules.

terms.name: Australian Wealth Management
terms.definition: IOOF and AWM merged in 2009

terms.name: Authorised Representative
terms.definition: Individual or Corporate Australian Financial Services Authorised Representatives (Individual or Corporate Adviser)

terms.name: Auto Rebalance
terms.definition: By selecting the Auto-Rebalance Plan, members can choose to automatically rebalance their investment fund allocation back to their nominated investment profile. The Auto-Rebalance Plan works by switching units between investment funds, excluding ANZ Term Deposits, in order to realign members investment allocation as per their nominated investment profile. A tolerance level may be nominated to prevent a rebalance for significantly low amounts. If a tolerance level is not nominated, auto-rebalances will only occur where the difference between the investment allocation and investment profile is greater than 5%. For example, a member initially invest 50% into Fund A and 50% into Fund B and you want to maintain this investment profile. Over time, unit price movements and transactions made on your account may change your investment allocation so that Fund A is now 60% and Fund B is 40%. If this change is outside your nominated tolerance level, the Auto-Rebalance Plan will automatically rebalance your profile at the frequency you nominate, to your initial Fund A 50% and Fund B 50% investment profile

terms.name: Automatic Acceptance Limit
terms.definition: The highest cover amount that a member can be granted without requiring underwriting.

terms.name: Automatic Income Distribution Facility
terms.definition: Automatic Income Distribution Facility (AIDF) is a feature within a superannuation or retirement income product that automatically calculates and distributes periodic income payments to a member from their account balance. The facility ensures compliance with minimum drawdown requirements and applies the distribution rules defined by the product or regulatory standards without requiring manual intervention by the member.

terms.name: Automatically Reactivated task
terms.definition: A status of a CRMS work task. This means the task has been automatically moved from Pend to Active status.

terms.name: Availability
terms.definition: The ability of a given audience to access information when it is needed.

terms.name: Average claim handling time
terms.definition: Estimated average claim duration, expressed in months.

terms.name: Average Weekly Ordinary Times Earnings
terms.definition: Delete.

terms.name: AWM Super & Investment System
terms.definition: AWM Super & Investment System

terms.name: AWM Superannuation and Investment System
terms.definition: The AWM (Asset and Wealth Management) superannuation and investment system typically refers to services and solutions designed to manage retirement savings and investments effectively. It encompasses strategies for wealth accumulation, asset allocation, and retirement planning, often tailored to individual financial goals and risk preferences. These systems aim to optimize returns while ensuring compliance with regulations and providing transparency in investment management.

terms.name: Balance Deduction (Member Paid)
terms.definition: Specifies the percentage of the premium that the member pays.

terms.name: Bank Account Debit Tax
terms.definition: A bank account debit tax is a type of tax levied on debit transactions made from bank accounts. This means that whenever money is withdrawn or transferred from an account, a small percentage is taxed. The purpose of such taxes can vary, but they are often used to generate government revenue or regulate financial transactions.

terms.name: Bank Account Debit Tax
terms.definition:
This is now obsolete.
Bank Account Debits Tax (BADT or BAD) was an Australian bank transaction tax levied on customer withdrawals from bank accounts with a cheque facility (both withdrawals made by cheque or by another means, such as EFTPOS).
The tax was introduced by the federal government in 1982. The power to levy the tax was transferred to the states in 1990, except for Norfolk Island which did not charge it. The tax was abolished by the states on dates between 1 July 2002 and 1 July 2005 as part of the package of reforms for the introduction of the Goods and Services Tax.

terms.name: Bank Cheque Register (ASA)
terms.definition: A bank cheque register is a tool used to record and track all transactions related to a checking account. It helps clients and Insignia professionals maintain accurate financial records by documenting deposits, withdrawals, issued checks, and other account activities. This register is essential for budgeting, preventing overdrafts, and reconciling bank statements.

terms.name: Bank Details Update
terms.definition:
A bank details update refers to the process of changing or modifying personal banking information with a financial institution. This can include updating account numbers, adding new payment methods, changing billing addresses, or modifying direct deposit information.

These updates are often necessary due to changes such as:

  • Switching to a new bank account
  • Updating contact details for security purposes
  • Changing payment information for automatic transactions
  • Correcting errors in existing details

Banks typically require verification through secure channels—such as online banking platforms, customer service, or in-person visits—to ensure the accuracy and security of such changes.

terms.name: Banking Executive Accountability Regime
terms.definition: Remuneration regulation by APRA which is to be replaced by FAR.

terms.name: Banking, Finance & Insurance Award
terms.definition: This is the award which covers Insignia Financial

terms.name: Base salary
terms.definition: The salary of an individual without any superannuation, allowances, rewards, etc.

terms.name: Benchmark
terms.definition: Benchmarks are usually market indices that are publicly available.

terms.full_name: Bendigo and Adelaide Staff Super Plan
terms.name: Bendigo and Adelaide Staff Super Plan
terms.definition: The Bendigo and Adelaide Staff Super Plan was a superannuation offering for employees of Bendigo and Adelaide Bank. It provided benefits such as additional super contributions for specific allowances, including home lending and first aid allowances.

terms.name: Bendigo SmartStart Super
terms.definition: The Bendigo SmartStart Super is a superannuation product designed to offer simple, low-cost options for managing retirement savings. It includes features like age-based investment strategies (Bendigo MySuper) and customizable insurance options, such as Death and Total & Permanent Disablement (TPD) cover

terms.name: Bendigo Staff Super Plan
terms.definition: The Bendigo Staff Super Plan is a superannuation (retirement savings) plan offered by Bendigo Bank, designed to help employees save for their retirement. It provides various investment options, including growth, balanced, and conservative funds, allowing members to choose based on their financial goals and risk tolerance. The plan may also include features like employer contributions, insurance options, and online account management.

terms.name: Bendigo Super Plan
terms.definition: The Bendigo Staff Super Plan was a superannuation offering by Bendigo Bank. However, Bendigo Superannuation has been sold to BetaShares as of September 2024.

terms.name: Bendigo Superannuation System
terms.definition:
Bendigo Superannuation System (BSS) is an administration system used to manage Bendigo Bank’s suite of superannuation products. It supports account administration, contributions, benefit payments, and compliance for Bendigo-branded superannuation plans.
Associated Products:

Bendigo SmartStart Super
Bendigo SmartStart Pension (if applicable)

terms.name: Beneficial Owner
terms.definition: A beneficial owner is an individual that controls or owns more than 25% of a company. Shell companies have long been a trick of the trade used in laundering proceeds of crime, and complications in determining beneficial ownership allow criminals a certain degree of anonymity in evading law enforcement.

terms.name: Beneficiary
terms.definition:
A beneficiary refers to one or more dependants nominated by the member to receive the member’s death benefit. If the member nominates their legal personal representative for all or part of the death benefit, that portion will be distributed in accordance with the member’s Will (if one exists) or under the laws of intestacy (if no Will exists).

 

Risk perspective: A person entitled to enjoy the benefits of an asset but does not legally own the assets. The term beneficiary typically relates to trusts and is distinctive from beneficial owner because a beneficiary is not the legal owner of the assets.

terms.name: Benefit
terms.definition: Means the amount payable under the insurance policy with respect to the insured member’s cover.

terms.name: Benefit calculation date
terms.definition: Defines the effective date used for the calculation of the Sum Insured.

terms.name: Benefit CalculationDate
terms.definition: Defines the effective date used for the calculation of the Sum Insured.

terms.name: Benefit Category
terms.definition: Benefit Class or Benefit Category is a type of benefit a member is classified into by the Employer under the fund for e.g. defined benefit, lifetime pension, etc.

terms.name: Benefit certificate
terms.definition:
A benefit certificate is a document issued by an actuary that relates to one or more defined benefit superannuation schemes. It specifies the notional employer contribution rate, which is the estimated long-term cost required to meet the minimum benefits for employees in a particular class.

Benefit certificates are used by employers to ensure compliance with superannuation guarantee obligations and typically remain valid for up to five years, unless a scheme is amended or a new certificate is issued

terms.name: Benefit Class
terms.definition: Benefit Class or Benefit Category is a type of benefit a member is classified into by the Employer under the fund for e.g. defined benefit, lifetime pension, etc.

terms.name: Benefit Design and Data Forum
terms.definition: The purpose of the BDDF is to review all new business arrangements and product design changes that fall outside of the product design standards.

terms.name: Benefit Expiry Age
terms.definition: A benefit payment advice is a notification of the payment made to the client.

terms.name: Benefit Formula
terms.definition: Details the formula that is used to calculate each of the benefit payment types.

terms.name: Benefit loading
terms.definition: An additional premium imposed by the Insurer, due normally to a medical risk, and will result in higher premiums.

terms.name: Benefit Payment Advice
terms.definition: A benefit payment advice is notification of the payment made to the client.

terms.name: Benefit Payment Form (withdrawal or rollout)
terms.definition:
A document or template used to facilitate or record payments related to specific benefits. These benefits can vary depending on the context, such as:

  • Government programs: A form might be used to claim unemployment benefits, social security payments, or other public assistance.
  • Employment benefits: It can track payments for things like retirement funds, healthcare reimbursements, or bonuses.
  • Insurance claims: A form may record payouts from health, accident, or disability insurance.

terms.name: Benefit Payment Period
terms.definition: Details the period that payments will be made for a claim.

terms.name: Benefit Payment Request
terms.definition: A benefit payment request is a request for payment by the owner of the account.

terms.name: Benefit payments
terms.definition:
Benefit payments refer to financial assistance provided by governments, employers, or insurance programs to individuals who qualify based on specific criteria. These payments can include pensions, welfare benefits, superannuation withdrawals, and insurance claims.

In Australia, benefit payments are commonly associated with Centrelink and include age pensions, disability support pensions, carer payments, and job seeker allowances. These payments help individuals manage financial needs due to retirement, unemployment, disability, or caregiving responsibilities.


Excludes: rollovers and successor fund transfers. Reference: SIS Regulations, Divisions 6.2 and 6.3; Superannuation Industry (Unclaimed Money and Lost Members) Act 1999, Part 4A. Reference link: https://www.legislation.gov.au/F1996B00580/2019-08-10/text

terms.full_name: Benefit payments made in accordance with actuarial recommendations
terms.name: Benefit payments made in accordance with actuarial recommendations
terms.definition:
Represents benefit payments paid to members in accordance with actuarial recommendations in a technical insolvency program as prescribed in the SIS Regulations.


Reference: SIS Regulations, r. 9.19(4).

Reference link: https://www.legislation.gov.au/F1996B00580/2019-08-10/text

terms.name: Benefit period
terms.definition: Applicable to Income Protection cover and is the maximum period an Income Protection Benefit will be paid for a condition or related condition

terms.name: Benefit Type
terms.definition: The benefit payment arrangement available to members based on them meeting pre-determined eligibility criteria.

terms.name: Benefits ceasing on TPD
terms.definition: Designates whether the SCI benefits related to this item cease on claim of a TPD benefit.

terms.name: Benefits of scale
terms.definition: As a business grows larger, more advantages can be gained. Benefits can include cost saving due to the size of the business, increase bargaining power with suppliers and enhanced efficiency in operations.

terms.name: Bereavement Benefit
terms.definition: If you die whilst in receipt of Income Protection benefits and have no Death cover in MLC MasterKey Super Fundamentals, the Insurer will pay an amount equal to 3 months of Total Disability benefits (or 6 months if your benefit period is to age 65), subject to the expiry of the benefit period.

terms.name: Binding Death Benefit Nomination
terms.definition:
A legal document that allows you to specify who will receive your superannuation benefits in the event of your death. By making this nomination, you ensure that the trustee of your super fund is legally bound to distribute your superannuation to the beneficiaries you have chosen, without discretion.

This type of nomination provides certainty and can help avoid disputes over your superannuation after your passing. However, it's important to keep it updated, as changes in relationships or circumstances could lead to unintended outcomes.

terms.name: Binding Death Nomination
terms.definition: A binding beneficiary nomination is a legally binding nomination that allows you to advise the trustee who is to receive your superannuation benefit in the event of your death.

terms.name: Board
terms.definition: Reference to IFL and its subsidiary boards including its licensed entity boards (combined or individually).

terms.name: Board Member
terms.definition: Employee Sub group to represent: Member of the Board at Insignia Financial.

terms.name: Borrowed Monies
terms.definition:
Relevant to WealthBuilder products range.

Borrowed monies is the scenario where a WealthBuilder product/account is funded by money borrowed externally.

If the Wealthbuilder account is funded by borrowed monies the client cannot secure a Loanback against that account. Borrowed Monies are borrowed externally.

terms.name: BPay Customer Reference Information
terms.definition:
BPay Customer Reference Information is a collection of individual elements that contain a unique BPay  reference number  for each of the following Super Contribution types -
- Personal
- SalarySacrifice
- Voluntary Employer
- Super Guarantee
- Spouse Contribution

terms.name: Broker
terms.definition: A professional / company that executes buy and sell orders of shares for their clients.

terms.name: Brokerage Costs
terms.definition: The amount paid to a broker when buying and selling underlying securities, e.g. shares and derivatives. These costs are incurred when the underlying fund managers trade investments as part of the ongoing management of the investment.

terms.name: Building Society
terms.definition: A building society is a financial institution that is owned by its members. It operates similarly to a bank but often focuses on savings accounts and mortgage lending. Building societies don't have external shareholders, and their profits are reinvested back into the organization to benefit members, rather than being distributed as dividends. They're particularly popular in the UK, though some exist in other countries as well.

terms.name: Bulk Switches
terms.definition: Bulk Switches are batch proceses. Individual  switching requirements are captured and saved, and all are executed in in a single batch process. Bulk switches also include processes where the monies belonging to a specific nominated  investment(s) are transferred for all/multiple members to a second nominated investment(s).

terms.name: Business Activity Statement
terms.definition: A Business Activity Statement (BAS) is a form that businesses in Australia use to report and pay various tax obligations to the Australian Taxation Office (ATO). It typically includes details about Goods and Services Tax (GST), Pay As You Go (PAYG) withholding, PAYG instalments, and other taxes like Fringe Benefits Tax (FBT) or Luxury Car Tax. Businesses registered for GST are required to lodge a BAS, which can be done monthly, quarterly, or annually depending on the business's turnover and reporting preferences.

terms.name: Business Affinity Program
terms.definition:
A business affinity program is a partnership between a business and an organization or association where the business offers special rates or services to the association's members. In return, both entities benefit from increased revenue and engagement.

These programs are particularly useful for associations looking to provide added value to their members while generating non-dues revenue. A well-structured affinity program can enhance member loyalty, offer exclusive perks, and create a steady income stream over time.

terms.name: Business All In One Superannuation
terms.definition: A consolidation of multiple superannuation accounts into a single account. Doing so may reduce fees and make management simple

terms.name: Business As Usual
terms.definition: Business as usual is a term that refers to the standard day-to-day business operations in an organisation.

terms.name: Business Capability Model
terms.definition:
The BCM is a high-level representation of IFL’s business capabilities.  The BCM is not strictly an information management model, but as a logical model derived from business processes and functions, it complements the BCS and the BIM to provide a comprehensive view of the breadth of IFL’s business functions, associated records and information holdings.

The BCM can be found via Transformation and Solutions’ Confluence pages.

terms.name: Business Classification Scheme
terms.definition: A classification of information and records into a hierarchical framework that organises the activities of the business.  The scheme presents a taxonomy of record types that can then be used for applications such as specifying a record retention schedule.

terms.name: Business Continuation Plan
terms.definition:
