A strategy designed to ensure that a business can continue operating smoothly in the face of unexpected disruptions, such as natural disasters, cyber-attacks, or other emergencies. It focuses on maintaining critical functions and minimizing downtime during crises.

Key elements of a business continuation plan include:

  • Risk Assessment: Identifying potential threats to business operations.
  • Business Impact Analysis (BIA): Evaluating the consequences of disruptions
  • Recovery Strategies: Developing methods to restore operations quickly
  • Roles and Responsibilities: Assigning tasks to team members during emergencies.
  • Testing and Updating: Regularly testing the plan and making improvements

terms.name: Business Data Lineage
terms.definition: A Data Lineage map depicting data flowing from one information asset to another. See also Technical Data Lineage.

terms.name: Business Development Manager
terms.definition: A Business Development Manager focuses on driving growth and identifying opportunities for a company. Their key responsibilities often include building strategic partnerships, expanding the client base, negotiating deals, and exploring new markets or industries. They also play a crucial role in developing strategies to increase revenue, maintain competitive advantage, and align business objectives with market demands.

terms.name: Business Information Model
terms.definition: An enterprise-wide view of information concepts.  The BIM provides a conceptual overview of the information landscape at Insignia, independent of organisational structure, business function, or technical architecture.  It is useful for understanding the breadth of information held by Insignia in order to properly govern and manage it.

terms.name: Business Intelligence
terms.definition: Business intelligence (BI) uses business analytics, data mining, data visualization, and data tools to help organizations make better data-driven decisions.

terms.name: Business Opportunity
terms.definition: This is formal documentation of a technology change request from the business.

terms.name: Business Partner
terms.definition: The Human Resources Partner who is aligned to a specific division or client group to support HR activities & needs

terms.name: Business Process Author
terms.definition: A Business Process Author is typically responsible for documenting, designing, and optimizing business processes within an organization. They create detailed workflows, process maps, and guidelines to ensure efficiency and consistency in operations. Their work often involves collaborating with teams to understand existing processes, identifying areas for improvement, and implementing changes to enhance productivity and compliance.

terms.name: Business Requirements Document
terms.definition:
A Business Requirements Document (BRD) is a formal document that outlines the objectives, needs, and expectations of a business project. It serves as a blueprint for stakeholders, ensuring alignment between business goals and project deliverables.

A BRD typically includes:

  • Project Scope – Defines what the project will and won’t cover
  • Objectives – The key goals the business aims to achieve.
  • Requirements – Specific needs, including functional and non-functional requirements.
  • Stakeholders – Identifies key parties involved and their roles
  • Assumptions & Constraints – Factors that may impact the project.
  • Budget & Timeline – Estimated costs and deadlines.

terms.name: Business Rules for Data Quality
terms.definition: Business-defined logic that specifies expected characteristics of data to ensure it meets quality requirements and supports business processes.  Business rules provide the conceptual basis for Data Quality Rules and may be implemented as Technical Data Quality Rules.

terms.name: Business Unit
terms.definition: A business unit (also referred to as a department, division, or functional area) is part of a company or organisation that represents a specific business function.

terms.name: Business Units / Functions / Entities
terms.definition: Reference to divisions, divisional areas, business line, region or entities within the Group.

terms.name: Business Verification Testing
terms.definition: Post Implementation testing i.e. testing that happens once changes have gone in within a Live environment

terms.name: Business, State and Branch Code
terms.definition: BSB stands for bank, state and branch number. It identifies the bank, branch and state that you opened your account in.

terms.name: Buy-sell spread
terms.definition: A buy-sell spread is a fee to recover costs incurred by the trustee of the superannuation entity in relation to the sale and purchase of assets of the entity.

terms.name: Buys
terms.definition:
A generic term referring to the "buying" of additional units in an Investment Fund(s).
Will typically include other information such as the Investment funds being bought, number of Investment Units, transaction date, etc.

terms.name: Candidate
terms.definition: Any Person who applies for a vacancy. It can be internal or External

terms.name: Capital Gain
terms.definition: Difference between the cost base of an asset when it is purchased and the proceeds from its sale.

terms.name: Capital Gains / Losses
terms.definition:
A Capital Gain is the current difference between an  investment fund's unit price value  and  the original purchase price, multiplied by the units, multiplied by the number of units. The Capital Gain excludes income that have been received by the fund as a result of dividends, contributions, etc. 
While an investment may carry a value / unit price above the original purchase pric, any gains while the asset is still being held are considered unrealised as the asset is only being valued at current market value. If selling an asset results in a loss, there is a realised loss instead.

terms.name: Capital Gains / Losses - Realised
terms.definition:
A Realised Capital Gain gain results from selling an investment at a price higher than the original purchase price. 
While an investment may carry a value / unit price above the original purchase pric, any gains while the asset is still being held are considered unrealised as the asset is only being valued at current market value. If selling an asset results in a loss, there is a realised loss instead.

terms.name: Capital Gains Tax
terms.definition: A tax applied on the increase in the capital value of an investment that is payable when the investment is sold.

terms.name: Carer
terms.definition: The name of the person in whose care communications are trusted.

terms.name: Carer's Name
terms.definition: The name of the person in whose care communications are trusted.

terms.name: CAS Team
terms.definition: The SAP success factors support team from SAP

terms.name: Cash Account Minimum
terms.definition: The minimum balance that a Client must maintain in their Cash Account Preferences

terms.name: Cash Account Preferences
terms.definition: A Client's Cash Account preferences acontrol how their Cash Account is managed. A Client typically  needs to retain a minimum balance in their Cash Account to cover fees, expenses, taxes, insurance premiums, pension payments and withdrawals (where applicable).

terms.name: Cash Holding Maximum
terms.definition: Can be  expressed as a percentage (%) of the Client's total Account balance, including Investments. Designates the maximum cash that a Client can have in their cash Account, before funds are accessed and distibuted across their Investments according to their Investment Authority.

terms.name: Cash Holding Minimum
terms.definition: Can be  expressed as a percentage (%) of the Client's total Account balance, including Investments. Designates the minimum cash that a Client can have in their cash Account, before the balance is restored by selling the Client's Investments's prportionally according to their Investment authority.

terms.name: Cash In - Checking
terms.definition: To convert an asset, investment, or something of value into cash. For example, selling shares of stock or redeeming a bond can be described as "cashing in."

terms.name: Cash In - Rework
terms.definition: To convert an asset, investment, or savings into cash.

terms.name: Cash In Rework - Check
terms.definition: The additional costs, including labor, materials, and time, incurred when correcting defective or non-conforming products or services that initially failed to meet quality standards.

terms.name: Cash Management Trust
terms.definition: A type of investment vehicle that pools funds from multiple investors to invest in short-term, high-quality securities like treasury notes and bank bills. These trusts are designed to provide investors with liquidity, safety, and competitive returns compared to traditional savings accounts. They are often used by individuals or businesses looking to manage their cash efficiently while earning some interest.

terms.name: Cash Out - Checking MKIS
terms.definition: Selling an investment, asset, or something of value to convert it into cash. For instance, if you sell your shares in a company, you're "cashing out."

terms.name: Cash Out Rework
terms.definition: "Cash out" refers to money leaving a business, while "rework" means redoing work that wasn't done correctly the first time, requiring additional resources.

terms.name: Cash Out Rework - Check
terms.definition: The assessment of the process of revisiting or modifying a previous cash-out transaction or strategy

terms.name: Cash Receipt
terms.definition:
A cash receipt is a document that records a transaction where cash is received by a business, individual, or organization. It typically includes details such as:

  • Date of transaction
  • Amount received
  • Payer's information
  • Reason for payment
  • Receipt number
  • Signature or authorization (if required)

Cash receipts are crucial for bookkeeping, financial tracking, and auditing. They help businesses and individuals maintain accurate records of income and expenses.

terms.name: Cash Top Up
terms.definition:
The Cash Account top up process aims to sell invested funds in a Client's Account, as directed by the Account's standing instructions, in the event that their cash Account is reduced to less than apecified minimum. The proceeds from the Investment sell-down are deposited into the Client's cash Account to restore it to the minimum balance.
The minimum can be expressed as a percentage of the total Account balance, including Investments. 
The amount it tops up to will  vary depending on the Account's standing instruction. It is most commonly dictated by the Product Minimum, but can also be higher or lower.

terms.name: Cashflow
terms.definition: Generally refers to movements of cash flowing In or Out of the company - Inflows, Outflows

terms.name: Casual
terms.definition: Employee Sub group to represent: Employee who has a causal contract with the company.

terms.name: Catalogue management
terms.definition: Catalogue Management is the maintenance and accessibility of a structured collection of products or services offered by suppliers by either the form of a digital punch out or internal systems catalogue. It streamlines the procurement process by providing a standard user-friendly platform for employees to browse and select, order items. This enhances the efficiency ensuring compliance with preferred purchasing options.

terms.name: Central bank account
terms.definition: A central bank account refers to an account held by commercial banks or financial institutions at a central bank. These accounts are used for managing reserves, settling interbank transactions, and facilitating monetary policy operations. Central banks, such as the Reserve Bank of Australia, oversee these accounts to ensure financial stability and regulate the money supply.

terms.name: Change Account Value - Check
terms.definition:
To "change account value check" likely refers to verifying or updating the value of your account holdings, which includes assets like cash, stocks, and other investments.
Checking can be conducted by:

  • Online Brokerage Platforms: Most online brokers provide real-time account balances and portfolio information. 
  • Mobile Apps: Many brokers offer mobile apps that allow you to view your account value on the go. 
  • Statements: Periodic statements from your broker will show your account value at the end of each period.

terms.name: Change Account Value - Rework
terms.definition: Updating or adjusting the value of assets or holdings within a financial account, potentially due to a change in market conditions or other factors.

terms.name: Change Account Value Rework - Check
terms.definition: Analyzing and checking updated or adjusted the value of assets or holdings within a financial account, potentially due to a change in market conditions or other factors.

terms.name: Change My Details - Checking
terms.definition:
"Change my details" typically refers to the process of updating personal or account information within a system, service, or organization. This could involve modifications such as:

  • Updating contact information (e.g., phone number, email address).
  • Changing payment details, like credit card information.
  • Modifying addresses for billing or delivery.
  • Adjusting preferences, such as subscription settings.

terms.name: Change My Details - Rework
terms.definition: The act of checking details that have been changed

terms.name: Change My Details Rework - Checking
terms.definition: Revising or updating personal information to ensure accuracy or compliance with specific requirements. The term "rework" generally refers to modifying or improving something to meet desired standards or specifications

terms.name: Change of Account Details
terms.definition: A "Change of Account Details" typically refers to the process of updating or modifying information associated with a particular account. This could involve changing personal details such as name, address, email, phone number, or payment methods. It is often required when users move, update contact information, or switch financial details linked to an account

terms.full_name: Change of address / Change of Advisor
terms.name: Change of address / Change of Advisor
terms.definition:
A change of address refers to the process of updating your mailing address when you move to a new location. This ensures that your mail is forwarded to your new address and helps you stay connected with important correspondence. It typically involves notifying postal services, government agencies, and other organizations about your new address.


A "change of advisor" typically refers to the process of switching from one advisor to another in a financial context. In financial settings, it could mean transferring advisory responsibilities to a new professional due to changes in circumstances or preferences.

terms.name: Change of Beneficial Ownership
terms.definition:
A change of beneficial ownership occurs when the person or entity that benefits from an asset (such as property, stocks, or a financial account) is altered. This change doesn't necessarily involve transferring legal ownership but shifts the entitlement to benefits like income, use, or profit derived from the asset.

Key aspects of this process include:

  • Legal Transfer: Depending on the asset type and jurisdiction, a change of beneficial ownership might require formal documentation or legal filings
  • Tax Implications: Such changes can trigger tax liabilities like capital gains tax or stamp duty, so careful planning is essential.
  • Trusts and Estates: In trust or estate planning, beneficial ownership changes often occur to distribute assets to beneficiaries or align with specific conditions in a trust deed.

terms.name: Change of name
terms.definition:
A change of name refers to the legal process by which an individual alters their name. This process is typically done through a government authority or registry and involves completing required paperwork, providing identification, and sometimes offering a valid reason for the change.

Common scenarios for a change of name include:

  • Marriage or divorce.
  • Personal preference or gender affirmation
  • Cultural or religious reasons
  • Adoption or family restructuring

terms.name: Channel
terms.definition: Historically in P&I, Channel refers to the grouping of our Advised and Non-Advised member data for reporting purposes and is based on the grouping of Dealer Groups. The current Channels include Aligned, Non-Aligned and Unadvised.

terms.name: Checking
terms.definition:
Compliance refers to the act of following laws, regulations, and industry standards to ensure ethical and legal conduct within an organization or profession. It helps maintain integrity and prevents misconduct.

Professional standards are guidelines, codes of conduct, and best practices that define the expected behavior and performance in a specific industry or job role. These ensure consistency, quality, and accountability among professionals.

terms.name: Cheque
terms.definition: Stop Payment/ Reissue (Unrepresented Cheque)

terms.name: Choice of Fund
terms.definition:
A Choice of Fund refers to the right of an employee to select the superannuation fund where their employer contributions will be deposited. In Australia, this is an important aspect of the Superannuation Guarantee (SG) system, which requires employers to contribute a percentage of an employee’s earnings into a super fund.

Employees generally have the flexibility to nominate their preferred fund, which can be an industry fund, retail fund, corporate fund, or a self-managed super fund (SMSF). However, some employment agreements or awards may specify a default fund if an employee does not make a choice.

terms.name: Choice of Super Fund
terms.definition: Eligible employees are permitted to nominate the super fund into which their employer pays their SG contributions.

terms.name: Classification
terms.definition:
The process of describing information or data according to a pre-defined list of classes. The most common classification scheme applied to IFL data is Confidentiality, with information classified as public, internal, confidential or highly confidential.

Information can be classified in many ways. While the Information Classification and Handling Standard specifies several of these, different classification schemes can be used for different business purposes. Examples of this include using the BIM or BCS to classify Data Elements, Records or Information Assets.

terms.name: Clawback
terms.definition: A clawback is a provision or action that allows an employer, government, or organization to reclaim money that has already been paid out, typically under certain conditions.

terms.name: Clearing House
terms.definition: Allows employers to make a single online payment covering their SG contributions for employees. An example of MLC's clearing house is MLC EmployerPay.

terms.name: Client
terms.definition: An individual or organisation working with Insignia in order to achieve their goals;

terms.name: Client & Technology
terms.definition: The division of Insignia Financial which is accountable for Technology supporting our business (including storage, data, infrastructure, build, test and support as well as the operational support of our customers with superannuation products

terms.name: Client Consent Form
terms.definition: A document used to obtain a client's permission for a specific action, service, or treatment. It ensures that the client understands the details, risks, and benefits involved and agrees to proceed.

terms.name: Client DataBase Identity
terms.definition:
A client database identity refers to the unique identification and authentication of clients within a database system. This is crucial for managing customer information securely and efficiently. Businesses use Client Relationship Management (CRM) systems to store and organize customer data, including names, contact details, purchase history, and preferences.

In cybersecurity, Identity and Access Management (IAM) ensures that only authorized users can access sensitive client data, protecting against unauthorized breaches.

terms.name: Client ID Consolidation
terms.definition:
Client ID consolidation typically refers to the process of merging or unifying multiple client identifiers into a single, centralized ID. This is often done to streamline data management, improve customer experience, and ensure consistency across systems. Here are some common scenarios:

  1. Identity Management: Tools like Auth0 can help consolidate multiple identity sources, allowing users to access various applications with a single set of credentials.
  2. Accounting and Reporting: Platforms like Thomson Reuters provide solutions to consolidate client data for reporting purposes, enabling cross-company comparisons and streamlined financial management

terms.name: Client Registration - Date of Registration
terms.definition: The date that a Client was granted access to the  online application relevant to their investment Account.

terms.name: Client Registration - Email Account
terms.definition: The Client's email Account is usually used as their login id to their online application

terms.name: Client Registration - Password
terms.definition: The Client's password used in conjunction with their login id to gain access to their online application

terms.name: Client Registration Details
terms.definition:
After a Client opens an Account they can then be registered with a usercode,  password and various permissions/preferences to use the online applications relevant to their Account.
This collective information is referred to as the Client Registration Details.

terms.name: Client Reporting Pack
terms.definition: Clients receive a statement (Client reporting) pack typically, every six months. The pack includes the statement, covering letter and may also contain other materials such as the Product Disclosure Document.

terms.name: Client risk assessment methodology
terms.definition: The methodology used by the Insignia Financial DBG for conducting and updating a client’s risk rating as required under the Program.

terms.name: Client Service Agreement
terms.definition: A Client Service Agreement (CSA) is a document that outlines the ongoing services that a financial adviser agrees to provide to a client, typically in exchange for a fee. It helps formalise the ongoing relationship between the adviser and the client, especially for services provided after the initial advice is delivered.

terms.name: Client Services (Employer Super)
terms.definition: "Client services" in the context of employer super refers to the support and assistance provided by a superannuation fund or service provider to employers and their employees. This can include helping employers manage their superannuation obligations, such as contributions, compliance with regulations, and providing tools or resources to streamline the process. It may also involve offering guidance to employees about their superannuation accounts, investment options, and retirement planning.

terms.name: Client Services Consultant
terms.definition: A Client Services Consultant is a professional who acts as the bridge between a company and its clients, ensuring the highest level of customer satisfaction. Their role typically involves understanding client needs, providing solutions, addressing concerns, and maintaining strong relationships. They may also assist in onboarding new clients, coordinating services, and ensuring that expectations align with delivered outcomes. Often, they're key in fostering trust and loyalty by delivering exceptional support and communication.

terms.name: Closing Balance
terms.definition: The closing balance is the total amount in the member’s investment options at the end of the reporting period. It may exclude fees and taxes applicable on account closure or annual tax credits not yet processed. If the member withdraws the benefit in full before tax credits are applied, these may be lost.

terms.name: Cloud Application Services Team
terms.definition: The SAP SuccessFactors support team from SAP

terms.name: CNB
terms.definition: Task on CRMS meaning a superannuation contribution from a MKBS member.

terms.name: Code of Conduct
terms.definition: Insignia Financial Code of Conduct policy

terms.name: Commencement Incentive
terms.definition: An industry term to define where new employees are offered either an inducement to join the company,  or a value to compensate for benefits lost by leaving their previous company and joining a new company

terms.name: Commercial Mortgage Backed Securities
terms.definition:
Commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) are investment products backed by a pool of commercial real estate loans. These loans are secured by income-producing properties such as office buildings, shopping malls, hotels, and apartment complexes.

CMBS are structured into different tranches, with varying levels of risk and return. Investors in higher-rated tranches receive payments first, while lower-rated tranches take on more risk but offer potentially higher yields.

They play a significant role in the real estate financing market by providing liquidity to lenders and offering investors exposure to commercial property assets

terms.name: Commission To
terms.definition: Commissions can be directed to an adviser other than the nominated adviser who is managing the Account, referred to as the Commissions To adviser

terms.name: Common Reporting Standard
terms.definition: The Common Reporting Standard (CRS) is a global framework developed by the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) in 2014 to combat tax evasion. It facilitates the automatic exchange of financial account information between tax authorities worldwide. Under CRS, financial institutions in participating countries are required to collect and report information about customers' tax residency and financial accounts

terms.name: Commonwealth Bank of Australia
terms.definition: Commonwealth Bank of Australia

terms.name: Commutation
terms.definition: Commutation is the process of converting some or all of a super income stream into a super lump sum

terms.name: Commutation Arrangements
terms.definition: The arrangements/rules for any full or part commutation.

terms.name: Compa Ratio
terms.definition: At Insignia this refers to the relationship of an individual’s salary against the midpoint of benchmark

terms.name: COMPASS
terms.definition: Database for MK clients.

terms.name: Compensation Module
terms.definition: The module in SuccessFactors that holds the compensation information for employees

terms.name: Completed Membership
terms.definition: The period from the members date of accrual to the date of calculation.

terms.name: Completed Membership Basis
terms.definition: The method used to express the completed membership period. E.g. years and days, years and months.

terms.name: Compliance
terms.definition: Compliance is the extent to which an organization conforms with regulatory requirements for its operations.

terms.name: Compliance and Professional Standards
terms.definition:
Compliance and professional standards are crucial in any industry—they ensure ethical behavior, legal adherence, and quality performance. Depending on the field, these standards can cover areas like corporate governance, regulatory requirements, ethical guidelines, and best practices.

For instance, in finance, compliance focuses on regulations like anti-money laundering (AML) and data protection. In healthcare, professional standards ensure patient safety and confidentiality. In tech, compliance often involves cybersecurity and privacy laws.

terms.name: Compliance Officer
terms.definition:
A Compliance Officer is a professional responsible for ensuring that an organization follows all relevant laws, regulations, and internal policies. Their main goal is to help the organization avoid legal risks, maintain ethical standards, and operate within the boundaries of regulatory requirements.
This is a required role in the AML/CTF Act. The responsibilities of this role are held by the Insignia Financial DBG’s AML/CTF Compliance Officer.

terms.name: Complying Pension Type
terms.definition: Lifetime Complying pensions are set for the ‘Life’ of the pensioner (including Reversionary if one has been nominated). Techincally, they don’t have an account balance held with IOOF. Lifetime pensions require an actuary to perform a  valuation to  determine if the fund is solvent to be able to continue paying future pension payments.

terms.name: Complying Super Fund
terms.definition: A regulated super fund qualifying for concessional tax rates by meeting statutory requirements.

terms.name: Component activity
terms.definition:
Components of performance are classified into the following component activities:

  • Investment;
  • Administration
  • Transaction; and
  • Advice.

A component of net return can only be characterised as being related to one component activity. Fees, costs and taxes may be incurred (or refunds received) in undertaking any of these activities. Return may be generated via Investment activities only.

terms.name: Compounding
terms.definition: A process that works for you when the money you invest earns more money and those earnings, in turn, are also invested and earn money (Earnings however, may be positive or negative).

terms.name: Comprehensive Income Products for Retirement
terms.definition: Comprehensive Income Products for Retirement (CIPRs) are financial products designed to provide retirees with a stable and regular income stream throughout their retirement. These products aim to address key challenges like longevity risk (outliving one's savings) and offer flexibility for accessing capital when needed. CIPRs are part of Australia's Retirement Income Framework, which encourages superannuation funds to develop strategies that help retirees manage their income effectively.

terms.name: Concessional
terms.definition: A concessional contribution is a before-tax contribution from which tax is deducted and paid to the Australian Taxation Office. This includes employer superannuation guarantee contributions and salary sacrifice contributions. Concessional contributions may appear as positive amounts if the member has claimed a tax deduction on voluntary contributions.

terms.name: Concessional Component
terms.definition:
One of the specially treated components of a lump sum eligible termination payment (ETP) benefit. Until 30 June 1994, the concessional component consisted of bona fide redundancy payments, invalidity payments and payments made to retirees under approved early retirement schemes. From 1 July 1994, approved early retirement and redundancy payments are not part of an ETP, and invalidity payments form a new separate component.

Concessional components are no longer able to be paid from a superannuation fund, although they may still be paid directly by employers. Concessional components paid before 1 July 1994 and rolled over will remain until they are finally paid out as benefits.

terms.name: Concessional contribution cap
terms.definition: The concessional contributions cap is the maximum amount of before-tax contributions you can contribute to your super each year without contributions being subject to extra tax.

terms.name: Concessional Contributions
terms.definition: Also known as before-tax contributions. Includes Super Guarantee (SG) contributions, salary sacrifice payments, award contributions and personal contributions for which you claim a tax deduction. Taxed at 15% as they enter clients' accounts.

terms.name: Concessional Contributions Cap
terms.definition: Cap limiting the amount of before-tax contributions clients can make into their super account in a financial year. Contributions above the annual cap incur additional tax.

terms.name: Concessional Contributions Cap (for Super)
terms.definition: The concessional contributions cap is the maximum amount of before-tax contributions you can make to your superannuation each year without incurring extra tax. As of 1 July 2024, the cap is $30,000. From 1 July 2021 to 30 June 2024, the cap was $27,500. If you have unused cap amounts from previous years, you may be able to carry them forward to increase your contribution caps in later years.

terms.name: Condition of release
terms.definition: Condition of release – a restriction which must be met before your super funds can be accessed. Conditions of release include retirement (permanent retirement on leaving an employment arrangement after turning 60), reaching age 65, death, permanent incapacity, and terminal illness.

terms.name: Condition of Release
terms.definition: A condition of release is a requirement that must be met before superannuation funds can be accessed. Examples include retirement, reaching age 65, permanent incapacity, or terminal illness.

terms.name: Confidentiality
terms.definition:
The extent to which access to information must be restricted and controlled to ensure privacy and/or comply with regulatory or business-confidentiality requirements. 

All information at Insignia should be classified according to its confidentiality and handled accordingly.

terms.name: Consent Order
terms.definition: An Order directed by a Court where both parties to the separation or divorce have agreed to splitting of the family assets.

terms.name: Consultant/Employee
terms.definition: Employee Sub group to represent: SOW resources

terms.name: Consultant/Managed Service
terms.definition: Employee Sub group to represent: SOW resources that are covered under a big agreement/ SAP resources/ Adobe Resources

terms.name: Consultant/Offshore
terms.definition: Employee Sub group to represent: All resources regardless of the type of the agreement who are off- shore

terms.name: Consultant/WIPRO P&I
terms.definition: Employee Sub group to represent: ANZ resources  ( either offshore - on shore)

terms.name: Consumer Price Index.
terms.definition: Represents the consumer price index.

terms.name: Content tracker
terms.definition: A calendar/schedule of all past and upcoming content and workflow for the Employee Experience and Communications team. This allows the team to plan, organise and prioritise enterprise and divisional communications and strategic priorities.

terms.name: Contingent - AET
terms.definition: Employee Sub group to represent: EQT resources

terms.name: Contingent Workforce
terms.definition: A contingent workforce refers to a labor pool made up of people who are hired on a non-permanent basis. These workers are typically not full-time employees and often work under short-term contracts, freelance agreements, or temporary arrangements.

terms.name: Continuation Option
terms.definition: The ability to retain insurance cover levels after leaving a fund without the need to supply additional medical information.

terms.name: Continuous Improvement
terms.definition:
Continuous improvement is an ongoing effort to enhance products, services, or processes through incremental improvements or breakthrough innovations. It is a key principle in business, manufacturing, and personal development, focusing on efficiency, effectiveness, and quality.

This concept is widely used in methodologies like Lean, Six Sigma, and Kaizen, where organizations strive to eliminate waste, improve workflow, and refine operations through small but meaningful changes.

terms.name: Contract management
terms.definition: Contract management is proactive management of contract obligations and relationships, involving contract renewal planning, performance evaluation, continuous improvement to drive efficiency and mitigate risks and to achieve optimal results.

terms.name: Contractor/Referred
terms.definition:
In Australia, a contractor—also known as an independent contractor—is someone who runs their own business and provides services to others. They typically:

  • Use their own tools and methods
  • Negotiate their own fees and working arrangements
  • Can work for multiple clients at once
  • Are responsible for their own tax, insurance, and superannuation obligations

They’re different from employees, who work in someone else’s business under that employer’s control regarding how, when, and where the work is done.

As for “referred”, it’s not a formal employment category but usually means someone who has been recommended or directed to a business or service—often by another person or agency. For example, a contractor might be referred to a client by a recruitment agency or another contractor.

Employee Sub group to represent: Resource pay rolled through our  payroll partner (On- Shore)

terms.name: Contractor/Sourced
terms.definition: Employee Sub group to represent: Resource sourced through recruitment agency (On- Shore)

terms.name: Contribution Payment Information
terms.definition:
A generic term representing a collaboration of elements associated with how a Member makes regular payments into their superannuation Account -

- Direct Debit or Cheque

- Default Cheque BSB

- Default Drawer Name

terms.name: Contribution Splitting
terms.definition: Rules allowing clients to split their super contributions with their spouse (married or de facto) providing they meet the eligibility criteria.

terms.name: Contribution Type
terms.definition: The type of contribution as it is commonly known by.

terms.name: Contributions in arrears
terms.definition: Represents the amount of contributions (as recommended by the actuary) expected to be received by the RSE but which were not received by the expected or agreed date. Includes: employer contributions.

terms.name: Contributions Tax
terms.definition: Tax applied to certain contributions such as employer and salary sacrifice contributions when they are paid into a superannuation Fund.

terms.name: Conversation ID
terms.definition:
In the context of SuperStream income stream rollovers, the Conversation ID is a unique identifier assigned to each rollover transaction initiated through the SuperStream system.

What is the Conversation ID?

  • The Conversation ID is a unique reference number generated when a rollover is initiated via SuperStream.
  • It typically looks like:
    Rollover.70116802058.xxxxxxxxx
  • This ID is used to track and trace the progress of a specific rollover transaction between the transferring and receiving super funds.

Why is it important?

  • It allows both the transferring and receiving funds (including SMSFs and APRA-regulated funds) to identify and follow up on a specific rollover.
  • If there are delays or issues, providing the Conversation ID helps the receiving fund or service provider locate the transaction quickly.
  • It is often required when contacting support teams or the ATO regarding a rollover status.

terms.name: Conversion Factor
terms.definition: Factor used to convert a lump sum to a pension or pension back to lump sum.

terms.name: Conversion Name
terms.definition: The name/code used to identify the conversion as noted in the definitions tab.

terms.name: Converted Loanback Indicator
terms.definition:
Indicates whether an  account has been marked as having a loanback against it.
Withdrawal transactions examine this indicator and
inform the operator if they need to check the  loanback
system.
** Loanbacks are now checked automatically

terms.name: Corporate Authorised Representative
terms.definition:
A Corporate Authorised Representative (CAR) is a company authorized by an Australian Financial Services Licensee (AFSL holder) to provide financial services on their behalf. This authorization is common in Australia's financial industry and is regulated by the Australian Securities and Investments Commission (ASIC).

Here are some key points about a CAR:

  • Legal Role: The CAR acts as a representative of the AFSL holder, delivering financial advice or services in line with the licensee's scope and conditions
  • Authorization Agreement: The relationship between the CAR and the AFSL holder is defined through a formal agreement specifying the services the CAR can offer
  • Compliance Obligations: The CAR must comply with the regulatory requirements and the AFSL holder's standards to ensure the services provided are ethical and legal.
  • Advantages: Establishing a CAR allows a company to provide financial services without needing its own AFSL, which can save costs and time.

terms.name: Corporate credit card
terms.definition: Corporate credit cards that are issued to staff for business approved procurement

terms.name: Cost Center Id
terms.definition: Unique Id of the Cost Center which is assigned as default to user. Cost Center  mentioned must be existing and active in the system, and mapped to the corresponding OU level 1 and 2 mentioned. Mandatory when eProc and eInvoice are assigned to the user

terms.name: Cost of Product
terms.definition: The cost of product gives a summary calculation about how ongoing annual fees and costs can affect your superannuation investment over a 1-year period for all superannuation products and investment options.

terms.name: Cost optimisation
terms.definition:
Cost optimisation is the work we are doing to simplify our business and lower our cost to serve.

  • The formation of Insignia Financial has given us significant opportunity in terms of scale and reach
  • we are already delivering better outcomes for more members by providing improved pricing and product performance.
  • To support this sustainably, we must continue to remove inefficiencies and manage our expenses tightly.
  • This will help lower our cost to serve - it is currently higher than industry standard.
  • ‘Simplifying our business’ and lowering our cost to serve is a continuation of our original strategy of acquiring P&I and MLC to unlock the benefits of scale.
  • As a part of our strategy to ‘simplifying our business’ our goal is to reduce the cost base of our organisation by $175-190 million of annualised benefits over financial year 2024 to 2026.

terms.name: Counter-Terrorism Financing
terms.definition: Terrorism financing is financial support - in any form - of terrorism or those who encourage, plan or engage in terrorist activities. It involves raising, moving or storing funds or other assets intended for a terrorist organisation or terrorist act.

terms.name: Counterparty Identifier
terms.definition:
Means a unique identifier representing the counterparty. The Legal Identifier (LEI) or, where applicable, the interim entity identifier of the entity should be used. The Legal Entity Identifier is a 20-digit, alphanumeric code issued by a Local Operating Unit in accordance with the International Organization for Standardization’s (ISO) International Standard ISO 17442.

Note: The Global Legal Entity Identifier Foundation maintains a free online searchable database of Legal Entity Identifiers, available at https://search.gleif.org/#/search/.

terms.name: Country
terms.definition: The country of Customer origin

terms.name: Court Order
terms.definition: An Order directed by a Court where both parties to the separation or divorce cannot agree to splitting of the family assets, where the Court makes a determination.

terms.name: Cover Cease Age
terms.definition: The age at which cover ceases.

terms.name: CPI-linked benefits
terms.definition: If you receive benefits for 12 consecutive months, they’ll be increased by the lesser of; CPI, or 5%, during each subsequent 12-month period. This does not apply for the two-year benefit period.

terms.name: Credit Ombudsman Service
terms.definition: A dispute service for credit providers

terms.name: Crediting Rate
terms.definition: Investment return (as a percentage) achieved annually by each investment option and credited to a super account as investment earnings.

terms.name: Critical Business Process
terms.definition: Defined business activities that are essential to the core operations of Insignia, the absence of which would have a major impact on the ability of the organisation to function.  Information and data used within critical business processes must be managed such that the operability of the process is assured.

terms.name: Critical Business Report
terms.definition: Defined, structured and formatted information that is produced in order to meet regulatory or market obligations, or is essential for critical business processes within Insignia, and/or governance or leadership decisions by senior executives or the Boards.

terms.name: Critical Data Element (CDE)
terms.definition:
A Data Element that has been assessed as critical according to the criticality assessment criteria outlined in the Critical Data Element Standard.  CDEs receive an elevated level of oversight and management, including data quality monitoring and reporting and prioritisation in issue management. 

Each CDE is allocated to one Data Domain. 

See also Domain Critical Data Element and Enterprise Critical Data Element.

terms.name: Critical Data Element (CDE) Owner
terms.definition: The person accountable for ensuring that a CDE is properly governed and managed.  CDE owners are the Domain Data Owner to whose domain a Critical Data Element has been allocated.

terms.name: CSA Delivery Status
terms.definition:
CSA Delivery Status:

Definitions of key milestones are:

Authorisation Status – record the “CSA Auth. Date Linked” date if the “CSA Auth. Benchmark Status” is completed.

Half Yearly - Meeting Booked – A Diary meeting has been booked matching Diary Subcategory “Half Yearly Review Meeting” within the CSA period (excluding meetings with “Not Held” status). Meeting date is recorded if found.
• Highlighting rules: Meeting booked within 182 days of CSA End Date = ORANGE | Meeting booked more than 182 days before the CSA End Date = GREEN | NAs and “No Diary Entries” = GREEN

Half Yearly - Meeting Held – Half Yearly - Review Meeting Note benchmark is completed.

Half Yearly - Advice Provided – Half Yearly Advice Review benchmark is completed.

Portfolio - Meeting Booked – A Diary meeting has been booked matching Diary Subcategory “Portfolio Review Meeting” within the CSA period (excluding meetings with “Not Held” status). Meeting date is recorded if found.
• Highlighting rules: Meeting booked within 182 days of CSA End Date = ORANGE | Meeting booked more than 182 days before the CSA End Date = GREEN | NAs and “No Diary Entries” = GREEN

Portfolio - Meeting Held – Annual Portfolio - Review Meeting Note benchmark is completed.

Portfolio - Advice Provided – Annual Portfolio Review benchmark is completed.

Annual Review - Meeting Booked – A Diary meeting has been booked matching Diary Subcategory “Advice Review Meeting” within the CSA period (excluding meetings with “Not Held” status). Meeting date is recorded if found.
• Highlighting rules: Meeting booked within 45 days of CSA End Date = ORANGE | Meeting booked more than 45 days before the CSA End Date = GREEN | No “Diary Entries” = GREEN

Annual Review - Meeting Held – “Advice Review - Review Meeting Note” benchmark is completed. Includes end of year half yearly.

Annual Review - Advice Provided – “Advice Review” benchmark is completed. Includes end of year half yearly.

terms.name: CSA Package Value
terms.definition: The total monetary value agreed for the Client Service Agreement (CSA)

terms.name: Currency Code
terms.definition: Valid ISO 4217 currency code

terms.name: Currency For Limits
terms.definition: The currency in which the users limits are defined

terms.name: Custodian
terms.definition: The role of the custodian is limited to holding the assets of the Trust as agent of the Responsible Entity. The custodian has no supervisory role in relation to the operation of the Trust.

terms.name: Customer Address
terms.definition: A customer address refers to the physical or mailing location provided by a customer for communication, delivery, or billing purposes. It can include details such as street name, city, state, postal code, and sometimes additional contact information like email or phone number. This address is essential for businesses to ensure accurate and efficient service delivery

terms.name: Customer Age
terms.definition: Customer age refers to the age of an individual who interacts with or purchases from a business. It is often used in demographic analysis to understand customer behavior, preferences, and trends. Businesses may use customer age data to tailor marketing strategies, product offerings, and services to specific age groups

terms.name: Customer Balance
terms.definition: Customer balance refers to the amount of money a customer owes to a business or has available in their account with the business. It can represent unpaid invoices, outstanding debts, or credits that the customer can use for future transactions. This term is commonly used in accounting and financial contexts to track customer payments and account statuses.

terms.name: Customer Due Diligence
terms.definition:
Measures taken to

a) identify a client and

b) verify the client’s identity by using documents or reliable and independent sources.

It may also include obtaining information about why the client is making use of the Insignia Financial DBG’s products or services. 
The gathering and recording of such information is also used to assist in identifying any unusual or suspicious transactions or activity.

terms.name: Customer Identification Process
terms.definition:
This process is a critical step in verifying the identity of individuals or entities before they can access certain services, particularly in financial institutions. This process is often part of compliance programs like Know Your Customer (KYC) and Anti-Money Laundering (AML) efforts.

Here's how it typically works:

  1. Information Collection: Gathering key details such as the customer's full name, date of birth, address, and identification number (e.g., passport or driver's license).
  2. Verification: Confirming the authenticity of the provided information through documents, databases, or direct contact.
  3. Risk Assessment: Evaluating the customer's risk profile based on factors like their occupation, source of funds, and country of residence.
  4. Ongoing Monitoring: Continuously tracking customer activities to detect and report suspicious behavior.

This process ensures compliance with legal requirements and helps prevent financial crimes like money laundering and fraud.

terms.name: Customer Information Brochure
terms.definition: A customer information brochure is typically a document provided by businesses to inform customers about their services, policies, or procedures. It may include details about account management, terms and conditions, privacy policies, and other relevant information to help customers understand their relationship with the company.

terms.name: Customer Liaison
terms.definition: A Customer Liaison serves as the critical link between a company and its clients, ensuring smooth communication and excellent service. They are responsible for addressing customer inquiries, managing concerns, coordinating resolutions, and keeping clients informed about updates or changes. This role often involves building and maintaining strong relationships to foster trust and loyalty while enhancing customer satisfaction.

terms.name: Customer Owned Banking Association
terms.definition: The Customer Owned Banking Association (COBA) is the industry body representing Australia's customer-owned banking institutions, including mutual banks, credit unions, and building societies. These institutions are unique because they are owned by their customers rather than external shareholders, allowing profits to be reinvested into better services and community benefits. COBA advocates for its members, promotes customer-first banking, and ensures compliance with regulatory standards

terms.name: Customer Request Management System
terms.definition: A workflow management system

terms.name: Customer Request Type
terms.definition:
A customer request type refers to the categorization of the various types of inquiries, needs, or issues that customers bring to a business or organization. It helps organizations organize and manage customer interactions effectively. Common customer request types include:

  • Product Information Requests: Questions about product features, specifications, or availability
  • Support Requests: Requests for assistance with troubleshooting or resolving an issue.
  • Order Inquiries: Questions about order status, delivery, or returns.
  • Feedback or Complaints: Sharing opinions or reporting dissatisfaction.
  • Service Requests: Asking for specific services or customizations

terms.name: Customer Requirements Team
terms.definition: The CRT is part of Operations and is based in Bengalaru (BLR). This team handles out-bound follow-ups for outstanding requirements on cases.

terms.name: Customer Risk Rating
terms.definition:
Customer Risk Rating is automatically calculated based on predefined parameters and values. There are also additional definitions for Customer Risk Rating:

- Customer's inherited risk rating: This risk rating can be inherited as a result of a relationship with another customer/entity.

- Manual risk rating. This risk rating is manually assigned to override the system and or inherited risk.

- System risk rating. This risk rating is automatically calculated by SAS AML based on predefined parameters and values.

terms.name: Customer Service Manager
terms.definition: A Customer Service Manager plays a key role in overseeing and improving the customer experience for a company. Their responsibilities typically include managing customer service teams, setting performance goals, resolving customer complaints, developing strategies to enhance service quality, and ensuring satisfaction. They may also analyze customer feedback and data to identify trends and make improvements to processes.

terms.name: Customer Service Professional
terms.definition:
A customer service professional is someone who interacts directly with customers to provide support, resolve issues, and ensure a positive experience with a company's products or services. They're often the face of the organization, and their work plays a crucial role in building customer loyalty and satisfaction.

Key traits of successful customer service professionals include:

  • Communication skills: Clear and empathetic communication is essential for understanding customer needs and resolving concerns effectively
  • Problem-solving abilities: The ability to think quickly and offer practical solutions helps to address customer issues efficiently
  • Patience and adaptability: Handling challenging situations or varied customer personalities requires a calm and flexible approach
  • Knowledge of the company: A deep understanding of the company's offerings enables professionals to provide accurate assistance.

In many roles, they might also handle inquiries via phone, email, chat, or social media, working to ensure that customers leave feeling heard and supported

terms.name: Customer Type
terms.definition:
Customer types refer to categories of customers grouped based on shared characteristics, needs, or behavior. These types are often used in marketing and sales strategies to tailor products, services, or communication approaches. Common customer types include:

  • Demographic-based: Age, gender, income, education.
  • Psychographic-based: Lifestyle, interests, values, personality.
  • Behavioral-based: Purchasing habits, loyalty, usage frequency.
  • Geographic-based: Location, climate, or region-specific preferences

terms.name: Daily rate
terms.definition: This is the FTE TFR divided by 260 (the number of work days in the year)  (for a permanent employee) -  for contractors this is a different calculation - as contractors are not paid for any leave taken,  and generally receive a premium for not being covered by terms and conditions of an EA or permanent employment contract

terms.name: Data
terms.definition: Broadly, data is information in the form of facts or statistics about the world, and so is a specific kind of information.  However, for the purposes of the Information Management policy suite, the terms data and Information are used interchangeably.

terms.name: Data Analytics and Insights
terms.definition:
Data analytics involves examining datasets to discover trends, patterns, and relationships, ultimately helping people make informed decisions. Insights derived from analytics can be transformative, guiding businesses in strategy, identifying inefficiencies, or even predicting future outcomes.

There are many types of analytics, including:

  • Descriptive Analytics: Summarizing past data to understand what happened.
  • Predictive Analytics: Using patterns in data to forecast future trends or events.
  • Prescriptive Analytics: Recommending actions based on predictions to achieve specific goals
  • Diagnostic Analytics: Delving into data to figure out why something occurred

terms.name: Data Asset
terms.definition: See Information Asset.

terms.name: Data at Rest
terms.definition: Information stored in an information asset.  Can be contrasted with Data in Transit.

terms.name: Data Centre of Excellence
terms.definition:
The Data Centre of Excellence is a part of the Data Shared Enterprise Service consisting of a number of teams tasked with delivering information governance and management services, several of which are directly concerned with information management and governance: data stewardship, data governance, information and records management, data quality and data modelling standards.

The data governance team provides advisory services to the business, including the Data Governance Forum and Working Group, on questions of data governance principles and policy with a view to minimising data risks and delivering value from information assets.

The data stewardship team provide a centralised enterprise stewardship service to directly manage data in collaboration with domain data stewards, and/or act as domain stewards where none can be provided by the business.

The information and records management team provide information and records management services including, managing our physical records repositories and providers, providing advisory services to the business on how to effectively and securely create records and manage them through their lifecycle, and making sure that the business is classifying records and managing their metadata.

The data quality team provides centralised data quality monitoring and reporting in support of both enterprise data quality requirements, in particular for enterprise critical data elements, and support for data domain data quality needs.

The data modelling standards team provides expert advice on best practice and standards for the information design of information assets, as well as conceptual and logical data models across IFL.

The Data Centre of Excellence provides direct support to the other bodies described below through membership and provision of secretariat services.

terms.name: Data Consumer
terms.definition: Any person or audience that receives and uses information for a purpose. Where data consumers take control or custody of a copy of data or information, they are also Data Custodians.

terms.name: Data Control
terms.definition: An activity or technique to reduce or mitigate a data risk as per the Risk Management Framework (see the Group Control Management Standard for details), specifically intended to manage the impact or likelihood of a data risk being realised (a risk predominantly concerning data or information).

terms.name: Data Custodian
terms.definition: A person responsible for ensuring that information in their control is responsibly cared for in a manner consistent with Insignia’s policies and standards.  A data custodian may have responsibility for information because it is in their possession (say as a file that they own) or because they control and manage the platform on which the information is stored.

terms.name: Data De-Indentification
terms.definition:
Modifications made to data to ensure that no individual person is identifiable.

See also Data Masking.

Note that this does not include Data Encryption. De-identified data must not be able to be re-identified, either through decryption or by deducing identifying information from contextual clues in the rest of the data collection.

terms.name: Data Domain
terms.definition:
An area of data governance responsibility. Data domains are allocated Data Owners (the accountable person for the data governance and management of the domain) and Data Stewards (responsible for many of the day-to-day data management tasks for the domain).

Domains are structured by information topic, generally corresponding to information concepts in the Business Information Model. This means the domain scope extends across organisational structures and technical architecture, providing consistency of governance and management for an information concept wherever it exists across the enterprise.

terms.name: Data Element
terms.definition:
A logical atomic item of data. As a logical concept, a data element represents the common meaning represented by multiple physical instances of data. For example, customer names are used in many different systems, forms and documents, but each of these many instances of the customer name can be represented by a single conceptual data element.


Data elements may be assessed for criticality and managed as Critical Data Elements.

terms.name: Data Encryption
terms.definition:
Encoding data or information in a manner that restricts access to the decoded version of the information only through a controlled mechanism such as a password or key.


Encryption provides security for both Data at Rest and Data in Transit against unapproved access. Note, though, that it doesn’t satisfy the requirements of Data De-Identification or Data Masking as encrypted data can be decrypted.

terms.name: Data Entry
terms.definition: Data entry is the process of inputting information into a computer system or database. This can involve typing, transcribing, or scanning data from various sources, such as paper documents, audio recordings, or digital files. The goal is to organize and manage information efficiently, ensuring accuracy and accessibility for future use.

terms.name: Data Ethics
terms.definition: A framework for thinking about right and wrong in relation to actions regarding data and information. This extends beyond just what we can do, given regulatory and policy obligations, but what we should do. Data ethics doesn’t tell us what the right thing to do is but rather allows those decisions to be made in a standardised, considered way. See the Data Ethics Standard for details.

terms.name: Data Governance
terms.definition: The allocation of decision-making rights and responsibilities regarding information resources to the appropriate roles within the enterprise.  Data governance ensures that appropriate oversight is applied to data management activities, and that information is used safely, appropriately and as a valuable resource.

terms.name: Data Governance Council (Forum)
terms.definition:
This is the peak data governance decision-making body for Domain Data Owners. The Council considers data governance questions that cross domains and/or have an enterprise impact. See the Information Management Framework for more information.


Formerly named the Data Governance Forum.

terms.name: Data Governance Forum
terms.definition:
The Data Governance Forum is the IFL-wide data governance body for decisions that span multiple data domains. Its purpose is to provide a forum for data owners across domains to align on strategies and approaches to the resolution of data quality and other data issues, review and endorse enterprise information management policies and standards and promote a shared culture of sound information practices across IFL.

In addition to domain data owners, the forum also has representation from the Data Centre of Excellence, Data Engineering, Business Intelligence, Privacy, Cybersecurity and Risk. The forum has oversight of the IFL Data Strategy, this Information Management Framework and its application, and provides endorsements for strategic and tactical decisions on major changes to the data environment.

Due to the dependence of artificial intelligence (AI) on data and information assets, the Data Governance Forum will also consider the use and management of AI tools at IFL. Good-quality data is essential to ensure AI tools are reliably trained, minimising harms and biases. AI governance ensures appropriate guardrails are designed and put in place to ensure the safe, ethical and beneficial use of these tools in a way that respects human rights.

terms.name: Data Governance Working Group
terms.definition: This is the primary forum for Data Stewards to discuss cross-domain and enterprise-wide data management questions. Both domain and enterprise data stewards participate in the Working Group. See the Information Management Framework for more information.

terms.name: Data Handling
terms.definition: See Information Handling

terms.name: Data In Transit
terms.definition: Information and data moving from one location to another, across both internal and external networks, via applications and collaboration tools such as Microsoft Teams, as well as movement of data via physical media. Can be contrasted with Data at Rest.

terms.name: Data Issue
terms.definition:
Problems that require a resolution to the following aspects of any information: Data Quality, data design, Metadata, compliance with information management or other data-related policies or regulatory requirements, and Data Use.

Data Issues are distinct from the concept of ‘issues’ as addressed by the Risk Management Framework (see the Group Risk Management Policy and the Data Risk Management Standard for more information). These risk-related issues are generally problems related to the effectiveness or coverage of risk controls and mitigants.

terms.name: Data Issue Owner
terms.definition:
The person responsible for working with the Data Issue Reporter to understand and document a Data Issue. They then oversee the investigation of the data issue, mediate solution design and collaborate with stakeholders and subject-matter experts to track progress on resolving the issue. This is a co-ordinating role, generally relying on other teams and experts to implement a solution or resolution to the issue.

They are usually a Data Steward.

terms.name: Data Issue Reporter
terms.definition: Any person in the business who identifies the existence of a data issue and reports the issue to a member of the data governance or data stewardship team.

terms.name: Data Issue Reviewer
terms.definition: Typically a Data Steward who performs an initial high-level analysis of a reported Data Issue and its scope of impact, assessing its priority, allocating a Data Issue Owner and confirming its status as a data issue requiring resolution.

terms.name: Data Lineage
terms.definition:
A model of the flow of data across the organisation’s technology ecosystem depicting how the value of a data item (a physical instance of a Data Element) is derived.

Data lineage maps are generally one of two types at different levels of abstraction: Business Data Lineage and Technical Data Lineage.

terms.name: Data Management
terms.definition: The people, processes, technologies and organisational structures that directly work with Information Assets and the data that they hold. Data management ensures that information is fit for purpose through addressing: the design of information assets, curation of their content, resolution of issues and data quality deficiencies, and archival and eventual disposal of information.

terms.name: Data Masking
terms.definition:
The use of techniques to modify a collection of data to reduce its Confidentiality classification. This is useful in situations where an audience or team involved in working with sensitive or internal information are not authorised to view that information.

There are several techniques that may be used to mask data, including redaction, obfuscation, replacement with synthesised data or statistical techniques that aggregate and group data.

It is a requirement of data masking that the original sensitive information must not be able to be recreated or deduced. For this reason, Data Encryption is not an acceptable technique.

terms.name: Data Owner
terms.definition:
Domain data owners are the primary delegate for accountability for the management of information within an information domain.  As discussed in the federated approach to IFL’s information governance and management, information domains are high-level information concepts on a given topic, based on the Business Information Model.

Data owners have end-to-end accountability for information across a domain throughout its lifecycle and across multiple systems and departments.  They are the key decision-maker in relation to that information and ensure that policies and standards are applied.  Typically, the data owner will also be the business owner responsible for the operations of the part of the business most closely aligned to the domain.

Data owners endorse or approve the purpose for which information assets are formed and are accountable for ensuring that they are curated and managed in order to be fit for purpose, and any associated data risks are managed appropriately.  They provide leadership and direction for the day-to-day activities of domain data stewards, as well as the strategic plan for information within their domain.

terms.name: Data Pipeline
terms.definition: An established flow of information from one Information Asset to another, including all intermediate steps involved in taking the data from its source, transporting it, transforming, storing and making it available within the destination information asset.

terms.name: Data Profiling
terms.definition: Establishing a statistical profile of data within an Information Asset. This provides an understanding of typical ranges and patterns of values within the asset, and to therefore identify outliers or other deviations from this norm. This may indicate an issue with Data Quality, or provide a means of identifying unusual patterns of behaviour, such as detecting fraud or security breaches.

terms.name: Data Quality
terms.definition: The degree to which an information collection is fit for purpose.  Data quality is a multi-dimensional, multi-perspective concept, with various context- and purpose-dependent measures of data quality possible,

terms.name: Data Quality Assessment
terms.definition: The task of evaluating the fitness for purpose of a collection of information.  This can be both an automated as well as manual process.

terms.name: Data Quality Dimension
terms.definition: One of potentially several aspects of defining fitness for purpose, or assessing data quality, for a given data collection.  The Data Quality Management Standard specifies a set of dimensions to consider, but relevant dimensions of quality depend on the information’s purpose and may extend beyond those included in the Standard.  Typical examples include correctness, completeness, and timeliness.

terms.name: Data Quality Management
terms.definition: People, processes, tools and technologies that ensure Insignia’s information is fit for purpose. This includes monitoring and reporting against Data Quality Rules, remediation of identified data quality issues, and the use of controls that encourage good-quality information at the point of collection or creation by design.

terms.name: Data Quality Rules
terms.definition: The degree to which an information collection is fit for purpose.  Data quality is a multi-dimensional, multi-perspective concept, with various context- and purpose-dependent measures of data quality possible,

terms.name: Data Risk
terms.definition: A risk that is predominantly related to a failure to properly govern, manage, collect or use information.  See the Group Risk Management Policy for the definition of the term ‘risk’.

terms.name: Data Risk Owner
terms.definition: The person accountable for ensuring an identified Data Risk is controlled and mitigated, and otherwise managed according to the Data Risk Management Standard and the Group Risk Management Policy.

terms.name: Data Sharing
terms.definition:
The act of provisioning a collection of information from an Information Asset to a recipient. Data sharing occurs when information from an asset (e.g. data contained in a database, information system, registry or other information collection) is extracted and made available to another group of information users for a secondary purpose and potentially forming part of another information asset. It may be systematic and continuous or ad hoc, as well as internal or external to Insignia.


Central to the concept of data sharing is that there is a recipient of the shared information who takes on Data Custodianship responsibilities for that information which then forms all or part of a new information asset.

terms.name: Data Sharing Agreement
terms.definition: A formally documented agreement between a Domain Data Owner or Data Custodian and a recipient data custodian that specifies the conditions under which Data Sharing will occur. Where a data sharing agreement is with an external third-party, the agreement should form part of an enforceable agreement (i.e. a contract) that ensures the shared data will be managed in a manner that ensures Insignia does not breach its regulatory and policy obligations.

terms.name: Data Steward
terms.definition:
The primary conduit between data consumers in the business and specialist roles such as information management and governance teams, technologists, data engineers and others. They have a close understanding of how information is used by the business and directly work to resolve information issues.

They are responsible for the hands-on implementation and management of information management policies and standards, ensuring information is fit for purpose.

See also Domain Data Steward and Enterprise Data Steward.

terms.name: Data Use
terms.definition: The collection, creation and application of information in a business process. Along with Data Custodianship, data use refers to working with data throughout all phases of the information lifecycle (See the Information Management Framework), from creation or collection through to Archival and Disposal.

terms.name: Date Joined Employer
terms.definition: The "date joined employer" refers to the specific date when an individual officially started their employment with a particular organization. This date is often used for record-keeping, calculating tenure, and determining eligibility for benefits or promotions. It can also be referred to as the "start date" or "date of employment."

terms.name: Date Joined Plan
terms.definition: Date the member/client joined the employer plan.

terms.name: Date Left Employment
terms.definition: The term "data left employment" typically refers to information or records related to an employee who has exited an organization. This data may include the employee's departure date, reason for leaving, final pay details, and any other relevant documentation. It is often used for record-keeping, compliance, and analysis purposes, such as understanding turnover trends or ensuring proper offboarding procedures.

terms.name: Date of Birth
terms.definition: The month, day, year a person is born

terms.name: Date of Death
terms.definition:
Date of death of the Client.
Date of Death is required to be recorded for payment of a Client's benefits to an estate.

terms.name: Date of Disablement
terms.definition: The first day after the expiry of 6 consecutive months immediately after the Event Date. Please refer to the applicable product Additional Information Guide or Product Disclosure Statement.

terms.name: Date of Exit
terms.definition: The date member has ceased with the fund

terms.name: Date of Retirement
terms.definition: This is the date that you formally retire from the workforce and is used to determine whther you meet the Preservation Age to access your superannuation.

terms.name: DB Entitlement
terms.definition: The method used to pay out the benefit type.

terms.name: Dealer Group
terms.definition: The Dealer Group employs financial planners or advisers where they provide advice and support services to clients for Insurance, Investment and Superannuation.

terms.name: Death and Total & Permanent Disablement
terms.definition: Provides a lump sum Benefit if you die or suffer from a Terminal Illness or become Totally and Permanently Disabled by injury or Illness

terms.name: Death Benefit
terms.definition: Benefits paid by a superannuation fund to a dependant or estate, following the death of a member of a superannuation fund.

terms.name: Death Benefit
terms.definition: A death benefit refers to the amount paid by the superannuation fund to the nominated beneficiary or legal personal representative following the member’s death.

terms.name: Death Benefit Pension
terms.definition: Pays a guaranteed income stream for life to members of a defined benefit super fund (commonly public servants and employees at large corporations).

terms.name: Death Benefit Rules
terms.definition: The rules of any extra pension or lump sum payment after death of the pensioner.

terms.name: Death Claim
terms.definition: Provides a lump sum Benefit if you die or suffer from a Terminal Illness.

terms.name: Death Cover
terms.definition: Pays a benefit to your dependants, estate, or legal beneficiaries if you die.

terms.name: Death Ex-Gratia Amount
terms.definition:
The Death Ex-Gratia Amount is the amount of ex-gratia payment added to a maturity by death value of an account to top it up to the guranteed minimum.
Only exists for products offering a maturity by death payments and and with applicable commencement date.

terms.name: Death Only Cover
terms.definition: Provides a lump sum Benefit on your death, inclusive in the death benefit, also includes a terminal illness benefit.

terms.name: Decision Note
terms.definition: A document the Insurer issues which contains specific terms and conditions which apply to your insurance cover.

terms.name: Declaration
terms.definition: The section of a form which is to be signed by the member, acknowledging and agreeing the terms and conditions.

terms.name: Declared Crediting Rate
terms.definition: Will be expressed as a rate for the period and as an annualised rate, determined on a simple basis, using 365 days in a year (or 366 for a financial year that includes 29 February).

terms.name: Declined Rate
terms.definition: Declined claims finalised as percentage of claims finalised.

terms.name: Deductible Amount of a Pension/Annuity
terms.definition:
A tax-free component of the annuity or pension. It is calculated as an annual amount, being the purchase price of the pension/annuity for which no previous tax deduction has been granted (the undeducted purchase price) divided by the remaining expected life of the person from the date the pension begins to be paid (the relevant number).

For pensions commenced from 1 July 2007, the deductible amount is replaced with a tax free component.

terms.name: Deductible Gift Recipients
terms.definition:
