In Australia, Deductible Gift Recipients (DGRs) are organizations that are eligible to receive tax-deductible gifts and donations. When individuals or businesses donate to a DGR, they can claim a tax deduction for their contribution.

To qualify as a DGR, an organization must be registered with the Australian Taxation Office (ATO) and meet specific criteria. Some common types of DGRs include charities, public benevolent institutions, environmental organizations, and educational institutions.

terms.name: Deduction Basis (Employer Paid)
terms.definition: Details how and from where the fee is deducted. i.e. from member account, employer account, reserve, invoiced.

terms.name: Deduction Frequency
terms.definition: Specifies the frequency that premiums are deducted charged.

terms.name: Default Cover
terms.definition: The agreed level of cover members receive upon joining the plan if an alternative nomination is not made.

terms.name: Default Fund
terms.definition: Super fund selected by an employer to receive SG contributions for its employees if they do not choose (or are not eligible to choose) an alternative super fund.

terms.name: Default Insurance
terms.definition: Insurance cover automatically provided to many fund members when they join their super fund. Usually includes death and total and permanent disability (TPD) cover.

terms.name: Default Organization Unit
terms.definition: Information about the Organization Units to which the user belongs. These are Mandatory only when the user is a P2P (eProc and eInvoice user). Default OU details information has to provided with all complete details or exclude completely

terms.name: Defective Nominations
terms.definition:
Member nomination will be defective if:

  • it is unclear to the Trustee (e.g. because it is illegible or because the nominated proportions do not total 100%)
  • the nominated beneficiary(ies) is not a dependent or there is no valid estate or the estate is insolvent
  • the member did not sign or date the form
  • it is not witnessed correctly (if applicable).

A nomination may also be defective if we receive information before paying the death benefit that, when the member made the nomination, they did not understand the consequences of making it. A nomination may become partially or fully defective after you make it if a nominated beneficiary dies or ceases to be a dependent while the member is still living.

terms.name: Deferral
terms.definition: This is a reward of monetary value which has been withheld for a period of time (usually a number of years)  to be paid when a milestone or pre-determined hurdle has been met.  This is often used as a means of retention

terms.name: Deferred Annuity
terms.definition: Annuity purchased to deliver regular income in retirement where the payments commence after a nominated period.

terms.name: Deferred Entry Fee
terms.definition: You can invest without an Entry Fee charged on your investments, however, generally, this option has higher Ongoing Fees than the Entry Fee option for the first four years of each investment and Withdrawal Fees may apply when you withdraw your money.

terms.name: Deferred Tax Asset
terms.definition:
An item on the company's balance sheet that represents a future tax benefit. It arises when a business overpays taxes or incurs tax-deductible expenses that cannot be immediately recognized. Essentially, it allows the company to reduce taxable income in future periods.

Deferred tax assets often result from:

  • Loss carryforwards: If a company experiences a financial loss, it can use that loss to offset taxable income in future years
  • Timing differences: When accounting rules and tax regulations recognize revenue or expenses at different times.
  • Tax credits: Certain tax incentives or deductions that can be applied in later periods.

terms.name: Defined Benefit
terms.definition: A defined benefit is a type of retirement plan where an employer guarantees a specific retirement benefit amount for the employee based on a formula.

terms.name: Defined benefit contribution
terms.definition: In Australian superannuation, a defined benefit contribution refers to the notional or calculated value of contributions made to a defined benefit fund, rather than actual dollar amounts paid into an individual’s account.

terms.name: Defined Benefit Fund
terms.definition: In a defined benefit fund, the retirement benefit is determined by a formula instead of being based on investment return.

terms.name: Defined benefit interests
terms.definition:
Defined benefit interests refer to superannuation benefits where the final payout is determined by a formula, rather than being based on investment returns. These benefits are typically calculated using factors like salary, years of service, and a predetermined accrual rate.

Key aspects of defined benefit interests:

  • Guaranteed payouts: Unlike defined contribution funds, the retirement benefit is fixed and does not fluctuate with market performance.
  • Employer-funded: Contributions are usually made by employers to ensure sufficient funds for future payouts.
  • Valuation methods: The Family Law (Superannuation) Regulations 2025 outline specific actuarial formulas for valuing defined benefit interests.
  • Tax considerations: The Australian Taxation Office (ATO) provides guidelines on how these interests are valued for tax purposes.

terms.name: Defined benefit members' benefits
terms.definition:
Defined benefit members' benefits refer to retirement benefits provided under a defined benefit superannuation fund, where the final payout is determined by a predetermined formula rather than investment returns.

The formula typically considers:

  • Employer contributions and member contributions.
  • Length of employment with the contributing employer.
  • Final Average Salary (FAS) at retirement.

Unlike accumulation funds, where retirement savings depend on investment performance, defined benefit funds guarantee a specific payout based on the fund's rules. These funds are often available to corporate or public sector employees, though many are now closed to new members.

terms.name: Defined benefit pensions
terms.definition:
A defined benefit pension is a retirement plan where an employer guarantees a specific payout to employees upon retirement, based on factors like salary history and years of service. Unlike defined contribution plans, where retirement savings depend on investment performance, defined benefit pensions provide predictable income regardless of market fluctuations.

Employers typically fund these pensions, and the benefits are calculated using a predetermined formula that considers an employee’s tenure and earnings. These plans are more common in government and large corporate sectors, but they have become less prevalent in private companies due to cost and financial risk

Reference link: https://www.legislation.gov.au/F1996B00580/2019-08-10/text

terms.name: Defined Benefit Reserve
terms.definition: This is a specific purpose reserve created for a specific employer plan for the purpose of paying a crediting rate to members in accordance with the requirements of SPS 160 Defined Benefit Matters.

terms.name: Defined Benefits
terms.definition: Defines that a member is in a Defined Benefit scheme and may include the rules associated with the Defined Benefit. With Defined Benefits, what the member receives upon retirement (or leaving their employer) is based on a formula in the rules of the defined benefit super fund.

terms.name: Defined Benefits Formula
terms.definition: The high level benefit design formula that details how the benefit is to be calculated. Also mentions if there is a pension option.

terms.name: Defined Contribution Fund
terms.definition: A defined contribution fund is a type of retirement plan where employees and employers contribute a specific amount to an individual account. The contributions are typically invested in the stock market or other securities, and the final retirement benefits depend on the performance of these investments. Unlike defined benefit plans, which guarantee a fixed payout, defined contribution plans place the responsibility for saving and investment decisions on the employee.

terms.name: Defined contribution interests
terms.definition: Refers to a superannuation interest where the benefits are determined by the contributions made to the fund and the investment earnings on those contributions.

terms.name: Defined contribution members' benefits
terms.definition:
Defined contribution members' benefits refer to the retirement savings accumulated in a defined contribution superannuation fund, where the final benefit depends on contributions made and investment returns over time. Unlike defined benefit funds, which guarantee a specific payout based on salary and years of service, defined contribution funds grow based on market performance.

Key aspects of defined contribution benefits:

  • Employer contributions: Typically 10.5% of salary (Superannuation Guarantee rate).
  • Member contributions: Voluntary contributions can boost retirement savings.
  • Investment earnings: Returns vary based on chosen investment options.
  • Withdrawals: Accessible upon reaching preservation age or meeting early release conditions.

terms.name: Delegated Authority
terms.definition: Permits the transfer of specified powers to another person for a defined period or activity. Delegations of authority are to be provided based on the principles contained in the Delegation of Authority Framework.

terms.name: Delisted
terms.definition: A company's shares no longer trading on the ASX.

terms.name: Delivery Point IDentifier
terms.definition: DPID is a unique barcode assigned to most street addresses in Australia. The barcode allows sorting machines within Australia Post to quickly and accurately read addresses on envelopes.

terms.name: Demerger
terms.definition: One company split into two, or a company 'offload' a smaller division which becomes a separate entity.

terms.name: Demonstrates
terms.definition: Insignia Financial Principles Rating

terms.name: Departing Australia Superannuation Payment
terms.definition: If you have worked and earned super while visiting Australia on a temporary visa, you can apply to have this super paid to you as a departing Australia superannuation payment (DASP) after you leave the country and your visa is no longer applicable.

terms.name: Department Id
terms.definition: Unique Id of the Department which is assigned as default to user. Department  mentioned must be existing and active in the system, and mapped to the corresponding OU level 1 and 2 mentioned. If the setting to link departments to OU levels in master data is OFF, any existing and active department can be assigned. Mandatory when eProc and eInvoice are assigned to the user

terms.name: Department of Human Services (Centrelink)
terms.definition: Department of Human Services (Centrelink) are responsible for delivering a range of welfare, health and child support payments along with other services to eligible Australian citizens and permanent residents.

terms.name: Department of Veterans' Affairs
terms.definition: Department of Veterans Affairs provide support to those who serve or have served in defence of our nation, and their families.

terms.name: Dependant
terms.definition: For the purposes of a superannuation death benefit, this includes your spouse (same-sex spouse or former spouse or de facto), any of your children regardless of age (including an adopted, step or ex-nuptial child), any person with whom you have an interdependent relationship or any person who is financially dependent on you. For tax purposes, a child must be aged under 18, otherwise they must be financially dependant on you.

terms.name: Dependent (Beneficiary)
terms.definition:
To receive benefits from a super account or a pension account upon the death of the member, beneficiaries must be classified as a ‘dependant’ under superannuation law (known as SIS Dependants). The three classifications of ‘SIS:  Dependants are;
- The Spouse/Partner/De-facto of the account owner.
- A child of the account owner.
- A person that holds an ‘interdependency relationship’ with the account holder.

terms.name: Derivatives
terms.definition: Derivatives are contracts that have a value derived from another source such as an asset, market index or interest rate. There are many types of derivatives including swaps, options and futures. They are a common tool used to manage risk or improve returns.

terms.name: Design and Distribution Obligations
terms.definition: The obligations, which came to effect from 5th October 2021, require issuers and distributors to develop and maintain effective product governance processes across the lifecycle of financial products, with the ultimate aim of improving consumer outcomes.

terms.name: Designated Business Group
terms.definition: Designated Business Group (DBG) is a group of two or more entities where each member has elected, in writing, to be a member of the group, which enables the associated business to share the administration of certain obligations under the AML/CTF Act.

terms.name: Designated Service
terms.definition:
A designated service is defined under Section 6 of the Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorism Financing (AML/CTF) Act 2006. It refers to a service that has been identified as posing a risk for money laundering or terrorism financing, and which meets a geographical link to Australia.

Designated services include a wide range of activities across sectors such as:

  • Financial services (e.g., banking, loans, remittance)
  • Bullion trading
  • Gambling services
  • Digital currency exchange services
  • Real estate transactions
  • Legal and accounting services (in certain contexts)

terms.name: Designation Code
terms.definition: Unique Id of the designation of the user. It must be an existing and active designation defined in the system. Mandatory only when the user is a P2P (eProc and eInvoice user)

terms.name: Deskbank
terms.definition: Deskbank is a banking software system provided by Westpac, primarily used for managing financial transactions and exporting bank statements. It allows users to perform tasks like exporting transaction details, managing balances, and processing payments efficiently

terms.name: Destination Balance
terms.definition: Specifies the balance the contribution money is allocated to.

terms.name: Developing
terms.definition: Insignia Financial Principles Rating

terms.name: Direct appointment
terms.definition: When someone gets appointed to a role without a recruitment process due to succession plan.

terms.name: Direct Debit Details
terms.definition: Contains the bank Account details to be charged when an Employer chooses to pay member contributions via Direct Debit

terms.name: Direct Debit Request
terms.definition: A Direct Debit request (DDR) is an authorisation from the customer to collect future payments from their bank account.

terms.name: Direct Investor
terms.definition: Investments in OnePath Wholesale Australian Share trust may be made by Direct Investors who wish to invest directly in the trust.

terms.name: Direct Payout Failed - Distribution
terms.definition:
A direct payout failure can happen for several reasons, including:

  • Insufficient funds in the recipient’s account
  • Bank account issues, such as incorrect details or a closed account.
  • Payment processing errors, including technical glitches or system downtime.
  • Fraud prevention measures, where banks flag transactions as suspicious
  • Exceeded transaction limits, especially for daily or monthly payouts.

If your payout failed, you may need to check your account balance, verify your banking details, or contact your financial institution for further assistance.

terms.name: Direct Payout Failed - Redemption
terms.definition:
A "direct payout failed redemption" could refer to a situation where a direct payout intended for redemption—such as a refund, reward, or financial transfer—was unsuccessful. This failure might occur due to reasons like:

  • Incorrect or invalid account details.
  • Insufficient funds in the source account
  • Technical errors during the transaction process.
  • Issues with the recipient's account, such as being closed or frozen

terms.name: Direct Report/s
terms.definition: Someone or a group of people who report to a particular manager

terms.name: Direct Termination Payment
terms.definition: This is a transitional ETP that customers direct employers to contribute into their complying superannuation account. Tax otherwise payable is not withheld but the fund is liable to 15% tax on the Taxable component.

terms.name: Discretionary Offer
terms.definition: Company want to raise money, offering shareholders new shares to buy. But not all new shares got taken up by its shareholders (there are “leftover new shares”). Company then offer these “leftover new shares” to its eligible shareholders and “qualifying applicants” to buy. Who are the “Qualifying applicants”? They are determined by the company for that corporate actions.

terms.name: Dishonour
terms.definition: An instance where the bank refuses to pay a cheque or direct debit due to insufficient funds or other reasons

terms.name: Disposal
terms.definition: The proper destruction of information or data from systems and records (Information Assets) after they have reached the end of their lifecycle and/or passed their Retention period.

terms.name: Distribution
terms.definition: Distributions are the payment of earnings (i.e. income and capital gains) to investors (unit holders). The distribution represents payments of the taxable income that the fund has earned over that ‘distribution period’, and is a way of making regular payments to the investors from the investment. Managed funds earn income in the form of dividends, interest and realised capital gains, that is, the profit made from selling an investment that has increased in value from the date of purchase.

terms.name: Distributions method
terms.definition: Investors have two options as to what they can do with their distribution income. One option is to "reinvest" their distribution income back into the fund, for which they will receive additional units in the fund. The second option would be to receive cash for their distribution.

terms.name: Diversification
terms.definition: The strategy of spreading your investment risk across different asset classes and potentially also across different investment managers.

terms.name: Dividends
terms.definition: Dividends represent the profits of a company/ managed fund which have been passed onto the investors. Please refer to Unfranked Dividends, Franked Dividends and Franking Credits for further information.

terms.name: Division Automatic  Switch Defaults
terms.definition: Controls whether new switch requests  automatically flow into Straight Through Processing

terms.name: Division Code
terms.definition: State location

terms.name: Division Details
terms.definition:
Each Fund is made up of one or more Divisions, which provide high level defaults for the assigned plans, including -
Allowable Super type (Accumulation/Pension)
Open to New Business/New members
Fee Basis Defaults
etc.

terms.name: Division Fee Base Defaults
terms.definition: See Division Details.

terms.name: Divison Investor Fee Category Details
terms.definition: Fee Categories are estalished at the Fund/Divison level and provide defaults for fee types and rates. They are then inherited by Employer plans assigned to the Fund/Divison combination.

terms.name: Divison Re-Investments
terms.definition: Indicates whether dividends/revenue received from investment are used to buy additional units/shares within the investment funds, or paid out to the client as cash (maybe credited to the client's cash account).

terms.name: Divison Re-weighting (Assets)
terms.definition: Applicable only for when Automatic Switching / Straight Through Processing is enabled for an account. If so, indicates whether an auto-reweight is applied immediately after the switching transactions are applied.

terms.name: Does not Demonstrate
terms.definition: Insignia Financial Principles Rating

terms.name: Dollar Cost Averaging
terms.definition: The Dollar Cost Averaging Plan aims to take the guesswork out of when to invest, by allowing you members to invest a set amount on a regular basis. This may help to manage and spread the risk of investing. Members can invest into any investment fund, except an ANZ Term Deposit option, within OneAnswer Frontier Personal Super or Pension and then nominate an amount to be switched into your target investment funds on a monthly or quarterly basis. These dollar cost averaging switches occur on the 22nd day of each month or the next Sydney business day. To establish the Dollar Cost Averaging Plan, members nominate the total amount to be switched each month or quarter and the amounts to be switched to each target investment fund, using either dollars ($) or percentages (%).

terms.name: Domain Critical Data Element (CDE)
terms.definition: A Critical Data Element that has not yet been assessed as, or does not meet the criteria for, an Enterprise Critical Data Element. Domain CDEs are still important to the business and are owned by a Domain Data Owner, but are not managed centrally with the elevated prioritisation afforded enterprise CDEs. They are largely monitored and managed within their domain.

terms.name: Domain Data Owner (or Data Owner)
terms.definition: The primary delegates for accountability for the management of information within a Data Domain. Data owners have end-to-end accountability for information within a domain throughout its lifecycle and across multiple systems and departments. While domain data owners do not have accountability for the various information systems across IFL, they do have accountability for their domain data residing within those information systems. Consequently, through their Domain Data Stewards, they work closely with platform owners (see Data Custodian) and business units to ensure data governance requirements are met.

terms.name: Domain Data Steward
terms.definition: A Data Steward that performs their duties specifically within a Data Domain, accountable to a Domain Data Owner.

terms.name: Downstream Arrangement
terms.definition: Involves the Insignia Financial DBG engaging a third party to perform an AML or CTF related business process or otherwise form part of the Insignia Financial DBG’s delivery mechanism.

terms.name: Draft Orders or Agreements
terms.definition: Under the Family provisions, the Trustee must be extended Procedural Fairness, This means the Trustee must extend the opportunity to review an Order or Agreement. The Trustee is required under the provisions to provide a written confirmation of its ability to comply or otherwise in a relation to the content of the Order or Agreement. Note the 'Final' Order or Agreement becomes binding on the Trustee (no payment can be made except as directed by the Final Order or Agreement).

terms.name: Due Diligence
terms.definition:
Due diligence refers to the process of thoroughly investigating or assessing a person, company, or situation before making a decision. It's often used in business, finance, and legal contexts to ensure informed decision-making. Key aspects include:

  • Financial Due Diligence: Analyzing financial records and performance
  • Legal Due Diligence: Reviewing legal compliance and risks.
  • Operational Due Diligence: Evaluating processes, infrastructure, and efficiency.
  • Market Due Diligence: Assessing market position and competition.

terms.name: Early Payment Discount
terms.definition: Indicates if Early Payment Discount is applied on this payment

terms.name: Early Release
terms.definition: Members may be able to access some of your superannuation (super) early depending on their circumstances. Members normally can’t access their super until they reach preservation age. Read about preservation age on the Australian Taxation Office (ATO) website.

terms.name: Early Retirement Benefit
terms.definition: The financial support or pension payments that individuals can receive if they choose to retire before reaching the standard retirement age. These benefits are often reduced compared to full retirement benefits, as they are distributed over a longer period. The reduction is typically calculated based on the number of months or years before the full retirement age.

terms.full_name: Earnings Before Interest, Tax, and Amortisation
terms.name: Earnings Before Interest, Tax, and Amortisation
terms.definition: EBITA stands for Earnings Before Interest, Tax, and Amortisation, and in this context, it includes Depreciation and Share-based Payments expenditure.

terms.full_name: Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation, and Amortisation
terms.name: Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation, and Amortisation
terms.definition: EBITDA stands for Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation, and Amortization. It is a financial metric used to evaluate a company's operating performance by stripping out the effects of financing decisions, accounting practices, and tax environments.

terms.name: Earnings on reserve
terms.definition: Refers to the investment earnings generated on amounts held in a reserve account within a superannuation fund.

terms.name: Earnings Tax
terms.definition: Earnings Tax is a tax on investment earnings that is completed at fund level for Super and TTR accounts.

terms.name: Easy Transact
terms.definition: EasyTransac is a payment solution designed to simplify transactions for businesses and individuals. It offers features like mobile payments, payment links, subscription management, and cashless payment options. The platform is user-friendly and provides tools for managing both online and in-store payments, making it adaptable to various business needs.

terms.name: Effective
terms.definition: Something that is in force or officially taking place.

terms.name: Effective Date
terms.definition: Effective date is the inception date of the investment option.

terms.name: Effective Date Guidelines
terms.definition: The effective date guidelines apply to all products offered by the Trustee within the MLC Super Fund. The objective is to ensure that fund members are not materially adversely impacted by a failure by MLC Wealth to meet service standards for administration transactions.

terms.name: Electronic Direct Mailing
terms.definition: Email correspondence sent to our members, clients and advisers.

terms.name: Electronic Funds Transfer
terms.definition: Electronic Funds Transfer (EFT) refers to the digital transfer of money from one bank account to another, either within the same financial institution or across different ones. This method uses electronic systems rather than paper-based methods like checks. Examples of EFT include direct deposits, online bill payments, wire transfers, and card transactions.

terms.name: Electronic Information
terms.definition: Any information stored within a computer system or computer network. See also Physical Information.

terms.name: Electronic Verification
terms.definition: This is a convenient, fast and secure way to verify your identity. By using items most people carry with them, such as a Drivers Licence and Medicare card, we're able to match your details against information held by external data sources to complete the verification process.

terms.name: Eligibility
terms.definition: The criteria that defines whether the member qualifies for the benefit.

terms.name: Eligibility Description
terms.definition: Details the eligibility criterion that applies to the employee type for the related insurance cover.

terms.name: Eligibility Notes
terms.definition: Details any additional information about the eligibility that has not been covered by the other related items.

terms.name: Eligible Person
terms.definition:
An eligible person may make an application to the trustee of an eligible superannuation plan for information about a superannuation interest of a member of the plan. In relation to a superannuation interest of a member of an eligible super plan, means; 
- a member.
- if the member has died, the legal personal representative of the member.
- a spouse of the member. 
- if the spouse of the member has died - the legal personal representative of the spouse.
- a person who intends to enter into a superannuation agreement with the member.

terms.name: Eligible Rollover Fund
terms.definition: Designed as a temporary repository, to accept the benefits of members: with low account balances who are leaving or changing employment who have become uncontactable.

terms.name: Eligible Service Date
terms.definition: The eligible service date is the earliest of the commencement date with the fund or the date provided by a previous fund on rollover. This date is used as the first date of the eligible service period.

terms.name: Eligible Service Period
terms.definition: The eligible service period is the number of complete days of employment or fund membership (or combination of both) that relates to your ETP. It is only applicable where part of the termination payment relates to pre-July 1983.

terms.name: Eligible Termination Payment
terms.definition: A lump sum payment made to an employee upon the termination of their employment. It can include payments for unused sick leave, rostered days off, gratuities, or compensation for loss of employment. ETPs are subject to specific tax treatments, which depend on factors like the timing of the payment and its components.

terms.name: Email
terms.definition: The email address  for when contacting the  Employer

terms.name: Employee Central
terms.definition: Module in Success Factor that holds the person information

terms.name: Employee Central Dev
terms.definition: Dev Environment of Success Factor

terms.name: Employee Central Payroll
terms.definition: Module in Success Factor used to run the payroll

terms.name: Employee Central Test
terms.definition: Test Environment of Success Factor

terms.name: Employee Due Diligence
terms.definition: The processes whereby a business screens its staff members for ML/TF and sanctions risk.

terms.name: Employee Experience
terms.definition:
The experience of an employee throughout their lifecycle with a company.  This includes the interactions an employee has with people, systems, policies, culture and the physical and virtual workspace. 

EX is also part of the function of the Employee Experience and Communications team within P&C.

terms.full_name: Employee Experience and Communications content tracker
terms.name: Employee Experience and Communications content tracker
terms.definition: A calendar/schedule of all past and upcoming content and workflow for the Employee Experience and Communications team. This allows the team  to plan, organise and prioritise enterprise and divisional communications and strategic priorities.

terms.name: Employee ID
terms.definition: The unique number/identifier attached to each team member at Insignia

terms.name: Employee Type
terms.definition:
Specifies the type of employee that the eligibility information relates to.
Can includes: Spouse, Full time, Part time, Executive members, Award members, Paid Monthly, Paid Fortnightly.

terms.name: Employee Value Proposition
terms.definition:
An Employee Value Proposition refers to the perception and reputation of an organisation as an employer

It represents an organisations principles, experience, offer, culture, and overall image in the eyes of current and potential employees.

terms.name: Employer
terms.definition: The definition of an employer is a person or a business that gives a paying job to one or more people.

terms.name: Employer ABN
terms.definition:
The Australian Business Number (ABN) is a unique 11-digit identifier issued by the Australian Business Register (ABR) which is operated by the Australian Taxation Office (ATO). 
The law requires each entity that carries on a business in Australia has an ABN and that the ABN appear on each tax invoice and other tax related documents issued by the entity.

terms.name: Employer Accumulation Basis
terms.definition:
For each Employer plan, the Accumulation Basis defines the types of Super Contributions that are applicable/allowable -
- Member Contributions
- Employer Additional Contributions
- Spouse Contributions
- etc.
and whether retirement proceeds are based on  performance growth (Accumulation) or Defined Benefits

terms.name: Employer Additional
terms.definition: Is a concessional contribution which is part of an arrangement between the member and employer that does not fall under SG or salary Sacrifice.

terms.name: Employer Address Details
terms.definition:
A generic term for all the Employer's  address details -
Street address
Postal address

terms.name: Employer Adviser Details
terms.definition: A generic term to include all details pertaining to an adviser who is managing the Employer's Account - adviser code and name, commission/fee details, etc.

terms.name: Employer Basic Details
terms.definition:
The definition of an Employer is a person or a business that gives a paying job to one or more people (Members). 
For Employer Super Accounts (also referred to as Business Super Accounts) the Employer is our main Client and it's employees have individual Member Accounts.
The basic details include the Employer's name, address, contact detaisl, Account status and other Business details.

terms.name: Employer brand
terms.definition: An Employee Value Proposition refers to the perception and reputation of an organisation as an employer It represents an organisations principles, experience, offer, culture, and overall image in the eyes of current and potential employees.

terms.name: Employer Category
terms.definition:
See Employer Benefit Category.
This is the default Employer Benefit Category automatically assigned to the Plan, based on Fund/Divison.

terms.name: Employer Category Details
terms.definition:
See Employer Benefit Category.
This is the default Employer Benefit Categor automatically assigned to the Plan, based on Fund/Divison.

terms.name: Employer Code
terms.definition:
This is the unique identifier for a given Employer.
Also referred to as Employer Number.

terms.name: Employer Commencement Date
terms.definition: Date that the employer commenced with the associated plan.

terms.name: Employer Contact Details
terms.definition:
A generic term for the communication contact details for when contacting the Employer -
Work Phone
Mobile
FreeCall
Fax
Email (address)

terms.name: Employer Contact Identification  Details
terms.definition: A gerneric term for the Title, Name and Salutation of the person that serves as the primary contact for communicating with the Employer.

terms.name: Employer Contribution Payment Details
terms.definition:
A generic term for the collection of contribution payment information established for an Employer -
Direct Debit bank Account details details
If the Employer requires contribution returns 
Employer Payments Cheque Account Details (when provided)
Super123 details (when provided)

terms.name: Employer Default Plan
terms.definition: Indicates if the employer has elected the plan as the default plan for their employees superannuation. (i.e. members who have not exercised choice of fund).

terms.name: Employer Division
terms.definition:
(Not applicable for Expand)
Each Superannuation Fund has multiple divisions, represented by a Divison Number and name.
Typically, separate Divisons are used for each type of Account - Employer Sponsored, Personal investment and Pension Accounts.

terms.name: Employer Fund
terms.definition: Super fund chosen by an employer to receive compulsory SG contributions if an employee does not nominate an alternative fund, or if they are ineligible to choose one.

terms.full_name: Employer Fund or Employer Sponsored Fund
terms.name: Employer Fund or Employer Sponsored Fund
terms.definition: A default super fund, also known as an employer-nominated fund, is the superannuation fund into which your employer will make your super contributions if you do not nominate a fund of your own choosing.

terms.name: Employer Given Name
terms.definition: The Given Name of the person that serves as the primary contact for communicating with the Employer.

terms.name: Employer Insurance Details
terms.definition: A generic term containing the Insurance that an Employer has on offer to it's employees (Members) - Type of Insurance Cover, Insureable amounts, whether it is paid by the Employer (Employer Paid Insurance), associated fees, etc.

terms.name: Employer Lost Details
terms.definition:
A generic term for for all information pertaining to a Lost Employer.
An Employer is considered Lost after consecutive returned mail and/or
a valid address has not been provided.

terms.name: Employer Mandatory
terms.definition: Contributions required under the SGAA laws or under a certified award or industrial agreement. Super only.

terms.name: Employer Name
terms.definition: The legal name that the employer operates under.

terms.name: Employer Number
terms.definition:
This is the unique identifier for a given Employer.
Also referred to as Employer Code.

terms.name: Employer Paid Fees
terms.definition:
Employers may choose to pay member account fees on their behalf. Payment of fees can be processed in one of two ways

  • As a contribution into the account (which must then be treated as a Contribution); or
  • Charged directly to the employer.

terms.name: Employer Payments Cheque Account Details
terms.definition:
(For Employers who continue to pay Employer contributions by cheque)
This refers to the Employer's default bank and account name that the cheque is drawn from -
- BSB, Bank and Address
- Defayult Drawer Name

terms.name: Employer plan subsidisation
terms.definition: The employer sponsor of an employer plan can elect to make additional contributions to reimburse member accounts for the cost of explicit fees or insurance premiums. If elected, additional fee/premium for all members in the plan information is provided to the employer for them to make the payment.

terms.name: Employer Salutation
terms.definition: The salutation used for the person that serves as the primary contact for communicating with the Employer.

terms.name: Employer Status
terms.definition: A reference to whether an Employer Account is still open to  receive contributions, rollovers or make benefit payments (Active) or has been closed / terminated

terms.name: Employer Surname
terms.definition: The Surname of the person that serves as the primary contact for communicating with the Employer.

terms.name: Employer System Code
terms.definition: The unique identifier used on the system to identify the employer.

terms.name: Employer Title
terms.definition: The Title (Mr., Miss, Mrs. etc.) , of the person that serves as the primary contact for communicating with the Employer.

terms.name: Employer-InsuranceOnly
terms.definition:
Applicable to Employers who provide Employer Paid Insurance Only
The Employer offers insurance to it's employees, even though they may not have a Superannuation Account with the product provider -
- Any employer contribution will ONLY be to cover the subsidized fees/premiums.
- Accounts will typically has zero investment balance
- Fees/premiums incurred in these Accounts are fully funded/paid by their employer

terms.name: Employer-Notes
terms.definition: The Evolve (and other platforms?) can capture general notes about about an employer as free text

terms.name: Employer-sponsor
terms.definition:
An employer-sponsor of a regulated superannuation fund is an employer who:

  • Contributes to the fund, or
  • Would contribute to the fund (apart from a temporary cessation of contributions),

for the benefit of:

  • A member of the fund who is an employee of:
    • The employer, or
    • An associate of the employer, or
  • The dependants of such a member in the event of the member’s death

terms.name: Employer-sponsors on a contribution holiday
terms.definition: Refers to employers who temporarily stop making contributions to a defined benefit superannuation fund, even though they are normally required to contribute.

terms.full_name: Employer-sponsors that did not pay certified minimum contributions at the recommended frequency
terms.name: Employer-sponsors that did not pay certified minimum contributions at the recommended frequency
terms.definition:
Represents employer-sponsors that did not pay at the actuary's recommended frequency of minimum contributions and would not be classified as on a contribution holiday.

Reference: SIS Regulations, r. 9.08(4) and r. 9.10(1)(g).
Reference link: https://www.legislation.gov.au/F1996B00580/2019-08-10/text

terms.full_name: Employer-sponsors that did not pay the recommended contribution rates
terms.name: Employer-sponsors that did not pay the recommended contribution rates
terms.definition: Refers to employer-sponsors that did not pay the recommended contribution rates" refers to employers who failed to make the full contributions suggested by an actuary or required under the fund’s governing rules, particularly in defined benefit superannuation funds.

terms.full_name: Employer-sponsors that have notified intent to cease contributions
terms.name: Employer-sponsors that have notified intent to cease contributions
terms.definition: Represents employer-sponsors that have notified intent to cease contributions

terms.name: EmployerPaidInsurance
terms.definition: If an Employer has chosen to pay for their employee's insurance fees, as opposed to the fees being deducted from each member's Account.

terms.name: Employment Termination Payment
terms.definition: Employment Termination Payment

terms.name: Encumbent
terms.definition:
The name of the company or person to which an account is encumbered (e.g. exists as a security for a loan), in which case different procedures apply to its surrender or cencellation.
Business rules :
1.  If the account is currently encumbered
transactions should not be made against it (warning).
2.  Accounts with super components cannot be encumbered.

terms.name: Encumbent Name
terms.definition:
The name of the company or person to which an account is encumbered (e.g. exists as a security for a loan), in which case different procedures apply to its surrender or cencellation.
Business rules :
1.  If the account is currently encumbered
transactions should not be made against it (warning).
2.  Accounts with super components cannot be encumbered.

terms.name: Encumbered
terms.definition: An account is encumbered if it exists as a security for a loan

terms.name: Encumbered Date Range
terms.definition:
See Encumbered.
If an account is encumbered, then a date range/period may apply to the encumberancy.

terms.name: End of Financial Year
terms.definition: Australian financial reporting year, represented as the year ending 30 June.

terms.name: End of Week Wrap
terms.definition: Weekly email sent to all employees wrapping the key topics of the week, and including important information, signed off by a senior leader or exec leader in the business.

terms.name: Enduring Power of Attorney
terms.definition: An Enduring Power of Attorney authorises the attorney to act for the donor even if the donor subsequently becomes legally incapacitated.

terms.name: Enduring Power of Guardianship
terms.definition:
An Enduring Power of Guardianship (EPG) is a legal document that allows you to appoint someone to make personal, lifestyle, and treatment decisions on your behalf if you become unable to do so. This person, known as an enduring guardian, can decide matters such as where you live, the support services you receive, and medical treatments. However, they cannot make financial or property-related decisions.

To create an EPG, you must be 18 years or older and have full legal capacity. You can also appoint joint enduring guardians, who must act together, or substitute guardians, who step in if the primary guardian is unable to continue

terms.name: Enhanced Client Due Diligence
terms.definition: A series of steps or actions undertaken to obtain further information or clarity about either the identity of the client or the bona fides of the client activity being undertaken. May include source of wealth information and reason for dealing with the Insignia Financial DBG.

terms.name: Enhanced Employee Due Diligence
terms.definition: A series of actions undertaken to obtain further information about an employee identified as a PEP or a close associate or close family member of a PEP, in order to assess the ML/TF, bribery and/or corruption risk.

terms.name: ENQX
terms.definition: General Enquiries - client not identified (Masterkey)

terms.name: Enterprise Agreement
terms.definition: An Enterprise Agreement registered with FWA, an industrial instrument that defines employment terms and conditions for those covered by it

terms.name: Enterprise Critical Data Element (CDE)
terms.definition:
A Critical Data Element that has been prioritised and endorsed by the Data Governance Council (Forum). Generally, enterprise CDEs have been recommended to the Council on the basis of prioritisation scoring as outlined in the Critical Data Element Guidance document due to their enterprise-wide impact and risk profile.

Enterprise CDEs receive elevated oversight with regard to risk and issue management, Data Quality monitoring and Data Lineage mapping. The identification and management of enterprise CDEs helps Insignia address its regulatory obligations to manage critical data.

terms.name: Enterprise Data Steward
terms.definition: A Data Steward belonging to a centralised enterprise pool of stewards. Enterprise stewards help address cross-domain and enterprise-wide data issues, as well as centralised data management functions such as managing the Data Issues registry. In some circumstances, they can provide assistance to Domain Data Owners if there is a need for additional data management resources beyond that which is available with the Domain Data Stewards.

terms.name: Enterprise Leadership Team
terms.definition: The layer of senior leaders who sit below the executive team.

terms.name: Entity
terms.definition: Role Type

terms.name: Entry Fee
terms.definition: Also known as a contribution fee. This fee is can be a fixed amount or a percentage deducted from contributions when they are invested into the account.

terms.name: Equity participation
terms.definition: Having a stake in a company's ownership by owning its stock. In this case, it is advisers who will have a stake in Rhombus Advisory.

terms.name: Estate Planning Accreditation Course
terms.definition: A CPD accredited course for advisers developed by AET.

terms.name: Estimate
terms.definition: Represents an estimate of vested benefits, which may or may not have been prepared by the actuary of the RSE.

terms.name: Ex-dividend Date
terms.definition: This date is dictated by stock exchange rules and is usually set to be one business day before the record date.

terms.name: Exceeds
terms.definition: Insignia Financial Goal Rating is used to evaluate how well Insignia has achieved a specific goal

terms.name: Excess Contributions Tax
terms.definition: If the member goes over their contribution cap for the year. Where the cap is breached, additional tax is imposed on excess concessional contribution made to the member's superannuation fund

terms.name: Excess Transfer Balance
terms.definition:
An excess transfer indicator is typically associated with superannuation and retirement funds in Australia. It refers to a situation where an individual's transfer balance account exceeds their transfer balance cap, triggering an excess transfer balance.

When this happens, the individual may need to commute (reduce) their retirement income stream to eliminate the excess, including any notional earnings. Additionally, an excess transfer balance tax may be imposed on the earnings. The tax rate is:

  • 15% for the first time an individual exceeds their cap
  • 30% for subsequent breaches.

terms.name: Exec Connect Webinar
terms.definition: Weekly all staff zoom webinar hosted by CEO Scott Hartley, to provide business updates and insights. Guest speakers are invited to attend each week to present on a range of topics.

terms.name: Executive
terms.definition: Position Level

terms.name: Executive Equity Plan
terms.definition: Executive Equity Plan that determines the Short Term and Long Term variable reward components for members of the Executive Team prior to FY24

terms.name: Executive Incentive Plan
terms.definition: Executive Incentive Plan that determines the Short Term and Long Term variable reward components for members of the Executive Team

terms.name: Executive Team
terms.definition: Renato and his direct reports

terms.name: Executive General Manager
terms.definition:
An Executive General Manager is a senior leadership role responsible for overseeing the overall operations and performance of an organization or a significant division within it.
Their responsibilities often include:

  • Strategic Planning: Developing and implementing strategies to achieve organizational goals and drive growth.
  • Operational Oversight: Managing day-to-day operations across various departments, ensuring efficiency and alignment with objectives
  • Financial Management: Monitoring budgets, analyzing financial performance, and identifying areas for cost optimization.
  • Team Leadership: Leading and motivating teams, fostering collaboration, and ensuring high performance.
  • Stakeholder Engagement: Building and maintaining relationships with key stakeholders, including clients, partners, and employees.
  • Compliance and Governance: Ensuring adherence to legal, regulatory, and organizational standards.

terms.name: Executor
terms.definition: The Executor administers the deceased person’s Will. The Executor must locate the Will and follow the instructions given in the Will for funeral arrangements and all assets. This includes finalising any outstanding liabilities and distributing the assets in accordance with the deceased person’s instructions.

terms.name: Exit Details
terms.definition: A generic term covering all things when a Client withdrawsl / rollovers all  funds from their Account

terms.name: Exit Fee
terms.definition:
Exit fees / Withdrawal Fee  are charges applied where a member exits a fund. Exit fees can be dollar based or percentage based.
Exit fees are a cost to the member leaving the fund. From 1 July 2019, superannuation funds were banned from charging exit fees as a result of the Budget 2018.

terms.name: Expected Fees to Date
terms.definition:
Expected Fees to Date - The amount of money expected to be received by the reporting effective date. This is calculated by distributing the CSA package value evenly over the 12-month agreement period and summing the expected amounts up to the reporting effective date.

• This method of tracking is needed until we can accurately identify the frequency and method of payments.

terms.name: Expense Recovery Fee
terms.definition: A charge imposed by a business or service provider to recover costs associated with certain expenses. These fees are typically added to customer invoices to offset operational costs such as administration, processing, or regulatory compliance.

terms.name:
Expense recovery fee
(recovery fees)
terms.definition:
If the super fund is required to pay expenses on  behalf of it's  members, the  super Accounts may be charged a fee to help recover the amount.
This often applicable when the super fund incurs unexpected system/admin costs to apply new regulatory changes.

terms.name: Extended Cover
terms.definition: The period of extended cover specified in the policy.

terms.name: External Continuation (Insurance option)
terms.definition: The member will be applying with the external insurer to continue the cover they had in the ANZSC account as a standalone cover outside of Super on de linking from their employer plan or exiting the fund.

terms.name: External Cover Transfer In
terms.definition: Details the rules that apply to the transfer of externally held cover into the Plan.

terms.name: External Identifiers
terms.definition:
Unique ID information is useful for external integration. This could be used for Log in of user over DSSO or for integration purposes. 
E.g. ERP ID of user or the employee ID of the user can be mentioned here

terms.name: External Transfer In
terms.definition: An external transfer refers to the process of electronically moving money between accounts held at different financial institutions. This can be used for transferring funds between your own accounts, sending money to others, or paying bills. External transfers are typically convenient and cost-effective, though they may have limits on transaction amounts or frequency.

terms.name: External Transfer In Quality Check
terms.definition: The process of transferring quality control methods, standards, or procedures from one facility, organization, or laboratory to another. This ensures that the quality checks performed at the receiving location are consistent with those at the originating location. It is often used in industries like manufacturing, pharmaceuticals, and research to maintain uniformity and reliability in quality assessments.

terms.name: External transfer Out
terms.definition: "External transfer out" typically refers to the process of moving funds or assets from one financial institution to another. This could be transferring money from a bank account to another bank, moving investment holdings from one brokerage firm to another, or even shifting retirement funds to a different provider

terms.name: External Transfer Out Quality Check
terms.definition:
"External transfer out quality check" generally refers to the process of verifying the accuracy, completeness, and compliance of assets, funds, or data being transferred from one entity to another. This can apply to financial transactions, supply chain logistics, or even outsourced processes in quality management systems.

For example, in financial institutions, a quality check ensures that external transfers meet regulatory and security standards before funds are moved. In manufacturing or business operations, it might involve verifying that outsourced processes meet predefined quality criteria.

terms.name: Externally Managed Funds
terms.definition: Asset holdings on Insignia management platforms (i.e. TAS) managed by external entities (e.g. Bendigo Wholesale funds).  In effect this is all funds not counted as FUA or FUM.

terms.name: Extra Cover
terms.definition: Clients can also apply for extra Death and TPD cover, subject to maximum benefit limits.

terms.name: Extract, Transform & Load
terms.definition: The process of combining multiple data sets and generating the SEP File

terms.name: Facilities Update
terms.definition: Improvements, upgrades, or modifications made to existing facilities to enhance their functionality, efficiency, or compliance with current standards. This could involve modernizing equipment, renovating spaces, or implementing new technologies.

terms.name: Factor
terms.definition: A factor applied to a defined benefit calculation, usually based on age or membership.

terms.name: Factor Table Name
terms.definition: The name/code used to identify the factor as noted in the definitions tab.

terms.name: Factor Type
terms.definition: The unit used for the factor.

terms.name: Fair Work Commission
terms.definition: the Australian industrial relations tribunal created by the Fair Work Act 2009 as part of the Rudd Government's reforms to industrial relations in Australia.

terms.name: Family Fee Aggregation Groups
terms.definition: Family Fee aggregation allows members to link their Account(s) for the purpose of calculating the Administration Fee payable. This can mean a reduction in the Administration Fee payable by all ‘linked’ members.

terms.name: Family Law
terms.definition:
Under Family Law, when a marriage or de facto relationship breaks down, super can be split through a super agreement between you and your ex-spouse or, if you can’t agree, as the Family Court decides.

Split a super benefit where you or your ex-spouse transfer an amount or percentage of your super to the other person’s fund

Flag a super benefit to protect it until a decision has been made or the decision executed.

terms.name: Family Law Fees
terms.definition: The Family Law Act enables client retirement savings to be divided between parties in the event of a breakdown of a marriage or de facto relationship. The Super Fund may be legally compelled to provide information to other parties in accordance with this legislation and may charge a fee for this service.

terms.name: Fax
terms.definition: The fax number  for when contacting the  Employer

terms.name: Fee Brochure
terms.definition: A guide which contains additional information about the fees and costs of a product.

terms.name: Fee Category
terms.definition:
See Divison Investor Fee Category Details.
This is the default Fee Category automatically assigned to the Employer Plan, based on Fund/Divison.

terms.name: Fee Cost ($)
terms.definition: Details the cost of the fee as  a dollar amount per annum. Regular fees are to be quoted as a per annum rate.

terms.name: Fee Cost (%)
terms.definition: Details the cost of the fee as a percentage amount per annum. Regular fees are to be quoted as a per annum rate. Percentages should state the formula used for calculations.

terms.name: Fee Deduction Frequency
terms.definition: Details the basis for how often the fee is charged.

terms.name: Fee Description
terms.definition: Additional Information supporting Fee Name details.

terms.name: Fee Disclosure Statement
terms.definition: A fee disclosure statement is a written document used to inform a customer or client of all fees and charges associated with a service or product. It is legally required in many parts of the world and must be provided to customers when a contract is signed.

terms.name: Fee Name
terms.definition: Identifies the type of fee based on a predefined list.

terms.full_name: Fee rebate for low account balances
terms.name: Fee rebate for low account balances
terms.definition:
Fee rebate for low account balances

A customer will receive a rebate if either of the following applies:

  • the customer’s account has less than $6,000 (net of accrued liabilities) at the end of the financial year; or
  • the withdrawal benefit payable on closure of the account is less than $6,000.

If an account balance for a product offered by the superannuation entity is less than $6,000 at the end of the entity’s income year, certain fees and costs charged by the superannuation entity in relation to administration and investment are capped at 3% of the account balance. Any amount charged in excess of that cap must be refunded. The Trustee does not charge investment costs to the customer’s account.

terms.full_name: Fee Rebate for Low Account Balances
terms.name: Fee Rebate for Low Account Balances
terms.definition: A rebate applies if the account balance is less than $6,000 at the end of the financial year or upon closure. Administration and investment fees are capped at 3% of the account balance, and any excess is refunded.

terms.name: Fee Tracking Status
terms.definition:
On Track – Indicates that the fees received are in line with the expected fees to date.

Acceptable Threshold – A tolerance range for slight shortfall in fees received that is considered acceptable. This is currently set at 2%, but the threshold can be adjusted by the business.

Early Full Payment – Indicates that the client has made a full payment of the CSA package value before the CSA start date. This status is flagged if payment/s equal to or exceeding the CSA value is received in a single month within the 3 months prior to the CSA start date.
• An additional buffer of 1% is applied to account for minor variances.

Missing Fees – Indicates that the fees received to date are less than the expected fees to date amount, and no early payments have been detected.

terms.name: Fee Type
terms.definition: Indicating the type of fee charged to the client.

terms.name: Fees and Costs
terms.definition: A generic term covering all things fees and costs charged to Employer's / Member's Accounts

terms.name: Fees And Costs Arrangement Identifier
terms.definition:
Means a unique identifier created by the RSE licensee to represent a fees and costs arrangement. The FCAI must satisfy the following conditions;

  • it must comprise no more than 20 alpha-numeric characters (with no special characters);
  • it cannot be the same as the FCAI used for another fees and costs arrangement within the same RSE, DB RSE or ERF;
  • it cannot change so long as the fees and costs arrangement exists; and
  • it must be reported consistently across reporting periods.

terms.full_name: Fees and Costs Deducted from Investment
terms.name: Fees and Costs Deducted from Investment
terms.definition: These fees include performance-based amounts and other costs that reduce investment returns but are not shown as direct transactions.

terms.full_name: Fees Deducted Directly From Member Account (performance component type) / (fees and costs component type)
terms.name: Fees Deducted Directly From Member Account (performance component type) / (fees and costs component type)
terms.definition:
Means the amount that is required to be inserted in a periodic statement under item 301(2)(a), Schedule 10 of the Corporations Regulations 2001.

Fees deducted directly from member account must be reported gross of:

  • (i) income tax and GST, after being reduced by any reduced inputs tax credits; and
  • (ii) stamp duty; and without adjustment in relation to:
    • (i) any tax deduction available to the responsible entity, operator or trustee; or
    • (ii) the extent to which any tax deduction may be passed on to members.

terms.name: Fees Received
terms.definition: The total fees received from the client during the CSA period up to the last payment cycle (reporting effective date). This includes initial and ongoing fees paid by the client towards the CSA package value within the specified agreement period.

terms.name: Fees Remaining
terms.definition: The difference between the CSA package value and the fees received. This represents the outstanding amount that is yet to be paid by the client according to the agreement.

terms.name: Final Orders or Agreements
terms.definition: Under the Family provisions, the Trustee must be extended Procedural Fairness, This means the Trustee must extend the opportunity to review an Order or Agreement. The Trustee is required under the provisions to provide a written confirmation of its ability to comply or otherwise in a relation to the content of the Order or Agreement. Note the 'Final' Order or Agreement becomes binding on the Trustee (no payment can be made except as directed by the Final Order or Agreement).

terms.name: Financial Accountability Regime
terms.definition: The Financial Accountability Regime (FAR) imposes a strengthened responsibility and accountability framework for entities in the banking, insurance and superannuation industries and their directors and senior executives. The FAR is designed to improve the risk and governance cultures of Australia’s financial institutions.

terms.name: Financial Action Task Force
terms.definition: An inter-governmental body whose purpose is the development and promotion of national and international policies to combat money laundering and terrorist financing.

terms.name: Financial Advice Association of Australia
terms.definition:
The Financial Advice Association Australia (FAAA) is the leading professional association for financial advisers in Australia. It was established in April 2023 through the merger of two major industry bodies:

The Financial Planning Association of Australia (FPA)
The Association of Financial Advisers (AFA)


Purpose and Role
The FAAA exists to:

Advocate for the interests of financial advisers and their clients.
Promote high professional standards across the financial advice industry.
Support members with education, professional development, and ethical guidance.
Represent the profession in policy discussions with government and regulators.

It aims to create a positive, professional, and collaborative environment for advisers, helping them build sustainable careers and deliver quality advice to Australians.

terms.name: Financial Adviser
terms.definition: A Financial Adviser is licenced to provide general and personal advice. There is usually a fee for the service.

terms.name: FInancial Adviser
terms.definition:
Financial Adviser

  • Definition: An individual who is authorised to provide personal financial advice to retail clients about relevant financial products (excluding basic banking, general insurance, or consumer credit insurance).
  • Registration: Must be listed on the Financial Adviser Register (FAR) maintained by ASIC.
  • Scope: Can provide tailored advice based on a client’s personal circumstances.
  • Best Interests Duty: Financial advisers are subject to the best interests obligations under the Corporations Act, meaning they must act in the best interest of their clients when providing advice.
  • Employment: May be employed directly by an Australian Financial Services (AFS) licensee or operate under another structure.

terms.name: Financial goals
terms.definition: Category of performance goal

terms.name: Financial Hardship (AKA Severe Financial Hardship)
terms.definition: It’s an early condition of release of Superannuation funds that has specific requirements to be eligible.

terms.name: Financial Institution Remuneration Group
terms.definition: This a collection of Financial institutions who come together to provide data to determine comparative market data for roles within the finance industry in Australia

terms.name: Financial Ombudsman Service
terms.definition: An independent body that resolves disputes between consumers, some small businesses and member financial services providers.

terms.name: Financial Planner Association
terms.definition: Providing advisers with lobbying assistance, professional standards compliance and professional development.

terms.name: Financial Planning or Financial Planner
terms.definition: A financial planner will assist their clients with planning their finances, not limited to investments.

terms.name: Financial Services Council (formerly IFSA)
terms.definition: The Financial Services Council (FSC) is a peak body representing Australia's financial services industry. It sets mandatory standards and develops policies for its members, which include retail and wholesale funds management businesses, superannuation funds, investment platforms, and financial advice licensees. The industry manages over $3 trillion on behalf of more than 15.6 million Australians

terms.name: Financial Services Guide
terms.definition: A Financial Services Guide (FSG) is an important document that outlines the types of products and services that each of the licensed entities are authorised to provide under each of their Australian Financial Services (AFS) licences. The FSG is designed to assist you in deciding whether to use the services offered by the licensed entities. This is issued to a customer by an adviser when advice is being provided.

terms.name: First Home Super Savings Scheme
terms.definition: The FHSS scheme was introduced by the Australian Government in the Federal Budget 2017–18 to reduce pressure on housing affordability. It allows you to save money for your first home inside your super fund. This will help first home buyers save faster with the concessional tax treatment of superannuation.

terms.name: Fiscal Year-to-date
terms.definition: FYTD (Fiscal Year to Date) refers to the period from the beginning of a company's fiscal year up to the current date. It is used to measure financial or operational performance over the ongoing fiscal year.

terms.name: Fitness for Purpose
terms.definition: The suitability of information to facilitate its intended use within the business. The purpose for information may be to facilitate a business process or support decision-making, and the same information may potentially have multiple purposes. Ensuring that information is fit for purpose is the primary concern of Data Quality Management.

terms.name: Fixed Fee
terms.definition: This fee applies regardless of the client's account balance and is charged monthly.

terms.name: Fixed Interest
terms.definition: These are typically longer-term loans (for example bonds) to government and semi-government bodies in Australia and overseas.

terms.name: Fixed Term Agreement (for ASF)
terms.definition:
A contract between ASF and an employee where the employment is set for a specific duration or tied to the completion of a particular task. The agreement clearly outlines the start and end dates or the conditions under which the contract will terminate. These contracts are commonly used for temporary roles, project-based work, seasonal jobs, or covering for an employee on leave.

Fixed-term agreements include the same protections as other employment contracts, such as remuneration, leave entitlements, and workplace rights. However, once the term ends, the employment relationship typically concludes unless renewed or extended.

terms.name: Fixed Term Service Agreement
terms.definition:
FTSA stands for Fixed Term Service Agreement.
It’s also referred to as FTCSA or CSA (Client Service Agreement).
In the context of advice reporting, it’s a formal agreement that a client must sign every 12 months.
This agreement is required for advisers to legally charge ongoing fees.

terms.name: Flagging Order
terms.definition: A Court direction preventing the Trustee from making any payment from a superannuation benefit (usually whilst the parties are negotiating the splitting of the family assets).

terms.name: Flat Dollar Amount
terms.definition: Means the expense or benefit attributable to a member expressed as a flat dollar amount.

terms.name: Flows
terms.definition: Refers to the movement of resources, money, or goods within markets or systems (e.g., cash flow, trade flow)

terms.full_name: For P&I, these transaction types form part of Other flows
terms.name: For P&I, these transaction types form part of Other flows
terms.definition: An umbrella term for other transactions for P&I.

terms.name: Foreign Account tax Compliance Act
terms.definition: FATCA is a regulator that imposes certain diligence and reporting obligations on Australian financial institutions.

terms.name: Foreign PEP
terms.definition: Is someone who holds a prominent public position or role with a government body in a country other than Australia.

terms.name: Form 6
terms.definition: The legislative format under which a valuation is to be provided.

terms.name: Fraction Worked
terms.definition: Minimum percentage of full time hours expressed as a decimal to be eligible for cover.

terms.name: Frank's daily email
terms.definition: Daily email sent by Frank Lombardo to all of Client and Technology. This includes important topics like news updates, wins and employee recognition.

terms.name: Frank's Note
terms.definition: Daily email sent by Frank Lombardo to all of Client and Technology. This includes important topics like news updates, wins and employee recognition.

terms.name: Franked Credits
terms.definition: The credit for the tax that an Australian company has already paid on its earnings, before these earnings are paid to you.   Australian residents may use these franking credits to offset tax payable on their taxable income and may also claim them back from the ATO if they have no tax payable.

terms.name: Franked Dividends
terms.definition: Dividends paid by an Australian company on which tax has been paid. The dividend comes with a franking credit.

terms.name: Free Look
terms.definition:
Free Look is a feature  life insurance products in which a new life insurance policy owner can terminate the policy without any penalties, such as surrender charges.  It is associated with a Free Look period .
During the Free Look period, the contract holder can decide whether or not to keep the insurance policy. If they are not satisfied and wish to cancel, they can receive a full refund of their premium(s).

terms.name: Free Look Period
terms.definition:
See Free Look.
During the Free Look period, the contract holder can decide whether or not to keep the insurance policy. If they are not satisfied and wish to cancel, they can receive a full refund of their premium(s).

terms.name: FreeCall
terms.definition: The FreeCall mobile number  for when contacting the  Employer

terms.name: Freelook
terms.definition:
**This feature is now obsolete **

Free Look is a feature life insurance products in which a new life insurance policy owner can terminate the policy without any penalties, such as surrender charges. It is associated with a Free Look period . During the Free Look period, the contract holder can decide whether or not to keep the insurance policy. If they are not satisfied and wish to cancel, they can receive a full refund of their premium(s).

terms.name: Freelook Waiver
terms.definition:
For investments in products which offer freelook, whether or not the account has waived the freelook (free cancellation) period in lieu of obtaining a loanback secured against one or more of the account options.
Only exists if the account's product indicates that freelook cancellations and loanbacks for the option are allowed.

terms.name: Frequent Switching
terms.definition: Is usually members who wish to switch their investments frequently in the pursuit of short-term gains. Super funds monitor all investment options for abnormal transaction activity because this sort of activity can have adverse impacts for other members.

terms.name: Frequently Asked Questions
terms.definition: A list of frequently asked questions and their answers about a given subject.

terms.name: Fringe Benefit Tax
terms.definition: Fringe Benefit Tax

terms.name: From Currency Exchange Rate
terms.definition: Currency exchange rate from source.

terms.name: Frontier Personal Super
terms.definition: OneAnswer Frontier Personal Super is a superannuation product designed for people looking to invest tax effectively.

terms.name: Frozen Funds (AKA Suspended Funds)
terms.definition: A Frozen Fund / Suspended Fund is a registered managed investment scheme where the responsible entity (also referred to as the fund manager) has suspended members' (investors') rights to redeem or withdraw their investments. Freezing a scheme is often a prudent measure to protect the interests of all members

terms.name: Full Form Personal Health Statement
terms.definition: A form or declaration where an individual provides details about their medical history, current health condition, and any pre-existing conditions. This information is often required by insurance providers to assess risk and determine coverage or premiums for policies.

terms.name: Full Time
terms.definition: Any job or persons hour that require a work of 37.5/38/40 hours per week.

terms.name: Full Time Equivalent
terms.definition: The calculation of full-time equivalent (FTE) is an employee's scheduled hours divided by the business hours for a full-time work week. This cannot be more than 1.

terms.full_name: Full time equivalent Total Fixed Remuneration
terms.name: Full time equivalent Total Fixed Remuneration
terms.definition: The base pay plus company contribution to superannuation which is the calculated amount for a full time employee

terms.full_name: Full Time Equivalent Total Fixed Remuneration with Higher Duties
terms.name: Full Time Equivalent Total Fixed Remuneration with Higher Duties
terms.definition: FTE TFR + Higher duties allowance  + super on HD

terms.name: Full-Time Hours
terms.definition: The number of hours per period considered as fulltime employment.

terms.name: FUM Performance
terms.definition: The total value of assets that a financial institution, such as an investment fund or management company, oversees on behalf of its clients. It's a key metric for assessing the size, scale, and performance of financial institutions

terms.name: Fund Assets
terms.definition: Fund assets signify the entire assets of an investment fund. They are calculated daily and comprise various assets. Fund assets are reduced by costs and fees incurred in the fund, e.g. management fees, custodian fees and transaction costs.

terms.name: Fund Code
terms.definition:
The Fund Code as it appears in the IFL's investment administration systems. This code identifies the system where the plan’s main benefit arrangement (e.g., accumulation or hybrid) is stored.

While the disclosed Fund Code typically represents the primary benefit arrangement for members, plans with dedicated pension sections may have those arrangements stored under separate fund codes such as PSFAP, PSFDEF, or PSFLP.

Certain plans have unique lifetime pension sections stored under specific fund codes, for example:

  • NABGSF plan → lifetime pension section in NABPEN
  • BHP Superannuation Fund → lifetime pension section in BHPB1PEN

Common Fund Codes include:

  • IDP – Investor Directed Portfolio Service
  • TSP – The Spectrum Platform
  • BSS – Bendigo Smart Start

Other fund codes include:

  • CPHYLD – Core Portfolio
  • SMFTA – SMF Trust Account
  • ASPX – AustChoice Super
  • AES – Australian Ethical Superannuation Fund
  • GLOBAL – SAMS Global
  • TPSGLP – TPS Group Life Pool
  • UPSTIREG – United PST Investment Register
  • GLP – SMF Group Life Pool
  • UPSTUREG – United Pooled Superannuation Trust
  • AIS – AustChoice Investment Service
  • MPSTUREG – SMF Master Pooled Superannuation Trust – Unit Register
  • MPSTIREG – SMF Master PST – Investment Register (Old)
  • MPSTIR – SMF Master PST – Investment Register
  • ERF – SMF Eligible Rollover Fund
  • ASP – AustChoice Super
  • BBS – Bendigo and Adelaide Bank Staff Superannuation Plan
  • MAP – Medical & Associated Professions Super Fund
  • JRB – Pitcher Partners Superannuation Fund
  • BIP – Bendigo SmartOptions

terms.name: Fund Division
terms.definition: The section of the fund e.g. MLC Super Fund to which the Plan belongs.

terms.name: Fund Name
terms.definition: Each registered Superannuation Fund has unique  name (and Superannuation Code).

terms.name: Fund Status
terms.definition:
Values are typically "Open" or "Closed"

A Superannuation fund that is Open is still able to accept New Business and is actively holding and managing member's monies.

A Superannuation Fund that is Closed is not open to New Business. The Fund may still be holding some monies, but these may be in the process of being withdrawn and/transferred out of this Fund to other Open Funds (called a Super Funds Transfer - SFT).

terms.name: Funding and solvency certificate
terms.definition: A formal document required under Australian superannuation law, specifically for defined benefit superannuation funds.

terms.name: Funds Under Administration
terms.definition: The total member balance snapshot at a particular point in time for all Insignia in-scope registries and platforms regardless of administrator. Usually calculated by total Units x Nav Price.

terms.name: Funds Under Advice
terms.definition: The total value of financial assets for which an advisor, financial planner, or consultancy provides advisory services.
