Unlike Funds Under Management (FUM), where the institution actively manages the assets, FUA indicates that the advisor offers guidance or recommendations but does not directly manage the assets. The asset owner retains decision-making authority and control over these funds.

terms.name: Funds Under Management
terms.definition: FINANCE - Represents all FUM that is internally managed, or in simple terms any money that our CIO office has influence in decision making on behalf of the investor.

terms.name: Funds Under Management & Administration
terms.definition: The sum of both Funds Under Management and Funds Under Administration. Note, the FUM can be double counted within FUA.

terms.name: Funds Under Management Advice & Supervision
terms.definition:
Funds under management (FUM) refers to the total value of assets that a fund manager oversees on behalf of clients. This can include investments in mutual funds, hedge funds, and other financial products. Here are some tips for managing funds effectively:

  1. Diversification: Spread investments across different asset classes to reduce risk
  2. Regular Monitoring: Keep track of market trends and adjust portfolios accordingly.
  3. Professional Advice: Consult financial advisors for tailored strategies
  4. Cost Management: Be mindful of fees and expenses associated with fund management.

terms.name: Future Membership/Service
terms.definition: Defines the period of membership from the Benefit Calculation Date to ceasing age as defined in the policy. E.g. Normal Retirement Date, Age 70.

terms.name: Future of Financial Advice
terms.definition: A set a regulatory reforms for implementation in 2012 and 2013.

terms.name: Gainful Employment Test - Work Test
terms.definition: The gainful employment test is a measure by which we can determine if a member is 'gainfully employed' (i.e meeting, exceeding or falling under a threshold of certain hours at work).

terms.name: Gatekeeper
terms.definition: A third party whose consent, approval or rating is necessary to effect a transaction. In organizations, gatekeepers manage permissions, passwords, or access rights to systems and data.

terms.name: Gatekeeper Direct Credit
terms.definition: A "direct credit" refers to the electronic transfer of funds directly into a gatekeepers' bank account, commonly used for payments like salaries, government benefits, or dividends, offering a secure and efficient method of payment.

terms.name: Gateway
terms.definition: Individual mandatory employee requirements that determine the eligibility to receive an incentive.

terms.name: Gateway Rejected RAS Out (Release Authority)
terms.definition:
The term "gateway rejected" typically refers to a transaction that was declined or blocked by a payment gateway. Payment gateways are services that process online payments, and a rejection might happen due to reasons such as:

  • Invalid payment details (e.g., incorrect card number or expiration date).
  • Insufficient funds in the account
  • Fraud detection systems flagging suspicious activity
  • Blocked by the issuing bank, often due to security concerns.
  • Technical issues with the gateway itself

terms.name: Gender
terms.definition: Defines which gender the rate relates to when the rates are separated by gender.

terms.name: Gender Pay Gap
terms.definition: The GPG measures the difference in average pay for one gender compared to the average pay of another gender,  this can be an overall population or broken down by level of work, layer of org, division or similar roles

terms.name: General Interest Charge
terms.definition: Interest applied to unpaid tax liabilities or overdue payments, particularly by revenue authorities like the Australian Taxation Office (ATO). The GIC is calculated on a daily compounding basis and aims to encourage timely payments while compensating for the delay.

terms.name: General Manager
terms.definition: Position Level

terms.name: General Power of Attorney
terms.definition: A General Power of Attorney authorises the attorney to do anything the principal may lawfully do by an attorney.

terms.name: Global Industry Classification Standard (GICS)
terms.definition:
The Global Industry Classification Standard (GICS) is an industry taxonomy developed in 1999 by MSCI and Standard & Poor's (S&P) for use by the global financial community. The GICS structure consists of 11 sectors, 25 industry groups, 74 industries and 163 sub-industries[1] into which S&P has categorized all major public companies. The system is similar to ICB (Industry Classification Benchmark), a classification structure maintained by FTSE Group.

GICS is used as a basis for S&P and MSCI indexes used in the financial field which each company is assigned to a sub-industry, and to an industry, industry group, and sector, by its principal business activity. "GICS" is a registered trademark of McGraw Hill Financial and MSCI Inc.

terms.name: Goal weighting
terms.definition: The distribution percentage for performance goals.

terms.name: Goods and Services Tax
terms.definition:
The Goods and Services Tax (GST) is a value-added tax levied on most goods and services sold for domestic consumption. It is paid by consumers but collected and remitted to the government by businesses. The GST system is designed to streamline taxation by replacing multiple indirect taxes with a single tax.

In Australia, the GST rate is generally 10%, and businesses that meet the turnover threshold must register for GST and include it in their pricing

terms.name: Government Paid Parental Leave
terms.definition: Government Paid Parental Leave

terms.name: Greentree
terms.definition: The Greentree accounting system

terms.name: Gross in-year benefits
terms.definition: Total benefits gained within our current financial year, before any deductions or adjustments are made.

terms.name: Group Insurance
terms.definition: Group Insurance refers to insurance coverage offered by a super fund to a large group of people or Employer under one policy.

terms.name: Group Insurance
terms.definition: Group insurance refers to insurance cover available to members under the fund’s group policy. Terms and conditions are outlined in the insurance guide.

terms.name: Group Life Insurance
terms.definition: Group life insurance is when a single insurance contract covers a group of people. In Australia, these are typically employees of a large-scale business or organisation, or members of a super fund. Unlike other life insurance cover, they typically don't require much personal information on each person's health status.

terms.name: Group Salary Continuance
terms.definition: Group Salary Continuance (GSC) is a type of insurance policy typically offered as part of an employee benefits package. It provides a regular income stream to employees who are unable to work due to illness or injury. This coverage is designed to offer financial support during challenging times, ensuring employees can maintain some level of income while they recover.

terms.name: Group Variable Reward Plan
terms.definition: NAB short term incentive inherited for MLC people -  this plan is now closed

terms.name: Growth Phase
terms.definition: A condition of release has been met, and the Trustee has no received a written instruction from the member as to how the benefit is to be paid (i.e. Lump sum, pension).

terms.name: Head Of
terms.definition: Position Level

terms.name: High Risk
terms.definition: High-risk customers are individuals or entities that, due to specific characteristics or circumstances, pose an elevated level of risk for businesses or financial institutions. These customers may be more likely to engage in activities associated with money laundering, financial crimes, or other illicit behavior. Identifying and managing high-risk customers is a crucial aspect of compliance with anti-money laundering (AML) and know-your-customer (KYC) regulations.

terms.name: High Value Opportunity
terms.definition:
A high-value opportunity refers to a business or investment prospect that has the potential to generate significant returns or strategic advantages. These opportunities are often characterized by:

  • Strong revenue potential: They can lead to substantial financial gains
  • Competitive advantage: They provide a unique edge over competitors.
  • Scalability: They have the ability to grow and expand over time.
  • Market demand: They align with current or emerging trends that drive consumer interest.

In marketing and sales, high-value opportunities often involve premium offerings or targeted strategies that maximize engagement and conversion rates. Businesses use high-ticket sales to focus on selling fewer but higher-priced products or services, ensuring profitability.

terms.name: Higher duties
terms.definition: Higher duties is an allowance (usually defined in an enterprise agreement) which is paid to an employee if they are requested to perform in a role at a higher level of

terms.name: Higher Duty Allowance
terms.definition: Higher duties is an allowance (usually defined in an enterprise agreement) which is paid to an employee if they are requested to perform in a role at a higher level of

terms.name: Hiring Manager
terms.definition: Individual responsible for the hiring of the role / who the role reports to

terms.name: Hiring Team
terms.definition: Individuals involve in the recruitment / hiring process

terms.name: Hold Financial Report
terms.definition:
This indicates whether the member wishes to receive financial reports or not. Valid values are limited to -

  • Member wishes to receive financial reports.
  • Member does not wish to receive financial reports.

terms.name: Hold statement
terms.definition:
Indicates whether the statement/s for this account are currently on hold so that they can be checked and ammended if necessary.
May be set by the operator through reversals or adjustments
maintenance.

terms.name: Home role
terms.definition: Sometimes called substantive role -  this is the position the person has permanent holding rights over -  it allows employees to be moved into secondments (in another role),  but know where they will be moved back to at the end of the secondment

terms.name: Homepage
terms.definition: Enterprise wide SharePoint homepage where the enterprise can keep connected and up to date with what is happening in the business. This site links out to important information and policies.

terms.name: Hourly rate
terms.definition: Is the Daily rate divided by the standard number of hours in a work day for a full time employee  (this is currently different depending on the EA you are covered by -  if a new EA is negotiated it is expected that this number will be standardised

terms.name: HQ
terms.definition: Enterprise wide SharePoint homepage where the enterprise can keep connected and up to date with what is happening in the business. This site links out to important information and policies.

terms.name: HR Ops
terms.definition: The HR Shared Services Team, contactable via the HR Enquiries inbox.

terms.name: Human Resources Business Partner
terms.definition: Human Resources Partner who is aligned to a specific division or client group to support HR activities & needs

terms.name: Human Resources Shared Services
terms.definition: The HR Shared Services Team, contactable via the HR Enquiries inbox.

terms.name: Hurdles
terms.definition: A Hurdle is a pre-determined set of criteria that an employee or company must meet in order for an incentive to be paid;  or an entitlement to be honoured (such as performance rights)

terms.name: Hybrid dash
terms.definition: A dashboard for leaders and employees to see how they're tracking against the hybrid expectation of 40% of time spent in the office. This pulls data from swipe cards, office maps and Wi-Fi data.

terms.name: Hybrid dashboard
terms.definition: A dashboard for leaders and employees to see how they're tracking against the hybrid expectation of 40% of time spent in the office. This pulls data from swipe cards, office maps and Wi-Fi data.

terms.name: IdentityType
terms.definition: Type of the unique ID for the user, which could be an employee ID, unique ID

terms.name: IFS
terms.definition: Database for TERP clients.

terms.name: In Confidential
terms.definition: Indicates whether the account details are confidential. i.e. viewing or raising transactions against such accounts,  is restricted to a supervisor only.

terms.name: In Dispute
terms.definition:
The status of the account in regard to disputes. e.g. over the ownership or value of the account.
Values include, but are not limited to:
Y - In dispute
N - Not in dispute
R - Dispute resolved.

terms.name: In Restriction
terms.definition: Indicates whether or not a restriction exists for this account.  A  restriction text will  be displayed for any transactions against the account.

terms.name: In specie
terms.definition: Transfer in its actual form, for example an MKIS account moving from one owner to another as units.

terms.name: Inactive Account
terms.definition: Inactive account is one that has; joined the fund more than two years ago, joined as an employer-sponsored member or the fund has not received a rollover or contribution in the last five years.

terms.name: Inactive Low Balance Account
terms.definition: The Treasury Laws Amendment (Protecting Your Superannuation Package) Act 2019 introduced a new category of accounts that need to be reported and paid to the ATO:  Inactive Low-balance Accounts.

terms.name: Inactive Member
terms.definition: Inactive member is defined as “Lost” due to inactivity, has not been reported to the ATO as 'Lost' in a previous reporting period or does not meet the definition of a small or insoluble account.

terms.name: Inactive Member Account
terms.definition: Super account that has not received any contributions, rollovers or transfers, or made any benefit payments within the past two years, but which has not been closed as the member is contactable.

terms.name: Inactive member account
terms.definition: Represents a member account that has not received any contributions, rollovers or transfers, or made any benefit payments within the last two years but which has not been closed as the member is contactable. Includes: members that joined an RSE, as a standard employer-sponsored member, more than two years ago and there have been no contributions or rollover amounts in respect of that member within the past five years. Excludes: lost member accounts and active member accounts.

terms.name: Incentive salary
terms.definition: Actual amount paid to a team member during the FY. Includes allowances, super etc

terms.name: Inception Date
terms.definition: Inception date is the date on which the fund, investment option or policy is launched.

terms.name: Incident Accident Insurance
terms.definition: If you have an accident while the Insurer assesses your application, you or your beneficiaries may be paid a lump sum of the amount you’ve applied for up to the maximum levels.

terms.name: Inclusive of Stamp Duty
terms.definition: Designates whether or not the insurance premium is inclusive of stamp duty.

terms.name: Income Protection
terms.definition: Income Protection (IP) Cover provides replacement income to help cover your expenses if you are unable to work due to an injury or illness.

terms.name: Income protection insurance
terms.definition:
Represents the temporary incapacity cover provided to members, where temporary incapacity insurance cover has the meaning given in the SIS Regulations, r. 6.01.
Reference link: https://www.legislation.gov.au/F1996B00580/2019-08-10/text

terms.name: Income Replacement Benefit
terms.definition: This is also known as Income Protection

terms.name: Income Tax
terms.definition:
Income tax amount represents the tax calculated on a member’s superannuation account based on assessable contributions and investment earnings (including capital gains), adjusted for applicable deductions, offsets and credits, and reconciled upon completion of the Fund’s annual income tax return.

Calculation basis

  • Assessable amounts:
    • Assessable contributions.
    • Investment earnings, including capital gains.
  • Deductions:
    • Administration fees and insurance premiums.
  • Special case – pension commencement:
    • Tax deducted on untaxed rollovers used to commence a pension.

Timing of deductions

  • The Fund generally deducts tax on contributions and investment income from the member’s account at the time payment to the Australian Taxation Office (ATO) is required.
  • This approach allows the account to accrue earnings on the full invested balance until the tax payment is made.

Offsets and credits

  • Where tax offsets are known during the financial year, the income tax amount is adjusted throughout the year.
  • Annual true‑up: Upon completion of the Fund’s income tax return, the Fund applies remaining offsets and credits, including:
    • Refunds of excess imputation (franking) credits; and
    • Credits for the utilisation of capital losses.

terms.name: Income Tax Assessment Act
terms.definition: The Income Tax Assessment Act is a key piece of legislation in Australia that governs the rules and regulations for income tax. It provides detailed guidelines on how taxable income is calculated, deductions, exemptions, and the obligations of taxpayers. The Act is divided into two main versions: the Income Tax Assessment Act 1936 and the Income Tax Assessment Act 1997, with the latter being the more modern and streamlined version.

terms.name: Incorporation By Reference
terms.definition: The act of including a second document within another document by only mentioning the second document.

terms.name: Incumbent
terms.definition: Incumbent is the permanent position holder of the position

terms.name: Independent Financial Adviser
terms.definition: AKA the name of an industry magazine

terms.name: Independent Financial Advisers
terms.definition: Independent financial advisers are professionals who offer independent advice on financial matters to their clients and recommend suitable financial products from the whole of the market.

terms.name: Independent Order of Odd Fellows
terms.definition: IOOF started in 1846 under the name of Independent Order of Odd Fellows (IOOF).

terms.name: Indexation
terms.definition: A member can choose to have their pension payments indexed to rise automatically by a fixed percentage each year, provided the payments are above the minimum pension level and subject to a maximum annual pension payment (10% of account balance if a TTR pension is used)

terms.name: Indexation Arrangement
terms.definition: The rules around any indexation.

terms.name: Indexation Date
terms.definition: The date at which the indexation occurs.

terms.name: Indirect Cost Ratio
terms.definition: This includes any costs that are incurred in managing the underlying assets of each investment option and are an additional cost to you. Indirect costs may vary from time to time are subject to change.

terms.full_name: Indirect Cost Ratio (ICR) (performance component type) / (fees and costs component type)
terms.name: Indirect Cost Ratio (ICR) (performance component type) / (fees and costs component type)
terms.definition: Means the ratio of the total indirect costs for an investment option, to the average net assets of the investment option over the reporting period. A fee deducted directly from a member’s account is not included in the indirect cost ratio.

terms.name: Indirect Investor
terms.definition: Investments in OnePath Wholesale Australian Share trust who may wish to invest in, or via an Investor Directed Portfolio Service (IDPS), IDPS-like scheme, nominee or custody service (together referred to as a master trust or wrap service)

terms.name: Individual contributor
terms.definition: Position Level

terms.name: Industry Super Network
terms.definition: The Industry Super Network, also known as Industry Super Australia (ISA), represents a group of not-for-profit superannuation funds in Australia. These funds are run solely to benefit their members, rather than shareholders, and are known for their low fees and strong long-term investment performance. ISA manages collective programs for participating funds, including marketing campaigns and advocacy efforts, to maximize the retirement savings of nearly 6 million members.

terms.name: Inflation
terms.definition: Inflation is usually measured by the movement of the Consumer Price Index (CPI), which measure the change in the prices of goods and services in the economy.

terms.name: Inflows
terms.definition: Inflows represents the sum of new investments and regular contributions, or deposits paid into superannuation, pension and investment accounts. This results in an increase to the member account balances.

terms.name: Inforce Premium, Annualised Premium
terms.definition:
Inforce Premium: This refers to the total premium amount for insurance policies that are currently active and providing coverage. These policies are considered "in force" as long as the policyholder meets the payment terms and conditions outlined in the insurance contract.

Annualised Premium: This is the total amount of premium paid annually for an insurance policy. It is calculated by multiplying the periodic premium (e.g., monthly or quarterly) by the number of payment periods in a year. For example, if the monthly premium is $200, the annualised premium would be $200 × 12 = $2,400.

terms.name: Informatica
terms.definition:
Two key technology platforms provide an integrated, centralised and curated set of information assets to support analytics, reporting and authoritative master data.
Informatica is IFL’s master data management (MDM) platform, providing so-called ‘golden records’ that are integrated, high-quality, common enterprise-wide versions of selected information domains, such as ‘client’ and ‘account’, etc.  While a separate concept, reference data (data whose valid values are determined through reference to a standard source, whether internal or external, such as a national or international standard) is also managed through the Informatica platform.

Master data and reference data management provide a single high-quality and curated reference data set for important data that might otherwise vary across systems and business areas.

terms.name: Information
terms.definition: Words, images, sounds and other things which can communicate meaning to an audience. For the purposes of the Information Management policy suite, the term is used interchangeably with Data.

terms.name: Information Asset
terms.definition:
A purposely designed, structured and curated collection of data and information. In many cases, an information asset is managed as a database residing on a technology platform or information system. Note that the platform or system itself is not the information asset, but rather it’s the organised information collection itself that is the asset.

An information asset is a resource that must be managed like any other enterprise asset, and represents value to the organisation through the capabilities that the organised information affords.

Information assets do not have to be traditional databases, and may exist as electronic files, datasets, or even organised physical documents.

terms.name: Information Asset Owner
terms.definition: A Data Custodian, accountable for the effective, safe and efficient management of an Information Asset in their control.

terms.name: Information Handling
terms.definition: Storage, sharing and transmission of information and data. The methods, tools and techniques used to handle data are determined by the Information Classification and Handling Standard in accordance with the confidentiality and availability classification of the information. It includes labelling, provisioning of access, storage, distribution and disposal of Information Assets.

terms.name: Information Management
terms.definition:
The broad set of people, processes and technologies concerned with ensuring information and data is protected, available and fit for purpose. Information management activities are subject to the oversight of Data Governance, and include development and management of Information Assets, facilitation of Data Use, resolution of Data Issues, management of Data Quality and Archival and Disposal of information.

See the Information Management Policy and Information Management Framework for an understanding of the policy suite and operating model governing Information Management at Insignia.

terms.name: Initial Customer Due Diligence
terms.definition: Initial customer due diligence (ICDD) is the process of verifying a customer's identity and assessing their risk profile before establishing a business relationship or providing designated services. It's a crucial part of the broader Know Your Customer (KYC) framework aimed at preventing financial crimes like money laundering and terrorism financing.

terms.name: Initial Fee
terms.definition: Similar to Upfront Fees where the fee is not deductible.

terms.name: Ins Additional Features - Description
terms.definition: The details of the additional feature for the insurance arrangement.

terms.name: Ins Additional Features - Feature
terms.definition: Identifies any additional or unique feature of the insurance arrangement that is not captured in the standard fields

terms.name: Insignia Financial Incentive Plan
terms.definition: The short term incentive plan allocated to a majority of the general population of  insignia -  those on a specialist incentive are not included in the IFIP

terms.name: Inspecie Transfer
terms.definition: An in-specie transfer refers to the process of transferring assets in their current form, rather than converting them into cash. This method is commonly used for transferring property, shares, or other holdings between accounts, trusts, or entities. It allows ownership to be reassigned without selling and repurchasing the assets, which can be beneficial for tax efficiency or maintaining investment positions.

terms.name: Inspecie Transfer Quality Check
terms.definition:
An in-specie transfer refers to the process of transferring assets in their current form, rather than converting them into cash. For example, transferring shares, property, or other financial assets directly from one account to another without selling them first. This method is often used to avoid triggering tax events or to maintain the original form of the asset.

When it comes to quality checks in this context, it likely involves ensuring that the assets being transferred meet specific criteria or standards. For instance, verifying the accuracy of asset details, ownership, and compliance with regulations before completing the transfer.

terms.name: Insurance
terms.definition:
Insurance is a financial arrangement in which a person or entity (the insured) pays premiums to an insurance company in exchange for protection against potential risks or losses. If an insured event occurs—such as damage, illness, or financial loss—the insurer provides compensation according to the policy terms.

There are various types of insurance, including:

  • Health insurance – Covers medical expenses and treatments.
  • Car insurance – Protects against vehicle damage, theft, and accidents.
  • Home insurance – Covers property damage and loss.
  • Life insurance – Provides financial support to beneficiaries upon the insured's passing.
  • Business insurance – Helps protect companies from liability, property damage, or operational risks.

terms.name: Insurance Administration Fee
terms.definition: The Trustee may charge insurance administration fees, in addition to the insurance premium paid to an insurance provider, for the cost of certain insurance related services on no more than a cost recovery basis.

terms.name: Insurance Claim
terms.definition: When you make a claim on an insurance policy, you are formally notifying the insurance company that you have suffered a loss or event that you believe is covered by the policy.

terms.name: Insurance Cluster
terms.definition: Means a collection of one or more superannuation insurance policies held by an RSE licensee, issued by the same life insurance company.

terms.name: Insurance Cluster Identifier
terms.definition:
Means a unique identifier created by the RSE licensee to represent an insurance cluster.

The ICI must satisfy the following rules:

• it must comprise no more than 20 alpha-numeric characters (with no special characters);

• it cannot be the same as the ICI currently or previously used for another insurance cluster within the same RSE, defined benefit RSE or ERF; and

• it cannot change so long as the insurance cluster exists and must be reported consistently across reporting periods.

terms.name: Insurance Cluster Subgroup
terms.definition: Means the description of a collection within an insurance cluster of one or more insurance policies offered to members where terms may vary from other members. For example, members from a specific employer may have different terms or conditions to general membership.

terms.name: Insurance Consolidation
terms.definition: Clients can apply to consolidate their insurance from other providers.

terms.name: Insurance CPI
terms.definition: The Consumer Price Index (all groups and all capital cities) published by the Australian Bureau of Statistics (ABS). If no such consumer price index is published, the CPI will be a figure determined by the Insurer in its discretion.

terms.name: Insurance Effective date
terms.definition: The effective date of the insurance record.

terms.name: Insurance Eligibility End Date
terms.definition: For accounts with default OnePath Life insurance, eligibility requirements apply.

terms.name: Insurance Eligibility Service Date
terms.definition: The Insurance Eligibility Start Date is used to define the start date of the insurance cover applied within the super account.

terms.name: Insurance Fee
terms.definition: A premium is the amount you pay an insurer for insurance cover with in Super. The insurance maybe paid by the superannuation fund/Trustee, the transaction can be made from the funds balance or the member has nominated to pay the premiums by another payment method.

terms.name: Insurance Fees
terms.definition: Insurance Fees refer to the various charges and costs associated with purchasing and maintaining an insurance policy.

terms.name: Insurance Guide
terms.definition: The Insurance in your super guide contains terms and conditions about insurance, including costs, your eligibility for cover, how much you can apply for, when cover starts and stops and limitations or exclusions.

terms.name: Insurance Only
terms.definition: Specify if the plan has Insurance Only, where member has chosen a different super fund for their superannuation-guarantee (SG) contributions and has no account balance & will be admitted to the plan as an Insurance Only member.

terms.name: Insurance premium
terms.definition: The cost of a member’s group or retail insurance policy, which is paid by the fund out of the member’s super benefits. For more information, please refer to the insurance guide available on the fund’s website.

terms.name: Insurance Premiums
terms.definition: Insurance premiums represent the cost of group insurance cover, deducted from the member’s superannuation account.

terms.name: Insurance Reviews
terms.definition:
Care required  to differentiate between  Insurance Review procesess verses  Insurance Review data
 generic term describing the data used to conduct (typically annual) insurance reviews.
Clients with insurance cover  are reassed periodically and their insurance cover amounts and premiums modified according to updated risk profiles - age and other factors.

terms.name: Insurance Table Arrangement Identifier
terms.definition: Means a unique identifier for a related group of insurance table identifiers, where each insurance table ID under the insurance table arrangement is available to the same member cohort, and is differentiated only by risk factors including worker category and smoker status; or insurance cover type.

terms.name: Insurance Table Identifier
terms.definition:
Means a unique identifier created by the RSE licensee used to represent insurance arrangements.

The insurance table identifier must satisfy the following rules:

• it must comprise no more than 20 alpha-numeric characters (with no special characters);

• it cannot be the same as the insurance table number currently or previously used for another insurance arrangement within the same RSE or defined benefit RSE; and

• it cannot change so long as the insurance arrangement exists and must be reported consistently across reporting periods.

terms.name: Insurer
terms.definition: The name of the insurer that the insurance policy is held with.

terms.name: Insurer Category Description
terms.definition: A description defining the category of insurance as it appears in the insurance policy.

terms.name: Insurer Category Long Name
terms.definition: A short description defining the category of insurance.

terms.name: Integra
terms.definition: Integra is a legacy OnePath Super product that is now closed to new business and decommissioned and does not hold any existing policies. The remaining policies was transferred to ANZ Smart Choice Employer Super.

terms.name: Inter manco
terms.definition: A switch of investment options held under different investment managers. The investment options are held on different registry systems.

terms.name: Interactive Voice Response
terms.definition: This is a system used within the contact centre.

terms.name: Interdependency relationship
terms.definition:
Generally, two persons (whether or not related by family) have an ‘interdependency’ relationship if:

  • they have a close personal relationship, and
  • they live together, and
  • one or each of them provides the other with financial support, and
  • one or each of them provides the other with domestic support and personal care.

An interdependency relationship also includes two persons (whether or not related by family):

  • who have a close personal relationship, and
  • who do not meet the other criteria listed in the paragraph above because either or both of them have a physical, intellectual or psychiatric disability or they are temporarily living apart.

terms.name: Interdivisional Transfer
terms.definition: If a client holds an IOOF Employer Super and they leave the employer, the account gets changed to a personal account (i.e. IOOF Personal Super). This transaction is completed via an IDT which keeps all the account details (i.e. personal details / investments) in tact when the account is transferred to the personal product.

terms.name: Interfunding
terms.definition: All asset holdings which represent investments by Insignia managed funds into other Insignia managed funds.

terms.name: Interim Cover
terms.definition: Interim Accident insurance cover is provided.

terms.name: Interim Crediting Rate
terms.definition: An Interim Rate is required to allocate investment earnings to exiting members’ accounts for the period from the last Declared Rate to the date of exit. It is also used to quote information to current members.

terms.name: Internal Transfer
terms.definition:
An internal transfer refers to the movement of funds, assets, or employees within the same organization or financial institution. It can have different meanings depending on the context:

  • Banking & Finance: Transferring money between accounts within the same bank, such as moving funds from a checking account to a savings account.
  • Business & HR: Shifting an employee to a different role, department, or location within the same company.
  • Inventory & Logistics: Moving goods or resources between different units or branches of the same organization.

terms.name: Internal Transfer Adviser and Description
terms.definition:
An internal transfer adviser typically assists students or employees in navigating the process of transferring within an institution, such as changing majors at a university or moving to a different department within a company. Their role often includes:

  • Guidance and Support: Helping individuals understand the requirements, deadlines, and procedures for internal transfers.
  • Eligibility Assessment: Reviewing qualifications to ensure the individual meets the criteria for the desired transfer.
  • Application Assistance: Providing advice on completing necessary forms or applications.
  • Career or Academic Counseling: Offering insights into how the transfer aligns with long-term goals.

terms.name: Internal Transfers
terms.definition: Contains Full and Partial transfers between platforms e.g. Super to Pension, Pension to Super, Super to TTR etc. Can also be used for SFT's

terms.name: International Employment
terms.definition: Employee Sub group to represent: International resources

terms.name: International Securities Identification Number (ISIN)
terms.definition: An ISIN (International Securities Identification Number) is a 12-character alphanumeric code that uniquely identifies a specific financial instrument like a stock, bond, or derivative for global trading, administration, and settlement processes. It functions like a passport for investments, enabling clear identification across different countries and markets, thereby ensuring transparency, efficiency, and accuracy in transactions and record-keeping.

terms.name: Interview
terms.definition: One of the method of selection during the recruitment process. Involves assessing potential recruits in person

terms.name: Interview Guide
terms.definition: A sets of questions for our Hiring Manager

terms.name: Intestate
terms.definition: A person has died without a will.

terms.name: Intra manco
terms.definition: A switch of investments options held under the same investment manager. The investment options are held on the same registry.

terms.name: Intra-Fund Advice Costs
terms.definition: The costs of providing intra-fund advice are collectively charged to all members and form part of the administration fees and costs shown in the Fees and Costs Summary in the PDS. These costs are different from adviser service fees, which are agreed between the member and the adviser and are charged directly to members.

terms.name: Intra-Fund Consolidation
terms.definition:
The Trustee is required by the SIS Act to, at least once each financial year:

  • identify all members who hold more than one superannuation account in a superannuation fund for which NULIS is trustee; and
  • where the member has two or more superannuation accounts in a fund, consolidate their accounts into one (provided that the trustee reasonably believes that it is in the best interests of the member to do so); and
  • not charge any fees for any consolidation of superannuation accounts (other than a buy/sell spread).

terms.name: Intra-Fund Transfer
terms.definition: Is a transfer of fund members and their benefits between superannuation products within a regulated superannuation fund.

terms.name: Intranet
terms.definition: Enterprise wide SharePoint homepage where the enterprise can keep connected and up to date with what is happening in the business. This site links out to important information and policies.

terms.name: Invalidity Component
terms.definition: Also known as post-June 1994 invalidity component. From 1 July 1994, this is the future service component of an eligible termination payment (ETP) paid as a result of permanent invalidity (It is that part of the ETP that represents a payment for the period between the date employment stopped, due to injury, and the time of normal retirement). This component is normally exempt from tax, including the superannuation contributions tax ("surcharge") and is not counted as part of the reasonable benefit limit but is able to be rolled over.

terms.name: Investment Activity Statement
terms.definition:
An investment activity statement typically refers to a section of a company's financial records that details cash flows related to investment activities. These activities include:

  • Purchases of assets: Such as property, equipment, or securities.
  • Proceeds from sales: Selling assets or securities to generate cash.
  • Acquisitions: Investments in other businesses or companies.

This statement is part of the broader cash flow statement, which also includes operating and financing activities. It provides insights into how a company is allocating resources for growth and long-term benefits

terms.name: Investment and Financial Services Association
terms.definition: The Investment and Financial Services Association (IFSA) is now known as the Financial Services Council (FSC). It serves as a peak body representing Australia's financial services industry, including retail and wholesale funds management, superannuation funds, investment platforms, and financial advice licensees. The FSC develops mandatory standards and policies for its members, aiming to enhance the industry's professionalism and trustworthiness.

terms.name: Investment charge type
terms.definition:
The investment charge type indicates the circumstances in which the fee or cost is incurred.
The investment charge types are:

  • Base;
  • Performance Linked; and
  • Not Applicable.

terms.name: Investment Earnings (Net Of Member Deductions)
terms.definition: Include investment earnings (e.g. interest income, trust distribution, realised and unrealised gains / losses, etc), net of investment expenses. Also include admin fees, policy fee, Adviser Service fee, and any other fees, insurance premium

terms.name: Investment Fee
terms.definition: Investment Fee (is an indirect cost) is a fee that relates to the investment of the assets of the Fund and includes fees in payment for the exercise of care and expertise in the investment of those assets (including performance fees) and costs that relate to the investment of assets of the Fund.

terms.name: Investment Fee Rebate
terms.definition: Investment fee rebate can be negotiated between the fund manager and the Trustee to reduce the investment fees for some of the investment options available through the Investment Menu.

terms.name: Investment Fees and Costs
terms.definition: Investment Fees are fees and costs that are not directly charged to clients' accounts. They're reflected in the daily unit price of each investment option and will reduce the net return on their investment.

terms.name: Investment Holdings
terms.definition: Individual investors holdings

terms.name: Investment Income Register
terms.definition:
The Investment Income Register is used to capture income received from Investment Funds, such as dividends, etc.
After capturing the components, the income is then distributed to the eligible member accounts.

terms.full_name: Investment Management Fee (AKA MER or ongoing fees)
terms.name: Investment Management Fee (AKA MER or ongoing fees)
terms.definition: Ivestment Management Fees are also known as Management Expense Ratio, MER's or Ongoing Fees (in OneAnswer products) and are applied from returns on investments or from the underlying assets by the Investment Manager.

terms.name: Investment Manager
terms.definition: Investment managers are individuals or organizations who handle activities related to financial planning, investing, and managing a portfolio for their clients.

terms.name: Investment manager level
terms.definition:
Fees and costs related to Investment activities are classified by their investment manager level.
The investment manager levels are:

  • Direct Investment Manager
  • Underlying Investment Manager; and
  • Not Applicable.

terms.name: Investment Menu
terms.definition: The Investment Menu provides clients information about their investments available through their super and pension.

terms.name: Investment Menu Identifier
terms.definition:
Means a unique identifier created by the RSE licensee used to represent an investment menu. The IMI must satisfy the following rules:

  • it must comprise no more than 20 alpha-numeric characters (with no special characters);
  • it cannot be the same as the IMI used for another investment menu within the same RSE, defined benefit RSE or ERF; and
  • it cannot change so long as the investment menu exists and must be reported consistently across reporting periods (including if the name of the investment menu or the investment options available change).

terms.full_name: Investment Mgmt. FUM, Asset Mgmt. FUM, Internal Trust FUM
terms.name: Investment Mgmt. FUM, Asset Mgmt. FUM, Internal Trust FUM
terms.definition:
• Investment Management FUM (Funds Under Management): This refers to the total value of assets that an investment management company oversees on behalf of its clients. It includes various financial instruments like stocks, bonds, and other investments managed to achieve specific financial goals
  • Asset Management FUM: Similar to Investment Management FUM, this term is used in the context of asset management firms. It represents the total market value of assets that the firm manages for its clients, including portfolios, mutual funds, and other investment vehicles.
  • Internal Trust FUM: This typically refers to funds managed internally within a trust structure. For example, in superannuation or pension funds, internal trust FUM might indicate the portion of funds managed directly by the trust rather than outsourced to external managers.

terms.name: Investment Mix
terms.definition: An investment mix, also known as an asset mix or portfolio mix, refers to the combination of different types of investments within a portfolio. This mix typically includes various asset classes such as stocks, bonds, cash, and real estate. The goal of an investment mix is to balance risk and return by diversifying investments across different categories

terms.name: Investment Name
terms.definition: An investment name or option is a specific asset available to the investor for investment within a product - effectively that it is part of the current product menu.

terms.name: Investment Objective
terms.definition: Describes what the investment option aims to achieve over a certain timeframe. Most investment options aim to produce returns that are comparable to a benchmark. The investment objective outlines whether returns used to judge an investment option’s success include or exclude certain fees and tax.

terms.name: Investment Option
terms.definition: An investment option is a specific asset available to the investor for investment within a product - effectively that it is part of the current product menu. For example, this is the individual assets available to eXpand Super members within the appropriate Investment Strategies Booklet.

terms.name: Investment Option Identifier
terms.definition:
Means a unique identifier created by the RSE licensee used to represent an investment option. The IOI must satisfy the following rules:

  • it must comprise no more than 20 alpha-numeric characters (with no special characters);
  • it cannot be the same as the IOI used for another investment option within the same RSE , defined benefit RSE or ERF; and
  • it cannot change so long as the investment option exists and must be reported consistently across reporting periods (including if the name of the investment option changes).

terms.name: Investment Parameter Review
terms.definition:
An investment parameter review is conducted bi-annually to ensure that the information provided to NAB Asset Servicing (NAS) which is used to generate unit prices, is consistent with:

  • The Product Disclosure Statements;
  • The fee information in Unison, managed by Product Operation Accounting; and
  • The parameter file managed by the Pricing, Profitability & Actuarial team.

terms.name: Investment reserve
terms.definition: Represents the difference between the cumulative value of investment income (net of investment expenses) allocated to members’ accounts compared to the cumulative investment income (net of investment expenses) earned. Includes: investment fees deducted from members accounts but not yet allocated for payment of investment related

terms.name: Investment return
terms.definition: In finance and superannuation, investment return refers to the gain or loss generated by an investment over a specific period, expressed as a percentage of the initial investment amount.

terms.name: Investment Return Objective
terms.definition: Investment return objective identifies what return the investment option aims to achieve. This is often stated in relation to a relevant index.

terms.name: Investment Risk
terms.definition: All investments come with some risk. Some investment options will have more risk than others, as it depends on an option's investment strategy and assets.

terms.name: Investment Savings Bond
terms.definition: Peviously known as the Tax Effective Investment Bond (TEIB), the Investment Savings Bond (ISB), is issued by OnePath Life, and is an investment vehicle that provides a simple, flexible and tax effective way to save. Closed to new business.

terms.name: Investment Strategy
terms.definition: Investment strategy describes how the investment option aims to achieve its objective. Usually involves a description of the relevant asset classes and an outline of the investment process, or combination of processes that will be used to manage the investment option.

terms.name: Investment Strategy Change
terms.definition: Changes the way future contributions (including any rollovers) to your account are invested.

terms.name: Investment Switch
terms.definition: Changes the way your existing assets in your account are invested.

terms.name: Investor Directed Portfolio Service
terms.definition: IDPSs are unregistered managed investment schemes (unregistered schemes) for holding and dealing with one or more investments selected by investors. They are managed investment schemes because investors have the expectation of cost savings (e.g. through the netting of transactions or the pooling of funds to acquire investments) or access to investments that would not otherwise be available to them.

terms.name: Investor Fee Category
terms.definition: See Divison Investor Fee Category Details

terms.name: Investor Name
terms.definition: Name of the Investor or Client/customer/Member.

terms.name: Investor Number
terms.definition:
An investor number is a unique identifier assigned to an investor by a financial institution, fund manager, or securities registry. It helps track investments, transactions, and account details efficiently.

In Australia, investor numbers are commonly used in:

  • Superannuation funds: To manage individual member accounts.
  • Share registries: To identify shareholders in listed companies.
  • Investment platforms: To track holdings across different assets.

Investor numbers may be linked to a Holder Identification Number (HIN) or Security Reference Number (SRN) for shares

terms.name: Investor Profile
terms.definition: An indication of your investment personality that considers factors such your investment time horizon, how comfortable you are with investment market fluctuations, your investment preferences and your investment experience. Keep in mind however that you may also need to take into account your personal needs and circumstances when making or changing your investment choice.

terms.name: Invoice Approval Amount Limit
terms.definition: Limit till which the created user can approve an invoice

terms.name: Invoice management
terms.definition: Invoice management involves overseeing the verification and processing of supplier invoices, leading to efficient and timely payment. It ensures financial transparency and minimises discrepancies within an organisation's procurement and finance processes.

terms.name: IOOF Balanced Investor Trust
terms.definition: IOOF Balanced Investor Trust (IBIT) is the default MySuper Investment option.

terms.name: IOOF Employer Super
terms.definition:
The IOOF Employer Super Accounts including employer super and personal super. 
Note: More modern accounts would use Expand instead.

terms.name: IOOF Investment Management Ltd
terms.definition: IOOF Investment Management is part of Insignia Financial, a leading financial services provider in Australia. They offer a range of investment products, including MLC MultiSeries, MLC MultiActive, and Expand Investment solutions, designed to provide diversification and competitive returns

terms.name: IOOF Portfolio Services
terms.definition: This is not to be confused with The Portfolio Services (TPS)

terms.name: Is Compound Tax
terms.definition: Indicates if the Tax is compound(Compound tax is applied on total value with tax.)

terms.full_name: Is participation of any employer-sponsor limited?
terms.name: Is participation of any employer-sponsor limited?
terms.definition: Represents where there are limits under the trust deed or rules of the RSE that set a cap on the amount or rate at which an employer-sponsor is required to make contributions in certain circumstances.

terms.name: Is Self Assessed Tax
terms.definition:
This defines if it is Self Assessable tax, e.g. Use tax which should not be added to the amount payable to the vendor. 
If the value is not received, for inbound, it will be assumed to be "FALSE" for "Is self assessed amount".

terms.name: Jira
terms.definition: Ticketing and work management system

terms.name: Job Description
terms.definition: The document which outlines the scope and core accountabilities of a particular job role, assigned to a position or a group of positions.

terms.name: Journal
terms.definition: This is when moving funds from one bank account to another internally.

terms.name: Justice of the Peace
terms.definition: Justice of the Peace (JP) is to act as an independent and objective witness to documents people use for official or legal purposes. JPs can; witness oaths and affidavits, take statutory declarations and affirmations, witness signatures, attest the execution of a document and certify a true copy of an original document.

terms.name: Key Performance Indicator
terms.definition:
A Key Performance Indicator (KPI) is a measurable value used to evaluate the success of an individual, team, or organization in achieving specific goals or objectives. KPIs are widely used in businesses and other organizations to track progress and performance in key areas. They can be quantitative (e.g., revenue growth) or qualitative (e.g., customer satisfaction).

For example:

  • In marketing, a KPI might be the conversion rate of leads into sales.
  • For customer service, it could be the average response time to inquiries.

terms.name: Key Risk Indicator
terms.definition: A key risk indicator (KRI) is an early warning indicator for a key risk to which the organization is exposed. KRIs are specific and identifiable pieces of information that act as proxies for or indicators of the current or potential status of key risks.

terms.name: Key Segment Client
terms.definition: Refers to a Key Segment client, based on Shadforth’s client categorisation model.

terms.name: Key Service Indicator
terms.definition: A Key Service Indicator (KSI) is a measurable metric used to evaluate the performance and quality of a specific service. KSIs help organizations monitor whether their services meet predefined standards or objectives, such as customer satisfaction, response times, or service availability. They are often used alongside Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) to ensure that both operational and strategic goals are being achieved.

terms.name: Know Your Customer
terms.definition: This refers to the process of verifying the identity of your customers, either before or during the time that they start doing business with you. Due diligence that financial institutions must carry out to identify their customers to prevent theft, financial fraud, money laundering and terrorist financing.

terms.name: Knowledge Management
terms.definition: This is linked to our knowledge platform site, Orbit. Most content loaded within Orbit will have a reference number starting with KM.

terms.name: KYC Verified
terms.definition: To confirm and substantiate a client’s identification information collected, using acceptable reliable and independent documents and sources, including electronic means, in order to ascertain that the client exists and is the same person claiming the identity (is who they say they are).

terms.name: Labelling
terms.definition: The use of document marking or other form of Metadata associated with information that indicates the application of some Classification. Commonly, this will be used to indicate Confidentiality, but can extend to any classification scheme that is considered appropriate. Labelling provides important context to a document, record or data that assists in either its use or management.

terms.name: Lapsing Binding Death Benefit Nomination
terms.definition: A lapsing binding nomination is a written direction that, if valid and in effect, binds the trustee to pay the benefit to nominated beneficiaries. This type of nomination expires after three years unless renewed.

terms.name: Large Plan Rebate
terms.definition: LPR is for MKBS accounts.

terms.name: Last Contact
terms.definition: Related to Lost Member management and reporting. This is the date that contact was last made with a member (and is isuaally associated with  the form of contact). A member is considered Lost if no contact has been made after 12 months (or their address details and contact are known to be invalid).

terms.name: Late Payment Interest
terms.definition: Late Payment Interest (LPI) for Workplace Super products is the interest applied to a member’s defined benefit lump sum (excluding any amount used to purchase a lifetime pension in the employer plan) component of their total benefit from the date their defined benefit crystallises (usually the date that they cease employment with the sponsoring employer) until the date of actual payment of their benefit from the employer plan.

terms.name: Leader
terms.definition: Anyone who has a direct line report under then.

terms.name: Leader Hub
terms.definition: A SharePoint site hosting information to help leaders at Insignia lead their teams.

terms.name: Leader update
terms.definition: Regular email update sent to leaders providing them with the latest need to knows and items to discuss with their people. Links back to the Leader Hub

terms.name: Leadership Team
terms.definition: Direct reports to ET

terms.name: Learning Management System
terms.definition: learning system that is used to assign learning modules to employees

terms.name: Leave Loading
terms.definition: Annual leave loading

terms.name: Leave Without Pay
terms.definition: Unpaid absence

terms.name: Legacy
terms.definition: A term used to describe a discontinued product - generally closed to new business.

terms.name: Legal Name
terms.definition: Legal name is the name that identifies a person for legal, administrative and other official purposes. The legal name is recorded on identification/registration documents and it is the name that appears on official documents or legal papers.

terms.name: Legal Personal Representative
terms.definition: A legal personal representative is an individual or entity appointed to act on behalf of another person, typically in legal or financial matters. This role is often associated with managing the estate of a deceased person, where the representative is responsible for tasks such as distributing assets, paying debts, and ensuring the wishes outlined in a will are carried out. The term can also apply to someone authorized to make decisions for another person, such as through a power of attorney.

terms.name: Legislated Outcomes Assessment
terms.definition: Each year the Legislated Outcomes Assessment (LOA) determines whether each individual MySuper and Choice product offered by the Trustee is promoting the financial interests of members.

terms.name: Letter of Authority
terms.definition: A letter of authority is a formal document that grants permission to a third party to act on behalf of the person issuing the letter. It is often used in situations where the issuer cannot perform certain tasks themselves, such as collecting documents, managing accounts, or handling official matters. The letter specifies the scope of authority and ensures accountability for the actions taken by the authorized individual.

terms.name: Letters of Administration
terms.definition: Letters of Administration are used when there is no Will or Executor. They are issued by a supreme court to a person (such as the next of kin), who is appointed as the Administrator of the Estate. This enables the Administrator to represent the estate and distribute assets to the beneficiaries.

terms.name: Levies
terms.definition: Levies – government costs that may be imposed on superannuation funds. For more information on levies, refer to the PDS available on the fund’s website.

terms.name: Licensee
terms.definition:
Australian Financial Services Licensee registered in ASIC. The entity that holds the AFS licence from the Australian Securities and Investments Commission (ASIC).

The ultimate legal entity responsible for ensuring the firm and its representatives provide efficient, honest, and fair services and comply with all financial services laws.

terms.name: Licensee Remuneration Agreement
terms.definition: A licensee remuneration agreement is a legal contract that outlines the terms under which a licensee is compensated for their role in using or distributing the licensor's intellectual property, products, or services. This agreement typically specifies payment structures, such as royalties, fees, or commissions, and may include conditions related to performance, exclusivity, or territory. It ensures clarity and fairness in the financial relationship between the licensor and licensee

terms.name: Life Annuity
terms.definition: In the context of pension, periodic payments (usually monthly) are provided by the terms of an insurance contract that will be paid for the lifetime of a person (the annuitant), or the person and his or her designated beneficiary. Annuities are normally purchased from insurance companies.

terms.name: Life Events
terms.definition: Events that are eligible for life event cover without underwriting.

terms.name: Life Expectancy
terms.definition: Life expectancy refers to the average number of years a person is expected to live based on statistical analysis of mortality rates. It is often calculated for specific populations or demographic groups and can vary depending on factors like age, gender, geographic location, and access to healthcare.

terms.name: Life insurance
terms.definition:
Life insurance is a contract between an insurer and a policyholder that guarantees a sum of money to the policy’s named beneficiaries upon the insured person's death. It acts as a financial safety net, helping loved ones cover expenses like housing, food, and utility bills.

There are different types of life insurance:

  • Term life insurance: Provides coverage for a specific period, such as 10 or 20 years, and is generally more affordable.
  • Permanent life insurance: Offers lifelong protection and includes a cash value component.

Premiums are determined by factors like age, health, and lifestyle, with younger and healthier individuals typically paying lower rates.

terms.name: Lifetime Pension
terms.definition: A lifetime pension is payable for your lifetime or until you fully commute your pension.

terms.name: Limited Cover
terms.definition: Details the limitations to the related insurance cover for this category of eligibility.

terms.name: Link/Unlink Investors
terms.definition: Life expectancy in a superannuation context refers to the average age a person is expected to live based on demographic factors, such as their current age and gender.

terms.name: LinkedIn
terms.definition: Online advertising platform

terms.name: LinkedIn headhunting
terms.definition: Platform for TA to search for non active candidates

terms.name: List of Investment Strategies
terms.definition:
Investment strategies refer to systematic approaches used by investors to manage their portfolios and achieve financial goals. These strategies can vary depending on risk tolerance, time horizon, and market conditions. Here are some common ones:

  • Value Investing – Buying undervalued stocks based on fundamental analysis, expecting their price to rise.
  • Growth Investing – Investing in companies with high potential for revenue and earnings growth.
  • Income Investing – Focusing on assets that generate regular income, such as dividend-paying stocks or bonds.
  • Momentum Investing – Following trends by buying stocks that have recently shown strong performance.
  • Dollar-Cost Averaging – Regularly investing a fixed amount to reduce the impact of market volatility.
  • Contrarian Investing – Going against market sentiment, investing in assets others may be avoiding.
  • Passive vs. Active Investing – Passive involves holding diversified index funds, while active requires frequent buying and selling to outperform the market.

terms.name: Loanback
terms.definition: Customers who invested certain products (e.g. Wealthbuilder bonds) are able to borrow against their account balance.  This is known as a Loanback.

terms.name: Location Id
terms.definition:
TMS - Unique Id of the Location which is assigned as default to user. Location  mentioned must be existing and active in the system, and linked to the corresponding OU level 1 and 2 mentioned. Mandatory when eProc and eInvoice are assigned to the user

Payment Term - Unique transaction ID for payment voucher. Same transaction Id can be used against different invoices.

terms.name: Locked Member
terms.definition: When set, this flag will prevent any withdrawals from being applied to the Account.

terms.name: Long Service Leave
terms.definition: Long service leave is a national workplace entitlement that most long serving employees, including casuals, are eligible for.

terms.name: Long term incentive
terms.definition: This is an industry standard acronym for incentives which are paid after a longer period of time (typically 3-5 years) offered to senior leaders to usually provide a reward in equity in the business,  if the business performs to a pre-defined level of improvement

terms.name: Loss on reserve
terms.definition:
Loss on reserve refers to the financial impact when an insurer's estimated reserves for future claims turn out to be insufficient. This can happen due to unexpected claim costs, miscalculations, or changes in risk factors. Insurers set aside loss reserves to cover future payouts, but if actual claims exceed these reserves, they incur a loss on reserve.

Loss reserves are crucial for maintaining financial stability, and insurers must carefully estimate them using actuarial models and risk assessments. If reserves are underestimated, it can affect profitability and solvency.

terms.name: Lost (Y/N)
terms.definition:
An Employer is considered Lost after consecutive returned mail and/or
a valid address has not been obtained.

terms.name: Lost Member
terms.definition: Client who is inactive (no contributions or rollovers) for five years, cannot be contacted, or who has transferred in as a lost member and has not been found or advised a new address.

terms.name: Lost member account
terms.definition:
A lost member account refers to a superannuation account belonging to a member who is classified as "lost" under Australian regulations. A member is considered lost if they are uncontactable, inactive, or have transferred from another fund as a lost member.

There are two main types of lost member accounts:

  1. Small lost member account – If the account balance is less than $6,000, it must be reported and paid to the Australian Taxation Office (ATO).
  2. Insoluble lost member account – If the member is inactive and cannot be contacted, their account may be classified as insoluble and transferred to the ATO.


Excludes: members that have confirmed their address in the past two years, members that have indicated that they want to remain a member, inactive member accounts and active member accounts. Reference: SIS Regulations r. 1.03A Reference link: https://www.legislation.gov.au/F1996B00580/2019-08-10/text

terms.name: Lost Member Register
terms.definition: A central register of lost superannuation fund members administered by the ATO.

terms.name: Low Account Balance Reinstatement
terms.definition: The period the member can reinstate their cover by applying in writing within (x) days of cover ceasing.

terms.name: Low Income Super Contributions
terms.definition:
Low-income earners in Australia can benefit from the Low Income Superannuation Tax Offset (LISTO). This government initiative provides a rebate of up to $500 annually, paid directly into your super account. It's designed to ensure that low-income earners don't pay more tax on their super contributions than on their take-home pay.

Eligibility criteria include:

  • Earning $37,000 or less per year.
  • Having concessional (before-tax) contributions made to your super fund.
  • Providing your tax file number (TFN) to your super fund

terms.name: Low Income Super Tax Offset
terms.definition:
The Low Income Super Tax Offset (LISTO) is a government initiative in Australia designed to help low-income earners boost their retirement savings. If you earn up to $37,000 annually, you may be eligible for a LISTO payment of up to $500. This payment is calculated as 15% of the concessional (before-tax) super contributions made by you or your employer.

The offset ensures that low-income earners don't pay more tax on their super contributions than on their take-home pay. To receive the LISTO payment, your super fund must have your tax file number (TFN), and you must meet specific eligibility criteria

terms.name: Low Rate Cap
terms.definition:
A low rate cap refers to a limit or maximum on interest rates that can be applied to a financial product, like a loan or a credit agreement, where the cap is set at a relatively low level. It ensures that the borrower won't pay more than the capped rate, even if market interest rates increase significantly.

Low rate caps are often designed to provide protection and predictability for borrowers, especially in variable or adjustable-rate scenarios. They can be part of policies, contracts, or regulatory measures aimed at promoting affordability and mitigating financial risks

terms.name: Low Rate Threshold
terms.definition:
The low rate threshold refers to the limit up to which eligible lump sum superannuation withdrawals are taxed at a concessional rate. In Australia, individuals who have reached their preservation age but are under 60 years old can withdraw superannuation lump sums, with amounts up to the low rate threshold being taxed at a lower rate.

Beyond the threshold, higher tax rates may apply. The threshold is indexed annually, meaning it can change over time. Once an individual turns 60, most superannuation withdrawals become tax-free.

terms.name: Lower quartile
terms.definition: This is a mathematical term which describes the data point which is at the 25th % point of the total number of data points in an data set

terms.name: Lump Sum
terms.definition: In the context of superannuation, the lump sum is the benefit taken as a single payment, rather than by instalments or as a pension or annuity.

terms.name: Lump Sum Tax
terms.definition:
A lump sum tax is a fixed amount of tax imposed on individuals or entities, regardless of their income, wealth, or economic activity. Unlike other tax systems that vary based on financial circumstances, a lump sum tax remains constant for everyone. For example, every individual in a community might be required to pay the same fixed amount, regardless of their earnings or assets.

This type of tax is considered economically efficient because it doesn't distort behavior or create "deadweight loss." However, it is often criticized for being regressive, as it can disproportionately burden those with lower incomes.

terms.name: Mail Annual Reports
terms.definition: Indicator whether Member Annual Statements are delivered electronically or via postal mail

terms.name: Managed Discretionary Account
terms.definition:
A Managed Discretionary Account (MDA) is a type of investment service regulated in Australia that allows a financial adviser or portfolio manager (the MDA provider) to manage a client's investments without needing to get prior approval for each transaction, as long as it's within an agreed framework.

Key Features of an MDA:
The client contributes assets to the account.
The MDA provider manages the portfolio on an individual basis, using their discretion.
The provider must act within agreed limitations and aim to generate financial returns or benefits for the client.
The arrangement is not a registered managed investment scheme, but it may still fall under the definition of a managed investment scheme or a financial investment facility under the Corporations Act.
Regulation:
MDAs are regulated under ASIC’s Regulatory Guide 179 (RG 179).
ASIC provides relief from certain provisions of the Corporations Act if MDA providers meet specific conditions.
This includes relief from managed investment scheme rules and some disclosure requirements.

terms.name: Managed Funds System
terms.definition:
A managed funds system refers to an investment structure where money from multiple investors is pooled together and managed by a professional fund manager. The fund manager invests this collective capital into a diversified portfolio of assets, such as stocks, bonds, property, or other securities, based on the fund's specific objectives and strategy.

Investors in a managed fund own units in the fund, which represent their share of the total investment. The value of these units fluctuates with the performance of the underlying assets. Managed funds can cater to different risk appetites and investment goals, offering options like growth-focused funds, income-generating funds, or balanced funds.

terms.name: Managed Investment Ratio
terms.definition: The Managed Investment Ratio (MAR) is a financial metric used to evaluate the risk-adjusted performance of investments. It compares an investment's Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR) to its Maximum Drawdown (the largest peak-to-trough decline in value). This ratio helps investors understand how well an investment balances returns with risk, particularly in volatile markets.

terms.name: Managed Investments
terms.definition: A managed investment (AKA managed fund) involves pooling together money from different investors into one fund that is invested and controlled by a professional investment manager. Funds differ in the types of assets you can invest in. You can invest in a single asset class, such as shares or fixed interest, or a multi-sector option, such as a Growth or Balanced fund, which contain a mix of different asset classes.

terms.name: Managed Portfolio Service
terms.definition:
A Managed Portfolio Service (MPS) is a financial product where a professional manager invests and manages a diversified portfolio of assets on behalf of clients, often through a discretionary account structure that allows for quick implementation of investment decisions. MPSs provide investors with access to expert management, a diversified range of asset classes (like equities and fixed income), and portfolios tailored to specific risk profiles and financial goals.

Examples include: Rhythm IOOF MPS on the HUB24 platform, BT MPS, IFL Expand, MLC Wrap

terms.name: Managed Service/Employee
terms.definition: Employee Sub group to represent: Created to move those who are currently in Consultant/managed services sub group that are not true sow resource s ( adobe, variant etc)

terms.name: Managed Service/Offshore
terms.definition: Employee Sub group to represent: Created to move those who are currently in Consultant/Offshore sub group but are as a part of a big agreement

terms.name: Management Expense Ratio
terms.definition: MER % - is the proportion of your investments you must pay your investment manager. Example: In an equity fund where the historical gross return might be 10%, a 1% expense ratio will consume approximately 10% of the investor's return. In a bond fund where the historical gross return might be 8%, a 1% expense ratio will consume approximately 12.5% of the investor's return. In a money market fund where the historical gross return might be 5%, a 1% expense ratio will consume approximately 20% of the investor's historical total return.

terms.name: Manager
terms.definition: Anyone who has a direct line report under then.

terms.name: Managers Expense Ratio
terms.definition:
The manager's expense ratio is a metric used to evaluate the cost efficiency of a fund manager or an investment fund. It represents the proportion of a fund's assets that is used to cover management fees and other operational expenses. The ratio is typically expressed as a percentage and is calculated as:

Expense Ratio = (Total Expenses / Total Fund Assets) × 100

For instance, if a fund has an expense ratio of 1%, it means that 1% of the fund's assets are being used annually to cover expenses. A lower expense ratio generally indicates better cost efficiency, which can benefit investors in the long term

terms.name: Market Entity Identification Number
terms.definition: Every National Australia Bank customer is allocated a unique number (MEID) that links all their banking products together for sales reporting back to the Bank. The Bank measures and rewards performances for each adviser and banker based on their targets. Each relevant client has their own MEID number. MEID customer numbers are 9 digits long.

terms.name: Market Linked Income Stream
terms.definition: MLIS are AKA Allocated Pensions (TAPs)

terms.name: Market Movement
terms.definition: Calculated field based on Movement (Opening Balance Less Closing Balance) -Netflows - Internal Transfers - Pension Payments.

terms.name: Marketing Development Manager
terms.definition: A Marketing Development Manager typically focuses on identifying and creating growth opportunities for a company or brand. Their responsibilities often include developing marketing strategies, researching new market segments, managing campaigns, and collaborating with sales teams to generate leads and drive revenue. They analyze market trends, identify customer needs, and ensure the company stays competitive in its industry.

terms.name: Master Pooled Super Trust
terms.definition:
A type of investment vehicle designed to manage superannuation funds collectively. It pools contributions from multiple investors, such as individuals or organizations, to achieve economies of scale and provide access to diversified investment opportunities. Here are some key features:

  • Diversification: Funds are invested across various asset classes, reducing risk
  • Cost Efficiency: Pooling resources lowers administrative and management costs.
  • Professional Management: Investments are overseen by experienced fund managers.

terms.name: Master trust
terms.definition: Client platform in C&T

terms.name: Master Trust Insurance
terms.definition: Historically known in P&I as Internal and External Master Trust Risk, it represents the Insurance on our Super platforms.

terms.name: MasterKey Business Super
terms.definition:
