MLC MasterKey Business Super is a superannuation product offered by MLC, designed to help individuals grow and manage their retirement savings. Here are some key features:

  • Investment Options: It provides a range of investment choices, including MLC MySuper, which is a default option for those who don't select a specific investment strategy.
  • Insurance Coverage: Members can access life insurance, total and permanent disability (TPD) insurance, and income protection insurance through their super account.
  • Employer Contributions: Employers can make super guarantee contributions, and members can boost their super with additional contributions like salary sacrifice or personal after-tax contributions
  • Cost Efficiency: The product aims to offer competitive fees and streamlined administration.

terms.name: MasterKey Investment Service
terms.definition:
The MLC MasterKey Investment Service is a financial product designed to help individuals manage their investments effectively. It offers features such as:

  • Investment Options: A variety of choices tailored to different financial goals, including managed funds and direct investments.
  • Flexibility: Options to switch between investments or adjust strategies as needed.
  • Professional Management: Access to expert fund managers who oversee investments.
  • Administration: Streamlined processes for managing accounts and transactions.

terms.name: MasterKey Investment Service Fundamentals
terms.definition:
The MLC MasterKey Investment Service Fundamentals is a financial product designed to help individuals manage their investments effectively. It offers features such as:

  • Investment Options: A variety of choices tailored to different financial goals, including managed funds and direct investments.
  • Flexibility: Options to switch between investments or adjust strategies as needed.
  • Professional Management: Access to expert fund managers who oversee investments.
  • Administration: Streamlined processes for managing accounts and transactions.

terms.name: MasterKey Personal Super
terms.definition:
MLC MasterKey Personal Super is a superannuation product offered by MLC in Australia. It provides members with various investment options, including portfolios like MLC Inflation Plus, MLC Horizon, and MLC Index Plus. These options cater to different risk preferences and financial goals, such as long-term capital growth or returns above inflation.

The fund also offers features like life insurance, total and permanent disability (TPD) insurance, and income protection insurance within super. Members can manage their accounts online and access term deposits within their super.

terms.name: MasterKey Unit Trust
terms.definition: The MLC MasterKey Unit Trust is an investment product offered by MLC Investments Limited. It provides a range of managed investment options, such as growth, balanced, and conservative portfolios, catering to different investor needs and risk preferences. These options are designed to help investors achieve their financial goals by diversifying across various asset classes like shares, property, and cash.

terms.name: MasterTrust Risk, Insurance on Super
terms.definition:
A master trust is a pooled investment structure that manages assets for multiple investors or beneficiaries. While it offers benefits like economies of scale and streamlined administration, there are risks to consider:

  • Market Risk: Changes in market conditions can affect the value of investments
  • Liquidity Risk: Difficulty in accessing funds during volatile periods
  • Governance Risk: Potential mismanagement or lack of transparency in operations

Regarding insurance on superannuation, most super funds in Australia provide:

  • Life Insurance: Pays a lump sum to beneficiaries in case of death
  • Total and Permanent Disability (TPD) Insurance: Offers financial support if you're unable to work due to a permanent disability.
  • Income Protection Insurance: Provides a regular income if you're temporarily unable to work due to illness or injury.

Premiums for these insurances are typically deducted from your super balance, making it convenient but potentially reducing your retirement savings. It's essential to review your coverage and ensure it aligns with your needs.

terms.name: Mat Leave
terms.definition: Parental Leave taken during child birth for the mother.

terms.name: Maternity Leave
terms.definition: Parental Leave taken during child birth for the mother.

terms.name: Maturing Investments
terms.definition:
Typically related to investments that have a predefined investment period such as term deposits.
Maturing deposits are investments that are reaching the end of theinvestment period 
(and the Client has to nominate instructions for the proceeds, such a reinvesting or withdrawing the funds)

terms.name: Maturity Date
terms.definition: The maturity date of an account or loanback.

terms.name: Max Term
terms.definition: An employment contract for an employee with an end date but is not an external/contingent.

terms.name: Max Term Contract
terms.definition: An employment contract for an employee with an end date but is not an external/contingent.

terms.name: Maximum Age
terms.definition: The maximum age at which a member can automatically receive default cover under the plan.

terms.name: Maximum Contribution Base
terms.definition: Maximum contribution Base set by the ATO -  business is not obligated to pay super above this amount. The maximum super contribution base is used to determine the maximum limit on any individual employee's earnings base for each quarter of any financial year.

terms.name: Maximum Cover
terms.definition: The maximum sum insured that is offered for the particular insurance cover type.

terms.name: Maximum Pension Payment
terms.definition: For TTR pensions, a maximum annual pension payment equal to 10% of the account balance will apply each year (the maximum is not pro-rated). The maximum pension payment is first calculated when a member starts their pension and is recalculated each year on 1 July, using the account balance at that date.

terms.name: Maximum term employee
terms.definition: Has finite date of employment

terms.name: Median quartile
terms.definition: This is a mathematical term which describes the data point which is at the median (middle or 50h percentile) of the total number of data points in an data set

terms.full_name: Medical and Associated Professionals Super Fund
terms.name: Medical and Associated Professionals Super Fund
terms.definition: The Medical and Associated Professionals Superannuation Fund (MAP Super) was a corporate pension fund based in Melbourne, Australia. It allowed individuals to tailor their superannuation accounts to meet specific needs, including contributions from employers, salary sacrifice, and spouse contributions. MAP Super provided comprehensive record-keeping and compliance reporting.

terms.name: Medicare Levy
terms.definition: The Medicare levy is a tax in Australia that helps fund the country's public healthcare system, known as Medicare. It is calculated as 2% of an individual's taxable income and is paid in addition to regular income tax. Certain exemptions or reductions may apply based on income levels, age, or specific circumstances.

terms.name: Member
terms.definition: Refers to an employee who has enrolled in a employer or pension plan and is accruing benefits for current service (employment).

terms.name: Member (Client) Can Transact Online
terms.definition: An indicator to dermine whther the member can login to the online portal and generate transactions and apply other changes, as opposed to just having read-only enquiries/reporting access.

terms.name: Member (Pension) Benefit Payment History
terms.definition: The complete history of all pension  payments to a Client made from a particular pension Account.

terms.name: Member account
terms.definition: Represents a distinct entry recorded in the register of member accounts (or other equivalent mechanism).

terms.name: Member account with a TFN
terms.definition:
A member account with a TFN refers to a superannuation account that has a Tax File Number (TFN) linked to it. In Australia, a TFN is a unique identifier issued by the Australian Taxation Office (ATO), and it plays a crucial role in managing superannuation contributions, rollovers, and tax obligations.

Having a TFN attached to a superannuation account allows:

  • Faster transactions: It helps link contributions and rollovers efficiently.
  • Lower tax rates: Without a TFN, higher tax rates may apply to contributions.
  • Account consolidation: Funds can use a TFN to identify multiple accounts and suggest consolidation options.

Superannuation funds must follow strict regulations when handling TFNs, including obtaining member consent before using them for certain searches

terms.name: Member account without a TFN
terms.definition: Represents a member account for which the RSE does not have a tax file number recorded to identify the member.

terms.name: Member Additional Accounts
terms.definition: Non-SG, Non-Employer accounts used for the calculation of cover where the formula calls out additional accounts separately.

terms.name: Member ADVCN
terms.definition: This is the Orion prior system adviser number  number for those accounts that transferred from the Orion platform.

terms.name: Member Advice Fee
terms.definition: MAF are fees that are charged to a member for financial product advice they received, this fee is specific to ANZ Smart Choice.

terms.name: Member Adviser Details
terms.definition:
A generic term to collectively refer to the details of the Adviser who may be managing the Account for the Member. Includes -
Adviser Code
Revenue Paid To
Member (Client) Can Transact Online

terms.name: Member Anti Money Laundering Details
terms.definition:
A generic term for the information collected to meet Anti Money Laundering (AML) regulatory requirements. Financial institutions are required by law to collect and verify customer information and follow processes to reduce the risk of Money Laundering. Is part of the Know Your Customer (KYC) process.

Information collected for AML includes:

- Politically Exposed Persons (PEP)

- EP Risk Rating

terms.name: Member Asset Fees
terms.definition: An asset-based fee is a percentage fee based on the member's  assets under management.

terms.name: Member Assets Amount
terms.definition:
The member assets amount refers to the total value of assets held by an individual or a couple, which can impact eligibility for certain benefits, such as the Age Pension in Australia. The limits vary based on factors like homeownership and relationship status. For example:

Homeowners:

  • Single: Up to $314,000 for a full pension; $697,000 for a part pension.
  • Couple (combined): Up to $470,000 for a full pension; $1,047,500 for a part pension.

No-Homeowners:

  • Single: Up to $566,000 for a full pension; $949,000 for a part pension.
  • Couple (combined): Up to $722,000 for a full pension; $1,299,500 for a part pension.

terms.name: Member Assets Amount
terms.definition:
Field required to be reported on APRA SRS 606.0 (Table 4) APRA SRS 606.1 (Table 2).

It is the aggregation of the value of the assets which back multiple member's benefits grouped by the superannuation product, investment menu and investment option (the grain of the table).

For MSF, this has been implemented by apportioning the value (net asset value from the unit registry Unison) of each fund to the APRA member segment by using member balance as the basis for apportioning. It has been observed that NAV is never equal to the sum of all balances. The apportioning only considers valid combinations of product, investment menu and investment option which is controlled by the business through the 605 'linkages' file. This is not the same as how field 'Members Benefits Fees And Costs Arrangement Amount' has been implemented for the APRA SRS 605.0 (Table 4). This is not the same approach to how field 'Members Benefits Demographic Amount' and 'Members Benefits Demographic Amount' has been implemented on the APRA SRS 611 (Table 1 and Table 2 respectively).

terms.name: Member ATO TFN List
terms.definition: This is a report obtainable from the ATO providing a member / TFN matching service

terms.name: Member Audit History
terms.definition: Audit History, also referred an Audit Trail is a full history of changes applied to a particualr Account record that allows the viewer to see which users applied changes, at what particular date/time and the actual changes applied.

terms.name: Member Basic Details
terms.definition:
A Member is a person who -
1) Is a member of a Superannuation Fund ; or 
2) Is a employee who works for an Employer/Business that has a Employer/Business Super Account with a Superannuation Fund.
Although the Employer is our main Client,  it's employees have individual Member Accounts.
The basic details include the Member's  name, address, contact detais, Account status and other personal Account details .

terms.name: Member Benefit Schedule
terms.definition:
A member benefit schedule is a document or summary that outlines the benefits available to members of a specific program, organization, or plan. It typically includes details such as:

  • Types of Benefits: Lists the services, coverage, or perks provided
  • Eligibility Criteria: Specifies who qualifies for each benefit
  • Limits and Maximums: Indicates any caps on usage or coverage.
  • Cost Sharing: Details member responsibilities like copayments or deductibles.

terms.name: Member Cash Holdings
terms.definition:
Cash holdings are member's funds that are held in their Cash Account (i.e. Cash at Bank) as opposed to their Investment options.
Cash holdings are accessible  anytime by the Investor, and may also be accessed by the registry platform to cover fees and maintain Investment and Cash balances in line with the Client's Investment Authority.

terms.name: Member Category
terms.definition:
See Employer Benefit Category.
This is the default Employer Benefit Category automatically assigned to the Member, based on Fund/Divison.

terms.name: Member Class
terms.definition:
Member class tells us the style of account the member has -

  • Full administration – General accumulation account – SG, Rollovers, Member Vol, Salary Sacrifice, Emp Vol contributions are accepted.
  • Insurance only Member – This account is set-up for insurance purposes only, the employer has an arrangement with us and has agreed to set requirements for us to administer this style of account. The only contribution accepted is Employer Voluntary  - this is to pay for the insurance and account fees.
  • Pensioner Member - these account types also have restrictions i.e not being able to accept contributions etc…
  • Beneficiary Pensioner

terms.name: Member Consent Transfer
terms.definition: A member consent transfer is where an employer appoints a new default fund. However, existing members need to provide their consent to have existing assets transfer to the new fund.

terms.name: Member Contact Details
terms.definition: This is the preferred Phone/Fax/Email to use, should the meber need to be contacted.

terms.name: Member Contribution Deposits
terms.definition:
A generic term covering all deposits made by Employers or members into investment Accounts. Can include;

  • Employer Super Guarantee contributions to members
  • Additional Employer Contributions
  • Member voulntary contribiutions

terms.name: Member Contribution Eligibility Register
terms.definition:
Includes -
- Basic Details
- Transaction Details
- First / Follow Up Letter Details
Members must meet age and other criteria to be eligible to make certain contribution types (e.g. Spouse contributions)
The Contribution Eligibilty register tracks follow processes to confirm eligibility for these  these contributions.

terms.name: Member Contribution Statement
terms.definition: A document or report that outlines the contributions made by a member towards a specific fund or program, such as superannuation funds, retirement accounts, or health insurance plans. It typically includes details like the amount contributed, the frequency of contributions, and any employer contributions or matching contributions.

terms.name: Member Contributions
terms.definition: Contributions to a superannuation fund made by a member of a fund and excluding employer contributions and salary sacrifice contributions.

terms.name: Member Count
terms.definition: The count of all active members at a particular point in time. A member is generally defined as the single owner of the contract or account.

terms.name: Member Deductions
terms.definition: Includes admin fees, policy fee, Adviser Service fee, and any other fees, insurance premium deductions.

terms.name: Member Electronic Communication Preferences
terms.definition: This indicates whether the member prefers to receive regulatory documents via electronic means (i.e. to an email Account), as opposed to standard mail. Applies to - - Benefit statements - Transaction confirmations - Event notices

terms.name: Member Family Account
terms.definition: Members linked  to a member Family Account will receive the same discounted fees and, choice of investments, easy online access, personalised service, and the option of applying for tailored insurance through their own Personal Super Account.

terms.name: Member Fee Basis
terms.definition:
Fee basis means the payment of an agreed sum for hosting/providing an employer's/member's Account. The agreement fee structure is usually detailed in the Product Disclosure Document.

Fees may consist of:

  • Member Fees
  • Admin fees
  • Expense Recovery Fees
  • Transaction Fees
  • Recovery Fees
  • Account Keeping Fees
  • Advice Fees

terms.name: Member Fees
terms.definition: Member Fees are  a pre-defined dollar fee expressed as an annual amount that is charged to the member for being part of the fund.

terms.name: Member Fraud Lock
terms.definition:
Secret Passphrase to protect Account
If the Client requests extra security procedures because they believe they ar at risk of Identity Fraud, the Fraud Lock passphrase facility can be used to capture a passphrase. This must then be provided by the Client before information can be passed to the Client or any transactions undertaken on their behalf.

terms.name: Member ICLN
terms.definition: This is the Orion prior system client/member  number for those accounts that transferred from the Orion platform.

terms.name: Member Income Protection
terms.definition: A type of Insurance, providing a regluar income to the member  if they are  unable to work because of an illness or injury.

terms.name: Member Incomplete
terms.definition: Set to True when not all essential member details have been captured for the account to become active.

terms.name: Member Know Your Customer(KYC)
terms.definition:
A generic term to collectively refer to the Know Your Customer (KYC) information  that has been collected.
A reporting entity must apply customer identification procedures to all  customers, as part of the  Anti-money Laundering (AML) and Countering terroism Funding (CTM) procedures, 
and must be completed before providing any designated services to them.

terms.name: Member Lapse Exclusions
terms.definition:
Relevant to Protect Your Super legislation, in which insurance premiums must  cease when a member's  Account balance falls below a predefined balance and/or  other conditions are triggered, and the insurance policy is lapsed. 
The member can choose to advise that their premiums continue and that they be excluded from this requirement. Such exclusions are recorded in this regisetr.

terms.full_name:
Member Lost Contact Details
(Lost Member Details)
terms.name:
Member Lost Contact Details
(Lost Member Details)
terms.definition:
A generic term that includes all details captured in maintaining the member's  regulatory  Lost Member status.A member is considered Lost  when their mail is returned a predefined number of times, and/or their address details are not held.
details includes-
- Last Contribution Date
- Missing Client Information
- Returned mail Details
- ATO Addres Verified

terms.name: Member Mandated Contribution (Post Tax)
terms.definition: The allowable levels of Member Mandated (Compulsory) Member contributions required, which partially fund defined benefit entitlements, where paid on a Post-Tax basis.

terms.name: Member Mandated Contribution (Pre tax)
terms.definition: The allowable levels of Member Mandated (Compulsory) Member contributions required, which partially fund defined benefit entitlements, where paid on a Pre-Tax (Salary Sacrifice) basis.

terms.name: Member MySuper Details
terms.definition:
BPay Customer Reference Information is a collection of individual elements as follows -
MySuper Status (MySuper/Transition/Choice)
Effective Date
Reason (Force/Elected)

terms.name: Member New Business - Incomplete Applications
terms.definition: A register of applications for new Business that are still pending, yet to complete the Quality Control/Authorisations

terms.name: Member Number
terms.definition:
The registered name of the Member's Employer that will be printed/reported on all correspondence.

In Workplace Super terms, in other words, it's the account number of the member it belongs to.

terms.name: Member Occupation
terms.definition: The member's Occupation for the purposes of determining their Rating for Insurance purposes.

terms.name: Member Office
terms.definition:
The MO advocates for members and supports the Registrable Superannuation Entity Licensee (RSELs) to discharge their fiduciary and regulatory obligations.
The primary role of the MO on behalf of the RSELs, is to challenge, oversight and monitor the service providers, provide guidance, and advocate for best outcomes for members, including members best financial interests.

terms.name: Member opt-out
terms.definition: A member opt-out refers to a situation where an individual chooses not to participate in a program, service, or agreement that they would otherwise be included in by default. This is commonly seen in areas like insurance, subscriptions, and workplace benefits

terms.name: Member Outcomes Assessment
terms.definition: The Member Outcomes Assessment is a measure of product appropriateness against key factors that can affect a superannuation product.

terms.name: Member Partial Transfers
terms.definition: As per Member Transfers, however not the entire balance of an account's funds have to be transferred.

terms.name: Member Pension  -Bank Account Details
terms.definition: This is the nominated bank Account that the pension is to be credited to

terms.name: Member Pension Calculations -  Reversionary Type
terms.definition:
Reversionary pensions are an alternative to payment of a death benefit.
With a reversionary pension, the benefit is paid as a pension to the  beneficiary.  Calculations Include -
- Period From/To
- Assets
- Annual Review Pension Calculation
- Pension Payment Schedule

terms.full_name: Member Pension Calculations - Claim Tax Free Threshold
terms.name: Member Pension Calculations - Claim Tax Free Threshold
terms.definition: (Applicable to pensions subject to Income Tax) The tax free threshold is a cap on how much someone can earn during the financial year before paying tax.

terms.name: Member Pension Calculations - Frequency
terms.definition: This is the nominated frequency that a customer wished to receive a pension payment. Options may vary between product, but may be one of Yearly, Half yearly, Quarterly, Monthly or Fortnightly.

terms.name: Member Pension Calculations - Indexation Rate
terms.definition: Related to the Indexation Type, this is the nominated percentage or dollar amount of the increase if percentage or dolloar amount is selected.,

terms.name: Member Pension Calculations - Indexation Type
terms.definition:
Related to the nominated Annual Payment, the customer can nominte whther the amount can be indexed for periodical increases (varies between products) -
- none
- nominated percentage
nominated amout
- CPI

terms.full_name: Member Pension Calculations - Net or Gross
terms.name: Member Pension Calculations - Net or Gross
terms.definition: Related to the Nominated Annual Payment, a customer can nominate whether the payment amount entered is either Gross (before tax and other deductions) or net (after tax and other deductions).

terms.full_name: Member Pension Calculations - Nominated Annual Income
terms.name: Member Pension Calculations - Nominated Annual Income
terms.definition: Annual nominated income is the amount  the customer wants to receive as regular payments in the financial year.

terms.full_name: Member Pension Calculations - Pension Level Required
terms.name: Member Pension Calculations - Pension Level Required
terms.definition:
Income level is the amount of pension that the a customer wishes to receive. For superannuation Accounts minimum and maximums are defined -
Minimum  income level - depending on the customer's age, this is the minimum amount that customer can withdraw from their pension Account for each financial year.
Maximum income level -  maximum income amount that customer has nominated to withdraw from their pension Account each financial year. Applicable to Compass (TTR only) and Capsil products.
For Accounts in the pre-retirement phase , a customer  they can only withdraw up to a pre-defined percentage of their balance each year as income payments.

terms.name: Member Pension Calculations - Purchase/Renewal Price
terms.definition: The amount calculated or requested to fund the pensioner's initial account balance.

terms.full_name: Member Pension Calculations - Schedule Payment Date
terms.name: Member Pension Calculations - Schedule Payment Date
terms.definition: related to the pension Calculation Payment Frequency, this is actual date of month/day of week that the payment will be made.

terms.full_name: Member Pension Calculations - Spike or Smooth
terms.name: Member Pension Calculations - Spike or Smooth
terms.definition:
Used when a change is applied to the annual pension payment amount. Changing to Smooth Remaining Payments will adjust the members remaining pension payments to ensure that they receive the requested amount for the entire year, by calcualting the  required increase for each future payment remaining for the financial year.
Changing to Spike will increase the the next payment only, as a backdated adjustment to achieve the required annual pension payment amount .

terms.name: Member Pension Details
terms.definition: See Member Pension Register

terms.name:
Member Pension Register  
(Member Pension Details)
terms.definition: A generic term representing a collection of elements required to be completed when a member transitions to / opens a Super pension.

terms.name: Member Pension Register  Current Status
terms.definition: ??Need to know all possible values. Activated and Commuted are two

terms.full_name: Member Pension Register - Pension Start Date
terms.name: Member Pension Register - Pension Start Date
terms.definition: When a Client was registered for their first actual pension payment

terms.name: Member Postal Address
terms.definition:
The recorded physical address that the member lives -
- Street Address
- Suburb
- State
- Postcode
- Country
- DPID

terms.name: Member Small and Insoluble Members
terms.definition:
Terms within the the Lost members and Unclaimed Monies definitions of Superannuation -
- Small Lost  Member Accounts have balances less than (as at June, 2024) $6,000.
- Insoluble Lost  Member Accounts  have been inactive for (as at June, 2024) 5 years or more .
Accounts have insufficient records to identify/trace  the owner of the Account.

terms.name: Member Status Details
terms.definition:
Member Incomplete
Locked Member
Skip Income Accrual
Mail Annual Reports (is this related to Member Electronic Communication Preferences?)
Family Law
Date of Retirement
Date of Death
Exit Details

terms.name: Member Street Address
terms.definition:
The recorded physical address that the member lives -
- Street Address
- Suburb
- State
- Postcode
- Country
- DPID

terms.name: Member SuperTick
terms.definition: Super funds use the ATO  SuperTICK service to validate an individual's tax file number (TFN) and accompanying identity information. These details are matched with the ATO Client register.

terms.name: Member SuperTick Postal Address
terms.definition:
A generic term representing a  collection of Member details used to validate a member via the the ATO SuperTick service -
- Address Lines 1 and 2
- Suburb
- State
- Postcode
- Country

terms.name: Member SuperTick Status
terms.definition: SuperTICK is a service used by superannuation funds in Australia to validate a member's Tax File Number (TFN) and identity details with the Australian Taxation Office (ATO). It helps ensure accurate processing of SuperStream rollovers and contributions once a member’s details are verified

terms.full_name: Member Transfer Register List - Incomplete Transactions
terms.name: Member Transfer Register List - Incomplete Transactions
terms.definition:
(In Registers| Transfer Register List)
Set to True when not all essential  details have been captured for a  Pension account for it to  become active.

terms.name: Member Transfers
terms.definition:
A generic term for when members can transfer funds between accounts or into / out of their current fund. Examples --
- Inward/outward rollovers
- Pension transfers
- Employer to persoanl account transfers

terms.name: Member Voluntary
terms.definition: Personal super contribution (AKA MV) are the amounts you contribute to your super fund from your after-tax income (that is, from your take-hom pay).

terms.name: Members Benefits Demographic Amount
terms.definition:
The members benefits demographic amount refers to the total value or financial allocation of benefits provided to members of a specific group, often influenced by demographic factors. These factors may include age, income level, employment status, geographic location, and family size.

In contexts such as insurance, pension funds, or healthcare programs, demographic data helps determine eligibility, coverage levels, and benefit distribution. For instance, in a retirement plan, older members may receive higher payouts based on years of contribution, while income-based benefits in healthcare programs may vary depending on socioeconomic status.

terms.full_name: Members Benefits Fees And Costs Arrangement Amount
terms.name: Members Benefits Fees And Costs Arrangement Amount
terms.definition:
Field required to be reported on APRA SRS 605 (Table 4).

It is the aggregation of multiple member's benefits by Fee and Cost Arrangement (the grain of the table).

For MSF, this has been implemented as the sum of all holdings balances (units x unit price), all non-unitised investments and any other type of benefit accrued by the member (eg. defined benefit). For accrued benefits such as DB, the most recent valuation on or before the reporting date is used; the timing of this form being run for the 30 June submission and when valuations are produced and transmitted by source (SuperB) means the most up to date numbers may be missing from the report. This is not the same as how field 'Member Assets Amount' has been implemented for the APRA SRS 606.0 (Table 4) and APRA SRS 606.1 (Table 2). This is the same approach to how field 'Members Benefits Demographic Amount' and 'Members Benefits Demographic Amount' has been implemented on the APRA SRS 611 (Table 1 and Table 2 respectively) with different grouping and filtering per the requirements of that form.

terms.name: Members Benefits MySuper Demographic Amount
terms.definition:
MySuper is a type of superannuation product in Australia designed to provide simple, cost-effective retirement savings. Here's a breakdown of the terms

  • Member Benefits: These include features like lower fees, straightforward investment options, and lifecycle strategies that adjust investments based on the member's age.
  • Demographics: This refers to the characteristics of MySuper members, such as age, gender, income levels, and employment sectors, which help funds tailor their offerings
  • Amount: This typically relates to the account balances or contributions made by members, reflecting their savings within the MySuper framework

terms.name: Members Best Interests
terms.definition: Super fund trustees are required to act and make decisions in the ‘best interests’ of fund members. APRA can impose penalties on trustees breaching this duty.

terms.name: Members’ benefit bracket
terms.definition:
Represents a segmentation of data based on the liability for member benefits owing to defined contribution members and defined benefit members. Excludes: unallocated contributions.

In the context of superannuation, members may fall into different benefit brackets based on their account type, contribution levels, or pension entitlements. These brackets can determine how benefits are calculated, including minimum pension payments and tax implications

terms.name: Memorandum of Transfer
terms.definition: MOT is a document provided when the account has been opened, it is required when transferring the ownership of the account.

terms.name: Merger
terms.definition: Two companies merge into one.

terms.name: Metadata
terms.definition: Information that describes or explains other data or information, or the Information Asset. Metadata is a broad term that can include technical metadata such as the structure of tables and columns in a database, through to consumer metadata such as Confidentiality, an author’s name, or the business meaning of a field in a report.

terms.name: Middle Name
terms.definition: Middle name of person.

terms.name: Min Hours per week
terms.definition: Specifies the minimum number of hours required to be worked per week for the type of employee if applicable.

terms.name: Minimum Age
terms.definition: The minimum age at which a member can receive default cover under the plan

terms.name: Minimum benefits
terms.definition:
Represents a member's minimum benefit that is required to be paid, where minimum benefit has the meaning given in SIS Regulations, r. 5.04.

Reference link: https://www.legislation.gov.au/F1996B00580/2019-08-10/text

terms.name: Minimum Pension Payment
terms.definition:
OneAnswer Frontier Pension gives members the flexibility to choose how much you would like your pension payment to be, subject to a minimum limit set by the government. The minimum annual pension payment is first calculated when a member start their pension and is recalculated each year on 1 July using your account balance and age at that date.

If a member choose to receive the minimum annual pension payment, we will make pension payments from 1 July, at the new minimum level. If the nominated annual pension payment is already higher than the new minimum, your pension payment will remain unchanged from that paid in the previous financial year

terms.name: Minimum Pension Payment Rules
terms.definition: Any rules on the minimum pension payments over the life of the pension.

terms.name: Miro
terms.definition: The application used for project management and wall boarding across Insignia Financial

terms.name: Miscellaneous Suspense
terms.definition: "Miscellaneous suspense" often refers to a temporary accounting category used to hold transactions or amounts that are not yet classified under a specific account. This term is commonly used in financial contexts, where funds or entries are placed in a suspense account until their proper allocation is determined.

terms.name: ML/TF Risk
terms.definition: The risk that the Insignia Financial DBG may reasonably face of its products or services (whether inadvertently or otherwise) being used to facilitate money laundering or the financing of terrorism.

terms.name: Mobile
terms.definition: The mobile number  for when  contacting the  Employer -

terms.name: Module
terms.definition:
A module is a functional unit within a larger system.
Risk Atlas (Protecht.ERM) is made up of several modules, including Compliance, KRIs, and Risk Analytics. Each module serves specific risk management processes.

terms.name: Monday webinar
terms.definition: Weekly all staff zoom webinar hosted by CEO Renato Mota, to provide business updates and insights. Guest speakers are invited to attend each week to present on a range of topics.

terms.name: Money Laundering
terms.definition: The process to disguise the origins of illegally obtained money.

terms.name: Money Laundering Reporting Officer
terms.definition: The person appointed as the ‘AML/CTF Compliance Officer’ for the Insignia Financial DBG.

terms.name: Mortgage Investment Corporation (MIC)
terms.definition: A Mortgage Investment Corporation (MIC) is an investment fund that pools capital from investors to lend borrowers in the form of private mortgages.

terms.name: Motor Vehicle
terms.definition: This is used in novated leave

terms.name: MQU
terms.definition: Request For Information - Do

terms.name: MultiSeries
terms.definition:
A range of multi-manager funds which blend actively managed and passive investments to reduce fees.
Each diversified trust provides access to a blend of different asset classes through a combination of 
professionally selected passive, factor-based and active investment managers

terms.name: Mutual Life Citizens
terms.definition: Generally not used in the long form any more

terms.name: Mutually Reactivated Task
terms.definition: A process or activity that has been paused or deferred and later resumed collaboratively by two or more parties. It often arises in teamwork or project management contexts, where all involved parties agree to revive and continue the task together.

terms.name: MyGov
terms.definition: myGov is a secure way to access government services online in one place.

terms.name: MyGovID
terms.definition: myGovID is application software designed to allow users to authenticate with Australian Government websites and services.

terms.name: MyInsignia
terms.definition: Refers to the SuccessFactors Application (Human Resource Management Software)

terms.name: MyIOOF
terms.definition: Refers to the SuccessFactors Application (Human Resource Management Software)

terms.name: MySuper
terms.definition: MySuper funds act as a default account for clients who don’t choose their own super fund when they start a new job. Applicable for Workplace Super products only.

terms.name: MySuper
terms.definition: MySuper is the default investment option for members who have not made an investment choice. It applies to employer division members without an investment selection or personal division members who transferred without making a choice.

terms.name: MySuper Glide Path Review
terms.definition:
The Mysuper Glide Path Review involves an ongoing periodic review of members' MySuper balance and future investment strategy to ensure their holdings and strategy are correct against the pre-determined glide path. The Review aims to:

- a member's MySuper future investment strategy is correct for their age;

- a member's Mysuper balance holding is correct for their age;

- validate where a member has had a change in Date of Birth (DOB) their MySuper balance has updated accordingly.

terms.full_name: MySuper interest as a result of member investment choice
terms.name: MySuper interest as a result of member investment choice
terms.definition:
Represents a MySuper interest where the member exercised choice of investment option to direct any portion of their interest into the MySuper product. A direction may be given as an express investment instruction, a completed application form or a completed member investment choice form.


Reference: SIS Act, s.10(1). Reference link: https://www.legislation.gov.au/C2004A04633/latest/text

terms.full_name: MySuper interest not as a result of member investment choice
terms.name: MySuper interest not as a result of member investment choice
terms.definition: Refers to a situation where a member's superannuation contributions are invested in a default MySuper product because the member did not actively choose an investment option.

terms.name: MySuper members’ benefit bracket
terms.definition: Represents a segmentation of data based on the liability for member benefits owing to defined contribution members. Excludes: defined benefits members’ benefits and unallocated contributions

terms.name: Name & Code Change
terms.definition: Both the companies name and ASX code changed (i.e. from BHP to BHB)

terms.name: Name Change
terms.definition: The company name changed however ASX remains the same.

terms.name: National Call Centre
terms.definition: A national call center is a centralized facility or department that handles a high volume of telephone calls, often for customer service, support, or sales purposes. These centers can operate within a single country and cater to nationwide inquiries, providing services such as resolving issues, answering questions, or offering information about products and services.

terms.name: National Customer Service Manager
terms.definition: A National Customer Service Manager is responsible for overseeing customer service operations across all regions or branches within a company. Their primary role is to ensure consistent, high-quality service delivery, drive customer satisfaction, and maintain brand reputation. They typically manage large teams, develop customer service strategies, implement best practices, analyze performance data, and address escalated issues at a national level. Additionally, they often play a crucial role in aligning customer service goals with broader organizational objectives.

terms.name: National Employment Standards
terms.definition: The National Employment Standards (NES) are the minimum employment entitlements that have to be provided to all employees.

terms.name: National Investment Trusts
terms.definition: A National Investment Trust is typically a government-established financial institution that manages funds on behalf of citizens, organizations, or other entities, investing them in various assets like stocks, bonds, or property. The goal is often to generate returns while supporting economic development or strategic industries. Specific structures and purposes vary by country, and these trusts may operate under different names

terms.name: National Taxable Contributions
terms.definition: This is related to defined benefits.

terms.name: Net Asset Value
terms.definition: Value of an asset minus fees and taxes

terms.full_name: Net assets available for member benefits (net of ORFR reserves)
terms.name: Net assets available for member benefits (net of ORFR reserves)
terms.definition:
Net assets available for member benefits refer to the total assets held by a superannuation fund that are available to meet members' retirement benefits. This figure is calculated by subtracting liabilities (such as operational costs and reserves) from the fund's total assets.

In Australia, AASB 1056 Superannuation Entities outlines financial reporting requirements for superannuation funds, including how net assets attributable to members should be disclosed. These assets typically include:

  • Investments: Stocks, bonds, property, and other financial instruments.
  • Cash holdings: Liquid funds available for immediate use.
  • Receivables: Contributions owed but not yet received.

terms.name: Net funds
terms.definition: Net funds generally refer to the total amount of money remaining after subtracting liabilities or expenses from the total assets or income. It provides a clear picture of the available financial resources that can be used or invested without obligations tying them down.

terms.name: Net Investment Returns
terms.definition: Net Investment Returns is the return a super fund delivers to clients less administration fees, investment fees and costs, and taxes.

terms.name: Net New Clients
terms.definition: Number of new advice clients less lost, deceased & disengaged clients

terms.name: Net Promoter Score
terms.definition: Net Promoter or Net Promoter Score is the percentage of customers rating their likelihood to recommend a company, a product, or a service to a friend or colleague.

terms.name: Net revenue
terms.definition: Gross revenue less direct costs (such as referral fees) - used in the Advice context

terms.name: Netflows
terms.definition: Netflows is the sum of Inflows and Outflows

terms.name: New Application - Checking MKIS
terms.definition: The application for a new product and service under MKIS

terms.name: New Application - Do
terms.definition: The application for a new product and service

terms.name: New Business
terms.definition: Internal use only. AKA New Applications.

terms.name: New Business Governance Forum
terms.definition: The purpose of the NBGF is to provide advice to assist the GM Workplace Super, or other accountable person, with the decision-making process for new business arrangements and changes to existing business arrangements.

terms.name: New Business Rework
terms.definition:
New Business Rework refers to the process of modifying or correcting aspects of a newly established business, product, or service to meet required standards or improve functionality. This can involve adjusting strategies, refining processes, or fixing errors that arise during the initial setup phase.

In a business context, rework often occurs when:

  • A product or service does not meet quality expectations and requires adjustments.
  • Marketing materials or branding need revisions to align with customer feedback.
  • Operational workflows need optimization to enhance efficiency

Rework can be costly, but it is sometimes necessary to ensure a business meets industry standards and customer expectations

terms.name: New Business Rework - Check
terms.definition: The assessment of the process of revisiting, revising, or refining elements of a business that are still in the early or developing stages. It often occurs when the initial strategies, plans, or outputs don’t meet expectations or require adjustment due to unforeseen challenges.

terms.name: New Business Waiting
terms.definition: In a business context, "new business waiting" typically refers to the period of time between a business's initial launch and when it achieves a consistent stream of revenue and/or market presence. It's the phase where a startup or new venture is in its formative stages, often requiring patience and strategic planning to build a sustainable foundation.

terms.name: New Investor - MKIS - Incomplete
terms.definition: The application for a new investor under MKIS

terms.name: New member account: Employer sponsor
terms.definition: Represents a superannuation account created for a new member where contributions are made by an employer, under a formal arrangement.

terms.full_name: New member account: Member, tax free phase
terms.name: New member account: Member, tax free phase
terms.definition:
A superannuation account that has entered the retirement phase, where earnings and withdrawals are tax-free, subject to certain conditions.

Here's what it means:

1. New Member Account

This simply indicates that a new superannuation account has been created for a member—often due to:

  • A rollover from another fund,
  • Commencement of a pension (retirement phase),
  • Or joining a new super fund.

2. Tax-Free Phase

This refers to the retirement phase of superannuation, where:

  • Investment earnings on the balance are tax-free.
  • Withdrawals (either as lump sums or income streams) are tax-free if the member is aged 60 or over and meets a condition of release (e.g., retirement).

3. Tax-Free Component

Within a member’s super balance, the tax-free component includes:

  • Non-concessional (after-tax) contributions,
  • Certain government co-contributions,
  • And amounts rolled over from other tax-free sources

terms.full_name: New member account: Member, taxed phase
terms.name: New member account: Member, taxed phase
terms.definition:
A new member account in the taxed phase refers to a superannuation account that is in the accumulation phase, where contributions and earnings are taxed at concessional rates. In Australia, most superannuation accounts start in this phase before transitioning to the retirement phase, where earnings become tax-free.

Key aspects of a taxed-phase member account:

  • Contributions: Employer and personal contributions are taxed at 15%, unless exceeding contribution caps.
  • Investment earnings: Earnings within the fund are taxed at 15%, with capital gains taxed at 10% if assets are held for over a year.
  • Withdrawals: Benefits withdrawn before reaching preservation age may be taxed, depending on the type of withdrawal.

terms.name: New Payments Platform
terms.definition: The New Payments Platform is a channel for payments which allow real time settlement between Australian bank accounts, being introduced to the entire banking industry.

terms.name: New position
terms.definition: When the need arises to create a headcount that does not exist currently

terms.name: Next Step
terms.definition: Next Step

terms.name: Nil Entry Fee
terms.definition: Note this is now closed to new business within OneAnswer products. This product used to offer two different fee payment options. The customer would have selected an option when they opened the account. One option was to select to pay the contribution fees upfront at the time when you make each investment into the managed investment scheme - this is referred as Entry Fee. The other option was to pay contribution fees later (i.e. on withdrawal of their investment or by way of other increased fees) - this is referred to as Nil Entry Fee.

terms.name: No Change of Beneficial Ownership
terms.definition: Situations where the underlying ownership of an asset, property, or entity remains the same, even if there are changes in its legal title or structure. Beneficial ownership means the person or entity that ultimately benefits from or controls the asset, regardless of whose name it is legally registered under.

terms.name: No Further Action Required
terms.definition: "No further action required" is a phrase commonly used to indicate that a particular process, review, or task has been completed and no additional steps are necessary. It signifies closure and confirms that everything has been satisfactorily addressed or resolved.

terms.name: Nominated Member Bank Account
terms.definition:
Nominated Member Bank Account
Contribution Payment Information
BPay Customer Reference Information

terms.name: Nomination of Beneficiary
terms.definition: A nomination of beneficiary is the act of designating a person or entity to receive benefits, assets, or funds in the event of the nominator's passing or under specific circumstances. This is commonly used in insurance policies, retirement funds, wills, and financial accounts to ensure that assets are distributed according to the individual's wishes.

terms.name: Non Binding Death Nomination
terms.definition:
A Non-Binding Death Nomination is a directive given by a superannuation fund member that expresses their preference for how their superannuation benefits should be distributed upon their death. Unlike a Binding Death Nomination, which legally obligates the trustee to follow the member’s instructions, a non-binding nomination serves as a guideline rather than a strict requirement.

With a non-binding nomination, the trustee retains discretion and may consider factors such as the member’s relationships, financial dependents, and circumstances at the time of death before making a final decision on the distribution of funds. This approach allows for flexibility but may not guarantee that the benefits will be allocated exactly as the member intended.

terms.name: Non financial goals
terms.definition: Category of performance goal

terms.name: Non-billable/Offshore
terms.definition: Employee Sub group to represent: Those we do not pay them and only managing the account or they are a replacement without a fee - Offshore

terms.name: Non-billable/Onshore
terms.definition: Employee Sub group to represent: Those we do not pay them and only managing the account or they are a replacement without a fee - onshore

terms.name: Non-binding Death Benefit Nomination
terms.definition: A non-binding nomination is a nomination of preferred dependants that may assist the trustee in determining who should receive the benefit upon the member’s death. The trustee is not bound by this nomination.

terms.name: Non-concessional
terms.definition: A non-concessional contribution is made from after-tax earnings. It may appear as a negative amount if the member has claimed a tax deduction on a voluntary contribution.

terms.name: Non-Concessional Contributions
terms.definition: Non-concessional contributions include: contributions you or your employer makes from your after-tax income. contributions your spouse makes to your super fund, unless. your spouse is contributing for you as your employer.

terms.name: Non-Concessional Contributions Cap
terms.definition: Super only

terms.name: Non-lapsing Binding Death Benefit Nomination
terms.definition: A non-lapsing binding nomination is a written instruction that does not expire and binds the trustee to pay the benefit to nominated beneficiaries.

terms.name: Non-lapsing nomination
terms.definition:
A non-lapsing nomination in the context of the OneAnswer Frontier Personal Super product is a type of beneficiary nomination that does not expire, ensuring that members superannuation benefits are paid according to their wishes upon death, unless they choose to change or revoke the nomination.

For OneAnswer Frontier Personal Super, a member can make a non-lapsing nomination. If you provide us with a valid non-lapsing nomination, the Trustee must pay your death benefit to the beneficiaries you have nominated and in such proportions as you have specified, provided:

  • each nominated beneficiary(ies) is a dependant or is your legal personal representative at the time of your death
  • you have not married, entered a de facto or like relationship with a person of either sex, or permanently separated from your spouse or partner since making your nomination
  • your non-lapsing nomination is in writing and two persons aged 18 years or over who are not nominated beneficiaries have witnessed you signing your nomination. Your nomination must not be defective.

terms.name: Non-paid
terms.definition: Employee Sub group to represent: Employee Sub Group for bots

terms.name: Non-Renounceable Rights Issue
terms.definition: A company gives shareholders the 'right' to buy new shares directly to the company, and shareholders cannot sell this 'right' on the stock market

terms.name: Non-Resident
terms.definition:
To be regarded as a non-resident of Australia, an individual must satisfy the following tests -

1. Resides test: the individual does not reside, or live, in Australia.

2. Permanent place of abode test: the individual has established a permanent place of abode outside of Australia.

terms.name: Normal Retirement Age
terms.definition: The basis for ceasing age for calculation of future membership/service.

terms.name: Normal Retirement Date
terms.definition: Date at which a member is normally entitled to an unreduced pension under the terms of the plan.

terms.name: Normal Retirement Multiple
terms.definition: Multiple used to calculate the Normal Retirement/Death benefit of a Defined benefit member

terms.name: Not Achieved
terms.definition: Insignia Financial Goal Rating

terms.name: Not For Profit
terms.definition:
A not-for-profit organization is one that operates primarily for purposes other than making a profit. Its main goal is to serve a cause, community, or public interest, rather than generating financial gain for its owners or members. Any surplus funds are typically reinvested back into the organization's mission and activities, rather than being distributed as profits.

Common examples of not-for-profits include charities, educational institutions, religious organizations, and community groups. They may have tax-exempt status in many countries, depending on their purpose and compliance with local laws.

terms.name: Notice
terms.definition: Payment in Lieu of Notice - the notice period that not worked

terms.name: Notice of Intent Claim
terms.definition:
A Notice of Intent to Claim is a formal declaration stating that an individual or entity plans to file a claim for a specific benefit, compensation, or legal entitlement. This notice is often used in various contexts, such as:

  • Tax and Superannuation Claims – In Australia, individuals can submit a Notice of Intent to Claim to deduct personal super contributions
  • Veterans Affairs Claims – In the U.S., veterans can file a Notice of Intent to File a Claim for compensation, pension, or survivor benefits.
  • Legal and Insurance Claims – Some legal or insurance processes require a formal notice before officially filing a claim

terms.name: Notifiable event
terms.definition:
Represents a notifiable event within the meaning given in SIS Regulations, r. 9.06(1).
Reference link: https://www.legislation.gov.au/F1996B00580/2019-08-10/text

terms.name: Notional Contribution
terms.definition: A notional contribution is a contribution where no financial transaction is applied (i.e. no cash or assets purchased).

terms.name: Notional Fee
terms.definition: Fees calculated for the purpose of calculating a benefit, but no actual fee is payable. (e.g. Expense Calculation for DB Minimum Requisite Benefit).

terms.name: Number of Withdrawal Singnatures
terms.definition:
The number of signatories required for a redemption.
Values include, but are not limited to:
0 - unknown
1 - default.

terms.name: Occupation
terms.definition: Defines which occupation the rate relates to when the rates are separated by occupation.

terms.name: Occupation Risk Category
terms.definition: The level of risk in your job determines how much your insurance premiums cost. Examples of these are blue collar, white collar, professional, etc.

terms.name: Ocupation Risk Category
terms.definition: The level of risk in your job determines how much your insurance premiums cost. Examples of these are blue collar, white collar, professional, etc.

terms.name: Off Sale
terms.definition: Legacy product that is longer available for new customers.

terms.name: Off-market Buy Back
terms.definition: A company want to buy back shares from their shareholders, outside the share market.

terms.name: Office Annualised Premium
terms.definition: An historic P&I measure, OAP is the Insurance equivalent of FUA, being an Inforce snapshot of annualised premium either for Retail, MasterTrust and Group Insurance.

terms.name: Office of Responsible Entity
terms.definition: The ORE assists and supports the Responsible Entities (RE)/ Service Operators of IDPS in ensuring they comply and fulfil their fiduciary and regulatory obligations in relation to operating the Schemes and IDPSs.

terms.name: Office of State revenue
terms.definition: Office of State revenue -  where we pay payroll tax

terms.name: Onboarding
terms.definition: Process to onboard/start a new employee into the organisation

terms.name: One Off Direct Debit Request
terms.definition: A one-off direct debit request is a payment arrangement where a single, non-recurring payment is authorized to be taken directly from a payer's bank account. Unlike regular direct debits, which are recurring, this type of request is used for one-time payments, such as settling an invoice or making a donation. It offers convenience and security, as the transaction is covered by the Direct Debit Guarantee.

terms.name: One-off Adviser Service Fee
terms.definition: A One-off ASF is a one-off payment to an adviser which is applied to new money invested in an account.

terms.name: One-Off Pension Payment
terms.definition:
A one-off pension payment refers to a single, lump-sum withdrawal from a pension or retirement fund rather than receiving regular ongoing payments. This type of payment is typically used when retirees or pension holders need immediate access to a portion or all of their retirement savings for a specific purpose.

Depending on the country and pension scheme, one-off pension payments may have tax implications or affect future entitlements. Some pension plans allow partial lump-sum withdrawals while maintaining regular pension income, whereas others may require full withdrawal, ending the pension arrangement.

terms.name: OneAnswer
terms.definition: This is a OnePath product we provide and service. Products are; OA Allocation Pension (EF & NEF), OA Allocated Personal Super (EF & NEF), OA Allocation Pension (EF & NEF), OA Investment Portfolio (AKA OA Trust), OA Frontier Personal Super, OA Frontier Pension & OA Frontier Investment Portfolio.

terms.name: OneCare
terms.definition: OneCare is a rearrange of insurance covers - this product is now administered by Zurich.

terms.name: OnePath
terms.definition: OnePath is a financial services provider in Australia, offering a range of products such as life insurance, income protection, and superannuation solutions. It is designed to help individuals and families manage their financial security and plan for the future. OnePath is known for its flexible and tailored insurance options, catering to various life stages and needs.

terms.name: OnePath Custodians
terms.definition: OnePath Custodians Pty Limited is a trustee and fund manager in Australia, specializing in superannuation and investment services. They hold an RSE (Registrable Superannuation Entity) license and manage various superannuation funds and investment portfolios. OnePath is also known for its insurance products, offering financial protection to individuals and families.

terms.name: OnePath Funds Management
terms.definition: OnePath offers a selection of funds managed by specialist managers who are experts in their particular asset classes.

terms.name: OnePath Growth Index
terms.definition:
The fund seeks to track the weighted average return of the 
various indices of the asset classes in which the fund invests, in 
proportion to the strategic asset allocation (SAA) for the fund, 
before taking into account fees, expenses, and tax

The fund is suitable for investors seeking a balance between 
income and capital growth through passive exposure to a 
diversified portfolio of assets and who are prepared to accept 
some variability of returns

terms.name: OnePath Life
terms.definition:
OnePath Life is a life insurance provider in Australia, offering a range of products including Life Cover, Total and Permanent Disability (TPD) Cover, Trauma Cover, and Income Protection. These policies help individuals and families manage financial risks associated with unexpected life events.

OnePath Life is part of Zurich Australia Limited, which acquired OnePath Life from ANZ in 2019. Their insurance solutions are designed to provide financial security in case of serious illness, injury, or death.

terms.name: Ongoing Adviser Service Fee
terms.definition: An on-going ASF is an ongoing fee payment made monthly to an adviser.

terms.name: Ongoing Customer Due Diligence
terms.definition: Monitoring customer activity and transactions that take place on an on-going and consistent basis throughout a client relationship. The intention is to identify where a customer’s actions are inconsistent with what might be expected of a customer of that type, given what is known about their business, risk and profile. May also include updating the identification and verification documentation held on a customer.

terms.name: Operational Management and Insights
terms.definition:
OMI stands for Operational Management and Insights and is a series of Tableau reports which show the operational efficiency, trustee claims and regulatory compliance across IFL. The data is sourced from the product registry systems and is transferred by around 40 ETL processes and this powers around 30 dashboards and 100 reports within Tableau.

This suite of reports are managed by the Business Intelligence team and are used across IFL, so being able to harvest lineage from these reports allows them to understand where the data is coming from, where it's being transformed and to identify any potential issues that could form future Data Quality objectives. It also allows us to identify where CDEs are being impacted and ensure proper governance is being applied

Knowledge base article: https://hq.ioof.com.au/display/DENG/OMI+Knowledge+Base

terms.name: Operational Risk Financial Requirement Reserve
terms.definition:
The Operational Risk Financial Requirement (ORFR) Reserve is a financial buffer that superannuation funds in Australia must maintain to cover potential losses arising from operational risks. It ensures that funds can manage unexpected events like system failures, fraud, or administrative errors without negatively impacting members' benefits.

Under Prudential Standard SPS 114, issued by the Australian Prudential Regulation Authority (APRA), superannuation trustees are required to:

  • Establish and maintain an ORFR strategy.
  • Hold financial resources to meet the ORFR target amount.
  • Use the reserve only for addressing operational risk events.

The ORFR is typically set at 0.25% of funds under management (FUM), but funds may adjust this based on their risk profile

terms.name: Operative Date

terms.name: Opportunity Canvas
terms.definition:
The Opportunity Canvas is a requirement for all Change the Business initiatives per the Tiered Governance Model.


Please ensure initiatives are raised through the Enterprise Front Door prior to creating/completing an Opportunity Canvas.

https://hq.ioof.com.au/display/DELIVERY/%5BTEMPLATE%5D+Opportunity+Canvas

terms.name: Ordinary Time Earnings
terms.definition: the amount you pay employees for their ordinary hours of work, including things like commissions and shift loadings

terms.name: Organisation Design

terms.name: Other Fees and Costs
terms.definition: Other fees and charges that may be charged if the client requests a service not currently offered in the PDS. The Super Fund will agree any additional fee with the client before providing the service.

terms.full_name: Other Fees And Costs (performance component type) / (fees and costs component type)
terms.name: Other Fees And Costs (performance component type) / (fees and costs component type)
terms.definition:
Means fees and costs that other than the available options. Other Fees And Costs must be reported gross of:
(i) income tax and GST, after being reduced by any reduced inputs tax credits; and
(ii) stamp duty; and
without adjustment in relation to:
(i) any tax deduction available to the responsible entity, operator or trustee; or

terms.name: Other Flows
terms.definition: Other flows represents flows that do not make up Inflows, Outflows, Pension Payments. For example, transactions such as Switches, Distribution Payments & Reinvestments, Fees, Insurance Premiums etc.

terms.name: Other Offset Accounts
terms.definition: Defines any accounts other than additional member accounts which impact the calculation of a member's sum insured.

terms.name: OU Level 1
terms.definition: OU Level 1 refers to the introductory level of study at the Open University (OU), typically for undergraduate students. It provides a broad foundation in a chosen subject area, focusing on essential skills and knowledge needed for further study. These modules are designed to help students transition into higher education and develop study skills for success in their academic journey.

terms.name: OU Level 2
terms.definition: OU Level 2 refers to the intermediate stage of undergraduate study at the Open University (OU). At this level, students dive deeper into their chosen subject, building upon the foundational knowledge gained at Level 1. The modules are more specialized and often require critical thinking and independent learning, preparing students for advanced study at Level 3.

terms.name: Our Work Lives
terms.definition: Daily email sent by Frank Lombardo to all of Client and Technology. This includes important topics like news updates, wins and employee recognition.

terms.name: Oustanding Requirements
terms.definition: The term "outstanding requirements" generally refers to tasks, conditions, or obligations that have not yet been fulfilled or completed. It might mean specific documentation or actions that are still pending to meet a particular requirement. In project management, it could refer to unmet criteria or deliverables necessary to move forward.

terms.name: Out of Cycle Review
terms.definition: A review that occurs outside of the review cycle, triggered by internal and/or external events such as updates to regulations, internal feedback or improvements identified by audits.

terms.name: Out of Force
terms.definition: "Out of force" generally refers to something that is no longer in effect or no longer valid. It can be used in legal contexts, such as when a law, regulation, or contract is no longer enforceable, or in general conversation to indicate that something has lost its influence or power

terms.name: Out performance incentive
terms.definition: These are long term incentive plans for specific employees within Asset management who are managing portfolios for long term investment of clients funds.  If their investment strategy significantly outperforms expectations over a 3 year period these employees are eligible for a small portion of the additional value they create

terms.name: Outflows
terms.definition: Outflows represent sum of withdrawals/redemptions, Death & TPD payments from superannuation, pension and investment accounts. This results in a decrease to the member account balances.

terms.name: Overseas Cover
terms.definition: Cover provided while overseas.

terms.name: Overtime
terms.definition: Overtime

terms.name: Owner of the role
terms.definition: Incumbent is the permanent position holder of the position

terms.name: P Card
terms.definition: Corporate credit cards that are issued to staff for business approved procurement

terms.name: Part Time
terms.definition: Any job or persons hour that require a work of less than 37.5/38/40 hours per week.

terms.full_name: Part VIIIB of the Family Law Act
terms.name: Part VIIIB of the Family Law Act
terms.definition: Legislation governing the splitting of a superannuation benefit.

terms.name: Partial Disability benefit
terms.definition: If you’re Totally Disabled for a period of 14 consecutive days or more, and then return to work in a reduced capacity, earning a reduced income, you may receive a Partial Disability benefit.

terms.name: Partially Achieved
terms.definition: Insignia Financial Goal Rating

terms.name: Participation Agreeement
terms.definition: The primary section of the Participation Agreement which contains the rules which apply to a category of members.

terms.name: Pat Leave
terms.definition: Parental Leave take during child birth for the father.

terms.name: Paternity Leave
terms.definition: Parental Leave take during child birth for the father.

terms.name: Pay As You Earn
terms.definition:
"Pay As You Earn" (PAYE) is a method of income tax collection used in several countries, where employers deduct income tax directly from employees' wages or salaries before paying them. This ensures that taxes are paid on an ongoing basis rather than at the end of the financial year. The PAYE system simplifies tax collection and helps individuals avoid large lump-sum payments when filing their tax returns.

The specifics can vary depending on the country—for example, in Australia, employers withhold income tax under the PAYG (Pay As You Go) system.

terms.name: Pay As You Go
terms.definition: AKA Payment Summary or Group Certificate. This is the withholding obligations in respect of income stream and lump sum payments from super, pension and life products. This involves: correct calculation of the amount of tax to be withheld, remittance of the tax withheld to the ATO in the required time frame and provision of relevant documentation to clients in correct formate i.e. PAYG Payment Summaries.

terms.name: Pay mix
terms.definition: Is how different attributes of pay are used to determine a total figure - for the general population this is base salary + SGC to make up TFR.  For more senior employees,  this can be the makeup of Base salary + Super + LTI + other benefits (Where they exist) to provide a total reward figure

terms.name: Pay-as-you-Go Withholding
terms.definition: PAYG withholding on behalf of employees.

terms.name: Payee
terms.definition: A payee is a party in an exchange of goods or services who receives payment. The payee is paid by cash, check, or another transfer medium by a payer. The payer receives goods or services in return.

terms.name: Payment Date
terms.definition: The date of the pension payments.

terms.name: Payment in Lieu of Notice
terms.definition: Payment in Lieu of Notice - the notice period that not worked

terms.name: Payment Instruction Form
terms.definition: A payment instruction form is typically used to provide details for processing payments, such as account information, payment amount, and payment method. These forms are commonly used in business transactions, tax payments, or personal financial arrangements

terms.name: Payment Phase
terms.definition: A condition of release has been met, and the member has given the Trustee a written instruction to pay the benefit.

terms.name: Payment Reference Number
terms.definition:
The Payment Reference Number (PRN) is a unique identifier used to match the electronic payment of the rollover amount with the corresponding data message sent via SuperStream. It ensures that the receiving fund can correctly reconcile the payment with the member's rollover request.

Why is the PRN important?


When rolling over superannuation benefits (e.g., from an SMSF to another super fund or vice versa), the process involves two components:

An electronic data message sent via SuperStream, which includes member and fund details.
A separate electronic funds transfer (EFT) for the actual money.

The PRN must be included in the EFT transaction reference, and it must match the PRN in the data message. This ensures the receiving fund can identify and process the payment correctly.

If the PRN is incorrect or missing, the rollover may fail or be delayed, and the transaction could be flagged for non-compliance with SuperStream standards.

terms.name: Payment Refund
terms.definition: A payment refund refers to the process of returning money to a customer or payer, typically when they are dissatisfied with a product or service, or when an overpayment has occurred. Refunds can also apply to taxes, where overpaid amounts are reimbursed by a government authority.

terms.name: Payment Split Notification
terms.definition: A written confirmation by the Trustee to the parties in the divorce on the split payment details.

terms.name: Payment Suspension
terms.definition: The Payment Suspension process is to be followed when an investment becomes an illiquid investment or the fair value of the assets of an investment option cannot be determined.

terms.name: Payroll
terms.definition: Module in Success Factor used to run the payroll

terms.name: Payroll Application
terms.definition: Module in Success Factor used to run the payroll

terms.name: Payroll Tax
terms.definition: Payroll tax

terms.name: Pended in progress
terms.definition: "Pended in progress" often refers to a status in administrative, legal, or project management contexts. It indicates that an action or decision is still underway and awaiting completion or approval. This status can apply in scenarios like claims processing, applications, or project tasks.

terms.name: Pension
terms.definition:
A pension refers broadly to any retirement income stream or arrangement designed to provide financial support during retirement. It typically involves periodic payments made to a member after retirement and may continue for the rest of their life. Upon the member’s death, payments may be made to any eligible beneficiary.


This term encompasses various types of retirement income products, including:

  • Defined Benefit Pension
  • Account-Based Pension
  • Term Allocated Pension
  • Transition to Retirement Pension
  • Annuities

Key Characteristics:

  • Funded by accumulated superannuation or other retirement savings.
  • Provides monthly, annual, or other periodic payments.
  • Payments may be fixed or variable depending on the pension type.

terms.name: Pension & Investments
terms.definition: P&I which derived from the ANZ Wealth business, it was purchased by IOOF and is now known as P&I heritage within Insignia.

terms.name: Pension Benefit
terms.definition: The total monthly, annual or other periodic amounts that start being paid to a member or former member at retirement and that continue for the rest of his or her life. When the member or former member dies, these payments would be made to any other person who may be entitled to receive them.

terms.name: Pension benefit
terms.definition:
A pension benefit refers to the regular payments made to individuals after retirement, typically from a superannuation fund, government pension scheme, or employer-sponsored plan. These benefits provide financial security for retirees and can be structured in different ways:

  • Defined Benefit Pension: Payments are based on a formula considering factors like salary history and years of service. These pensions offer predictable income but are becoming less common in private sector employment.
  • Account-Based Pension: Funds are drawn from a superannuation account, with payments varying based on investment performance and withdrawal rates.
  • Government Age Pension: Provided by the government to eligible retirees based on income and asset tests.


Excludes: transfers, including rollovers and successor fund transfers, within the superannuation system.
Reference: SIS Regulations, Divisions 6.2 and 6.3. Reference link: https://www.legislation.gov.au/F1996B00580/2019-08-10/text

terms.name: Pension Class
terms.definition:
Pension Accounts in ASIS are identified by their membership class and/or their Division.
Pension classes consist of -
- Retirement Phase Pension (RPP) 
- Transition to Retirement Pension (TTR)

terms.name: Pension Frequency
terms.definition: The frequency of the pension payments.

terms.name: Pension Payments
terms.definition: Represents retirement pensions or pre-retirement / transition to retirement (TTR) pension payments made to members. A pension is to provide members, who have met the eligible condition of release (e.g. reached retirement phase or preservation age), a regular income stream instead of a lump sum. Note, not part of Outflows.

terms.name: Pension Registered On Date
terms.definition: The date that the pension commenced

terms.name: Pension Start Date
terms.definition: The start date of the pension.

terms.name: Pension Type
terms.definition: Type of pension.

terms.name: Pensions Loans Scheme
terms.definition:
The Pension Loans Scheme (PLS) is a program provided by the Australian government. It allows eligible individuals, typically retirees, to access a portion of their home equity as a loan to supplement their retirement income. It's similar to a reverse mortgage and is designed to help those on a pension who need additional financial support.

Under this scheme:
