  • The loan is secured against the individual's property.
  • Payments are made fortnightly, like pension payments.
  • Interest accrues on the loan until it's repaid, usually from the sale of the property.

It's an option for retirees who may need extra funds while retaining ownership of their home.

terms.name: People & Culture
terms.definition: used to refer to the People and Culture Division

terms.name: People and Culture Studio Dashboard
terms.definition: The project tracker and workflow management system for our in house design studio. This is where we submit requests for design work to be completed, see work in progress and communicate with the Studio team.

terms.name: People Leader
terms.definition: Anyone who has a direct line report under then.

terms.name: People Leader HQ
terms.definition: A SharePoint site hosting information to help leaders at Insignia lead their teams.

terms.name: People Leader Hub
terms.definition: A SharePoint site hosting information to help leaders at Insignia lead their teams.

terms.name: PEP Indicator
terms.definition: A PEP Indicator refers to a marker used to identify a Politically Exposed Person (PEP). PEPs are individuals who hold or have held prominent public positions, such as government officials, judges, or military leaders. Due to their influence, they are considered higher-risk customers in financial transactions, as they may be more susceptible to corruption or money laundering. Financial institutions use PEP Indicators as part of their Know Your Customer (KYC) and Anti-Money Laundering (AML) processes to ensure compliance and manage risks effectively

terms.name: Per Annum
terms.definition: Something that happens every year

terms.name: Percentiles
terms.definition: A value on a scale of one hundred that indicates the percent of a distribution that is equal to or below it. a percentile score of 95 is a score equal to or better than 95 percent of the scores

terms.name: Performance and Rewards
terms.definition: Department within People & Culture responsible for the Performance and Reward streams of work across the organisation.

terms.name: Performance component type
terms.definition:
Components of performance are classified into the following performance component types:

  • Indirect Costs
  • Indirect Cost Ratio
  • Fees Deducted Directly From Member Account
  • Other Fees And Costs
  • Tax; and
  • Gross Investment Return.

terms.name: Performance Fees
terms.definition: Performance fees are fees paid to investment managers when their performance exceeds a specified level. Different performance fees may apply to different investment managers and performance fees vary depending on each investment manager's performance.

terms.name: Performance Linked (investment charge type)
terms.definition: Means the portion of the expense or benefit which is determined in whole or in part by reference to the performance of an investment made by an investment manager on behalf of the RSE licensee.

terms.name: Performance rights
terms.definition: Performance rights are an offer that gives the employee the entitlement to be issued shares in a company subject to the satisfaction of a set of pre-determined criteria,  such as company performance, tenure etc;

terms.name: Permanent Employment
terms.definition: A permanent employee is someone who works on an ongoing basis. This means that their job will continue until they (or their employer) decides to end their employment.

terms.name: Personal Information
terms.definition:
The Australian Privacy Act 1988 defines personal information as:

Information or an opinion about an identified individual, or an individual who is reasonably identifiable:

a. whether the information or opinion is true or not; and

b. whether the information or opinion is recorded in a material form or not.

Personal information does not include information about corporations or similar entities, except where, for example, the name of the entity includes the name of an identifiable person.

See the Privacy Act 1988, the Office of the Australian Information Commissioner’s website, and Insignia’s Group Privacy Standard for more definitions and information.

See also Sensitive Personal Information.

terms.name: Personal Member (Employer Super)
terms.definition: A member who's account was created under an Employer Plan and then leaves the original employer - it is transferred to the Personal Division. It was mostly related to the Corporate Super, ANZ Super Advantage & Integra Super products within OnePath.

terms.name: Personal Super
terms.definition: Personal super refers to a superannuation (retirement savings) account that is managed by an individual rather than through an employer. It allows individuals to choose their own super fund, investment options, and contribution levels to suit their financial goals and retirement plans. This type of superannuation provides flexibility and control over how retirement savings are managed.

terms.name: Personal Super Calculator
terms.definition: A tool or calculator to designed for members who are saving for their retirement and whose financial needs are straightforward.

terms.name: Phone Screening
terms.definition: One of the method of selection during the recruitment process

terms.name: Physical Information
terms.definition: All information stored on media not part of a computer system or computer network. Examples include printed documents, handwritten notes on paper, microfiche, physical artwork, compact disks and USB drives. See also Electronic Information.

terms.name: Pillar
terms.definition: Insignia Financial strategic Pillar

terms.name: Plan Actuary
terms.definition: The organisation providing actuarial services for the plan.

terms.name: Plan Code
terms.definition: A plan code is a unique identifier used to classify or specify a particular plan, policy, or program. It is commonly used in administrative systems, such as insurance, payroll, or retirement plans, to distinguish between different types of coverage, benefits, or payment structures.

terms.name: Plan Commencement Date
terms.definition: Date that the plan commenced with the fund. This includes the period they first joined the Plum Superannuation Fund if prior to the MLC Super Fund SFT in July 2016. This does not include any period prior to joining the Plum Superannuation Fund where they were administered by Plum but as a stand alone client.

terms.name: Plan for Life
terms.definition: Plan For Life is an external research house that provides consolidated views and analytics of Australian and New Zealand retail and wholesale financial services, investment, retirement and life insurance industries.

terms.name: Plan Management Fee
terms.definition: Plan management fees are charges associated with the employer or client funding or insurance plan.

terms.name: Plan Name
terms.definition: The plan name as defined in the participation agreement

terms.name: Plan Operating Manual
terms.definition: Internal only - A POM provided important information on the procedures of a specific employer plan. It is an internal document that outlines information and processes for members employed with the company. It is specific to the ANZ Smart Choice Employer product.

terms.name: Plan Summary
terms.definition:
For each new business, the Employer Plan summary is generated for the Employer and outlines key components of the policy agreement between the Fund privider and the employer -

- Fees

- Insurance offerings

- Online access information

terms.name: Platform
terms.definition: a platform is a group of products under a single umbrella - for example eXpand and Insignia Essential.  A platform may be either an administration platform (eXpand and Insignia Essential) where assets are administered on behalf of clients or non-platform business (TAS) where assets are managed directly for clients.

terms.name: Platform Channel
terms.definition: A grouping of products into Workplace, Advised and Personal. E.g. Employer Super products are grouped under Workplace.

terms.name: Platform Commission Basis Details
terms.definition: Related to Member Insurance Benefits, the Commission Basis details the surrounding commission being applied to the insurance component. The three options for Commission Basis available are Level, High Upfront and Hybrid.

terms.name: Platform Details
terms.definition:
Includes the Platform Code and Platform Name.
The Platform Code is used to differentiate the type of account. Examples are -
- IDP (Investor Directed Portfolio Services)
- IPS (IOOF Portfolio Service)
- ERF (Eligible Rollover Fund)

terms.name: Platform FUM
terms.definition: Platform Funds Under Management (Platform FUM) refers to the total value of assets managed through investment platforms. These platforms are typically used by financial advisors, wealth managers, or individual investors to access a range of investment products, such as mutual funds, ETFs, or managed accounts. Platform FUM is a key metric for assessing the scale and performance of these platforms within the financial services industry

terms.name: PMIF Reinstatement
terms.definition: The period the member can reinstate their cover by applying in writing within (x) days of cover ceasing.

terms.name: Point of Sale
terms.definition: Not to be confused with Channel, POS usually refers to the method of the initial sale made by the member. E.g. Internet, Call Centre

terms.name: Policy Start Date
terms.definition: The date that the insurance policy commenced from.

terms.name: Politically Exposed Person
terms.definition: An individual who holds or previously held a prominent public position or role in a government body or international organisation, either in Australia or overseas. Immediate family members and/or close associates of these individuals are also considered PEPs. i.e. directors, deputy directors and members of the board or equivalent functions.

terms.name: Pooled Superannuation Trust
terms.definition: A type of investment vehicle regulated under the Superannuation Industry (Supervision) Act 1993 in Australia. It allows superannuation funds, approved deposit funds, and other eligible entities to pool their investments together for efficient management and potentially better returns. PSTs are not open to individual investors; they are specifically designed for institutional investors.

terms.name: Portfolio Holding Disclosure
terms.definition:
The Government introduced legislation to provide additional transparency about investments to superannuation fund members.

You can invest your super or pension in a range of investments including term deposits, direct shares and managed investments. Our products offer managed Investments which are managed by entities related to Insignia Financial Limited (formerly IOOF Holdings Limited) and several external investment managers.

Portfolio Holdings Disclosure is about additional transparency for the managed investments offered by us (related investment). For these related investments, we show the assets that sit within that investment option.

The information is updated every 6 months and is available on our Portfolio Holdings Disclosure page.

terms.name: Portfolio Management Fee
terms.definition: Portfolio Management Fee - the fee payable to the Managed Discretionary Account (MDA) operator, for services in managing your investments as agreed to separately in your MDA Investor Agreement.

terms.name: Portfolio Online
terms.definition: An online portfolio is a digital collection of your work, achievements, and skills, often showcased on a website or platform. It serves as a visual and interactive representation of your professional or creative abilities, making it easier to share your expertise with potential employers, clients, or collaborators. Online portfolios are commonly used by artists, designers, writers, and other professionals to highlight their projects and accomplishments.

terms.name: Portfolio Order Manager
terms.definition: A Portfolio Order Manager is a person that handles both portfolio management and order management functionalities. They help streamline workflows for investment managers by integrating tasks like portfolio modeling, order creation, compliance checks, and real-time tracking. These processes are widely used in the financial industry to enhance efficiency and reduce errors

terms.name: Position Description
terms.definition: Often interchanged with role description -  However from an SAP architecture perspective,  a position is unique,  and therefore a position description is unique to the particular position (where as a job description can be used in a more broad sense)  E.g.  the Job description of client solutions specialists could be used for 50 positions doing the same thing.  However the position description for Client solution specialist - ESL support,  may have a specific extra requirement for the incumbent to speak a particular second language

terms.name: Post-30 June 1983 Component
terms.definition: The part of the eligible termination payment (ETP) which relates to service after 30 June 1983. For those aged 55 and above, it is subject to a tax-free threshold, above which tax rates are higher than those applying to most other components in an ETP.

terms.name: Post-Capitalisation
terms.definition: Post-capitalisation processes are run after the annual bonus has been declared. The process will pay outstanding bonus amount to clients.

terms.name: Postal Address
terms.definition: The employee's postal/contact  address (could be a post office box, for example)

terms.name: Power of Attorney
terms.definition: A power of attorney is a deed authorising a person or persons (attorney/s) to act for another (the donor), usually when the doner is overseas or incapacitated.

terms.name: Pre-1 July 1983 Component
terms.definition: The part of an eligible termination payment which relates to service before July 1983. Only 5% of this component is assessable to the taxpayer as income where the payment was made up to 30 June 2007.

terms.name: Pre-existing Condition
terms.definition: A PEC is defined as any ailment, illness or condition where in the opinion of a medical adviser appointed by the health insurer, the signs or symptoms of that illness, ailment or condition that may have an impact on insurance premiums and claims. The PEC waiting period applies to new members and members upgrading their policy to any higher level benefits under the new policy.

terms.name: Prefer Name
terms.definition: Alias or preferred name for person.

terms.name: Premium Basis
terms.definition: The basis for calculating the insurance premiums charged to the member for the insurance cover type that is held.

terms.name: Premium Notes
terms.definition: Any additional notes that apply to the insurance premiums that have not been captured in the existing related items.

terms.name: Premium Rate Factor
terms.definition: An additional premium factor applied the premium rate.

terms.name: Preservation
terms.definition: The legal requirement that certain superannuation benefits be maintained either in as superannuation fund, approved deposit fund (ADF) or retirement savings account (RSA) until permanent retirement after the fund member reaches preservation age.

terms.name: Preservation Age
terms.definition: The age at which the fund member can gain access to preserved benefits which have built up in a superannuation fund, approved deposit fund (ADF) or retirement savings account (RSA) provided the fund member has permanently retired from the workforce.

terms.name: Preserved Amount
terms.definition: Represents the portion of a member’s superannuation balance that is preserved, meaning it cannot be accessed until the member meets a legislated condition of release (e.g., retirement, reaching preservation age). This classification is critical for compliance with SIS Act rules and ensures correct benefit payment eligibility.

terms.name: Preserved Amount
terms.definition: The preserved amount is the portion of the member’s superannuation benefit that cannot be accessed until a condition of release is met.

terms.name: Primary Citizen
terms.definition: The main or primary country of citizenship, often the country of birth or the country where a person has lived and been a citizen for the longest period.

terms.name: Prime Minister
terms.definition: The Prime Minister of Australis

terms.name: Principal  Employer Name
terms.definition: The Employer Name of the primary employer sponsor for a plan

terms.name: Principal Employer
terms.definition: The primary employer sponsor for the plan.

terms.name: Principal Employer Code
terms.definition: The Employer Code of the primary employer sponsor for a plan

terms.name: Principal Employer's ABN
terms.definition: The primary employer's ABN.

terms.name: Prioity Offer
terms.definition: company want to raise money, allowing their shareholders abd the public to buy additional shares however its shareholders are given priority over the general public.

terms.name: Prior Comparable Period
terms.definition:
Prior Comparable Period (PCP) refers to a previous time frame used as a benchmark for comparing financial or operational performance. Typically, it corresponds to the same period in a prior year (e.g., Q1 2024 vs. Q1 2023) to account for seasonality and cyclical trends.

This comparison helps businesses assess growth, identify trends, and evaluate the impact of strategic decisions over time. It is commonly used in financial reporting, budgeting, and performance analysis.

terms.name: Priority Plans
terms.definition: These are the top employer plans within ANZ Smart Choice Employer product.

terms.name: Private Wealth Adviser
terms.definition: Financial planner in Shadforth business

terms.name: Probationary period
terms.definition: 6 months from an employee original start date

terms.full_name: Problem Logging System - obsolete technology system.
terms.name: Problem Logging System - obsolete technology system.
terms.definition:
Many organizations struggle with outdated logging systems that can lead to inefficiencies, security vulnerabilities, and compatibility issues.

Here are some key risks of using obsolete technology in problem logging systems:

  • Slow Performance: Older systems may lag, making issue tracking inefficient
  • Security Vulnerabilities: Outdated software is more prone to cyber threats.
  • High Maintenance Costs: Legacy systems often require expensive upkeep
  • Limited Integration: Older logging systems may not work well with modern tools.

terms.name: Procedural Fairness
terms.definition: As required under the Family Law provisions, a Trustee must be given the opportunity to review its position in relation to an Order or Agreement as to whether it can meet the requirements on a Trustee.

terms.name: Process Engineer
terms.definition: A Process Engineer focuses on designing, analyzing, and optimizing industrial processes to improve efficiency, productivity, and safety. Their role typically involves tasks such as developing process improvements, troubleshooting issues, implementing new technologies, and ensuring compliance with safety and environmental regulations.

terms.name: Product
terms.definition: A product is anything that has a Product Disclosure Statement [PDS] (whether or not the PDS is current), for example eXpand Super, Insignia Essential Pension, Supersaver.

terms.name: Product Disclosure Roll
terms.definition: A document that financial service providers must provide to you when they recommend or offer a financial product. It must include information about the product's key features, fees, commissions, benefits, risks and the complaints handling procedure.

terms.name: Product Disclosure Statement
terms.definition: A PDS is a document that financial service providers must provide to the client when they recommend or offer a financial product. It must include information about the product's key features, fees, commissions (if any), benefits, risks and the complaints handling procedure.

terms.name: Product Reference Database
terms.definition: Internal Product web based tool which stores Plum plan details, fees, benefit design, investments and insurance.

terms.name: Product Transfers, Product Swaps
terms.definition:
Product Transfers: This typically refers to the process of moving a product, along with its associated rights, responsibilities, or manufacturing processes, from one entity, location, or system to another.

Product Swaps: In finance, a product swap is a derivative contract where two parties exchange the cash flows or liabilities of two different financial instruments. For instance, an interest rate swap involves exchanging fixed-rate payments for floating-rate payments based on a benchmark interest rate. These swaps are often used for hedging or speculative purposes

terms.name: Production
terms.definition: Is an historic P&I measure used for reporting Insurance New Business premium based on selected movement types over an annualised period. Not to be confused with an Application environment.

terms.name: Production Environment
terms.definition:
A technology environment (a collection of technology components used to implement an information system) that has been designed, and is managed and secured, for its intended use on a business-as-usual basis.

This can be contrasted with non-production environments that are intended to allow replicas of production information systems to be instantiated, often for either technology development or testing purposes. Production environments are considered appropriate places to safely store the information they are intended to manage in support of normal business activities.

terms.name: Protect Your Super
terms.definition: PYS changes relate mostly to fees and inactive accounts. It included the removal of all exit fees, limiting administration and investment fees on accounts below $6,000 and cancellation of insurance for accounts that are inactive.

terms.name: Public Information Release Process
terms.definition: PIRP is the due diligence process for the preparation, review, approval, and public release of regulated and non-regulated communications to customers

terms.name: Purchase Amount Limit
terms.definition: Limit till which the created user can purchase

terms.name: Purchase order management
terms.definition: Purchase Order Management is the control and coordination of the entire lifecycle of purchase orders. This includes creating, reviewing , updating, approving and tracking purchase orders to ensure timely and accurate procurement of goods or services while maintaining transparency and accountability throughout the purchasing process.

terms.name: Putting Members' Interests First
terms.definition: Putting Members' Interests First (PMIF), aims to protect clients with low balances (under $6,000) from being reduced by insurance arrangements which might not be right for them.

terms.name: PYS Reinstatement
terms.definition: The period the member can reinstate their cover by applying in writing within (x) days of cover ceasing.

terms.name: Qualifying Recognised Overseas Pension Scheme
terms.definition: AKA ROPS - Recognised Overseas Pension Scheme

terms.name: Quality Check ADD - MKIS
terms.definition:
Quality check refers to the process of inspecting and evaluating a product, service, or process to ensure it meets predefined standards. This can involve testing materials, verifying assembly accuracy, assessing performance, and checking compliance with regulations.

In finance, quality checks can involve verifying the accuracy of transactions, account values, and financial reports.

terms.name: Quality Check New Business
terms.definition: A quality check for new business refers to the process of evaluating and verifying the standards, procedures, and outcomes of a newly established business or its operations. This involves assessing whether the business meets regulatory requirements, adheres to industry standards, and delivers products or services that align with customer expectations. Quality checks can include reviewing processes, testing products, and ensuring compliance with legal and ethical guidelines.

terms.name: Quality Check Release of Funds
terms.definition:
A quality check release of funds refers to the process of verifying that all necessary conditions, compliance requirements, and quality control measures have been met before disbursing funds. This is commonly used in financial transactions, lending, and project funding to ensure that the recipient meets predefined criteria

For example, in mortgage lending, lenders conduct post-closing quality control reviews to assess the accuracy and integrity of loan documentation before releasing funds. These reviews help mitigate risks and ensure compliance with regulations.

terms.name: Quality Check Switch - MKIS
terms.definition:
A quality check switch typically refers to a control mechanism or process used to verify the quality, accuracy, or functionality of a product, system, or service. It can apply to various industries:

  • Manufacturing: A physical quality check switch ensures products meet standards before moving to the next stage.
  • Software Development: A toggle or setting that activates extra verification steps in a system or software.
  • Process Management: A point in a workflow where a manual or automated review occurs to maintain high-quality output.

terms.name: Quality Development Coordinator
terms.definition:
A Quality Development Coordinator is typically responsible for overseeing and enhancing quality standards within an organization. Their role often involves:

  • Developing and implementing quality control systems to ensure consistent product or service quality.
  • Monitoring and auditing processes to detect deviations from quality standards.
  • Training staff on quality practices and promoting a culture of excellence
  • Collaborating with teams to improve production procedures and customer satisfaction
  • Ensuring compliance with industry regulations and standards

This position requires strong analytical skills, attention to detail, and the ability to work across departments. It can be found in various industries, including manufacturing, healthcare, and education

terms.name: Quality Review
terms.definition: A quality review is a systematic evaluation process used to assess the quality of a product, service, or system. It involves examining whether the outputs meet predefined standards, requirements, or objectives. Quality reviews are often conducted by teams or individuals with expertise in the relevant area, and they can be applied in various fields, such as project management, manufacturing, or healthcare.

terms.name: Quick Reference Guide
terms.definition: A quick reference guide is a compact, easy-to-use resource that provides essential information at a glance. It's designed to help users quickly find key details about a topic, process, or tool without reading lengthy instructions or manuals. These guides often include step-by-step instructions, key definitions, shortcuts, or diagrams to make learning or troubleshooting more efficient

terms.name: Rate End date
terms.definition: Specifies the last date the rate is applicable in calculations.

terms.name: Rate Name
terms.definition: The Name of the rate being described.

terms.name: Rate of Return
terms.definition: The Rate of Return simply shows the percentage movement of the unit price over the financial year, less fees and taxes (net earnings).

terms.name: Reactivation Accounts
terms.definition:
Closed Accounts that are reopened, typically if additional funds are received for the Account after closure, such as income distributions from Investment Funds.
Accounts can also be reopened if an outward rollover is returned.
See also Residual Balance

terms.name: Reasonable Benefit Limit
terms.definition:
The Reasonable Benefit Limit (RBL) was a system used in Australia to cap the amount of superannuation benefits that could receive concessional tax treatment. It was designed to ensure that retirement savings were used for their intended purpose rather than excessive tax advantages.

However, the RBL was abolished in 2007, allowing individuals to accumulate superannuation savings without a tax-imposed cap. Before its removal, benefits exceeding the RBL were taxed at the highest personal tax rate.

terms.name: Reasonable Benefit Limits
terms.definition: The Reasonable Benefit Limit (RBL) is the maximum amount of retirement and termination of employment benefits that a person can receive over their life time at concessional tax rates.

terms.name: Rebalancing
terms.definition: The re-aligning of your investment mix back in line with its original investment strategy.

terms.name: Recognised Overseas Pension Scheme
terms.definition: AKA QROPS - Qualifying Recognised Overseas Pension Scheme

terms.name: Recognition incentive
terms.definition: Industry term for a monetary inducement  offered to an employee as a recognition that they hold a specific set of skills/capabilities which hold value to the business over and above general role accountabilities

terms.name: Reconciliation Action Plan
terms.definition:
A Reconciliation Action Plan (RAP) is a strategic framework developed by an organization to promote reconciliation between Indigenous and non-Indigenous communities. It outlines specific actions, commitments, and goals aimed at fostering respectful relationships, creating equitable opportunities, and acknowledging the cultural contributions of Indigenous peoples.

RAPs are widely used in Australia and are often guided by Reconciliation Australia, a non-profit organization that supports efforts toward reconciliation. These plans typically focus on areas such as cultural awareness, employment opportunities, partnerships, and policy improvements to enhance inclusivity.

terms.name: Record
terms.definition: Information created or received and maintained as evidence of a business transaction or activities undertaken in satisfaction of legal obligations.  Records may have many forms such as documents, spreadsheets, email messages, images and voice recordings, or as entries in a computer database.

terms.name: Record Date
terms.definition: A date set by the company's board of directors. it refers to the date by which investors \ shareholders must be on the company's books if they want to participate in corporate actions.

terms.name: Record of Advice
terms.definition: A Record of Advice (ROA) is a document used in financial services to summarize personal financial advice provided to a client. It is typically used when the advice given is not significantly different from previous advice documented in a Statement of Advice (SOA). The ROA outlines the basis of the advice, the client's relevant circumstances, and any recommendations made. It serves as a simplified record compared to an SOA and is often used for ongoing advice scenarios.

terms.name: RecordPoint
terms.definition: RecordPoint is IFL’s solution for the management of records in place. It supports the scanning, classification, and disposal of unstructured data held across all of IFL’s information repositories. As part of the classification process, it also provides a registry of what information is stored and where. This permits the construction of reports and action plans for IFL’s information storage and management and to develop strategies to ensure that the risk associated with that information is managed correctly.

terms.name: Records Management
terms.definition:
People, processes and technologies related to ensuring that Records are reliably created or received, that they form an authentic and reliable record of the events and activities that they purport to record, and that they are managed throughout their lifecycle through to archival and disposal in accordance with Insignia policies and regulatory obligations.

Records management is a subset of Information Management, specifically concerned with managing information making up records and record collections.

terms.name: Recruiter
terms.definition: Talent Acquisition Partner who is aligned to a specific division or client group to support recruitment activity & talent needs

terms.name: Recruitment
terms.definition: Recruitment team

terms.name: Recruitment Agency
terms.definition: An external party that sources candidates for job vacancies on behalf of Insignia Financial.

terms.name: Recruitment Support Officer
terms.definition: An officer who is aligned to a TA Partner to support the scope of recruitment & system activity

terms.name: Recurring Disability
terms.definition: Your waiting period may be waived if your Total Disability (or Partial Disability) recurs within six months of your return to your usual Occupation or a different Occupation because it will be considered part of your earlier claim.

terms.name: Redemptions
terms.definition: The process of regaining possession of something in exchange for payment or clearing a debt.

terms.name: Reduced Input Tax Credit
terms.definition: A Reduced Input Tax Credit (RITC) is a partial credit for the Goods and Services Tax (GST) that businesses in Australia can claim on specific purchases. These purchases, known as "reduced credit acquisitions," are typically related to making financial supplies, which are generally input-taxed and do not allow for full GST credits. Businesses can claim 75% of the GST paid on these reduced credit acquisitions, as outlined in GST regulations.

terms.name: Ref
terms.definition: Reference checking process

terms.name: Reference
terms.definition: Reference checking process

terms.name: Reference Data
terms.definition: Prescribed lists of valid values that may be assigned to certain data items.  Reference data may be defined internally (such as a list of codes specifying a type of fee that applies to an account), or externally by a suitable authority (such as a list of valid state or territory names forming part of an address).

terms.name: Referral Fee
terms.definition: $1000 KUDOS voucher for our employee who refer a candidate into a role (via My Insignia). Payable once they complete 6 months with Insignia Financial.

terms.name: Referral Source Management
terms.definition:
Referral source management is the process of tracking, optimizing, and maintaining relationships with individuals or organizations that refer clients or customers to a business. It is commonly used in industries like healthcare, legal services, and financial consulting to ensure a steady flow of referrals

Key aspects of referral source management include:

  • Tracking referrals: Using software or databases to monitor where referrals come from.
  • Building relationships: Strengthening connections with referral partners through communication and incentives.
  • Optimizing referral strategies: Identifying high-performing sources and improving engagement.
  • Automating processes: Leveraging technology to streamline referral tracking and follow-ups.

terms.name: Refs
terms.definition: Reference checking process

terms.name: Refund / Reference
terms.definition:
"Refund" refers to the process of returning money to a person who paid for a product, service, or transaction, typically due to dissatisfaction, cancellation, or errors in the purchase.

"Reference," in this context, usually means a unique identifier, such as a number, code, or transaction ID, associated with the refund. This reference helps to track, verify, and manage the refund transaction in a systematic way.

Both terms are commonly paired together in financial systems, customer service interactions, and online transactions to ensure smooth operations and proper recordkeeping.

terms.name: Register
terms.definition: A register is essentially a shell that contains one or more sections of information. A section contains logically grouped fields for the entry and display of information and data (essentially, a form).

terms.name: Registered Superannuation Entity
terms.definition:
A Registered Superannuation Entity (RSE) is an Australian superannuation fund that is regulated by the Australian Prudential Regulation Authority (APRA). It must be registered under the Superannuation Industry (Supervision) Act 1993 (SIS Act) to operate as a superannuation provider.

Key Features of an RSE:

  • Must be registered with APRA to accept contributions and manage superannuation benefits.
  • Operated by an RSE licensee, which is an entity that holds an APRA-granted RSE Licence.
  • Subject to strict prudential and reporting standards to ensure the security of members' retirement savings.
  • Includes different types of funds, such as public offer funds, corporate funds, industry funds, and retail super funds.

terms.name: Registrable Superannuation Entity
terms.definition: A registrable superannuation entity (RSE) means a regulated superannuation fund or an approved deposit fund or a pooled superannuation trust but does not include a self-managed superannuation fund. Registration of RSEs is affected under Part 2B of the Superannuation Industry (Supervision) Act 1993 (SIS Act).

terms.name: Registrable Superannuation Entity Licensee
terms.definition: An RSE licensee is a constitutional corporation, body corporate, or group of individual trustees, that hold an RSE licence granted by APRA under section 29D of the Superannuation Industry (Supervision) Act 1993.

terms.name: Registry (Shares)
terms.definition: Company that maintain or manage various companies share registers (name of shareholders, addresses).

terms.name: Regular Deposits
terms.definition:
Regular deposits are funds automatically paid into a member's account.
The source bank account details, frequency and amount are setup in advance.

terms.name: Regular Draw Down
terms.definition: A RDD is an easy way for you to receive regular payments from your investment. You can elect to receive a fixed amount paid to your nominated account each month or quarter.

terms.name: Regular Drawdown Plan
terms.definition: A regular drawdown plan is a financial arrangement that allows individuals to withdraw funds from their investment or retirement accounts at regular intervals, such as monthly or quarterly. This type of plan is often used by retirees to create a steady income stream while keeping the remaining funds invested. The withdrawals can usually be customized based on the individual's financial needs and the terms of the account.

terms.name: Regular Investment Facility
terms.definition:
A regular investment facility allows individuals to invest a fixed amount of money at regular intervals, such as monthly or quarterly, into a specific investment option like mutual funds, stocks, or other financial instruments. This approach is often used to build wealth over time through disciplined investing and to take advantage of market fluctuations via dollar-cost averaging.

For example, some financial institutions offer regular investment facilities where you can set up automatic contributions to your investment account. This can be done through methods like direct debit or BPAY, depending on the provider.

terms.name: Regular Investment Plan
terms.definition: Customers can make regular contributions to their OA Personal Super or Investment Portfolio by direct debit from a nominated bank account using the regular investment plan.

terms.name: Regular Savings Plan
terms.definition:
A regular savings plan is a financial strategy designed to help individuals save money consistently over time. These plans are particularly useful for achieving long-term goals, such as buying a house, funding education, or building an emergency fund. Here's how they generally work:

  • Consistent Contributions: You set aside a fixed amount of money at regular intervals (e.g., weekly, monthly).
  • Automatic Transfers: Many plans allow you to automate transfers from your bank account to ensure you stay on track.
  • Interest Growth: Your savings typically earn interest, helping your money grow over time.
  • Flexibility: Some plans offer the option to adjust contributions based on your financial situation

terms.name: Regular Withdrawal Facility
terms.definition: A regular withdrawal facility is a feature offered by certain investment or savings accounts that allows account holders to withdraw funds at regular intervals, such as monthly, quarterly, or annually. This facility is often used to provide a steady income stream, especially for retirees or individuals relying on their investments for living expenses. The withdrawals are typically scheduled and can be customized based on the account holder's preferences and the terms of the account.

terms.name: Regular Withdrawals
terms.definition:
The Regular Withdrawals function ality enables a Client to withdraw a nominated amount of their  funds from their Account on a regular basis at predefined periods, e.g. monthly, quarterly, etc.
The functionality includes the destination of the proceeds, usually the Client's nominated bank Account details.

terms.name: Regular Withdrawals - Payment amount
terms.definition: The nominated amount of the Regular Withdrawal

terms.name: Regular Withdrawals - Payment Frequency
terms.definition: The period upon which the Regular Withdrawal will be made, e.g. Monthly, Quarterly

terms.name: Regular Withdrawals - Payment to
terms.definition: This is the name of the payee  for the regular Withdrawal. Usually the name of the nominated bank Account details.

terms.name: Regulator
terms.definition: Usually refers to Government authorities charged with the supervision and regulation of finance instituations such as Insurers, Banks and Fund Managers.

terms.name: Rehabilitation Expenses Benefit
terms.definition: The Insurer will pay the rehabilitation expenses directly to the service providers or provide the rehabilitation services to you. The Insurer will not pay any monies (directly or indirectly) to you.

terms.name: Reinvested Income
terms.definition: Income received from investment earnings that is used to buy additional investment units, typically of the same investment, rather than credited tpo the Client

terms.name: Relationship Manager
terms.definition:
A Relationship Manager is a professional who maintains and nurtures relationships between an organization and its clients or customers. Their primary goal is to ensure client satisfaction, strengthen partnerships, and identify opportunities for growth.

Key responsibilities of a Relationship Manager often include:

  • Building trust: Serving as the main point of contact for clients, they foster strong, long-term relationships.
  • Understanding client needs: They carefully analyze customer requirements to tailor services or products accordingly.
  • Problem-solving: Addressing client concerns promptly to maintain satisfaction and loyalty.
  • Promoting business: Identifying opportunities to expand services and drive revenue.
  • Team collaboration: Working closely with internal teams to ensure smooth delivery of solutions.

It’s a versatile role, combining communication, strategic thinking, and customer-focused skills.

terms.name: Release Authority
terms.definition: Release Authority is a document that the ATO provide to Super Funds or Super Fund members when a member exceeds their concessional or non-concessional contribution caps and is required to pay excess concessional or non-concessional tax.

terms.name: Release of Funds
terms.definition: The process of making funds available for use, often after certain conditions or requirements have been met. For example, in legal or financial contexts, it might involve the disbursement of escrowed funds once specific obligations are fulfilled.

terms.name: Relevant Number
terms.definition: This number is used to reduce pension income assessed under the Centrelink income test. The income assessed by Centrelink is the gross annual income reduced by the deduction amount. The formula for the deduction is the “original purchase price less commutations divided by the relevant number”. The “original purchase price” is the money committed to the pension or income stream.Calculating the relevant number will depend on the type of income stream.

terms.name: Rem
terms.definition: Abbreviation of Remuneration: Total compensation received by an employee

terms.name: Remediation
terms.definition: The process of identifying and addressing errors or misconduct that have negatively impacted customers, often involving rectifying the situation through compensation or refunds.

terms.name: Remittance Advice and Recovery Notice
terms.definition: The RARN report notifies super providers of super member's credit payments and requests for repayment.

terms.name: Remuneration
terms.definition: Total compensation received by an employee

terms.name: Renounceable Rights Issue
terms.definition: Company provides shareholders the 'right' to buy new shares directly to the company, but shareholder can sell this right on the stock market.

terms.name: Replacement
terms.definition: When the incumbent is leaving the role and you are looking to replace them as is

terms.name: Reporting Entity
terms.definition: A Reporting Entity (RE) is an entity within the group that has a licensing or other obligation to ensure that Altas records are specifically reported to the relevant entity boards and committees.

terms.name: Repurpose
terms.definition: When the incumbent is leaving the role and the replacement role is different to the current role

terms.name: Req
terms.definition: The approval to hire for any open position. It is termed as Requisition.

terms.name: Request Distribution Centre
terms.definition: A distribution center is a specialized facility in the logistics industry that serves as an intermediary point for receiving, storing, and redistributing goods. Unlike traditional warehouses, which focus on long-term storage, distribution centers are designed for efficient movement of goods, ensuring they reach their final destination quickly and accurately.

terms.name: Request for Information
terms.definition: An eligible person requesting that the Trustee provides a valuation or information on the member's account.

terms.name: Request for Information - Rework
terms.definition: "Request information" typically refers to the act of seeking specific details or data from someone or somewhere. It can apply in various contexts, such as asking for records, documents, explanations, or clarification on a topic

terms.name: Request for Information Rework - Checking
terms.definition: A formal document used to seek clarification or additional details about a project, product, or service. Rework in the context of RFIs refers to the process of revising or resubmitting an RFI due to incomplete, inaccurate, or unclear information provided in the initial request. This can happen when the original RFI fails to address critical aspects or creates confusion among stakeholders.

terms.name: Request Information - Checking
terms.definition: "Request information" typically refers to the act of seeking specific details or data. It can apply in various contexts, such as asking for records, documents, explanations, or clarification on a topic

terms.name: Requisition
terms.definition: The approval to hire for any open position. It is termed as Requisition.

terms.name: Requisition processing
terms.definition: Purchase requisition processing involves the evaluation and approval of requests for purchasing goods or services. Once a requisition is approved, it can be converted into a purchase order, which is a formal document that authorises a supplier to provide the specified goods or services at agreed-upon terms and conditions.

terms.name: Requistion Approval Amount Limit
terms.definition: Limit till which the created user can raise a requisition

terms.name: Reserve
terms.definition: The Government requires superannuation fund trustees to hold adequate financial resources (Reserve) to cover any losses that members incur due to operational errors.

terms.name: Reserve Account details
terms.definition: (should this be separated out?) - is Account / Account Type

terms.name: Reserve Accounts
terms.definition: Each Employer will have a Bank Account that Contributions and Contribution Refunds  are transferred  from / into

terms.name: Reserve Type
terms.definition: A pool of assets held at a plan level for specific purposes. For example a defined benefit reserve used to hold assets to meet DB entitlements.

terms.name: Residential Mortgage Backed Securities
terms.definition:
Residential Mortgage-Backed Securities (RMBS) are a type of financial asset created by pooling together residential home loans. These loans are typically mortgages that individuals take out to buy homes. Once pooled, they are packaged into securities and sold to investors.

Investors who purchase RMBS receive payments derived from the interest and principal payments made by homeowners on their mortgages. These securities are often divided into tranches, which vary in risk and return depending on factors like the creditworthiness of the borrowers and the structure of the mortgage pool.

RMBS play a significant role in the financial markets by providing liquidity to the mortgage industry and offering investors exposure to residential real estate. However, they can also carry risks, particularly if homeowners default on their loans.

terms.name: Responsible Entity
terms.definition:
A responsible entity, being the company named in ASIC’s record of the scheme’s registration as the responsible entity of the investment scheme in accordance with s601EB of the Corporations Act.

An RE is an AFS Licence.

REs within the Group include, MLC Investments Limited, Navigator Australia Limited, Antares Capital Partners Limited, IOOF Investment services Limited (IISL), OnePath Funds Management Limited (OPFM).

terms.name: Responsible Investment
terms.definition: Responsible investment is the practice of considering Environmental, Social and Governance (ESG) factors in the research, analysis, selection and management of investments and the implementation of good stewardship practices.

terms.name: Restricted Non-preserved
terms.definition: Certain limited amounts that were held within the superannuation system as at 30 June 1999 may be classified as 'restricted non-preserved' benefits. It is no longer possible to accrue further restricted non-preserved benefits unless they are already held elsewhere and are rolled into our super fund.

terms.name: Restricted Non-Preserved Amount
terms.definition: Superannuation benefits which, in the case of employees, can be paid on termination of employment. They can also be paid at the time that preserved benefits can be paid.

terms.name: Restricted Non-preserved Amount
terms.definition: The restricted non-preserved amount is the portion of the member’s benefit established from voluntary employer contributions made before 1 July 1999. Access is restricted until a condition of release or termination of employment occurs.

terms.name: Restriction
terms.definition: Indicates whether or not a restriction exists for this account.  A  restriction text will  be displayed for any transactions against the account.

terms.name: Restriction Text
terms.definition:
See Restriction.
Text entered by the operator explaining a restriction against this account.
It will be displayed to the operator

terms.name: Retail Contribution
terms.definition:
Retail contribution refers to the financial metric that measures how much a product or service contributes to covering the indirect costs of running a retail business after accounting for its direct costs. It is calculated by subtracting direct expenses (like production or purchase costs) from the gross margin. This remaining amount helps cover fixed costs and ultimately contributes to profitability.

Understanding retail contribution is crucial for pricing decisions, identifying profitable products, and managing overall business strategy.

terms.name: Retail insurance
terms.definition: Retail insurance – insurance cover available to individuals. Requires underwriting and may provide additional features not available to members of a group policy.

terms.name: Retail Maintenance
terms.definition: Retail maintenance refers to the process of managing and maintaining the physical facilities and infrastructure of retail stores to ensure smooth operations, safety, and a positive customer experience. This includes tasks such as cleaning, HVAC system checks, equipment repairs, lighting maintenance, and compliance with safety standards.

terms.name: Retention
terms.definition: Maintaining the security, accessibility and integrity of information and data through appropriate storage for a specified period of time. Retention occurs from the point of creation or receipt of the information, through to archival and ends with the Disposal of the information.

terms.name: Retention incentive
terms.definition: Industry term for a monetary inducement  offered to an employee as an incentive to remain in the organisation; particular position in order to complete a particular task or project,

terms.name: Retention period
terms.definition: A retention period refers to the length of time that records, data, or information are kept before being discarded or archived. This period is determined based on various factors, such as legal requirements, organizational needs, or historical value. Retention periods ensure that records are available for reference, compliance, or operational purposes while avoiding unnecessary storage costs

terms.name: Retirement income
terms.definition:
Retirement income refers to the money or financial resources that an individual receives after they stop working, typically to support their living expenses during retirement. This income can come from various sources, such as:

  • Pensions or superannuation funds.
  • Social security benefits or government programs.
  • Personal savings and investments.
  • Annuities or rental property income.

The goal is to ensure financial stability and maintain one's lifestyle after leaving the workforce.

terms.name: Retirement Phase Pension
terms.definition: A superannuation pension account status indicating that a member has entered retirement phase, under which benefits are paid as an income stream and earnings on assets supporting the pension are generally tax-exempt in accordance with superannuation legislation.

terms.name: Retirement Savings Account
terms.definition: A retirement savings account acts like a superannuation account however it's provided by a bank, building society, credit union or life insurance company.

terms.name: Return of Funds
terms.definition: A request that is made to have funds returned when proof is provided that a genuine mistake has been made in which the client did not intend to make the contribution to the fund, but due to an error or oversight monie were contributed to the fund that were not intended to be a super contribution.

terms.name: Return to Sender
terms.definition: There are occasions when non-members call through to verbally advise of 'return to sender (RTS)' mail that they have received for one of our members who no longer resides at the address we have on file. When a verbal RTS notification is received, all future mail needs to be suspended and our Lost Member process must be initiated to locate our member and obtain updated address details.

terms.name: Return to work
terms.definition: Plan to return to work

terms.name: Reversionary Pensioner
terms.definition:
A reversionary pensioner in the context of a OneAnswer Frontier Pension account refers to a nominated beneficiary who will continue to receive the pension payments after the original pension member passes away.

A member can nominate their spouse as a reversionary pensioner. In the event of the members death, their pension will continue to be paid to their surviving spouse. If the reversionary pensioner dies before you, or ceases to be your spouse, the Trustee will normally pay your benefit in accordance with a valid non-lapsing nomination or pursuant to the Fund’s Trust Deed, in the absence of a non-lapsing nomination. If the member decide to nominate your spouse as a reversionary pensioner, it is important to note that a valid reversionary nomination will override any valid non-lapsing nomination.

terms.name: Rights Issue
terms.definition: Company want to raise money, giving their shareholders to buy new shares off-market on a pro-rata basis, in proportion to their current holding.

terms.name: Risk Appetite Framework
terms.definition: Risk Appetite Framework (RAF) and the RAS which define the roles and responsibilities, measures and tolerances.

terms.name: Risk Appetite Statement
terms.definition: The purpose of the Risk Appetite Statement (RAS) is to articulate the level of risk that Insignia Financial Ltd (IFL) and its subsidiaries and related bodies corporate, which include Australian Prudential Regulation Authority (APRA) Regulated Entities (AREs), Responsible Entities (REs) and Australian Financial Services (AFS) licensees (collectively referred to as ‘Group’ in this RAS) are prepared to accept in pursuit of strategic and business objectives, giving consideration to the interests of Clients1 and shareholders.

terms.name: Risk Based Checking
terms.definition: Risk-based checking refers to a method where organizations assess and prioritize risks to allocate resources effectively. This approach is commonly used in areas like compliance, anti-money laundering (AML), and quality control. Instead of treating all cases equally, risk-based checking focuses on higher-risk areas or transactions, ensuring that the most critical issues are addressed first. This helps improve efficiency and reduce potential threats.

terms.name: Risk Management Plan
terms.definition:
A risk management plan is a structured document that outlines how to identify, assess, mitigate, and monitor risks that could impact a project, organization, or activity. It serves as a proactive approach to minimize potential negative effects and maximize opportunities.

Key components of a risk management plan typically include:

  • Risk Identification: Listing potential risks that could arise
  • Risk Assessment: Evaluating the likelihood and impact of each risk.
  • Risk Mitigation Strategies: Developing actions to reduce or eliminate risks.
  • Roles and Responsibilities: Assigning team members to monitor and address risks.
  • Monitoring and Review: Continuously tracking risks and updating the plan as needed.

terms.name: Risk Measurement
terms.definition:
Risk measurement refers to the process of evaluating and quantifying potential losses associated with a decision, action, or investment. It helps prioritize the severity of risks and allocate resources effectively to maximize returns while managing uncertainty.


For each material risk category, the appetites are expressed through high level statements (Low, Medium or High), and further supported by quantitative or qualitative measures.

terms.name: Risk Rating
terms.definition: Defines which occupation the rate relates to when the rates are separated by occupation.

terms.name: Risk-Based Approach
terms.definition: A risk-based approach involves identifying, assessing and understanding potential ML/TF risks associated with clients, products/services and transactions which allows the Insignia Financial DBG to focus on clients and activity posing higher risks. The risk-based approach involves the establishment of appropriate robust mitigation and management controls in accordance with the identified level of risk.

terms.name: Role Based Permission/s
terms.definition: Permissions assigned in Success Factors depending upon the role of the employee

terms.name: Role Description
terms.definition: This is a generic term  - a role description is a document which holds the responsibilities and accountabilities of a position within an organisation - often interchanged with position description

terms.name: Role models
terms.definition: Insignia Financial Principles Rating

terms.name: Rollover
terms.definition: Paying a lump sum superannuation benefit from one superannuation fund into another superannuation fund, approved deposit fund or retirement savings account.

terms.name: Rollover Benefit Statement
terms.definition: This is a statement with all the rollover information required by the receiving fund.

terms.name: S290
terms.definition: This is a Notice of Intent to Claim a Tax Deduction. Other references include Tax Questionnaire or Section 290-170.

terms.name: SailPoint
terms.definition: System that integrates to My Insignia from Identity Access Management

terms.name: Salaried
terms.definition: Employee Sub group to represent: Employee who is paid salary by IFL on regular pay-runs and is eligible for all employee benefits as defined in the contract.

terms.name: Salary Continuance Indexation Basis
terms.definition: Where indexation applies, details the basis for calculating the indexation for SCI cover.

terms.name: Salary Continuance Insurance
terms.definition: Also known as Income Protection, pays a benefit if you temporarily couldn't work because of an illness or injury.

terms.name: Salary Continuation Insurance
terms.definition: Salary continuation insurance is a type of plan where an employer agrees to continue paying an employee's salary if they are unable to work due to disability or illness. This plan ensures financial stability for the employee during their recovery period. It may also include provisions for retaining the employee in the workforce with adjusted responsibilities.

terms.name: Salary Definition
terms.definition: The definition of salary as applied to the cover formula. This may differ from the definition used in other components of the plan.

terms.name: Salary Sacrifice
terms.definition: A salary sacrifice contribution is a concessional contribution. This is deducted from the members pre-tax wages/salary and contributed by the employer on request by the member.

terms.name: Salary Sacrifice Contributions
terms.definition: Salary sacrifice is an arrangement with your employer to make additional superannuation contributions from your pre-tax salary each pay cycle. Your employer makes additional contributions on your behalf. These contributions are taxed at 15% instead of at your personal income tax rate.

terms.name: Salary Type
terms.definition: The type of salary used in the formulas.

terms.name: Sale Method, Distribution Channel
terms.definition:
Sale Method: This refers to the approach or technique used to sell a product or service to customers. Common sales methods include direct sales (e.g., in-person or online), retail sales, telemarketing, B2B (business-to-business) sales, and more. The choice of method depends on factors such as the target audience, the nature of the product, and the market strategy.


Distribution Channel: This describes the path or route through which a product or service travels from the producer to the end customer. Common types of distribution channels include:

  • Direct Channels: Where the producer sells directly to the customer (e.g., online stores or factory outlets).
  • Indirect Channels: Where intermediaries like wholesalers, retailers, or distributors are involved.
  • Hybrid Channels: Combining both direct and indirect approaches.


Each plays a key role in ensuring that the product reaches the target market efficiently.

terms.name: Sales
terms.definition: Sales refers to the process of exchanging goods or services for money, typically involving persuading, promoting, and negotiating to close a deal. It can occur between individuals, businesses, or organizations and is a fundamental aspect of commerce.

terms.name: Sanction
terms.definition:
Punitive or coercive measures taken by a national government or international organisation intended to bring about changes in the policy or behaviour of others. They are often imposed on countries/regions to prevent crimes against humanity, financing of terrorists and terrorist acts, the sale of weapons of mass destruction, illegal arms trading and narcotics trafficking.

Sanctions may be country/region based, (countries the Insignia Financial DBG cannot deal with or assist others to deal with), List based (people or entities the Insignia Financial DBG cannot deal with or assist others to deal with) or product or industry based (products, services or industries that the Insignia Financial DBG cannot deal in or assist others to deal in).

terms.name: SAP SuccessFactors
terms.definition: Refers to the SuccessFactors Application (Human Resource Management Software)

terms.name: SAP SuccessFactors Application
terms.definition: SAP SuccessFactors is a cloud-based software application designed for human capital management (HCM). It provides tools for managing various HR functions, such as recruitment, onboarding, performance management, payroll, and employee development. It operates on a software-as-a-service (SaaS) model, making it accessible and scalable for businesses of all sizes.

terms.name: Satisfactory financial position
terms.definition:
A fund is considered to be in a satisfactory financial position when:

  • The value of the fund’s assets is sufficient to cover the vested benefits of all members, and
  • The fund is able to meet its liabilities as they fall due

This means the fund has enough assets to pay out all benefits that members would be entitled to if they were to leave the fund immediately.

terms.name: Savings management
terms.definition: Savings management is the approach to identify in achieving and tracking cost reductions, efficiency and value enhancement throughout the procurement process. It involves analysing expenditures , negotiating with suppliers, implementing cost saving initiatives , contract consolidations and gauging the effects on the organization's performance.

terms.name: Scheme Name
terms.definition: Applicable to Masterkey retail products, Scheme Name refers to the Account Name for the individual or company.

terms.name: Scheme Number
terms.definition: Applicable to Masterkey Retail products, Scheme Number is the account number for the individual or company.

terms.name: Scheme of Arrangement
terms.definition: A court-approved arrangement between a company and its shareholders or creditors. Usually its about mergers and amalgamations (i.e. a company reached an agreement with its shareholders about an upcoming takeover, that shareholders will get $X.XX fir every share that they have).

terms.name: Scope Id
terms.definition: Unique Id of the Scope to be assigned for the user. The scope must be existing and active in the system, and linked to the corresponding scope type  mentioned.

terms.name: Scopes
terms.definition: Scopes to be assigned for the user. Only one scope can be selected from a scope type.

terms.name: Seamless Transfer
terms.definition:
Transferring between OneAnswer Frontier Personal Super and Pension

To be eligible for a Seamless Transfer a member must transfer between like for like investment funds and both accounts must be in the name of the same member. A Seamless Transfer is available for full and partial transfers across any eligible combination of super and pension accounts.

terms.name: Secondment
terms.definition: A temporary move into a role, at the end of the period, employee will be going back to their home role

terms.name: Section 7
terms.definition: Section 7 is a Medical & Palliative Care Ace SA Declaration - legal documents that allow you to convey your decisions about end of life care ahead of time.

terms.name: Security Code
terms.definition: A "security code" in investment is typically an International Securities Identification Number (ISIN), a 12-character alphanumeric code that uniquely identifies a global security like a stock or bond for trading, clearing, and settlement.

terms.name: Security Holder Offer
terms.definition: Same as 'Priority Offer', but the issuing company is usually a financial institution (i.e. a bank).

terms.name: SEEK
terms.definition: Online advertising platform

terms.name: Self Managed Super Fund
terms.definition: Like other superannuation funds, SMSFs are a way of saving for retirement. The difference between an SMSF and other types of funds is that, generally, the members of an SMSF are also the trustees. This means the members of the SMSF run it for their own benefit.

terms.name: Self-insurance reserve
terms.definition:
A self-insurance reserve is a financial fund set aside by an organization to cover potential losses or claims that are not insured externally. Instead of purchasing traditional insurance, the entity maintains this reserve to manage risks internally.

Self-insurance reserves are commonly used by municipalities, universities, and large corporations to cover expenses related to property damage, liability claims, and employee benefits. The balance of the reserve is typically determined based on past claims history and a percentage of the organization's overall risk exposure.

terms.name: Self-Insured Benefit
terms.definition:
Self-insured benefits are those where the insurance liability is not insured by a:

  • Life company registered under section 21 of the Life Insurance Act 1995; or
  • General insurance company or Lloyd’s underwriter that is authorised, or taken to be authorised under the Insurance Act 1973.

Self-insured employer plans provide the insured component themselves under actuarial guidance for the ongoing funding provisions.

terms.name: Self-Managed Super Fund
terms.definition: Self Managed Super Fund's (SMSFs) are funds that can be established by an individual or family as a means of looking after their own super savings.

terms.name: Senior Leader Out performance incentive
terms.definition: This is a long term incentive plan which is specifically designed for senior roles within Asset mgt based on their strategic management of long term investments

terms.name: Senior Manager
terms.definition: Position Level

terms.name: Senior Quality and Conformance Coach
terms.definition: A Senior Quality and Conformance Coach is typically responsible for ensuring that quality standards and compliance measures are met across an organization. Their role often involves developing and implementing quality assurance processes, coaching teams to adhere to best practices, and driving continuous improvement initiatives. They may also analyze performance data, identify areas for improvement, and collaborate with cross-functional teams to enhance overall quality and conformance.

terms.name: Senior Specialist
terms.definition: Position Level

terms.name: Sensitive Personal Information
terms.definition:
Personal Information that is referred to in the Australian Privacy Act 1988 as “sensitive information.” The Act defines sensitive information as:

a. information or an opinion about an individual’s:

i. racial or ethnic origin; or

ii. political opinions; or

iii. membership of a political association; or

iv. religious beliefs or affiliations; or

v. philosophical beliefs; or

vi. membership of a professional or trade association; or

vii. membership of a trade union; or

viii. sexual orientation or practices; or

ix. criminal record;

that is also personal information; or

b. health information about an individual; or

c. genetic information about an individual that is not otherwise health information; or

d. biometric information that is to be used for the purpose of automated biometric verification or biometric identification; or

e. biometric templates.

See the Privacy Act 1988, the Office of the Australian Information Commissioner’s website, and Insignia’s Group Privacy Standard for more definitions and information.

terms.name: SEP file
terms.definition: Usually used to describe the data file used to send to NAB for MLC cohort

terms.name: Separately Managed Accounts
terms.definition: A Separately Managed Account or SMA is an investment portfolio run by an investment manager. An investment professional manages the portfolio but, unlike a managed fund, the client retains beneficial ownership of the holdings. SMAs can offer you and your clients a range of efficiencies including transparency, cost-effectiveness and choice.

terms.name: Seperate Statements
terms.definition:
Only applies to Friendly Society Bond (SP and MP) accounts

Indicates whether or not separate statements are required for this account, or it is sufficient to send a consolidated statement covering all accounts owned by a common set of members in a particular product. Other account types typically do not allow combined statements.

terms.name: Service Centre Manager
terms.definition: A Service Centre Manager oversees the operations of a service center, ensuring smooth day-to-day functioning. Their responsibilities typically include managing staff, maintaining customer satisfaction, improving service efficiency, and ensuring compliance with organizational policies and standards. They often handle budgets, monitor performance metrics, and implement strategies to optimize operations. Essentially, they're in charge of making sure the service center runs effectively and meets both customer and business expectations.

terms.name: Service Delivery Manager
terms.definition:
A Service Delivery Manager (SDM) is a critical role in many organizations, responsible for overseeing the delivery of services to clients or customers. They ensure that service commitments are met, maintain strong relationships with stakeholders, and improve service quality and efficiency over time.

Their responsibilities often include:

  • Managing teams to deliver services effectively
  • Acting as the main point of contact between the company and its clients
  • Monitoring performance metrics to ensure service levels are achieved
  • Addressing any issues or escalations promptly
  • Identifying opportunities for process improvement

It’s a role that blends leadership, problem-solving, and relationship management.

terms.name: Service Fee Update
terms.definition: A service fee update typically refers to a change or adjustment in the fees charged by a business or organization for providing specific services. These updates can occur due to various factors, such as inflation, changes in operational costs, or modifications to the scope of services offered. Service fee updates are often communicated to customers in advance to ensure transparency and maintain trust.

terms.name: Service Level Agreement
terms.definition: A Service Level Agreement is a commitment to provide certain tasks within a certain time-frame.

terms.name: Service One
terms.definition:
A credit union originating from NSW.
Made up through the combing of the following credit unions: Credit Union of Canberra, Snowy Mountains Credit Union, Hospitals Credit Union and Bemboka Community Credit Union

terms.name: Service Provider
terms.definition: A service provider is a person or company assigned to manager clients' superannuation, pension and investments. Some examples of IFL's service provider are - MLC, Shadforths, Plum.

terms.name: Servicing Rights Transfer
terms.definition: The process where the rights to manage and administer a loan, such as a mortgage, are transferred from one financial institution to another. This includes tasks like collecting payments, managing escrow accounts, and handling customer inquiries. The borrower is typically notified of the transfer, but the terms of the loan itself remain unchanged.

terms.name: Settlement Costs
terms.definition: Includes fees paid to custodians or agents to manage transaction settlements.

terms.name: Settlor
terms.definition:
The settlor of a trust is the person who creates a trust.
They provide the initial asset, the “settled sum”, kick-starting the trust.

terms.name: SG waiver under SCI
terms.definition: Any Superannuation Guarantee waiver under SCI

terms.name: Share of Wallet
terms.definition: Share of Wallet also known historically as Funds under Advice represents all FUM for the Aligned Dealer Groups that is sold via both Internally (IFL Platforms) and Externally (such as BT Super). SOW is reported to  external companies such as Plan for Life (PFL).

terms.name: Share Purchase Plan
terms.definition: Company want to raise money, allowing their shareholders to buy up to $15,000 of the new shares directly from them (no via share market) usually at discounted price. Benefits for shareholders are they get new shares at discounted price, and no need to pay for a brokerage fee.

terms.name: Shell Bank
terms.definition:
Any bank which does not maintain a place of business in its country of incorporation at which:

  • it employs one or more individuals on a full-time basis to perform banking-related duties;
  • maintains operating records related to its banking activities; and
  • it is subject to inspection by the Regulator which licenses its banking activities.   

It excludes any bank which is affiliated with a bank which is not a shell bank by being a subsidiary of, or subject to common control with that other bank.

terms.name: Short Form Personal Health
terms.definition: Short Form Personal Health Statement generally refers to a simplified health declaration form, often used for insurance purposes, requiring less detailed information than a full health statement.

terms.name: Short term incentive
terms.definition: This is an industry standard acronym for short term payment of funds to employees which can be seen as an incentive or as a bonus.  Usually paid once a year,  after performance of the company has been announced

terms.name: Shortfall limit
terms.definition:
A shortfall limit is a financial threshold set to ensure that a fund, typically a defined benefit superannuation fund, maintains a minimum level of assets relative to its liabilities.

In Australia, under Superannuation Prudential Standard 160 (SPS 160), the shortfall limit is determined by the trustee and represents the lowest acceptable funding level before regulatory action is required. If a fund falls below this limit, it may trigger an actuarial investigation and require a restoration plan to bring the fund back to a satisfactory financial position

terms.name: Shortfall Offer
terms.definition: Company want to raise money, offering shareholders new shares to purchase. However not all shares got taken up by its shareholders (there are 'leftover new shares'). Company then offered this 'leftover new shares' to its eligible shareholders to buy.

terms.name: Shortlisted
terms.definition: Selected list of candidate's applications that are progressed to the next step during the recruitment process.

terms.name: Signatory
terms.definition: Customer who is signatory for the account. Must match a valid Customer Number in the Customers file.

terms.name: Significant Dealing
terms.definition: A significant dealing is a dealing to clients outside of the TMD for a product. Whether a dealing is significant will be a matter for the issuer and/or distributor to determine in the particular circumstances.

terms.name: Significant Event Notice
terms.definition: A SEN is a document advising the member of important material changes or significant events that could impact their accounts with us.

terms.name: Simplified Verification Procedure
terms.definition: Simplified Verification Procedure is currently being implemented throughout operations with a targeted go-live Oct-24. It will become important to identify clients onboarding with SVP in downstream processes such as TM, KYC Refresh, Customer Screening, ECDD.

terms.name: Single Entry Point
terms.definition: Usually used to describe the data file used to send to NAB for MLC cohort

terms.name: Single Touch Payroll
