terms.definition: Single Touch Payroll is an Australian Government initiative to reduce employers' reporting burdens to government agencies.

terms.name: Small APRA Fund
terms.definition: A Super fund managed by a professional trustee and regulated by APRA.

terms.name: Smart Choice / Secondary Citizenship
terms.definition: ANZ Smart Choice is a super or pension product.

terms.name: SMSF Verification Service Response
terms.definition:
The SMSF Verification Service (SVS) is an electronic tool provided by the Australian Taxation Office (ATO) to verify details of a Self-Managed Super Fund (SMSF) before processing a rollover request. When a rollover request is made, the SVS checks key information such as:

  • Whether the SMSF's ABN is registered and compliant.
  • The SMSF's bank details and Electronic Service Address (ESA) match ATO records.
  • The member's Tax File Number (TFN) is linked to the SMSF and not compromised.
  • There is no verified date of death for the member

The SVS provides either a "verified" or "non-verified" response. A verified response allows the rollover to proceed, while a non-verified response indicates an issue with either the SMSF or the member's details. In such cases, corrective actions may be required, such as updating records with the ATO.

terms.name: Snr Mgr
terms.definition: Abbreviation of Senior Manager

terms.name: Solix
terms.definition: Solix is IFL’s long term data and information archiving platform. It supports the management of information that we may need for long periods of time for either reference or business processes. It also supports the appropriate disposal of that information once the retention period has passed.

terms.name: Source of Funds
terms.definition:
Consists of both:
the initial funds used for the account opening, such as a transfer from another bank; and
the funds used throughout the business relationship, such as salary from employment or cash from business activities.

terms.name: Source of Wealth
terms.definition: Describes the economic activities that have contributed to the client’s, or any beneficial owner’s, accumulated wealth.

terms.name: Specialist
terms.definition: Position Level

terms.name: Spend analysis
terms.definition: Spend Analysis is the process of evaluating an organisations expenditure to gain insights into purchasing patterns to identify cost saving opportunities and enhance overall procurement efficiency.

terms.name: Stamp Duty
terms.definition: A tax imposed by Governments on transfer of certain assets or property.

terms.name: Standard Risk Measure
terms.definition: SRM helps clients compare investment risk across the investment options offered. The SRM is based on industry guidance and is the estimated number of negative annual returns over any 20 year period.

terms.name: Standard Transfer Form
terms.definition: A standard transfer form is a document used to facilitate the transfer of ownership, rights, or responsibilities from one party to another. It typically includes details about the parties involved, the item or rights being transferred, and any terms or conditions of the transfer. These forms are commonly used in financial, legal, and business transactions to ensure clarity and compliance.

terms.name: State / Public Trustee
terms.definition: Public Trustees, otherwise known as state trustees, perform the role of Trustee of a deceased’s estate where no Executor has been appointed, or the appointed Executor has declined to take on these duties, and there is no other suitable person for this role. Every state and territory in Australia offer a Public Trustee service.

terms.name: Statement of Additional Advice
terms.definition: A Statement of Additional Advice (SoAA) is a document provided by financial advisers in Australia when offering additional advice to a client who has already received an initial Statement of Advice (SoA). The SoAA allows advisers to avoid repeating information already included in the original SoA, focusing instead on the new advice being given. This approach streamlines the process while ensuring compliance with regulatory requirements.

terms.name: Statement of Advice
terms.definition: The purpose of this document is to explain what is being recommended, why it is being recommended, why it is recommended and how this will benefit the client.

terms.name: Statement of work
terms.definition: A document that provides a description of a given project's requirements. It defines the scope of work being provided, project deliverables, timelines, work location, and payment terms and conditions.

terms.name: Statutory Declaration
terms.definition: A statutory declaration is a written statement that the member (the declarant) sign and declare to be true and correct in the presence of an authorised witness (i.e. JP). By signing this declaration, the member agees that the information in it is true. The member can be charged with a criminal offence if the information is false.

terms.name: Strategic Account Manager
terms.definition: Strategic account managers monitor sales performance metrics and prepare monthly, quarterly, and annual reports and forecasts. They ensure client satisfaction and identify new opportunities to create increased results.

terms.name: Strategic Accounts
terms.definition:
Strategic accounts refer to key clients or customers that hold significant value for a business, either because of their revenue contribution, potential growth, or influence in the market. Managing strategic accounts usually involves creating tailored strategies to maintain strong relationships, address their specific needs, and drive mutual success.

Effective management of strategic accounts often includes:

  • Customized Plans: Building unique solutions and aligning them with the client's business objectives.
  • Dedicated Teams: Assigning specialized account managers or teams for focused attention.
  • Proactive Communication: Regularly engaging to anticipate needs and offer value.
  • Data-Driven Insights: Using analytics to understand patterns and forecast future opportunities.

terms.name: Strategic Advice Team
terms.definition:
A Strategic Advice Team typically consists of experienced professionals who provide expert guidance to businesses or organizations on strategic planning, decision-making, and long-term growth. These teams help companies navigate complex challenges, optimize operations, and align their strategies with business goals.

Strategic advisors offer insights on market trends, competitive positioning, and risk mitigation, ensuring that organizations make informed decisions. They often work closely with leadership teams to refine business models, improve efficiency, and drive sustainable success.

terms.name: Strategic sourcing
terms.definition: Strategic sourcing is structured process of efficiently acquiring goods and services by carefully selecting suppliers, negotiating contracts, and managing relationships to achieve cost efficiencies and improving overall value.

terms.name: Street Address
terms.definition: The employee's physical (street) address

terms.name: Structured Data
terms.definition:
Information that is stored in a manner that complies with a pre-defined inherent structure. Contrasted with Unstructured Data. Examples of structured data include data in table-oriented databases (relational databases), some spreadsheets, some XML or JSON files, and others.

The pre-defined structure applies to every instance of a record within the information collection. As an example, a record about an account may include elements such as an account number, a customer number, an account name, the date it was opened, and so on. Records of every account must conform to that structure.

terms.name: Studio Dash
terms.definition: The project tracker and workflow management system for our in house design studio. This is where we submit requests for design work to be completed, see work in progress and communicate with the Studio team.

terms.name: Studio Dashboard
terms.definition: The project tracker and workflow management system for our in house design studio. This is where we submit requests for design work to be completed, see work in progress and communicate with the Studio team.

terms.name: Sub Channel
terms.definition: Sub Channel is a subset of Channel and usually represents a Dealer Group E.g. Under the Aligned Channel, we have Sub Channels of RI, Bridges, Consultum and Shadforth.

terms.name: Sub Process
terms.definition:
A "sub-process" is a smaller, distinct part of a larger process. It represents a specific set of tasks or actions that contribute to the overall goals of the main process. Sub-processes are often used to break down complex workflows into manageable sections, making them easier to analyze, execute, or optimize.

In business or technical contexts, sub-processes might be independent units that can operate on their own or function as interconnected components within the larger system. For example, in a manufacturing process, assembling parts could be a sub-process of the larger product production cycle.

terms.name: Subject Matter Expert
terms.definition: A person with deep knowledge and expertise in a particular field or industry. SMEs are often consulted for their specialized skills and insights, helping organizations make informed decisions, solve complex problems, or develop new strategies.

terms.name: Substantive role
terms.definition: Sometimes called home role -  this is the position the person has permanent holding rights over -  it allows employees to be moved into secondments (in another role),  but know where they will be moved back to at the end of the secondment

terms.name: Successor Fund Transfer
terms.definition: A Successor Fund Transfer (SFT) occurs when allmembers' accounts are transferred from one registrable super entity (RSE) to another RSE with a different Australian business number (ABN)

terms.name: Successor Fund Transfer
terms.definition: An SFT is a transfer of a registrable superannuation entity’s (RSE’s) existing members to a different RSE (successor fund) without the consent of those individual members.

terms.name: Successor fund transfer
terms.definition:
A successor fund transfer is a process in the superannuation industry where members and their benefits are transferred from one superannuation fund to another, typically due to consolidation or regulatory changes. The key requirement is that the receiving fund must provide members with equivalent rights to those they had in the original fund, allowing the transfer to occur without member consent.


Note transfer adheres to the meaning given in r. 1.03 of the SIS Regulations.

Reference: SIS Regulations, r. 6.29.

Reference link: https://www.legislation.gov.au/F1996B00580/2019-08-10/text

terms.name: Sum Insured - Primary Formula
terms.definition: The benefit formula that is used to calculate the amount of sum insured that applies for a member based on the values of the formula components defined.

terms.name: Sum Insured - Secondary Formula
terms.definition: A secondary benefit formula may be used in some cases to calculate the sum insured for a member where this is used in conjunction with the primary formula.

terms.name: Super Cap
terms.definition: Maximum contribution Base set by the ATO -  business is not obligated to pay super above this amount. The maximum super contribution base is used to determine the maximum limit on any individual employee's earnings base for each quarter of any financial year.

terms.full_name: Super Complaints Tribunal (Replaced by AFCA - Australian Financial Complaints Authority)
terms.name: Super Complaints Tribunal (Replaced by AFCA - Australian Financial Complaints Authority)
terms.definition: SCT has ceased operations and now replaced with AFCA.

terms.name: Super Contribution Employer Detail
terms.definition:
Employers in Australia are required to make superannuation contributions for their eligible employees. Here are some key details:

  1. Who is eligible?
    Employers must pay super contributions for employees who are 18 years or older and earn more than $450 per month. For employees under 18, they must work at least 30 hours a week to qualify.
  2. How much to contribute?
    The minimum contribution rate, known as the Super Guarantee (SG), is currently 11% of an employee's ordinary time earnings. This rate is set to increase to 12% by July 2025.
  3. When to pay?
    Super contributions are generally due quarterly, with deadlines on:
    28 January (for October–December)
    28 April (for January–March)
    28 July (for April–June)
    From July 2026, payments may be required with every pay cycle.
  4. How to pay?
    Employers can use a clearing house, such as the ATO's Small Business Superannuation Clearing House, to make contributions electronically.
  5. Penalties for non-compliance:
    Late or missed payments can result in penalties, including the Super Guarantee Charge (SGC), which is not tax-deductible.

terms.name: Super Contributions Benefit
terms.definition: Pays your super contribution amounts while you’re receiving an Income Protection claim up to 15% of your monthly income.

terms.name: Super Guarantee charge
terms.definition: Minimum super amount set by the tax office

terms.name: Super Guarantee Contributions
terms.definition:
From 1 July 2023, employers are generally required to contribute 11% of their employees’ ordinary time earnings into super. These are known as superannuation guarantee (SG) contributions.

SG contributions are employer contributions. These contributions are generally taxed at up to 15%1 in the fund and count towards your concessional contribution cap.

The SG rate increases by 0.5% each financial year until it reaches 12% from 1 July 2025.

terms.name: Super Ratings
terms.definition: External data provided on product performance

terms.name: Super to Pension transfer bonus
terms.definition: If a member seamlessly transfer (no buy-sell spreads) your OneAnswer Frontier Personal Super account balance to OneAnswer Frontier Pension in the same investment funds, allocations and amounts, the member may be entitled to a ‘Super to Pension transfer bonus’. The bonus represents a portion of any unrealized capital gains tax (CGT) held by the super fund in the investment funds transferred by you. If you are eligible, the bonus will be added to the amount transferred out of your OneAnswer Frontier Personal Super account and will form part of the opening balance in your OneAnswer Frontier Pension account.

terms.name: Superannuation Asset Management System
terms.definition:
Superannuation asset management systems are designed to help manage and optimize the investment portfolios of superannuation funds. These systems often include features like:

  1. Portfolio Tracking: Real-time updates on asset allocation and performance.
  2. Risk Management: Tools to assess and mitigate investment risks.
  3. Compliance Monitoring: Ensures adherence to regulatory requirements.
  4. Member Insights: Provides data-driven insights to improve member engagement and satisfaction.

terms.name: Superannuation Guarantee / SG Charge
terms.definition: The most common type of contribution regularly going into your super account is likely to be the Superannuation Guarantee or SG for short. This contribution is a mandated contribution your employer is required to make into a super fund on the members behalf.

terms.name: Superannuation Guarantee Administration Act 1992
terms.definition: AKA SGA

terms.name: Superannuation Guaranteed %
terms.definition: The % of salary that must be paid to a employee into their nominated super fund

terms.name: Superannuation Industry (Supervision) Act and Regulations 1994
terms.definition: The Superannuation Industry (Supervision) Act 1993 (SIS Act) is the key legislation governing the superannuation system in Australia. It sets out the rules and regulations for the operation, management, and supervision of superannuation funds to ensure they are managed prudently and in the best interests of members. The Act is supported by various regulations, including the Superannuation Industry (Supervision) Regulations 1994, which provide detailed guidelines on compliance, reporting, and operational standards.

terms.name: Superannuation Industry (Supervision) Regulations 1994
terms.definition: The statutory bible for all superannuation funds

terms.name: Superannuation Product Identification Number
terms.definition: AKA USI. The USI is used to identify super funds and individual super fund products for electronic rollover and contributions. A super fund may have one USI or it may have a USI for each product. The USI allows money to be moved between funds. Also known as USI.

terms.name: Superannuation Product Identifier
terms.definition:
Means a unique identifier created by the RSE, defined benefit RSE or ERF used to represent a superannuation product.

The SPI must satisfy the following rules:

  • it must comprise no more than 20 alpha-numeric characters (with no special characters);
  • it cannot be the same as the SPI used for another superannuation product within the same RSE, defined benefit RSE or ERF; and
  • it cannot change so long as the superannuation product exists and must be reported consistently across reporting periods (including if the name of the superannuation product changes).

terms.name: Superannuation Reporting Standards
terms.definition: The Product teams are responsible for the review and attestation of a sub-set of the Superannuation Reporting Standards (SRS) submissions for the MLC Super Fund (as well as the MLC Superannuation Fund, DPMRS, PCRS & MLC Pooled Superannuation Trust and other IFL entities).

terms.name: Superannuation system
terms.definition:
Australia's superannuation system, often referred to as "super," is a mandatory savings program designed to help individuals fund their retirement. Employers are required to contribute a percentage of an employee's income—currently set at 11.5%—into a superannuation fund. This rate is set to increase to 12% from 1 July 2025.

Super funds invest these contributions in various assets like stocks, bonds, and property, allowing the funds to grow over time. Employees can also make additional contributions, either pre-tax (concessional) or post-tax (non-concessional), to boost their retirement savings. Contributions are taxed at a concessional rate of 15%, which is generally lower than most individuals' income tax rates.

terms.name: Superchoice
terms.definition: Super clearing house

terms.name: Superlink
terms.definition:
SuperLink allows members to link their Life or TPD 
cover held inside super to cover that is held outside super. This arrangement allows them to access more benefits and features that do not meet a condition of release inside super. However, any premium payable outside super cannot be paid from the OneAnswer Frontier Personal Super account and must be paid from non-super money

terms.name: SuperTick Member Details
terms.definition:
A generic term representing a  collection of Member details used to validate a member via the ATO SuperTick service -
- Title
- Surname
- Given Names
- Date of Birth
- Gender
- TFN is Valid/Invalid

terms.name: Supplier management
terms.definition: Supplier management is a structured program to manage suppliers and improve their impact within an organisation. It includes managing vendor deliverables, managing compliance, supplier relationship , onboarding procedures.

terms.name: Surrender
terms.definition:
In financial terms, "surrender" often refers to the process of terminating or giving up a financial product or agreement, such as an insurance policy, annuity, or bond, before it reaches maturity. For instance:

  • Insurance Policies: Surrendering a life insurance policy means canceling it before its term ends, often receiving the "surrender value" (the cash value of the policy minus any fees or penalties).
  • Annuities: Surrendering an annuity can involve withdrawing funds early, which may lead to surrender charges or penalties, especially during a specific period outlined in the contract.

terms.name: Surrender Value
terms.definition: The amount payable to a person who surrenders a life insurance policy. This term is rarely used now as the Integra Super product has now been decommissioned.

terms.name: Suspense
terms.definition: In financial terms, suspense usually refers to a temporary account used in bookkeeping to hold transactions or entries until their proper classification is determined. A suspense account is typically employed when there are uncertainties, such as when a payment is received but its source or purpose is unclear. Once the uncertainty is resolved, the funds or transactions in the suspense account are reallocated to the correct accounts.

terms.name: Suspicious Activity
terms.definition: Means that based on the knowledge and experience of a staff member, the client activity is not considered normal, usual or is possibly known to be of an illegal nature. This may include large or complex transactions as well as those where doubts exist to the identity of the client.

terms.name: Suspicious Matter Report
terms.definition: An SMR must also be submitted if the reporting entity has reasonable grounds to suspect the customer or an agent of the customer is not who they say they are.

terms.name: Switch
terms.definition: The movement of funds from one investment option to another. It involves the sale of units from one option and the purchase of units in the new option.

terms.name: Switch Trade OS - MKIS
terms.definition: When a position is simultaneously sold from a model portfolio and another position is brought

terms.name: Switching Fee
terms.definition: A switching fee for a superannuation product other than a MySuper product, is a fee to recover the costs of switching all or part of a member’s interest in the superannuation entity from one investment option or product in the entity to another.

terms.full_name: System Access Request - to request access to various business systems.
terms.name: System Access Request - to request access to various business systems.
terms.definition: A system access request is essentially a formal process by which an individual seeks permission to access specific systems, networks, applications, or resources within an organization. This usually involves submitting a detailed request that outlines what access is needed, why it’s required, and potentially for how long. The request is then reviewed and either approved, modified, or denied based on the organization’s policies, security protocols, and role-based access controls.

terms.name: System Integrated Testing
terms.definition: The testing & Automation Centre of Excellence (CoE) have teams of testers (referred to internally as SIT) that focus on technical design, architecture testing, system intergration, regression testing, performance testing and security testing. Often a combined approach and automated test suite.

terms.name: System of record
terms.definition: A system that we rely on as a business to maintain certain records. The authoritative data source for a given data item or set of information.

terms.name: Tailored Investment Options
terms.definition:
For the tailored investment option to be offered to clients of a specific employer plan, NULIS would need to consider the following:

  • A clearly stated investment objective and strategy;
  • The ability to provide daily unit prices; and
  • Sufficient liquidity to be able to process redemptions and rollovers out within three business days

terms.name: Talent Acquisition
terms.definition: Recruitment team

terms.name: Talent Acquisition Partner
terms.definition: Talent Acquisition Partner who is aligned to a specific division or client group to support recruitment activity & talent needs

terms.name: Talent Acquisition Support Officer
terms.definition: An officer who is aligned to a TA Partner to support the scope of recruitment & system activity

terms.name: Target Market Determination
terms.definition:
A Target Market Determination (TMD) describes who the product may be suitable for, based on likely needs, objectives, and financial situation. It also sets out who can distribute our products, how they can do this, and situations when we may need to review our products and the target market.

The central concept underpinning DDO is that of the target market.

Product issuers are obliged to:

  • identify a target market of consumers, and design products suitable for that target market, and
  • monitor consumer outcomes and review products to ensure that consumers are receiving products that are likely to align to their likely objectives, financial situation and needs.

Issuers and distributors must take ‘reasonable steps’ to ensure those products reach consumers in that target market. The Target Market Determinations (TMDs) will describe the typical objectives, financial situation and needs of consumers in the target market, describe the product features, and explain why those features will meet consumers’ needs.

terms.name: Target Market Determinations
terms.definition:
The central concept underpinning DDO is that of the target market.

Product issuers are obliged to:

  • identify a target market of consumers, and design products suitable for that target market, and
  • monitor consumer outcomes and review products to ensure that consumers are receiving products that are likely to align to their likely objectives, financial situation and needs.

Issuers and distributors must take ‘reasonable steps’ to ensure those products reach consumers in that target market. The Target Market Determinations (TMDs) will describe the typical objectives, financial situation and needs of consumers in the target market, describe the product features, and explain why those features will meet consumers’ needs.

terms.name: Tax (performance component type)
terms.definition: Means the tax expense or benefit derived from RSE activities specified by the component category.

terms.name: Tax Agent Services Act
terms.definition: Requiring financial planners to undertake further education in taxation matters to provide advice in the area.

terms.name: Tax Benefit
terms.definition: A tax benefit may apply to insurance premiums charged to client's super account.

terms.name: Tax deductions
terms.definition: Tax Deductions - an amount that reduces the amount of tax payable by the fund. Where the fund receives a tax deduction, the benefit is passed onto you.

terms.name: Tax Deductions
terms.definition: Tax deductions reduce the amount of tax payable by the fund. When the fund receives a deduction, the benefit is passed on to members.

terms.name: Tax File Number
terms.definition: A TFN is a unique number the Australian Taxation Office (ATO) creates for you. Generally, for your to use for taxation and superannuation purposes throughout your life.

terms.name: Tax File Number Declaration Form
terms.definition:
A Tax File Number (TFN) Declaration is a form used in Australia to provide your tax file number to your employer or payer. This helps them determine how much tax to withhold from your payments.

You can complete the TFN declaration online or on paper. If you're filling it out online, you may need to link your myGov account to the Australian Taxation Office (ATO) and access the form through ATO online services. If your employer has their own electronic employment form, you might need to complete that instead.

terms.name: Tax free phase
terms.definition: The tax-free phase generally refers to a period when certain income or assets are exempt from taxation. In Australia, this term is often used in the context of superannuation, where retirees who have reached the eligible age can receive tax-free pension payments from their super fund.

terms.name: Tax Offset
terms.definition: Offset used to reduce or eliminate your income tax liability. Tax offsets can’t be used to create a tax refund.

terms.name: Tax on Investment Earnings
terms.definition: Investment earnings are taxed at a rate of up to 15%. Tax paid or payable on investment earnings is paid by the Fund and is reflected in the daily unit price for each investment option.

terms.name: Tax Questionnaire
terms.definition:
A Tax Questionnaire is a document or form used to collect essential information about an individual's or a business's financial situation for tax-related purposes. It typically includes questions about income, deductions, credits, and other relevant details necessary for tax filings or assessments.

Tax professionals, accountants, or tax authorities may use these questionnaires to ensure accurate reporting and compliance with tax laws. The complexity and contents can vary based on the jurisdiction or the type of tax being filed

terms.name: Taxable Income
terms.definition: Income on which an individual or super fund is required to pay income tax, less any allowable tax deductions.

terms.name: Taxable Taxed
terms.definition: Refers to the taxed element of a member’s taxable component within their superannuation balance. This amount has already been subject to contributions tax and impacts tax treatment on withdrawal or rollover. Accurate classification is essential for ATO reporting and member benefit calculations.

terms.name: Taxable Untaxed
terms.definition: Represents the untaxed element of a member’s taxable component, typically arising from certain employer contributions or transfers from untaxed funds. This component attracts higher tax rates on withdrawal and is critical for ensuring correct tax calculations and compliance.

terms.name: Taxed Element
terms.definition: That part of the post 30 June 1983 component of an eligible termination payment (ETP) which has been paid from a taxed source.

terms.name: Taxed phase
terms.definition:
The taxed phase is the period during which:

  • Contributions (especially concessional ones) are taxed at 15% when they enter the fund.
  • Investment earnings (interest, dividends, capital gains) within the fund are also taxed at 15% 

This is in contrast to the retirement phase, where:

  • Investment earnings are generally tax-free, subject to the transfer balance cap (a lifetime limit on how much you can move into retirement phase accounts)

terms.name: Taxed Source
terms.definition: Refers to superannuation funds, approved deposit funds or Retirement Savings Accounts (RSA) which pay tax under the standard superannuation tax provisions. The rate of tax payable on the taxable component of an eligible termination payment depends on whether the payment is from a taxed or an untaxed source.

terms.name: Team Leader
terms.definition: Anyone who has a direct line report under then.

terms.name: Technical Data Lineage
terms.definition: A Data Lineage map that depicts the flow of data within a system, not just between systems as is the case for Business Data Lineage.

terms.name: Technical Data Quality Rules
terms.definition: Specified metrics and thresholds that are applied to data to determine whether it meets quality expectations and supports business needs, being fit for purpose.  These rules are implemented within a data quality monitoring tool, or as a control embedded in an information system.

terms.name: Technically insolvent
terms.definition: Technically insolvent refers to a financial situation where a company or individual is unable to meet their debt obligations as they come due, even though their total assets may still exceed their liabilities.

terms.name: Telegraphic Transfer
terms.definition: A telegraphic transfer (TT), also known as a wire transfer, is an electronic method of transferring funds between banks or financial institutions, often used for international transactions. It involves sending money via a secure network, like SWIFT, to ensure the payment is processed quickly and safely.

terms.name: Temporary Resident Notification
terms.definition: A temporary resident notification typically refers to a formal communication or declaration made by an individual who is residing in a country temporarily. This could involve notifying relevant authorities about their temporary status, purpose of stay, or any changes in their circumstances. The specifics of such notifications vary depending on the country and its immigration or tax regulations.

terms.name: Temporary Total Disability
terms.definition: TTD means “Temporary Total Disability.” When an employee is hurt at work, they may have access to TTD benefits under their employer's workers' compensation insurance.

terms.name: Term Allocated Pension
terms.definition: A TAP is a superannuation account that provides for your super to be paid to you as a regular income for a fixed period.

terms.name: Term Deposit
terms.definition: A TD is a cash investment for an agreed rate of interest over a fixed amount of time, or term. The money is there for the pre-determined period. Typically the money can be withdrawn at the end of the period, reinvested or taken earlier with a penalty attached.

terms.name: Terminal Illness
terms.definition: Pays a benefit if you’re medically certified as likely to die within 24 months — applies if you have either life cover or TPD.

terms.name: Terminal Illness Certification Period
terms.definition: The certification period for Terminal Illness in months

terms.name: Termination Date
terms.definition: If an Account has been closed/terminated, indicates the date that the Account was closed

terms.name: Termination Details
terms.definition: A generic term that encompass all the particulars around the clouse of a Client's Account - Date of closure, reason for closure, and withdrawl amounts and recipient of withdrawal funds

terms.name: Termination Type
terms.definition: If an Account has been closed/terminated, indicates the the type of closure applied

terms.name: Terrorism Financing
terms.definition: The collection or provision of funds, whether it be legitimate or sourced through criminal acts, to fund terrorist activity or support terrorists.

terms.name: Test Revenue
terms.definition: The measuring of changes in total revenue against changes in price to gauge how sensitive demand is to price fluctuations

terms.name: Testamentary Discretion Trust
terms.definition:
A testamentary discretionary trust is a trust that is established through a will and only comes into effect after the death of the person who created the will (the testator). Its main purpose is to provide flexibility in distributing the deceased's assets to beneficiaries according to their specific needs and circumstances.

Key features of a testamentary discretionary trust include:

  • Discretionary Powers: The appointed trustee has the discretion to decide how and when to distribute the income and capital of the trust among the beneficiaries
  • Tax Benefits: These trusts can help reduce tax liabilities by distributing income to beneficiaries who might be in lower tax brackets
  • Asset Protection: They can protect assets from creditors, relationship breakdowns, or other financial risks faced by the beneficiaries
  • Flexibility: Beneficiaries can benefit according to their current needs without being bound by rigid distributions.

terms.name: The Dash
terms.definition: The project tracker and workflow management system for our in house design studio. This is where we submit requests for design work to be completed, see work in progress and communicate with the Studio team.

terms.full_name: The Employee Retirement Plan - now traded up to MKBS.
terms.name: The Employee Retirement Plan - now traded up to MKBS.
terms.definition: Now traded up to MKBS

terms.name: The Spectrum Platform
terms.definition:
The Spectrum Platform (TSP) is an administration system used to manage a group of superannuation products. It serves as the core system for processing, maintaining, and reporting on these products within the organization.
Associated Products:

Expand Extra Super (most frequent)
IOOF Personal Super
IOOF Employer Super
Shadforth Portfolio Service – Super
IOOF Employer Super
Shadforth Portfolio Service – Super

terms.name: The tracker
terms.definition: A calendar/schedule of all past and upcoming content and workflow for the Employee Experience and Communications team. This allows the team to plan, organise and prioritise enterprise and divisional communications and strategic priorities.

terms.name: The webinar
terms.definition: Weekly all staff zoom webinar hosted by CEO Renato Mota, to provide business updates and insights. Guest speakers are invited to attend each week to present on a range of topics.

terms.name: Third Party Authority
terms.definition: A third party is an authority held on file for the member giving authority for a nominated person, company or adviser to access information on the members' behalf.

terms.name: Ticker Code
terms.definition: Similar to APIR code, it's a symbol representing a fund or a company's stock on an exchange. The ticker can be the most dependable way to identify a security because it is less likely to change than a security name.

terms.name: Tier arrangement
terms.definition:
The tier arrangement is a number used to distinguish between different tiering structures.
A different number should be assigned to each tiering structure within an RSE, defined benefit RSE or ERF. The tier arrangement should be set to 1, 2, 3... etc. for each additional tiering structure that is reported.

terms.name: Tier number
terms.definition:
Where a tiering structure is in place, the tiering structure should have the same values in fields (superannuation product identifier, investment menu identifier, investment option identifier, component type, component activity, investment manager level, investment charge type, tier type, tier arrangement). The tier number should be set to 1, 2, 3... etc. for each additional tier that is added.
Where the tier type is not applicable, this field has no meaning and should be reported as 0.

terms.name: Tier type
terms.definition:
Means the method by which the value of the fee, expense or benefit changes based on their balance. The tier type options are:

  • Flat;
  • Marginal; and
  • Not Applicable.

terms.name: Time to fill
terms.definition: Number of days it takes for a any req status to move to ready to hire state from the date the requisition was approved.

terms.name: Tipping Off
terms.definition:
To disclose that a suspicion has been formed or a report lodged regarding suspicious matters/activities about a client to any person or entity other than the AML/CTF Compliance Officer or their delegate or:

• an employee’s line manager;

• members of the Financial Crime team;

• business unit senior management;

• the AML regulator or appropriate government agency;

• a person acting on the Insignia Financial DBG’s behalf to report suspicious matters/activities; and

• subject to applicable law, the Insignia Financial DBG’s legal counsel.

terms.name: TMD Transmittal
terms.definition: A Target Market Determination (TMD) is made up of a number of mandatory and optional data points which have to be transmitted via agreed methods to Distributors including APIR/Morningstar/FE Fundinfo etc each time a TMD is updated by the issuer. This is done in a system called Keystone via a custom built process called Transmittals. This process is completed by the teams who produce the TMD: Asset Management Disclosure or Mastertrust Disclosure teams, (Mastertrust Disclosure currently updates and transmits on behalf of the Wrap teams as well) The process is completed post the TMD goes live on the website.

terms.name: To Amount
terms.definition: The destination amount (To Currency)

terms.name: To Currency
terms.definition: Destination currency for funds.

terms.name: To Currency Exchange Rate
terms.definition: Decimal value reflecting exchange rate used for transaction.

terms.name: To-be-deleted
terms.definition: Date and time of latest change to the record, ie, record effective date. [J3E - If you don't have this date, we'll use the date in the file name instead. This is in the context of Customer and Account input files into Jade ThirdEye for AML TMP].

terms.name: Total & Permanent Disability Cover
terms.definition: Pays a benefit if you’re unable to ever work again due to injury or illness.

terms.name: Total and permanent disability insurance
terms.definition:
Total and Permanent Disability (TPD) insurance provides financial protection if you become permanently disabled due to illness or injury and can no longer work. It typically pays out a lump sum to help cover medical expenses, rehabilitation costs, and ongoing living expenses.

There are two main types of TPD coverage:

  • Own occupation: Covers you if you're unable to work again in the specific job you had before your disability.
  • Any occupation: Covers you if you're unable to work in any job suited to your education, training, or experience. This type is generally harder to claim but is more affordable.

Many Australians have TPD insurance through their superannuation funds, but it can also be purchased separately

terms.name: Total Fixed Remuneration
terms.definition: Refers to the Total Fixed Remuneration Package (Base Salary + Superannuation) of an employee

terms.name: Total Permanent Disability / Disabled
terms.definition: TPD is a form of insurance held within a superannuation policy and provides a financial safety net to help support you and your family if you suffer a permanent injury or illness making it impossible to return to work.

terms.name: Total shareholder return
terms.definition: Total shareholder return is a measure of the performance of an organisation and shares over time. It combines share price appreciation and dividends paid to show the total return to the shareholder expressed as an annualised percentage.

terms.name: TPD Tapering - Tapering Description
terms.definition: Details the tapering that applies to the TPD insurance cover. This may be formatted as a table as required.

terms.name: Trade OS - Withdrawal Reprint - MKIS
terms.definition: A record of withdrawal through Trade OS (an operating system for business-to-business trades).

terms.name: Trading As Name
terms.definition: Trading name for entity if different to EntityName.

terms.name: Transaction Costs
terms.definition: Transaction costs are costs associated with the sale and purchase of assets of the superannuation entity other than costs that are recovered by the superannuation entity charging buy-sell spreads.

terms.name: Transaction Fees
terms.definition: Transaction Fees is a type of a fee when the Account is charged for every moment it processes a payment. Transaction Fees can vary among the services/type of payment

terms.name: Transaction Monitoring Program
terms.definition: A program for monitoring businesses’ transactions with the aim of identifying transactions that appear to be abnormally complex, unusually large, or part of an unusual pattern of transactions for a client and which have no apparent economic or visible lawful purpose. It may comprise both manual and/or automated processes.

terms.name: Transaction Processing Centre
terms.definition: TPC manages the paper request received from Customer or Advisors for Contributions, Customer On-Boarding, Customer Maintenance and Cheque Processing.

terms.name: Transaction Type
terms.definition: A transaction type refers to the classification of a financial transaction based on its purpose or nature. For instance, common transaction types include purchases, sales, withdrawals, deposits, transfers, and payments. These classifications help in tracking, managing, and analyzing financial activity across accounts or systems

terms.name: Transfer into reserve
terms.definition:
Transfer into reserve refers to the process of allocating funds from a company's profits or retained earnings into a reserve account for future use. Reserves are typically set aside for specific financial purposes, such as covering unexpected expenses, funding future investments, or ensuring financial stability.

There are different types of reserves, including:

  • Revenue reserves – Created from operational profits and used for general business needs.
  • Capital reserves – Set aside for long-term investments or major financial obligations.

terms.name: Transfer out of reserve
terms.definition:
A transfer out of reserve refers to the movement of funds from a designated reserve account to another financial account or expenditure category. This process typically results in a decrease in the reserve balance, as the funds are allocated for specific purposes such as paying member benefits, covering expenses, or funding projects.

In accounting and financial reporting, transfers out of reserves are recorded to ensure transparency and compliance with Australian Accounting Standards. These transfers may be governed by statutory or discretionary reserve policies, depending on whether the funds are legally required to be used for a particular purpose or allocated at the discretion of an organization.

terms.name: Transfers
terms.definition: Includes the transfer of assets as part of an SFT, member consent transfer, Intra-fund Transfer or Transfer of assets out of the fund.

terms.name: Transition To Retirement
terms.definition: A strategy for older people to reduce their working hours without reducing their income but topping up part-time income with a regular 'income stream' from their super savings.

terms.name: Transition to Retirement Income Stream
terms.definition:
A Transition to Retirement Income Stream (TRIS) is a type of superannuation pension that allows individuals to access their superannuation savings while still working, provided they have reached their preservation age. A TRIS helps people ease into retirement by supplementing their income while they continue working part-time or full-time.

Under TRIS, there are annual withdrawal limits, and the tax treatment varies depending on the individual's age. Once a person fully retires or meets a condition of release, their TRIS can be converted into a standard retirement income stream, which may offer tax benefits

terms.name: Transition to retirement pension
terms.definition:
A transition to retirement (TTR) pension allows individuals who have reached their preservation age (between 55 and 60, depending on birth year) to access their superannuation savings while continuing to work.

This strategy enables people to:

  • Reduce work hours while maintaining income.
  • Boost super savings through tax-effective contributions.
  • Ease into retirement without fully stopping work.

terms.name: Trust Administration System
terms.definition: Trust Administration System

terms.name: Trustee
terms.definition: Name of the trustee overseeing the fund

terms.name: Trustee Benefit Review Team
terms.definition: A Trustee Benefit Review Team typically refers to a group responsible for evaluating and overseeing the benefits provided by a trust or similar financial arrangement. This team may include trustees, legal advisors, and financial experts who work together to ensure that the benefits align with the trust's objectives and comply with legal and fiduciary responsibilities

terms.name: Trustee Directed Products
terms.definition: Trustee-directed products are products where the trustee has control over the design of the investment strategy of the product.

terms.name: Trustee Levy
terms.definition: This fee is charged monthly as a percentage of your total account balance. The Trustee Levy covers some of the costs related to the running of the MLC Super Fund. It is paid into the MLC Super Fund general reserve.

terms.name: Unallocated contributions
terms.definition: Unallocated contributions refer to contributions made to a superannuation fund that have not yet been assigned to a specific member's account. These contributions are temporarily held in an unallocated contributions account (UCA), often used in self-managed superannuation funds (SMSFs) to manage contribution timing and tax strategies.

terms.name: Unclaimed Super Monies
terms.definition: Super funds are required to report and pay their unclaimed super to the ATO.

terms.name: Unfranked CFI Dividends
terms.definition: Any part of an un franked dividend made by an Australian corporate tax entity that it declares to be a Conduit Foreign Income (CFI). Non-residents of Australia are not required to pay Australian tax on un franked CFI dividends.

terms.name: Unfranked Dividends
terms.definition: Dividends paid by an Australian company on which tax has not been paid. There is no franking credit attached to un franked dividends.

terms.name: Unified Data Platform
terms.definition:
Two key technology platforms provide am integrated, centralised and curated set of information assets to support analytics, reporting and authoritative master data.

UDP is an in-house developed platform that provides a centralised data repository in support of analytics and reporting.  While both MDM and UDP integrate data from multiple sources, UDP is designed specifically for analytics on multiple subject areas, including time-variant historical data, whereas MDM provides a single authoritative record for IFL data entities as a snapshot at a point in time.

terms.name: Unique Superannuation Identifier
terms.definition:
The Unique Superannuation Identifier (USI) is a distinctive code assigned to each superannuation product or fund within the Australian superannuation system. It is used to uniquely identify individual superannuation funds and their specific products for regulatory and transaction purposes, such as contributions, rollovers, and SuperStream data exchanges.

Purpose:
The USI facilitates standardized and efficient communication between employers, superannuation funds, and the Australian Tax Office (ATO) when processing contributions and other transactions. It ensures that payments and data transfers are directed to the correct superannuation product and fund.

Context:
In the context of the Australian superannuation system, the USI plays a critical role in simplifying and automating superannuation processes. It is essential for employers making superannuation contributions and for members consolidating super accounts through rollovers. Every superannuation fund or product that accepts contributions has a unique USI, which is necessary for compliance with SuperStream, the data standard for electronic transactions within the superannuation industry.

terms.name: Unique TFN
terms.definition: Represents each distinct tax file number recorded to identify a member, but which may be recorded multiple times across multiple member accounts.

terms.name: Unit
terms.definition: When you invest in a managed fund, you are allocated a number of units in each of the funds you have selected. Each of these units represents an equal part of the market value of the portfolio of investments that the managed fund holds.

terms.name: Unit Price
terms.definition: Each unit has a dollar value or 'unit price'. This unit price is calculated by taking the total market value of all of a managed fund's assets on a particular day, adjusting for any liabilities, and then dividing the net fund value by the total number of units held by all investors on that day.

terms.name: Unit Price Date
terms.definition:
The value of a managed fund's units is determined by the value of the fund's assets divided by the number of issued units. This is known as the Unit Price.
The Unit Price Date is the date that the value of the fund's assets were valued and the Unit price subsequently calculated.

terms.name: Unit Trust
terms.definition: A unit trust is a type of investment fund where individuals pool their money together, and a fund manager invests it in various assets, such as stocks, bonds, or property. The trust is divided into units, and investors hold these units, which represent their share of the fund. The value of the units fluctuates based on the performance of the investments. Unit trusts are popular for their diversification and ease of entry into the market.

terms.name: United Kingdom Transfer
terms.definition: This refers to transferring money within the UK or internationally to or from the UK, such as a bank transfer, wire transfer, or SWIFT payment.

terms.name: Unrestricted non-Preserved amount
terms.definition: UNP benefits do not require a condition of release to be met, and may be paid on demand by the member. They include, for example, benefits for which a member has previously satisfied a condition of release and decided to keep the money in the super fund.

terms.name: Unrestricted Non-preserved Amount
terms.definition: The unrestricted non-preserved amount is the portion of the member’s benefit that can be accessed at any time.

terms.name: Unrestricted Non-Preserved Amounts
terms.definition: Can be accessed at any time in whole or in part (subject to the rules of your fund). These benefits can be paid out to a member/holder at any time on demand, irrespective of age, employment situation or financial position, providing the fund or RSA rules allow the payment. These are generally benefits that the member has already become entitled to be paid but has voluntarily decided to keep within the superannuation system.

terms.name: Unsatisfactory financial position
terms.definition: An unsatisfactory financial position is defined under both prudential standards and legislation, particularly for defined benefit funds.

terms.name: Unstructured Data
terms.definition:
Information that does not conform to a pre-defined inherent information structure. Contrasted with Structured Data.

While unstructured data may be stored in a file or location that itself has a predefined structure (e.g. a Word document conforms to the file structure defined by Microsoft for Word file formats), that file structure doesn’t constrain the way the information itself is structured.

As an example, a written document may have a standard structure using sections, headings, paragraphs and words, etc. However, the actual information contained within that document format can be structured in any way the author sees fit. Two documents will have fundamentally different information structures for their content, even if they use the same document template.

terms.name: Unusual Activity
terms.definition: Client activity that occurs or is attempted which is out of character for what would be considered of a normal nature for that client.

terms.name: Upfront Fee
terms.definition: In the Financial Advice context, upfront fees or initial fees are not deductible.

terms.name: Upper quartile
terms.definition: This is a mathematical term which describes the data point which is at the 75th % point of the total number of data points in an data set

terms.name: User Acceptance Testing
terms.definition: UAT is testing from the perspective of the end user (investor, adviser, staff). It focuses on business process and use-ability. Ensures the changes statisfies the business need and is fit for intended purpose - predominately manual testing.

terms.name: User Level Limits
terms.definition: The limits in terms of the amount is to be defined for the user in terms of purchase, requisition and approvals

terms.name: Variable
terms.definition: The index used to locate the relevant factor.

terms.name: Variable Basis
terms.definition: The type of index used.

terms.name: Vested Benefit
terms.definition: Vested benefits refer to the portion of a member’s superannuation entitlements that they are legally entitled to receive if they were to leave the fund at a given point in time.

terms.name: Vesting period
terms.definition: The Period which an employee must wait before the value which has been offered under an LTI can be rewarded, depending on whether the award has met the conditions

terms.name: Visa Entitlement Verification Online
terms.definition: VEVO checks used to validate the status of working rights & any associated employment conditions with the Department of Immigration

terms.name: Vital Record
terms.definition:
Vital records are those records, without which agencies could not continue to operate, are irreplaceable, or would require significant resources to recreate.
 
They contain information essential to effectively restore the agency’s business operations during, or following an exceptional event (e.g. network failure, a disaster). Vital records protect the assets and interests of an agency, its clients and stakeholders, and are usually associated with the organisation’s infrastructure, legal and financial matters.

terms.name: Viva Engage
terms.definition: This is our internal corporate social network where we share information and promote employee connection and engagement. We have a number of communities groups where we communicate important business messages, including exec messages, Diversity and Inclusion, workplace and office, Community.

terms.name: Volume hire
terms.definition: Recruiting in bulk for example contact centre recruitment

terms.name: Volume Weighted Average Price
terms.definition: A "VWAP" takes the average price of shares over a pre-determined period to determine the price of performance rights for Insignia Financials' Equity Plans

terms.name: Voluntary Contributions
terms.definition: A voluntary contribution is money that you add to your super from your after-tax income or other money that you can invest.

terms.name: Voucher In / Cash Out
terms.definition:
Voucher Input – Cash Out transaction

This code is used in some trust‑accounting systems to classify a particular type of trust ledger transaction, usually one where:

Money is paid out of the trust account, and
The transaction is supported by a voucher or instruction.

Why it appears in reports

Systems often group transactions by type (e.g., RECEIPT, PAYMENT, JOURNAL, VICO). A VICO transaction date is simply the date that this voucher‑based cash‑out entry was processed.

terms.name: Wage growth
terms.definition:
Wage growth refers to the increase in wages over time, typically measured as a percentage. It can be expressed in nominal terms (without adjusting for inflation) or real terms (adjusted for inflation).

In macroeconomics, wage growth is a key indicator of economic health, reflecting changes in purchasing power and living standards. Higher wage growth can signal strong labor demand, while sluggish wage growth may indicate economic stagnation or low productivity

terms.name: Wage Type Reporter
terms.definition: Wage type report produced from ECP

terms.name: Waiting Period
terms.definition: Details the waiting period that applies before an SCI claim can be paid.

terms.name: Waiver of Premiums
terms.definition: While you receive monthly benefits, you don’t pay any Income Protection insurance premiums.

terms.name: Wealth Resolve
terms.definition: The WR team is a team of Relationship Managers primarily dedicated to improving the adviser's experience. WR team are able to assist with complex queries and calculations for advisers.

terms.full_name: Weighted average term of defined benefit liabilities
terms.name: Weighted average term of defined benefit liabilities
terms.definition: The weighted average term of defined benefit liabilities refers to the average duration of expected future benefit payments, weighted by the present value of those payments. It helps assess the sensitivity of liabilities to changes in discount rates and is commonly used in actuarial calculations for pension and employee benefit plans

terms.name: Whole of income cap
terms.definition: the whole-of-income cap has been set at $180,000 since its introduction, and it is not indexed. The cap is reduced by any other taxable income earned in the income tax year either before or after receiving the ETP.

terms.name: Will
terms.definition: A Will specifies how a person’s assets are to be divided when they die and nominates an Executor who is responsible for looking after their estate.

terms.name: Withdrawal
terms.definition: Withdrawal of funds from super or pension can be made if legislative conditions of release are met.

terms.name: Withdrawal Benefit
terms.definition: The withdrawal benefit is the account balance adjusted for fees and taxes applicable on closure, or that may not yet be charged.

terms.name: Withholding Tax
terms.definition: Withholding tax is a government requirement where a portion of income, such as salaries, dividends, interest, or royalties, is deducted (or "withheld") at the source before the recipient receives it. This amount is then paid directly to the tax authority. Essentially, it's a way to ensure that taxes are collected upfront, making it easier for authorities to track and enforce compliance.

terms.name: Work In Progress
terms.definition: A "work in progress" refers to something that is currently being developed, improved, or completed. It can apply to various contexts, such as projects, ideas, artistic creations, or even personal growth. The term suggests that while it is not yet finished, progress is being made toward completion.

terms.name: Work Item Number
terms.definition: A WIN is generated when a task has been raised for a process. Generally this WIN is associated with P&I products.

terms.name: Work Order Request Management System
terms.definition: A tech system to monitor requests, obsolete.

terms.name: Work Phone
terms.definition: The work phone number for when contacting the Employer -

terms.name: Work Schedule Rule
terms.definition: Work schedule Rule - work pattern

terms.name: Workplace Gender Equality Agency
terms.definition: The Workplace Gender Equality Agency is an Australian Government statutory agency responsible for promoting and improving gender equality in Australian workplaces.

terms.name: Workplace giving
terms.definition: Workplace giving

terms.name: Workplace Health & Safety
terms.definition: Workplace Health & Safety

terms.name: Workplace Relations
terms.definition: The Workplace Relations team within People & Culture

terms.name: Wrap
terms.definition: Client platform in C&T

terms.name: Yammer
terms.definition: This is our internal corporate social network where we share information and promote employee connection and engagement. We have a number of communities groups where we communicate important business messages, including exec messages, Diversity and Inclusion, workplace and office, Community.

terms.name: Year to Date
terms.definition: YTD (Year to Date) refers to the period from the beginning of the current calendar year (January 1) up to the present date. It is commonly used in financial reporting and performance analysis to measure progress or compare metrics over the current year.

terms.name: Your Future Your Super
terms.definition:
The Your Future Your Super (YFYS) performance test was introduced by the Australian Government, to hold superannuation trustees accountable for the performance of investment options offered to the members.

The test considers the combination of the following for each tested investment option:

Investment performance relative to a defined set of benchmark indices for the option’s asset allocation over the long-term – generally 10 years for FY24

Standard (disclosed) administration fees and costs charged for accessing the investment option via the fund for an account balance of $50,000 against the median charged for investment options tested for similar super funds – generally over the preceding 12 months

Underlying the Government initiated test is the desire to improve member outcomes which is an intent we share.

The Products that are currently tested are:

  • The performance test has applied to MySuper since 2021 
  • In 2023 it was expanded to include Trustee-Directed Products (TDPs) for: 
    • Master Trusts, referred to as non-platform products
    • Wraps, referred to as platform products
